$$P_{M,8} = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 5.3349 + 50{,}000 \times 16.0307$$

$$P_{M,8} = 8{,}00{,}235 + 8{,}01{,}535 = 16{,}01{,}770 \text{ ₹}$$

$$PW_8 = 30{,}00{,}000 + 16{,}01{,}770 - \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{2.143589}$$

$$PW_8 = 46{,}01{,}770 - 1{,}86{,}600 = 44{,}15{,}170 \text{ ₹}$$

$$EAC_8 = 44{,}15{,}170 \times 0.18744 \approx 8{,}27{,}656 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Step 5: Compare and conclude.**

$$EAC_6 = 8{,}46{,}248 \text{ ₹/year}, \quad EAC_8 = 8{,}27{,}656 \text{ ₹/year}$$

$$EAC_8 < EAC_6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Keep the bus for 8 years (from an EAC perspective).}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why?** Even though maintenance costs grow, the spreading of capital cost over a longer life plus the residual salvage value in Year 8 outweighs the rising operating costs. This counter-intuitive result is the **classic insight of EAC analysis** and is a common KTU exam question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Vehicle Disposal Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle in Active Service] --> B{Performance OK?}
    B -- No --> F[Initiate Disposal Process]
    B -- Yes --> C{Maintenance Cost > 60% of Depreciation?}
    C -- Yes --> F
    C -- No --> D{Compliance Fitness Valid?}
    D -- No --> F
    D -- Yes --> E{Strategic Replacement Age Reached?}
    E -- Yes --> F
    E -- No --> G[Continue in Service]
    G --> A
    F --> H{Resale Market Favorable?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Choose Resale Path]
    H -- No --> J[Choose Scrap / Salvage Path]
    I --> K[Auction / Trade-in / Private Sale]
    J --> L[Sell to Recycler or Scrap Dealer]
    K --> M[Update Fleet Register and Deregister]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Vehicle Removed from Fleet]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Vehicle Life Cycle Stages with Disposal Focus

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1: Acquisition] --> S2[Stage 2: Operation and Maintenance]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Performance Monitoring]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: Disposal Decision]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5a: Resale to Secondary Market]
    S4 --> S6[Stage 5b: Scrapping for Salvage]
    S5 --> S7[Stage 6: Deregistration and Documentation]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[Stage 7: Replacement Planning]
    S8 --> S1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fleet Disposal Management System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Fleet Operations Database] --> node2[Vehicle Performance Tracker]
    node2 --> node3[Cost Analysis Engine]
    node3 --> node4[Depreciation Calculator Module]
    node4 --> node5[Optimal Replacement Age Solver]
    node5 --> node6[Disposal Recommendation Generator]
    node6 --> node7{Decision: Resale or Scrap?}
    node7 -- Resale --> node8[Auction and Tender Module]
    node7 -- Scrap --> node9[Recycling and Salvage Module]
    node8 --> node10[Documentation and Deregistration Handler]
    node9 --> node10
    node10 --> node11[Fleet Register Updater]
    node11 --> node12[Finance and Accounting Sync]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note on Diagram 3:** This block architecture reflects modern **Fleet Management Information Systems (FMIS)** such as those used by **Geotab**, **Samsara**, and **Trimble**. The flow highlights that disposal is not an isolated event — it is a **data-driven output** of continuous monitoring, not a one-time managerial judgment.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024]
**Define vehicle disposal. List any four methods of vehicle disposal commonly adopted in fleet management.**

**Model Answer:**

Vehicle disposal is the planned process of retiring a vehicle from active fleet service and transferring ownership through sale, scrapping, or donation.

The four common methods are:
1. **Open Auction** — public sale to the highest bidder.
2. **Trade-in Exchange** — exchange with a dealer for a new vehicle.
3. **Sealed Tender** — closed-bid process used by government/PSU fleets.
4. **Scrap or Salvage** — sold for parts or metal to recyclers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always add a concluding line tying it back to the **fleet management context**. Definitions alone fetch only partial marks.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is meant by "salvage value" of a vehicle? How does it influence the depreciation calculation?**

**Model Answer:**

**Salvage value** is the estimated residual monetary worth of a vehicle at the end of its useful life in the fleet. It represents the amount the fleet owner expects to recover through resale or scrap.

**Influence on depreciation:** In the straight-line method, depreciation is computed as $D = (C - S) / N$. A higher salvage value $S$ reduces annual depreciation $D$, which in turn raises the book value and the resale price. Conversely, a vehicle with low salvage value depreciates faster on paper, prompting earlier disposal.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Reminder:** Part B questions in ESE (End Semester Examination) carry **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. You must answer **ONE full question** (both sub-parts) out of the choice provided.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **Straight-Line Depreciation (SLD)** and **Declining Balance Depreciation (DBD)** methods with suitable formulas. Compare their impact on the resale value of a vehicle over a 5-year period. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A logistics company purchases a delivery van for **₹ 15,00,000**. The expected useful life is **6 years**, with a salvage value of **₹ 3,00,000**. Calculate the annual depreciation, the book value at the end of Year 3, and the Resale Value Ratio at Year 3, using the straight-line method. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**SLD Formula:** $D = (C - S) / N$ and $BV_t = C - t \cdot D$.

SLD allocates an **equal depreciation amount** every year. The book value decreases linearly from $C$ to $S$ over $N$ years.

**DBD Formula:** $BV_t = C \cdot (1 - r)^t$.

DBD applies a **fixed percentage** to the *previous year's* book value, so depreciation is large in early years and small in later years.

**Comparison over 5 years (assume $C = 10,00,000$, $S = 1,00,000$, $r = 0.20$):**

| Year | SLD Book Value (₹) | DBD Book Value (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 10,00,000 | 10,00,000 |
| 1 | 8,20,000 | 8,00,000 |
| 2 | 6,40,000 | 6,40,000 |
| 3 | 4,60,000 | 5,12,000 |
| 4 | 2,80,000 | 4,09,600 |
| 5 | 1,00,000 | 3,27,680 |

> **Impact on Resale:** DBD preserves a **higher resale value** in later years but reflects a **steeper drop early on**, which mirrors real-world car depreciation. SLD is simpler but understates the actual loss in the early years.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** $C = 15,00,000$, $S = 3,00,000$, $N = 6$.

$$D = \frac{15{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000}{6} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{6} = 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Step 2:** Book Value at Year 3:

$$BV_3 = 15{,}00{,}000 - 3 \times 2{,}00{,}000 = 15{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 = 9{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 3:** Resale Value Ratio at Year 3:

$$RVR_3 = \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 60\%$$

**Conclusion:** The van retains **60%** of its original value at the end of Year 3.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the **factors that influence the resale value** of a fleet vehicle. How does each factor affect the pricing decision? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A state transport corporation is disposing of **50 buses** through a public auction. Each bus has a book value of **₹ 6,00,000** and an expected salvage of **₹ 1,50,000**. Auctioneer's commission is **5%** of the hammer price, and the corporation sets a reserve price **10% above** the salvage value. Calculate the **minimum bid per bus** the corporation should accept and the **total expected revenue** if all 50 buses are sold at the reserve price. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The resale value of a fleet vehicle is influenced by:

1. **Age and Odometer Reading** — Older vehicles with higher km command lower prices; this is the dominant factor.
2. **Brand and Model Reputation** — Brands like Toyota, Maruti retain value better than mass-market commercial brands.
3. **Maintenance History** — A well-documented service log increases buyer confidence and resale value.
4. **Body and Mechanical Condition** — Accident history, rust, engine health directly impact the price.
5. **Market Demand and Fuel Type** — Diesel SUVs had higher resale until 2020; petrol/CNG is now preferred in some segments. EVs have a more volatile resale curve.
6. **Regulatory Compliance** — Vehicles meeting BS-VI norms or with valid fitness certificates fetch higher prices.
7. **Color, Aesthetics, and Accessories** — Neutral colors (white, silver) have a broader resale market.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Compute the reserve price per bus.

$$\text{Reserve Price} = S + 10\% \text{ of } S = 1{,}50{,}000 + 15{,}000 = 1{,}65{,}000 \text{ ₹/bus}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the net amount received by the corporation after the auctioneer's 5% commission.

The commission is deducted from the hammer price. If the bus is sold at the reserve price, the gross receipt per bus is ₹1,65,000, and the commission is:

$$\text{Commission} = 5\% \times 1{,}65{,}000 = 8{,}250 \text{ ₹}$$

$$\text{Net Receipt per Bus} = 1{,}65{,}000 - 8{,}250 = 1{,}56{,}750 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 3:** Compute total expected revenue for 50 buses.

$$\text{Total Revenue} = 50 \times 1{,}56{,}750 = 78{,}37{,}500 \text{ ₹}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "reserve price" with "minimum acceptable bid."** The reserve price *is* the minimum bid below which the auctioneer cannot sell, but the *net revenue* must deduct the commission. Many students forget the commission and lose **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Always state the assumptions** — e.g., "Assuming all buses are sold at the reserve price with no bidding war."
> 3. **Unit consistency** — Express revenue in **lakhs or crores** if the number is large; this shows professional presentation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memorize Before the Exam:**

- **Disposal** = retirement from service; **Resale** = transfer to a secondary market user.
- **Six disposal decision gates:** Performance, Cost, Compliance, Economic (EAC), Strategic, Market.
- **Six disposal methods:** Auction, Tender, Trade-in, Private Sale, Scrap, Donation.
- **Straight-Line Depreciation (SLD):** $D = (C - S) / N$ — constant annual drop, simple but unrealistic for vehicles.
- **Declining Balance (DBD):** $BV_t = C(1 - r)^t$ — realistic for vehicles that lose value fast in early years.
- **Resale Value Ratio (RVR):** $P_{resale} / P_{original} \times 100$ — key metric reported in used-car markets.
- **Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC):** Use **CRF** and arithmetic gradient present worth to compute it; the **lowest EAC** indicates the **optimal replacement age**.
- **Auction reserve price** must factor in auctioneer's commission when computing net revenue.
- **Salvage value** is *not* the same as **scrap value** — salvage assumes continued use; scrap is for end-of-life.
- **Companies Act, 2013** + **Income Tax Act, 1961** govern the *legal* depreciation in India; KTU tests the *engineering economic* depreciation, not the tax one.
- **Indian context tip:** KSRTC, BMTC, and BEST buses are typically auctioned at **8–10 years of age** or after **8,00,000 km**, whichever comes first.
- **Real-world link:** Use **Cost per km** as a practical fleet KPI — when it rises above the industry benchmark (e.g., ₹6–₹8/km for cars), disposal should be evaluated.
- **Avoid in answers:** Confusing "book value" with "market value" — book value is an accounting concept, market value is a real-world pricing concept.
- **Use in answers:** Always convert final answers to **₹ lakhs** for readability; examiners reward clean presentation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fleet Operations - Fleet Scheduling and Routing
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fleet Scheduling** refers to the systematic planning and allocation of vehicles within a fleet to specific tasks, time windows, routes, and operational duties over a defined planning horizon (typically daily, weekly, or shift-based). It establishes *which vehicle*, *which driver*, *which departure time*, and *which load assignment*.

**Fleet Routing** is the computational and analytical process of determining the optimal sequence of stops (customers, delivery points, pickup locations, or service nodes) that each vehicle must visit, given a set of constraints such as vehicle capacity, time windows, service durations, and distance/cost minimization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEAUT633 - Module 2):**
> Fleet Scheduling & Routing is the operational backbone of transport management, encompassing the *Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)*, its variants (CVRP, VRPTW, DVRP), classical heuristic algorithms (Clarke-Wright Savings, Nearest Neighbor, Sweep), and the mathematical programming formulations used to minimize total fleet operating cost while satisfying service-level constraints.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Morning Newspaper Delivery"**
>
> Imagine you run a newspaper distribution agency in Kochi with **3 delivery vans** and **15 customer locations** scattered across the city. Every morning before sunrise, you must decide:
>
> 1. *Which van serves which customers?* (Fleet Scheduling)
> 2. *In what order should each van visit its assigned customers?* (Routing)
> 3. *Which routes minimize fuel, time, and driver fatigue?* (Optimization)
>
> If you simply assigned vans randomly, one van might end up driving 80 km while another drives 15 km. **Fleet Scheduling and Routing** is the scientific discipline that prevents such imbalance — it uses mathematical models and algorithms to produce the *cheapest, fastest, and most balanced* delivery plan.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Board Favourites)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Fleet Operating Cost | $C_T$ | **₹ / day** | Sum of fuel, driver wage, depreciation, and maintenance cost |
| Vehicle Capacity | $Q_v$ | **kg / units** | Maximum load a vehicle $v$ can carry |
| Service Time at Customer $i$ | $s_i$ | **minutes** | Time spent loading/unloading at stop $i$ |
| Travel Time between $i$ and $j$ | $t_{ij}$ | **minutes** | Driving duration along arc $(i,j)$ |
| Distance between $i$ and $j$ | $d_{ij}$ | **km** | Shortest road distance |
| Time Window at Customer $i$ | $[e_i, l_i]$ | **[hours]** | Earliest and latest permissible service start |

---

## 1.4 Classification of Routing Problems (Hierarchy)

The hierarchy of routing complexity, from simple to advanced, is:

> [!TIP]
> **TSP → VRP → CVRP → VRPTW → DVRP → VRPPD**
>
> - **TSP** — Traveling Salesman Problem (single vehicle, all customers)
> - **VRP** — Vehicle Routing Problem (multiple vehicles, capacity unlimited)
> - **CVRP** — Capacitated VRP (capacity constraints added)
> - **VRPTW** — VRP with Time Windows
> - **DVRP** — Dynamic VRP (real-time updates)
> - **VRPPD** — VRP with Pickup and Delivery

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) — Conceptual Breakdown

The classical VRP can be understood through **five foundational pillars**:

### Pillar 1 — The Objective Function
The planner's goal is to minimize total cost. Mathematically, the objective is to find a set of routes $R = \{R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_m\}$ such that:

$$Z = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \sum_{(i,j) \in R_k} c_{ij}$$

where $c_{ij}$ represents the cost of traversing arc $(i,j)$.

### Pillar 2 — The Decision Variables
Two binary decision variables govern VRP formulations:
- $x_{ijk} = 1$ if vehicle $k$ travels directly from node $i$ to node $j$
- $y_{ik} = 1$ if customer $i$ is served by vehicle $k$

### Pillar 3 — Capacity Constraints
Each vehicle cannot exceed its carrying capacity:

$$\sum_{i \in C} q_i \cdot y_{ik} \leq Q_v \quad \forall k \in \{1, \ldots, m\}$$

where $q_i$ is the demand of customer $i$.

### Pillar 4 — Visit-Once Constraint
Every customer must be served exactly once:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{m} y_{ik} = 1 \quad \forall i \in C$$

### Pillar 5 — Flow Conservation
What goes into a node must come out:

$$\sum_{j \in N} x_{ijk} = \sum_{j \in N} x_{jik} = y_{ik} \quad \forall i \in C, \forall k$$

## 2.2 The Clarke-Wright Savings Algorithm — Stepwise Logic

The **Clarke-Wright Savings Method** (1964) is the most commonly tested heuristic in KTU board examinations.

**The Core 'Why':**
When a single depot serves all customers individually (separate trips), total distance is:
$$D_1 = 2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_{0i}$$
where 0 is the depot.

When two customers $i$ and $j$ are merged into one trip:
$$D_2 = d_{0i} + d_{ij} + d_{j0}$$

**The Savings** from merging:
$$S_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A higher $S_{ij}$ value indicates stronger justification to merge customers $i$ and $j$ on the same route. The algorithm sorts all $S_{ij}$ values in descending order and greedily merges compatible routes.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$ | Clarke-Wright Savings | Solving small VRPs manually |
| 2 | $Z_{\min} = \sum_k \sum_{(i,j)\in R_k} c_{ij} x_{ijk}$ | VRP objective | Mathematical formulation |
| 3 | $TT_{route} = \sum t_{ij} + \sum s_i$ | Total route time | Time-window feasibility check |
| 4 | $\rho = \frac{\sum q_i}{m \cdot Q_v}$ | Load utilization ratio | Fleet efficiency KPI |
| 5 | $D_{avg} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{k=1}^{m} D_k$ | Avg route distance | Balanced routing metric |
| 6 | $C_{vehicle\text{-}km} = \frac{C_T}{\sum_k D_k}$ | Cost per vehicle-km | Cost benchmarking |
| 7 | $e_i \leq a_i \leq l_i$ | Time window check | VRPTW feasibility |
| 8 | $L_k = \sum_{i \in R_k} q_i$ | Route load | Capacity constraint |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Applications of Fleet Routing:**
> - **E-commerce delivery** (Amazon, Flipkart) — last-mile optimization
> - **Public transit planning** — KSRTC bus route design
> - **Waste collection** — municipal solid waste routing in Kochi Corporation
> - **Fuel distribution** — IOC/BPCL tanker routing
> - **School bus routing** — private fleet operators in Kerala
> - **Cold chain logistics** — vaccine and perishable food transport

The engineering utility lies in **operational cost reduction (15–30%)**, **carbon footprint reduction**, and **service level improvement**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Clarke-Wright Savings Formula

We begin with the **pre-merge** scenario: a dedicated round-trip from depot 0 to each customer.

**Step 1 — Individual Trip Cost:**
For customer $i$, a dedicated round trip costs:
$$D_i^{single} = d_{0i} + d_{i0} = 2\, d_{0i}$$
(since $d_{ij} = d_{ji}$ for symmetric road networks).

For two customers $i$ and $j$ served separately:
$$D_{i+j}^{separate} = 2\,d_{0i} + 2\,d_{0j}$$

**Step 2 — Combined Trip Cost:**
When customers $i$ and $j$ are merged on one trip:
$$D_{i+j}^{merged} = d_{0i} + d_{ij} + d_{j0}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Savings:**
$$S_{ij} = D_{i+j}^{separate} - D_{i+j}^{merged}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting and Expanding:**
$$S_{ij} = (2\,d_{0i} + 2\,d_{0j}) - (d_{0i} + d_{ij} + d_{j0})$$

**Step 5 — Algebraic Simplification:**
$$S_{ij} = 2\,d_{0i} + 2\,d_{0j} - d_{0i} - d_{ij} - d_{j0}$$
$$S_{ij} = d_{0i} + 2\,d_{0j} - d_{ij} - d_{j0}$$

Wait — careful re-evaluation since the road network is **symmetric** ($d_{0j} = d_{j0}$):

$$S_{ij} = d_{0i} + 2\,d_{j0} - d_{ij} - d_{j0}$$
$$S_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{j0} - d_{ij}$$
$$\boxed{S_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}}$$

This matches the classical Clarke-Wright formulation.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> [!TIP]
> **Example Setup:** Depot at node **0**. Three customers at nodes **A, B, C**.
> Distance matrix (km):
>
> | From \ To | 0 | A | B | C |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | **0** | 0 | 8 | 12 | 15 |
> | **A** | 8 | 0 | 6 | 10 |
> | **B** | 12 | 6 | 0 | 9 |
> | **C** | 15 | 10 | 9 | 0 |
>
> Each customer demand $q_A = q_B = q_C = 10$ units. Vehicle capacity $Q_v = 20$ units.

**Step 1 — Compute all savings $S_{ij}$:**

For pair (A,B):
$$S_{AB} = d_{0A} + d_{0B} - d_{AB} = 8 + 12 - 6 = 14 \text{ km}$$

For pair (A,C):
$$S_{AC} = d_{0A} + d_{0C} - d_{AC} = 8 + 15 - 10 = 13 \text{ km}$$

For pair (B,C):
$$S_{BC} = d_{0B} + d_{0C} - d_{BC} = 12 + 15 - 9 = 18 \text{ km}$$

**Step 2 — Sort savings in descending order:**

| Rank | Pair | Savings (km) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | (B, C) | **18** |
| 2 | (A, B) | **14** |
| 3 | (A, C) | **13** |

**Step 3 — Initial Solution (separate trips):**
Routes: $0 \to A \to 0$, $0 \to B \to 0$, $0 \to C \to 0$
Total distance: $2(8 + 12 + 15) = 70$ km

**Step 4 — Greedy Merge #1 (B,C):**
Merge B and C → Route: $0 \to B \to C \to 0$
Load = $q_B + q_C = 10 + 10 = 20 \leq Q_v = 20$ ✓ (exactly at capacity)
Distance: $12 + 9 + 15 = 36$ km

**Step 5 — Greedy Merge #2 (A,B):**
Attempt to merge A into the route. Two options:
- Option 1: $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ — Load = $30 > 20$ ✗ (capacity violated)
- Option 2: $0 \to B \to C \to A \to 0$ — Load = $30 > 20$ ✗ (capacity violated)

Since A cannot be inserted into the (B,C) route, A starts its own route.

**Step 6 — Final Solution:**
- Route 1: $0 \to A \to 0$ — Distance = 16 km
- Route 2: $0 \to B \to C \to 0$ — Distance = 36 km
- **Total = 52 km** (down from 70 km — a 25.7% reduction)

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Comparison for KTU

| Criterion | Nearest Neighbor | Clarke-Wright | Sweep Method | Integer Programming |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Solution Quality** | Poor (greedy trap) | Good | Moderate | Optimal |
| **Computation Time** | Very Fast | Fast | Fast | Exponential |
| **Problem Size** | Up to 100 nodes | Up to 50 nodes | Up to 30 nodes | Up to 15 nodes |
| **Capacity Handling** | Manual check | Built-in | Built-in | Built-in |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ |
| **Best For** | Quick estimate | Industrial practice | Geographic clusters | Theoretical optimum |

## 3.4 Capacity Utilization Calculation

For the final solution above, the **load utilization ratio** is:

$$\rho_1 = \frac{q_A}{Q_v} = \frac{10}{20} = 0.50 = 50\%$$

$$\rho_2 = \frac{q_B + q_C}{Q_v} = \frac{20}{20} = 1.00 = 100\%$$

$$\bar{\rho} = \frac{\rho_1 + \rho_2}{2} = \frac{0.50 + 1.00}{2} = 0.75 = 75\%$$

This indicates **suboptimal fleet utilization** — Route 1 is underloaded. A better solution would re-balance the loads.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fleet Scheduling & Routing Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Customer Demand Data] --> procA[Demand Aggregation]
    inputB[Vehicle Availability] --> procA
    inputC[Time Window Constraints] --> procA
    inputD[Depot Location] --> procA
    procA --> procB[VRP Formulation]
    procB --> procC{Problem Size}
    procC -- Small <=15 nodes --> procD[Integer Programming]
    procC -- Medium 16 to 50 --> procE[Clarke-Wright Savings]
    procC -- Large 50 plus --> procF[Metaheuristic GA or Tabu Search]
    procD --> procG[Optimal Routes]
    procE --> procG
    procF --> procG
    procG --> outA[Route Assignment]
    procG --> outB[Driver Allocation]
    procG --> outC[Schedule Dispatch]
    outA --> kpiA[Cost per km]
    outB --> kpiB[Driver Hours]
    outC --> kpiC[On Time Performance]
```

## 4.2 Clarke-Wright Savings Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Read Distance Matrix and Demands] --> stepA[Initialize each customer as a separate route]
    stepA --> stepB[Compute all pairwise savings Sij]
    stepB --> stepC[Sort savings in descending order]
    stepC --> stepD{Take highest savings pair}
    stepD --> stepE{Capacity constraint satisfied}
    stepE -- Yes --> stepF{Same route constraint}
    stepE -- No --> stepD
    stepF -- Yes --> stepG[Merge routes ij]
    stepF -- No --> stepD
    stepG --> stepH{All savings processed}
    stepH -- No --> stepD
    stepH -- Yes --> stepI[Output final route set]
```

## 4.3 Fleet Scheduling Time Horizon Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1[Long Term Planning] --> L2[Months to Years]
    L2 --> L3[Fleet Sizing Decisions]
    L3 --> L4[Vehicle Purchase or Lease]
    L4 --> L5[Network Design]
    L1 --> M1[Medium Term Planning]
    M1 --> M2[Weeks to Months]
    M2 --> M3[Driver Scheduling]
    M3 --> M4[Maintenance Planning]
    M4 --> M5[Budget Allocation]
    M1 --> S1[Short Term Planning]
    S1 --> S2[Days to Week]
    S2 --> S3[Daily Route Assignment]
    S3 --> S4[Load Planning]
    S4 --> S5[Dispatch Sequence]
```

## 4.4 VRP Variant Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Does each vehicle have capacity limit}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Do customers specify time windows}
    q1 -- No --> base[Base VRP]
    q2 -- Yes --> q3{Are demands revealed dynamically}
    q2 -- No --> cvrp[Capacitated VRP]
    q3 -- Yes --> dvrp[Dynamic VRP]
    q3 -- No --> q4{Both pickup and delivery needed}
    q4 -- Yes --> vrppd[VRP with Pickup Delivery]
    q4 -- No --> vrptw[VRP with Time Windows]
```

## 4.5 Input-Process-Output Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT
        I1[Customer Locations]
        I2[Demand Quantities]
        I3[Vehicle Capacity]
        I4[Time Windows]
        I5[Cost Matrix]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS
        P1[Data Validation]
        P2[Distance Matrix Build]
        P3[Algorithm Selection]
        P4[Route Construction]
        P5[Constraint Verification]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT
        O1[Route Maps]
        O2[Vehicle Assignments]
        O3[Schedule Timetable]
        O4[KPI Report]
    end
    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P1
    I4 --> P1
    I5 --> P2
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> O1
    P5 --> O2
    P5 --> O3
    P5 --> O4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP). List any two variants of VRP.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)** is a combinatorial optimization problem that seeks to determine the optimal set of routes for a fleet of vehicles to service a set of customers, starting and ending at a central depot, while minimizing total cost (distance, time, or fuel) and respecting operational constraints such as vehicle capacity. *(2 marks)*

Two variants of VRP are:
1. **Capacitated VRP (CVRP)** — vehicles have a maximum carrying capacity constraint.
2. **VRP with Time Windows (VRPTW)** — each customer must be serviced within a specified time interval $[e_i, l_i]$. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Clarke-Wright savings formula and explain its physical significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Clarke-Wright savings for merging customers $i$ and $j$ on the same route is:

$$S_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Physical Significance:** *(2 marks)*
- $d_{0i} + d_{0j}$ is the cost of serving customers $i$ and $j$ via two separate depot round-trips.
- $d_{ij}$ is the cost of serving them on a single combined trip.
- A **higher $S_{ij}$** indicates that merging these customers yields a **greater reduction in total travel**, so the algorithm prioritizes such pairs first.
- $S_{ij} = 0$ implies no benefit, and negative values mean merging would *increase* cost.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Formulate the mathematical model of the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP) with clear notation. State the objective function and all constraints. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Nearest Neighbor heuristic algorithm for solving TSP/VRP. State **two** advantages and **two** disadvantages of this method. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Solve the following VRP instance using the Clarke-Wright Savings algorithm. **(7 marks)**

Depot = node 0. Customers: A, B, C, D. Vehicle capacity = 30 units.

Distance matrix (km):

| | 0 | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 |
| A | 10 | 0 | 8 | 12 | 18 |
| B | 15 | 8 | 0 | 10 | 14 |
| C | 20 | 12 | 10 | 0 | 9 |
| D | 25 | 18 | 14 | 9 | 0 |

Demands: $q_A = 12$, $q_B = 15$, $q_C = 8$, $q_D = 10$.

**(b)** What are the limitations of the Clarke-Wright Savings algorithm? Suggest **two** improvements. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Solutions to Part B — Question A

#### Solution to A(a) — CVRP Mathematical Formulation

**Sets and Parameters:**
- $N = \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ = set of customers
- $V = \{1, 2, \ldots, m\}$ = set of vehicles
- $N_0 = N \cup \{0\}$ = customers plus depot (node 0)
- $c_{ij}$ = cost of traveling from $i$ to $j$
- $q_i$ = demand of customer $i$
- $Q_v$ = capacity of vehicle $v$

**Decision Variables:**
$$x_{ijv} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if vehicle } v \text{ travels from } i \text{ to } j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

$$y_{iv} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if customer } i \text{ is served by vehicle } v \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Objective Function:**
$$\min Z = \sum_{v \in V} \sum_{i \in N_0} \sum_{j \in N_0} c_{ij} \cdot x_{ijv} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Constraints:**

**(C1) Each customer is served exactly once:**
$$\sum_{v \in V} y_{iv} = 1 \quad \forall i \in N \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**(C2) Vehicle capacity:**
$$\sum_{i \in N} q_i \cdot y_{iv} \leq Q_v \quad \forall v \in V \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**(C3) Flow conservation:**
$$\sum_{j \in N_0} x_{ijv} = \sum_{j \in N_0} x_{jiv} = y_{iv} \quad \forall i \in N, \forall v \in V \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**(C4) Each route starts and ends at depot:**
$$\sum_{j \in N} x_{0jv} = 1, \quad \sum_{i \in N} x_{i0v} = 1 \quad \forall v \in V \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**(C5) Binary conditions:**
$$x_{ijv} \in \{0, 1\}, \quad y_{iv} \in \{0, 1\} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Verification of constraints:** Capacity (C2) ensures no overloading, while (C1) and (C3) together guarantee a closed tour from depot. *(1 mark for clarity and labeling)*

---

#### Solution to A(b) — Nearest Neighbor Heuristic

**Algorithm Steps:** *(4 marks)*

1. Start at the depot (node 0).
2. From the current node, examine all unvisited customers.
3. Move to the **nearest unvisited customer** (minimum $d_{ij}$ or $t_{ij}$).
4. Mark the visited customer.
5. Repeat steps 2–4 until all customers are visited.
6. Return to the depot.

**Pseudo-Flow:** Depot → nearest → nearest of remaining → ... → depot.

**Advantages:** *(2 marks)*
1. **Computationally simple** — $O(n^2)$ time complexity, suitable for large fleets.
2. **Easy to implement** — requires only a distance matrix and no optimization solver.

**Disadvantages:** *(1 mark each)*
1. **Greedy myopia** — early decisions may lead to globally poor solutions; the algorithm gets trapped in local optima.
2. **Asymmetric routes** — tends to produce unbalanced routes with widely varying lengths.

---

### 5.4 Solutions to Part B — Question B

#### Solution to B(a) — Clarke-Wright Worked Solution

**Step 1 — Compute all pairwise savings $S_{ij}$:** *(3 marks)*

$$S_{AB} = d_{0A} + d_{0B} - d_{AB} = 10 + 15 - 8 = 17 \text{ km}$$

$$S_{AC} = d_{0A} + d_{0C} - d_{AC} = 10 + 20 - 12 = 18 \text{ km}$$

$$S_{AD} = d_{0A} + d_{0D} - d_{AD} = 10 + 25 - 18 = 17 \text{ km}$$

$$S_{BC} = d_{0B} + d_{0C} - d_{BC} = 15 + 20 - 10 = 25 \text{ km}$$

$$S_{BD} = d_{0B} + d_{0D} - d_{BD} = 15 + 25 - 14 = 26 \text{ km}$$

$$S_{CD} = d_{0C} + d_{0D} - d_{CD} = 20 + 25 - 9 = 36 \text{ km}$$

**Step 2 — Sort savings (descending):** *(1 mark)*

| Rank | Pair | $S_{ij}$ (km) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | (C, D) | **36** |
| 2 | (B, D) | **26** |
| 3 | (B, C) | **25** |
| 4 | (A, C) | **18** |
| 5 | (A, B) | **17** |
| 6 | (A, D) | **17** |

**Step 3 — Initial routes (separate trips):**
Routes: $0\to A\to 0$, $0\to B\to 0$, $0\to C\to 0$, $0\to D\to 0$
Total = $2(10+15+20+25) = 140$ km

**Step 4 — Greedy merges:** *(2 marks)*

**Merge #1: (C, D)** → Route $0\to C\to D\to 0$. Load = $8+10 = 18 \leq 30$ ✓
Distance = $20+9+25 = 54$ km

**Merge #2: (B, D)** → Insert B into route. Options:
- $0\to B\to C\to D\to 0$: Load = $15+8+10 = 33 > 30$ ✗
- $0\to C\to D\to B\to 0$: Load = $33 > 30$ ✗
So B starts a new route: $0\to B\to 0$.

**Merge #3: (B, C)** — B already isolated; merging with the (C,D) route violates capacity as shown. Skip.

**Merge #4: (A, C)** — Try $0\to A\to C\to D\to 0$: Load = $12+8+10 = 30 \leq 30$ ✓
Distance = $10+12+9+25 = 56$ km

**Step 5 — Final Solution:** *(1 mark)*

- **Route 1:** $0\to A\to C\to D\to 0$ — Distance = 56 km, Load = 30
- **Route 2:** $0\to B\to 0$ — Distance = 30 km, Load = 15

**Total Distance = 86 km** (saving = $140 - 86 = 54$ km, ~38.6% reduction)

**Capacity Utilization:**
$$\bar{\rho} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{30}{30} + \frac{15}{30}\right) = \frac{1+0.5}{2} = 0.75 = 75\%$$

---

#### Solution to B(b) — Limitations and Improvements

**Limitations of Clarke-Wright:** *(4 marks)*

1. **Capacity feasibility checked late** — the algorithm merges greedily by savings without considering route load until after the merge is proposed, leading to infeasible merges and sub-utilization.
2. **Greedy trap** — once a merge is performed, it is never undone; the algorithm cannot recover from early poor choices.
3. **No time-window handling** — the basic CW method ignores customer time windows, making it unsuitable for VRPTW problems.
4. **Ignores geographical clustering** — customers on opposite sides of the depot may be merged even when geographically inefficient.
5. **Initial seed rule sensitivity** — different starting rules (largest seed, farthest seed, time-based seed) can produce different solutions.

**Two Improvements:** *(3 marks)*

1. **Modified Savings with Time Penalty:** Introduce a time-window penalty factor $\lambda_{ij}$ into the savings formula so that infeasible merges are penalized early:
$$S'_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij} - \lambda \cdot \max(0, a_i + s_i + t_{ij} - l_j)$$

2. **Two-Phase Algorithm:** Run Clarke-Wright first, then apply a **2-opt local search** post-processing step to each route to remove crossing arcs and shorten distance. This hybrid CW + 2-opt approach typically improves solution quality by 5–10%.

3. *(Alternative)* Use **Multiple-Seed CW** — run the algorithm with several initial seed customers (e.g., farthest, earliest deadline, highest demand) and select the best overall solution.

---

## 5.5 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Fleet Routing Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the symmetric assumption** — students often write $d_{0i} \neq d_{i0}$ and produce wrong savings values. Always state *"assuming symmetric road distances"* at the start.
>
> 2. **Skipping capacity check** — even after computing the savings, examiners deduct 2–3 marks if the merged route's total load vs. $Q_v$ is not verified.
>
> 3. **Wrong savings formula sign** — Clarke-Wright is $d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$, NOT $d_{ij} - d_{0i} - d_{0j}$. Mixing signs is the most common error.
>
> 4. **Not reporting initial and final distance** — examiners award 1 mark for explicitly stating *"Initial total = X km, Final total = Y km, saving = (X-Y)/X × 100%"*. Missing this loses a mark.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the depot return** — every route must end at node 0; routes like $0\to A\to B$ without $B\to 0$ are incomplete and lose 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Binary variable confusion** — in mathematical formulation questions, students mix $x_{ij}$ and $x_{ijk}$. Use three-index notation $x_{ijk}$ for vehicle-routing problems.

---

## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> - **VRP** is an NP-hard combinatorial optimization problem; integer programming becomes intractable beyond ~15 nodes.
> - **Clarke-Wright Savings Formula:** $S_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$; sort descending; merge greedily with capacity check.
> - **Nearest Neighbor** is fast but greedy; produces ~10–25% suboptimal solutions.
> - **CVRP** adds capacity constraint $L_k \leq Q_v$.
> - **VRPTW** adds time window feasibility: $e_i \leq a_i \leq l_i$.
> - **Objective Function** is always *minimize total cost/distance/time*.
> - **Flow Conservation** constraint ensures each route is a closed loop.
> - **Capacity utilization** $\bar{\rho} = \frac{\sum q_i}{m \cdot Q_v}$ should ideally exceed **80%** for efficient operations.
> - **Heuristics vs. Metaheuristics** — Clarke-Wright is a heuristic; Genetic Algorithm and Tabu Search are metaheuristics for larger problems.
> - **Symmetric assumption** — most textbook problems assume $d_{ij} = d_{ji}$; always state this explicitly.
> - **Every customer visited exactly once** — never twice, never zero.
> - **Routes start AND end at depot** — single-depot VRP is the standard KTU variant.
> - **KTU board tests** the formula derivation more than the algorithm implementation — memorize $S_{ij}$ derivation.
> - **Industrial savings** from good routing: typically **15–30%** reduction in fleet operating cost.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trip Planning and Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT633 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Trip Planning and Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trip Planning and Management** is the systematic process of scheduling, routing, dispatching, and monitoring the movement of commercial vehicles from an origin to a destination, ensuring optimal utilization of fleet resources (vehicles, drivers, fuel, and time) while satisfying service-level commitments, regulatory constraints, and cost-efficiency targets.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAUT633)** framework, Trip Planning and Management is classified as a core sub-function of *Fleet Operations Management* under Module 2. It encompasses the decision-making processes that occur **before a vehicle is dispatched** (planning phase), **during transit** (monitoring phase), and **after trip completion** (analysis phase). The objective is to maximize **vehicle utilization**, minimize **dead mileage**, and ensure **on-time performance (OTP)**.

Key technical terms (as per KTU 2024 syllabus glossary):

| Term | KTU Definition |
|------|---------------|
| **Trip** | A single one-way movement of a vehicle from origin to destination carrying a defined load. |
| **Route** | The physical path selected between origin and destination. |
| **Schedule** | The time-table assigning departure, transit, and arrival times. |
| **Dispatch** | The operational act of releasing a vehicle for a trip after documentation. |
| **Dead Mileage** | Distance run by a vehicle without payload. |
| **Backhauling** | Carrying a return load to avoid empty running. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Family Wedding in a Distant Town**
>
> Imagine your cousin is getting married 400 km away on a Sunday morning. You have a single car, a fixed budget of ₹3,000, and three friends who also need to attend.
> 
> **Trip Planning** = deciding *which car to take, what time to leave, which highway to drive, and who to share fuel costs with.*
> **Trip Management** = keeping track of fuel, tolls, food stops, tyre pressure during the journey, and on the way back, *asking around* to see if anyone in the wedding town needs a ride to your city (backhauling).
>
> Scale this to a transport company with 200 trucks, 50 destinations, and 1,000 customers — and you have **Trip Planning and Management** at the fleet level.

## 1.3 Standard Industry Metrics (Used in KTU Problems)

The following metrics are universally tested in KTU University Examinations. Memorize them.

- **Total Trip Time (T_total)** = Loading Time + Running Time + Unloading Time + Idle Time
- **Vehicle Utilization (η)** = Revenue-earning Running Time / Total Trip Time
- **Dead Mileage Ratio (DMR)** = Distance without load / Total distance run
- **Load Factor (LF)** = Actual payload / Rated payload capacity
- **Turnaround Time (TAT)** = Total time from vehicle reporting empty to next empty reporting
- **Trip Frequency** = Number of trips per day per vehicle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Speed Assumption (for KTU numericals):** Unless otherwise specified, assume average commercial vehicle speed = **40 km/h** for long-haul, **25 km/h** for urban roads, and **60 km/h** for expressways.

## 1.4 Objectives of Trip Planning and Management

1. **Maximize vehicle utilization** (minimize idle time).
2. **Minimize total trip cost** (fuel, tires, depreciation, driver wages).
3. **Ensure on-time delivery** (customer satisfaction).
4. **Reduce empty running** (backhauling opportunities).
5. **Optimize driver hours** (compliance with driving-hour regulations).
6. **Maintain safety and regulatory compliance**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Trip Planning and Management Cycle

A commercial trip, as analyzed in KTU Module 2, follows a **5-stage cycle**:

### Stage 1: Trip Generation (Order Booking)
- Customer order received with origin, destination, commodity, weight, and required delivery date.
- Order is matched against fleet availability.

### Stage 2: Trip Scheduling
- Assignment of vehicle, driver, departure time, and estimated arrival time.
- Considers driver availability, vehicle fitness, route congestion, and load capacity.

### Stage 3: Route Planning
- Selection of the optimal route considering:
  - Shortest distance vs. shortest time.
  - Toll vs. toll-free roads.
  - Road conditions and gradient.
  - Regulatory restrictions (e.g., night driving bans on certain highways).

### Stage 4: Trip Dispatch & Execution
- Vehicle dispatched with trip sheet, way-bill, and load documents.
- Real-time monitoring via GPS/telematics in modern fleets.

### Stage 5: Trip Closure & Analysis
- Trip sheet returned, fuel and expense reconciled.
- Performance metrics calculated (cost/km, OTP, load factor, dead mileage).
- Data fed back into Stage 1 for continuous improvement.

## 2.2 Types of Trips in Fleet Operations

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification (commonly asked as 3-mark or 7-mark question):**

1. **Loaded Trip** — Vehicle carries payload from origin to destination.
2. **Empty Trip (Dead-head Trip)** — Vehicle runs without payload (e.g., returning to base).
3. **Local Trip** — Origin and destination within the same city/region (<100 km).
4. **Long-haul Trip** — Inter-city or inter-state trip (>100 km, often overnight).
5. **Pick-up Trip** — Vehicle goes to load cargo.
6. **Delivery Trip** — Vehicle delivers cargo to customer.
7. **Round Trip** — Origin → Destination → Origin with load on at least one leg.
8. **Multi-stop Trip** — Multiple pickups/deliveries in a single dispatch.
9. **Shuttle Trip** — Repeated short trips between two fixed points.

## 2.3 Factors Influencing Trip Planning Decisions

- **Distance & Terrain** — affects fuel consumption, vehicle wear, and trip time.
- **Load Characteristics** — weight, volume, perishability, hazard class.
- **Vehicle Type & Capacity** — truck, trailer, tanker, refrigerated body.
- **Driver Hours of Service (HOS)** — KTU assumes a single driver max 8–10 hours/day.
- **Delivery Window** — customer's required delivery time.
- **Toll, Tax & Permit Costs** — especially on inter-state routes.
- **Fuel Availability** — diesel bunks en route, fuel price differential across states.
- **Traffic & Weather** — peak hours, monsoon, fog-prone zones.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable under Module 2. Units are SI where applicable.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Remarks |
|---|--------------|----------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Trip Running Time | $T_r = \dfrac{D}{V}$ | hours | D = distance, V = avg speed |
| 2 | Total Trip Time | $T_{total} = T_L + T_r + T_U + T_{idle}$ | hours | L=Load, U=Unload |
| 3 | Vehicle Utilization | $\eta = \dfrac{T_r}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Higher is better |
| 4 | Dead Mileage Ratio | $DMR = \dfrac{D_{empty}}{D_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Lower is better |
| 5 | Load Factor | $LF = \dfrac{W_{actual}}{W_{rated}} \times 100$ | % | Ideal = 100% |
| 6 | Trip Cost | $C_{trip} = C_{fuel} + C_{driver} + C_{toll} + C_{maint} + C_{depr}$ | ₹/trip | Sum of all cost heads |
| 7 | Cost per km | $C_{km} = \dfrac{C_{trip}}{D}$ | ₹/km | Used to compare routes |
| 8 | Cost per ton-km | $C_{tkm} = \dfrac{C_{trip}}{W \times D}$ | ₹/ton-km | Used in tender bids |
| 9 | Fuel Cost | $C_{fuel} = \dfrac{D \times FC \times P_{fuel}}{1000}$ | ₹ | FC in litres/100 km |
| 10 | Turnaround Time | $TAT = T_{loading} + T_{running} + T_{unloading} + T_{waiting}$ | hours | Critical for asset productivity |
| 11 | Trips per Day | $N_{trips} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{H_{available}}{T_{total}} \right\rfloor$ | trips/day | Integer (floor function) |
| 12 | Empty Running Penalty | $C_{empty} = D_{empty} \times C_{km}$ | ₹ | Cost without revenue |

> **Critical LaTeX Note:** In markdown tables, vertical bars inside formulas are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the table syntax. The `$\vert$` symbol is used above for absolute-value clarity in equations.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Logistics Companies** (e.g., DHL, FedEx, Delhivery) use these formulas in their **Transport Management Systems (TMS)** to compute route profitability.
- **Public Transport Authorities** apply trip planning to optimize bus frequencies and minimize fleet size.
- **Construction Industry** uses trip planning to schedule dump-truck movements between quarry and site.
- **E-commerce Last-mile Delivery** applies multi-stop trip optimization to reduce per-order cost.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Trip Cost (Component Build-up Method)

The total trip cost is built up by adding the cost incurred on each resource consumed during the trip. Each component is derived from first principles.

### Step 1: Fixed Cost per Trip (Depreciation Component)

A vehicle of capital cost $C_v$ ₹ has a useful life of $N$ years and runs $D_{annual}$ km per year. The total km over its life is $N \times D_{annual}$. The depreciation cost per km is:

$$C_{depr/km} = \frac{C_v}{N \times D_{annual}}$$

For a single trip of distance $D$ km:

$$C_{depr,trip} = C_{depr/km} \times D = \frac{C_v \times D}{N \times D_{annual}}$$

> **Logic:** The capital cost of the vehicle is spread over its total expected life-distance. The fraction used in one trip is the trip-distance divided by total life-distance.

### Step 2: Fuel Cost per Trip

If fuel consumption is $FC$ litres per 100 km and fuel price is $P_f$ ₹/litre, the fuel cost per km is:

$$C_{fuel/km} = \frac{FC \times P_f}{100}$$

For a trip of $D$ km:

$$C_{fuel,trip} = C_{fuel/km} \times D = \frac{FC \times P_f \times D}{100}$$

### Step 3: Driver Cost per Trip

If driver monthly salary is $S_d$ ₹, working days per month is $d_m$, average km per day is $D_{day}$, and trip distance is $D$ km:

$$\text{Driver cost per km} = \frac{S_d}{d_m \times D_{day}}$$

$$C_{driver,trip} = \text{Driver cost per km} \times D = \frac{S_d \times D}{d_m \times D_{day}}$$

### Step 4: Maintenance & Tire Cost

Maintenance cost is approximately proportional to distance. If maintenance cost per km is $C_{maint/km}$:

$$C_{maint,trip} = C_{maint/km} \times D$$

Tire cost: if a tire set costs $C_t$ and lasts $L_t$ km, the tire cost per km is $C_t / L_t$, and per trip:

$$C_{tire,trip} = \frac{C_t \times D}{L_t}$$

### Step 5: Total Trip Cost

Combining all components:

$$C_{trip} = C_{depr,trip} + C_{fuel,trip} + C_{driver,trip} + C_{maint,trip} + C_{tire,trip} + C_{toll,trip}$$

Substituting the derived expressions:

$$C_{trip} = \frac{C_v \times D}{N \times D_{annual}} + \frac{FC \times P_f \times D}{100} + \frac{S_d \times D}{d_m \times D_{day}} + C_{maint/km} \times D + \frac{C_t \times D}{L_t} + C_{toll}$$

Factor out $D$ (distance is the common scaling factor for variable costs):

$$C_{trip} = D \times \left[ \frac{C_v}{N \times D_{annual}} + \frac{FC \times P_f}{100} + \frac{S_d}{d_m \times D_{day}} + C_{maint/km} + \frac{C_t}{L_t} \right] + C_{toll}$$

Define $C_{km}$ as the **bracketed expression** (per-km cost):

$$C_{trip} = C_{km} \times D + C_{toll}$$

This compact form is used in nearly all KTU numerical problems.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cost per Ton-Kilometer (Benchmark Metric)

The cost per ton-km measures the *transport productivity* and is used to compare carriers, modes, and route options. If trip cost is $C_{trip}$ for a payload $W$ tons over $D$ km:

$$C_{tkm} = \frac{C_{trip}}{W \times D}$$

Substituting $C_{trip} = C_{km} \times D + C_{toll}$:

$$C_{tkm} = \frac{C_{km} \times D + C_{toll}}{W \times D}$$

$$C_{tkm} = \frac{C_{km}}{W} + \frac{C_{toll}}{W \times D}$$

> **Interpretation:** Cost per ton-km decreases as load $W$ increases. This is the economic justification for **maximizing the load factor** in trip planning.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Vehicle Utilization and Trips-per-Day

Total trip time has four components:

$$T_{total} = T_L + T_r + T_U + T_{idle}$$

Vehicle utilization is defined as the fraction of total time spent in revenue-earning running:

$$\eta = \frac{T_r}{T_{total}}$$

If a vehicle is available for $H$ hours per day and total trip time is $T_{total}$, the number of complete trips per day is:

$$N_{trips} = \left\lfloor \frac{H}{T_{total}} \right\rfloor$$

> **Logic:** You can only execute an integer number of complete trips in a fixed time window. The floor function `\lfloor` is used because a partial trip cannot be billed as a full trip.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Dead Mileage Ratio and Backhaul Economics

For a round trip, total distance is $D_{loaded} + D_{empty}$, and the dead mileage ratio is:

$$DMR = \frac{D_{empty}}{D_{loaded} + D_{empty}} \times 100$$

If a backhaul is found, $D_{empty}$ reduces. The cost saving from backhauling is:

$$C_{saved} = D_{empty,old} \times C_{km} - D_{empty,new} \times C_{km} - C_{backhaul\_handling}$$

If $C_{saved} > 0$, the backhaul is economically justified.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical, 7-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A truck has a capital cost of ₹25,00,000, useful life 10 years, annual km = 80,000. Fuel consumption = 25 litres/100 km, fuel price = ₹95/litre. Driver salary ₹25,000/month, 25 working days, 200 km/day average. Maintenance cost ₹2/km. Tire life 60,000 km, tire set cost ₹1,20,000. Toll = ₹1,500. Trip distance = 600 km, payload = 12 tons. Find: (a) total trip cost, (b) cost per km, (c) cost per ton-km.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Depreciation per km**

$$C_{depr/km} = \frac{25,00,000}{10 \times 80,000} = \frac{25,00,000}{8,00,000} = 3.125 \text{ ₹/km}$$

**Step 2: Fuel cost per km**

$$C_{fuel/km} = \frac{25 \times 95}{100} = 23.75 \text{ ₹/km}$$

**Step 3: Driver cost per km**

$$C_{driver/km} = \frac{25,000}{25 \times 200} = \frac{25,000}{5,000} = 5.00 \text{ ₹/km}$$

**Step 4: Maintenance cost per km** = ₹2.00/km (given).

**Step 5: Tire cost per km**

$$C_{tire/km} = \frac{1,20,000}{60,000} = 2.00 \text{ ₹/km}$$

**Step 6: Cost per km (sum)**

$$C_{km} = 3.125 + 23.75 + 5.00 + 2.00 + 2.00 = 35.875 \text{ ₹/km}$$

**Step 7: Total trip cost (excluding toll)**

$$C_{trip,var} = 35.875 \times 600 = 21,525 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 8: Total trip cost (with toll)**

$$C_{trip} = 21,525 + 1,500 = 23,025 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 9: Cost per ton-km**

$$C_{tkm} = \frac{23,025}{12 \times 600} = \frac{23,025}{7,200} = 3.198 \text{ ₹/ton-km}$$

> **Final Answers:** (a) ₹23,025, (b) ₹38.375/km, (c) ₹3.20/ton-km.

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix (KTU Exam Favourite — 7 marks)

**Scenario:** A transport company must choose between 2 routes for a 500-km delivery. Data given below. Recommend the optimal route.

| Parameter | Route A (NH-44) | Route B (SH-42) |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| Distance | 500 km | 560 km |
| Avg Speed | 60 km/h | 40 km/h |
| Fuel Consumption | 20 L/100km | 25 L/100km |
| Toll | ₹800 | ₹200 |
| Surface Condition | Good | Moderate |
| Risk of Delay | Low | Medium |

**Analytical Step-by-Step:**

Time on Route A: $T_A = 500/60 = 8.33$ h.
Time on Route B: $T_B = 560/40 = 14.00$ h.

Fuel cost A (assume ₹95/L): $20 \times 5 \times 95 = 9,500$ ₹; plus toll = 10,300 ₹.
Fuel cost B: $25 \times 5.6 \times 95 = 13,300$ ₹; plus toll = 13,500 ₹.

> **Decision:** Route A costs ₹10,300 vs Route B's ₹13,500. Route A also saves 5.67 hours. **Choose Route A**, unless the surface condition of B justifies a higher vehicle depreciation premium on A.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Trip Planning and Management — End-to-End Functional Flow

The following Mermaid block represents the full **Trip Planning and Management Workflow** as a sequential process, with feedback loops for continuous improvement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Order Receipt from Customer] --> B[Trip Generation Module]
    B --> C{Is Vehicle Available?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Vehicle & Driver Assignment]
    C -- No --> E[Subcontract to External Carrier]
    D --> F[Route Planning Module]
    F --> G[Schedule and Dispatch Time]
    G --> H[Trip Sheet Generation]
    H --> I[Vehicle Dispatch and Execution]
    I --> J[Real Time GPS Monitoring]
    J --> K{On Schedule?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Continue Trip to Destination]
    K -- No --> M[Reroute or Reschedule]
    M --> L
    L --> N[Arrival and Delivery Confirmation]
    N --> O[Unloading and Documentation]
    O --> P[Empty Return Trip Planning]
    P --> Q{Backhaul Available?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Plan Backhaul Load]
    Q -- No --> S[Return Empty to Base]
    R --> T[Trip Closure and Cost Reconciliation]
    S --> T
    T --> U[Performance Analysis and KPI Update]
    U --> V[Update Fleet Database]
    V --> B
```

## 4.2 Trip Cost Build-up Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Trip Distance Input] --> B[Fixed Cost Calculator]
    A --> C[Variable Cost Calculator]
    B --> B1[Depreciation per km]
    B --> B2[Insurance and Permit Allocation]
    C --> C1[Fuel Cost Block]
    C --> C2[Driver Wage Block]
    C --> C3[Maintenance Block]
    C --> C4[Tyre Wear Block]
    C --> C5[Toll and Tax Block]
    B1 --> D[Summation Node]
    B2 --> D
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> E[Total Trip Cost Output]
    D --> F[Cost per km]
    D --> G[Cost per ton km]
    E --> H[Decision Module]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Route or Vehicle Selection Output]
```

## 4.3 Trip Scheduling Gantt-style Architecture (Block View)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Trip Planning Timeline for a Single Vehicle in a Day
    dateFormat HH:mm
    axisFormat %H:%M
    section Trip 1
    Loading and Dispatch :a1, 06:00, 1h
    Running Loaded      :a2, after a1, 4h
    Unloading at Dest   :a3, after a2, 1h
    Empty Return        :a4, after a3, 4h
    section Idle
    Driver Rest         :b1, after a4, 1h
    section Trip 2
    Loading Trip 2      :c1, 17:00, 1h
    Running Trip 2      :c2, after c1, 3h
    Unloading Trip 2    :c3, after c2, 1h
```

## 4.4 Diagram Notes and Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Guide for KTU Examiner's Perspective:**
>
> 1. The **flowchart in 4.1** shows the closed-loop nature of trip management. The output of every trip feeds back into the planning system — this is why modern TMS platforms use *historical data analytics*.
> 2. The **block diagram in 4.2** separates *fixed* vs *variable* cost components. In a KTU 14-mark problem, you are often asked to compute the cost per km by itemizing each block.
> 3. The **Gantt chart in 4.3** illustrates *vehicle utilization* visually. Notice the *Idle* (Driver Rest) block — a higher utilization means fewer idle blocks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Trip Planning. List any four objectives of trip planning and management. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Trip planning is the systematic process of scheduling, routing, and dispatching a commercial vehicle from origin to destination in a manner that optimizes fleet resources while meeting customer delivery commitments.

**Four Objectives (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. Maximize vehicle utilization.
2. Minimize total trip cost (fuel, driver, toll, maintenance).
3. Reduce empty running / dead mileage.
4. Ensure on-time delivery and customer satisfaction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Do NOT write generic objectives like "improve business." Examiners expect *transport-engineering-specific* objectives. Writing "maximize profit" instead of "minimize cost per ton-km" loses 0.5 marks.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Loaded Trip, Empty Trip, and Backhaul. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Trip Type | Definition | Revenue? |
|-----------|------------|----------|
| **Loaded Trip** | Vehicle carries payload from origin to destination. | Yes |
| **Empty Trip (Dead-head)** | Vehicle returns or runs without any payload. | No |
| **Backhaul** | Empty return trip is converted into a loaded trip by securing a return load. | Yes |

Backhauling is a *strategic decision* taken during trip planning to convert dead mileage into revenue-earning mileage.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write "backhaul is a return trip with load" — this is incomplete. You must mention that backhaul *converts empty running into a revenue-earning trip*, which is the key economic benefit. Lose 0.5 marks if missed.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the various stages involved in Trip Planning and Management. Draw a neat flowchart of the trip planning cycle.

**Model Answer:**

The Trip Planning cycle consists of **5 sequential stages** (1.4 marks each):

**Stage 1 — Trip Generation:**
Triggered by a customer order containing origin, destination, commodity, weight, and required delivery date. The planner matches the order against available fleet capacity.

**Stage 2 — Trip Scheduling:**
A vehicle and driver are assigned. Departure time, expected arrival, and route constraints (HOS, permits, tolls) are finalized. Output: a scheduled trip record.

**Stage 3 — Route Planning:**
The optimal route is selected considering distance, time, toll, fuel, and road condition. The trade-off is shortest-distance vs shortest-time.

**Stage 4 — Trip Dispatch and Execution:**
The trip sheet and documents are issued. The vehicle is dispatched. GPS-based monitoring tracks position and triggers alerts for delays.

**Stage 5 — Trip Closure and Analysis:**
The trip sheet is reconciled. Cost components (fuel, toll, driver) are recorded. KPIs (load factor, OTP, dead mileage) are computed and fed back into the planning system.

> **Flowchart** (drawn in SECTION 4.1 above, 1 mark awarded for neat, labeled diagram with feedback loop).

**(b) [7 marks]** A truck has the following data: Capital cost ₹20,00,000; life 8 years; annual km 1,00,000; fuel consumption 20 L/100 km; fuel price ₹90/L; driver salary ₹30,000/month, 25 working days, 250 km/day; maintenance cost ₹1.50/km; tire life 80,000 km, tire set cost ₹1,00,000; toll ₹1,000 per trip. Compute: (i) total trip cost for a 400-km trip carrying 10 tons, (ii) cost per km, (iii) cost per ton-km.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Depreciation per km:**
$$C_{depr/km} = \frac{20,00,000}{8 \times 1,00,000} = \frac{20,00,000}{8,00,000} = 2.50 \text{ ₹/km}$$

**Step 2 — Fuel cost per km:**
$$C_{fuel/km} = \frac{20 \times 90}{100} = 18.00 \text{ ₹/km}$$

**Step 3 — Driver cost per km:**
$$C_{driver/km} = \frac{30,000}{25 \times 250} = \frac{30,000}{6,250} = 4.80 \text{ ₹/km}$$

**Step 4 — Maintenance per km** = ₹1.50/km (given).

**Step 5 — Tire cost per km:**
$$C_{tire/km} = \frac{1,00,000}{80,000} = 1.25 \text{ ₹/km}$$

**Step 6 — Cost per km:**
$$C_{km} = 2.50 + 18.00 + 4.80 + 1.50 + 1.25 = 28.05 \text{ ₹/km}$$

**Step 7 — Variable trip cost:**
$$C_{var} = 28.05 \times 400 = 11,220 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 8 — Total trip cost (with toll):**
$$C_{trip} = 11,220 + 1,000 = 12,220 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 9 — Cost per ton-km:**
$$C_{tkm} = \frac{12,220}{10 \times 400} = \frac{12,220}{4,000} = 3.055 \text{ ₹/ton-km}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) ₹12,220, (ii) ₹28.05/km, (iii) ₹3.06/ton-km.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Errors:** Forgetting to convert fuel consumption to *per km* (the "per 100 km" is the most common trap). Forgetting to add toll. Mixing monthly and annual units. Each error costs 1–2 marks.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the factors that influence trip planning decisions in a commercial transport fleet.

**Model Answer (7 marks — 1 mark per factor, plus 1 mark for synthesis):**

1. **Distance and Terrain** — affects fuel use, vehicle wear, and travel time.
2. **Load Characteristics** — weight, volume, perishability, and hazard class of goods.
3. **Vehicle Type and Capacity** — must match the load; overloading is illegal and unsafe.
4. **Driver Hours of Service (HOS)** — regulatory and fatigue limits.
5. **Delivery Window** — customer's time-of-delivery requirement.
6. **Toll, Tax, and Permit Costs** — varies by route and state.
7. **Traffic and Weather Conditions** — peak-hour congestion, monsoon blockages.

**Synthesis (1 mark):** All seven factors are interdependent. The trip planner's role is to find the optimum trade-off (e.g., a longer route with lower toll may beat a shorter toll-heavy route when fuel and time savings are weighed).

**(b) [7 marks]** A transport operator runs a truck between City A and City B (300 km apart). Outbound trip is fully loaded. Return trip is empty. The operator has a chance to backhaul a 5-ton load from B to A at a freight rate of ₹2,000/ton. Without backhaul, the dead mileage cost is ₹30/km. With backhaul, additional handling cost at B is ₹1,500. Should the operator accept the backhaul? Justify with calculations.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cost of empty return (no backhaul):**
$$C_{empty} = 300 \times 30 = 9,000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 2 — Revenue from backhaul:**
$$R_{backhaul} = 5 \times 2,000 = 10,000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 3 — Net benefit:**
$$\text{Net Benefit} = R_{backhaul} - C_{handling} = 10,000 - 1,500 = 8,500 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 4 — Decision:** Since the backhaul revenue (₹10,000) exceeds the handling cost (₹1,500) by ₹8,500, **the operator should accept the backhaul**. Additionally, the dead mileage is converted into revenue-earning mileage, and the DMR drops from 50% to 0%.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Errors:** Comparing backhaul revenue to *empty return cost* directly (apples-to-oranges — ₹10,000 vs ₹9,000). Correct comparison is *revenue minus additional cost of backhauling*. The base trip cost of empty return is a *sunk cost* that occurs whether or not backhaul is accepted. Lose 2 marks if this distinction is missed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points (Must memorize before exam):**

- [x] **Trip Planning** = schedule, route, dispatch, monitor, analyze.
- [x] **Five stages:** Generation → Scheduling → Routing → Dispatch → Closure.
- [x] **Vehicle Utilization η = T_running / T_total** (aim for >70%).
- [x] **Dead Mileage Ratio** = D_empty / D_total (aim for <20%).
- [x] **Load Factor** = W_actual / W_rated (aim for ~100%).
- [x] **Cost per km = sum of all per-km cost heads** (depreciation + fuel + driver + maintenance + tires).
- [x] **Fuel cost per km = (FC × P_f) / 100**, where FC is in L/100 km.
- [x] **Depreciation per km = Capital Cost / (Life × Annual km).**
- [x] **Cost per ton-km = C_trip / (W × D)** — used in tender pricing.
- [x] **Backhauling** converts dead mileage into revenue-earning mileage.
- [x] **Toll** is a *fixed cost per trip* (not per km).
- [x] **Driver cost per km = Salary / (Working days × Daily km).**
- [x] **Trips per day = floor(H_available / T_total)**.
- [x] **Turnaround Time (TAT)** = loading + running + unloading + waiting.
- [x] **Empty running is a penalty**, not just an opportunity cost — it is a *cash cost* (fuel, tires, depreciation) without revenue.
- [x] **Trip sheet** is the primary legal and accounting document for a trip.
- [x] **OTP (On-Time Performance)** is a customer-facing KPI.
- [x] **Tender bids** in transport contracts are typically quoted in **₹/ton-km**.
- [x] **Modular exam tip:** Most 7-mark Part B questions on this topic ask for (i) derivation of cost/km, (ii) computation of total trip cost, (iii) interpretation of a decision matrix.

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Know your five stages, your seven cost components, and your three KPIs (η, DMR, LF) — and you will clear Module 2."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Driver Management and Training
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT633 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Driver Management and Training

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Driver Management and Training** is the systematic, scientifically engineered process of recruiting, qualifying, training, motivating, supervising, and continuously developing the human operator of commercial/fleet vehicles to ensure **road safety, operational efficiency, regulatory compliance, and asset protection** throughout the vehicle's operating life.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it is formally classified as a sub-domain of **Human Resource Management (HRM)** applied to the **Road Transport Sector**, governed by:
- The Motor Vehicles Act, **1988 (Central Act No. 59 of 1988)**
- The Central Motor Vehicles Rules, **CMVR 1989**
- The Road Transport Corporations Act, **1950**
- AIS (Automotive Industry Standards) codes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Driver is treated as a *“Human Subsystem”* inside the larger **Man-Machine-Road-Vehicle (MMRV)** operating model. A driver is not just an operator — they are a **dynamic decision-making controller** whose skill level directly influences fuel efficiency, accident rate, vehicle wear, and freight/passenger safety.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of the driver as the **CPU (Central Processing Unit)** of a fleet vehicle.

- The **truck/bus** is the hardware (processor + chassis + engine).
- The **fleet manager/dispatcher** is the operating system (provides routing & schedules).
- The **driver** is the actual CPU executing decisions every second (braking, steering, gear shifting, hazard perception).
- **Driver Management** = the firmware/microcode update system that keeps the CPU optimized.
- **Driver Training** = the BIOS-level programming that teaches the CPU how to handle complex instructions.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a high-performance computer without updated firmware becomes vulnerable to crashes, a fleet without trained drivers becomes vulnerable to accidents, legal liability, and profit erosion.

## 1.3 Core Metrics & Standards

Standard performance metrics used in industry:

| Metric | Standard Reference Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Driver-to-Vehicle Ratio | **1 : 1.5 to 1 : 2** | drivers/vehicle |
| Annual Training Hours | **40 – 60** | hours/year |
| Driver Accident Rate | **< 0.5** | per 100,000 km |
| Fuel Efficiency Variance | **± 5%** | deviation from baseline |
| Defensive Driving Course | **AIS-052 / NTT** | certification |
| Working Hours Limit | **8 hrs/day, 48 hrs/week** | hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **Motor Transport Workers Act, 1961**, a driver **cannot work more than 8 hours in a 24-hour cycle** without mandatory rest of at least 9 hours.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Driver Management

### Pillar 1 — Driver Recruitment & Selection
The entry-gate of the human subsystem. Selection criteria must filter for **skill + attitude + health**.

**Selection Filters (in sequence):**
1. **Age Eligibility Check** → Min **20 years** (HMV), **18 years** (LMV)
2. **License Verification** → DL validity, badge (for HMV), endorsements
3. **Medical Fitness** → vision (6/6 with/without glasses), hearing, color blindness (Ishihara test), cardiovascular fitness, reflex test
4. **Driving Skill Test** → practical road test, hill driving, night driving
5. **Background Verification** → police clearance, prior accident record
6. **Psychometric Evaluation** → reaction time, alertness, decision-making speed

### Pillar 2 — Driver Training
A structured curriculum divided into:

**A. Pre-Service Training (Initial Qualification)**
- Classroom modules on traffic rules, vehicle systems, load distribution
- Simulator-based training (modern KTU expectation)
- Yard maneuvering training
- On-road supervised training — minimum **500 km supervised**

**B. In-Service Training (Continuous Development)**
- **Defensive Driving Courses (DDC)** — refreshers every **6 months**
- Eco-driving / **eco-fuel driving** modules
- Advanced braking systems (ABS/EBD) familiarization
- ADR (Accord Dangereux Routier) for hazardous goods drivers
- First-aid and fire-fighting training
- Customer service (for passenger fleet)

### Pillar 3 — Driver Performance Evaluation
Continuous monitoring through:

- **Telematics-based scoring**: hard braking, harsh acceleration, over-speeding, idle time
- **Fuel consumption benchmarking** per driver per trip
- **Accident & incident reporting** analysis
- **Customer feedback** (for passenger fleets)
- **Trip inspection compliance** scores
- **Annual performance appraisal**

### Pillar 4 — Driver Motivation & Welfare
Drivers are high-stress operators. Welfare drives retention:

- Fair wages + overtime + night allowance
- Health insurance & periodic free check-ups
- Rest rooms at depots (clean, hygienic)
- Recognition programs — *“Driver of the Month”*
- Career progression: Driver → Senior Driver → Training Instructor → Fleet Supervisor
- Family welfare schemes

### Pillar 5 — Driver Scheduling & Duty Roster
- Comply with **Section 8 of Motor Transport Workers Act**
- Avoid driver fatigue through two-driver relay for long-haul
- Plan route + rest + meal breaks

## 2.2 The Driver Competency Triangle

A driver must master three competencies:

| Competency | Domain | Tool/Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Technical Knowledge** | Vehicle systems, mechanics | Pre-trip inspection score |
| **Operational Skill** | Driving skill | Practical test score |
| **Behavioral/Safety Attitude** | Risk perception | Defensive driving index |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Rule | Expression | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Driver Utilization Efficiency | $\eta_{D} = \frac{\text{Actual Driving Hrs}}{\text{Total Duty Hrs}} \times 100$ | Schedule optimization |
| Driver Cost per km | $C_{D} = \frac{\text{Driver Salary + Benefits}}{\text{Total km Driven}}$ | Fleet costing |
| Accident Rate | $A_{R} = \frac{\text{Number of Accidents}}{\text{Lakh km driven}}$ | Safety KPI |
| Driver Score (Composite) | $D_{score} = 0.4F + 0.3S + 0.3C$ | Performance evaluation |
| Training ROI | $ROI_{T} = \frac{\Delta \text{Profit (post-training)}}{\text{Training Cost}} \times 100$ | Training justification |
| Crew Utilization | $U_{C} = \frac{\text{Hrs in Motion}}{\text{Hrs Available}} \times 100$ | Shift planning |

Where:
- $F$ = fuel-efficiency score
- $S$ = safety score
- $C$ = customer/conduct score
- All scores normalized on a 0–100 scale

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fleet Owners** use these systems to reduce insurance premiums (insurers offer discounts for trained drivers).
- **Logistics Companies** use telematics-linked driver scores for performance-based incentive payouts.
- **Public Transport Operators** (KSRTC, BMTC, BEST) deploy these frameworks to comply with RTO audits.
- **Driver Management Systems (DMS)** are now integrated with **GPS + IoT + AI dashcams** to give real-time driver coaching alerts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Driver Cost per Kilometer

**Statement:** Derive the expression for total driver cost per km for a fleet operation.

**Step 1 — Identify Cost Components**
Total driver cost $C_{T}$ comprises:

$$
C_{T} = S + B + OT + N + T + P
$$

Where:
- $S$ = Basic monthly salary
- $B$ = Benefits (PF, ESI, bonus, medical)
- $OT$ = Overtime pay
- $N$ = Night/remote allowance
- $T$ = Training cost per driver per year
- $P$ = Protective gear / uniform cost

**Step 2 — Convert to Per-Month Basis**
Training and gear are annual — divide by **12**:

$$
C_{monthly} = S + B + \overline{OT} + N + \frac{T + P}{12}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Daily Basis**

$$
C_{daily} = \frac{C_{monthly}}{26}
$$

(26 working days per month average)

**Step 4 — Convert to Per-Kilometer Basis**
If driver covers $D_{km}$ km per day:

$$
C_{km} = \frac{C_{daily}}{D_{km}} = \frac{S + B + \overline{OT} + N + \frac{T+P}{12}}{26 \times D_{km}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Typical industry value: **₹2.50 – ₹5.00 per km** for commercial drivers in India (varies by fleet type).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Driver Performance Score (Composite Index)

**Step 1 — Define the Three Normalized Sub-Scores**
Each sub-score is on a 0–100 scale:

- Fuel-efficiency score $F$
- Safety score $S_{s}$
- Conduct/Customer score $C$

**Step 2 — Apply Weighted Aggregation**

$$
D_{score} = w_{F} \cdot F + w_{S} \cdot S_{s} + w_{C} \cdot C
$$

Subject to constraint:

$$
w_{F} + w_{S} + w_{C} = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Industry-Standard Weighting**
A typical safe-fleet weighting is:

$$
w_{F} = 0.4, \quad w_{S} = 0.4, \quad w_{C} = 0.2
$$

Hence:

$$
D_{score} = 0.4F + 0.4S_{s} + 0.2C
$$

**Step 4 — Performance Bands**

$$
\text{Band} = \begin{cases} \text{Excellent}, & D_{score} \geq 85 \\ \text{Good}, & 70 \leq D_{score} < 85 \\ \text{Average}, & 55 \leq D_{score} < 70 \\ \text{Poor}, & D_{score} < 55 \end{cases}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Driver Training ROI

**Step 1 — Quantify Pre-Training Annual Loss**

$$
L_{pre} = A_{pre} \cdot C_{A} + F_{excess} \cdot C_{F} + I_{pre} \cdot C_{I}
$$

Where:
- $A_{pre}$ = pre-training accidents/year
- $C_{A}$ = cost per accident (avg)
- $F_{excess}$ = excess fuel consumed vs benchmark
- $C_{F}$ = fuel cost per litre
- $I_{pre}$ = insurance premium
- $C_{I}$ = incremental insurance cost

**Step 2 — Quantify Post-Training Annual Loss**

$$
L_{post} = A_{post} \cdot C_{A} + F_{post} \cdot C_{F} + I_{post} \cdot C_{I}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Annual Savings**

$$
\Delta L = L_{pre} - L_{post}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute ROI**

$$
ROI_{T} = \frac{\Delta L - C_{training}}{C_{training}} \times 100
$$

A typical well-designed fleet training program achieves $ROI_{T} \geq 300\%$ within 18 months.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Training Method Effectiveness

| Training Method | Skill Transfer | Cost (₹/driver) | Retention (months) | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Classroom only | Low | 2,000 | 2 | Theory foundations |
| On-road supervised | High | 8,000 | 9 | Skill building |
| Simulator-based | High | 15,000 | 12 | Hazard training |
| Telematics-driven coaching | Very High | 5,000/year | Continuous | In-service |
| Classroom + Simulator + On-road | Excellent | 22,000 | 18 | Gold standard |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Driver Management System — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Recruitment Funnel] --> A2[License Verification]
    A2 --> A3[Medical Fitness Test]
    A3 --> A4[Psychometric Test]
    A4 --> A5[Driving Skill Test]
    A5 --> A6[Background Verification]
    A6 --> A7[Onboarding]

    A7 --> B1[Initial Training Program]
    B1 --> B2[Classroom Modules]
    B1 --> B3[Simulator Sessions]
    B1 --> B4[Supervised Road Driving 500 km]
    B1 --> B5[Defensive Driving Certification]

    B5 --> C1[Active Service Deployment]
    C1 --> C2[Telematics Monitoring]
    C2 --> C3[Performance Scoring Engine]
    C3 --> C4{Score Threshold}

    C4 -->|Score >= 85| C5[Recognition & Incentive]
    C4 -->|Score 55 to 85| C6[Coaching & Retraining]
    C4 -->|Score less than 55| C7[Review for Suspension]

    C6 --> B5
    C5 --> C1

    C1 --> D1[Annual Medical Review]
    C1 --> D2[Refresher Course 6 Monthly]
    C1 --> D3[Welfare and Career Progression]
```

## 4.2 Driver Competency Triangle Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Technical Knowledge] --> OUT[Safe and Efficient Fleet Operation]
    P2[Operational Driving Skill] --> OUT
    P3[Behavioral Safety Attitude] --> OUT

    P1 --- P2
    P2 --- P3
    P3 --- P1
```

## 4.3 Training Lifecycle Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Need Analysis] --> L2[Curriculum Design]
    L2 --> L3[Delivery Classroom Simulator On-road]
    L3 --> L4[Assessment and Evaluation]
    L4 --> L5[On-job Performance Tracking]
    L5 --> L6[Feedback and Improvement]
    L6 --> L1
```

## 4.4 Driver Duty Cycle (Regulatory Compliance Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DC1[Duty Start] --> DC2[Pre-trip Inspection 15 min]
    DC2 --> DC3[Driving Phase max 4 hours continuous]
    DC3 --> DC4{Mandatory Break}
    DC4 -->|Yes| DC5[Rest 30 min minimum]
    DC5 --> DC6[Continue Driving]
    DC6 --> DC7{Total 8 hours reached}
    DC4 -->|No| DC3
    DC7 -->|Yes| DC8[Duty End Rest Period 9 hours]
    DC7 -->|No| DC3
    DC8 --> DC1
```

## 4.5 Causes of Driver-Related Accidents — Causal Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Driver Fatigue] --> ACC[Accident]
    R2[Substance Use] --> ACC
    R3[Over Speeding] --> ACC
    R4[Distraction Mobile Phone] --> ACC
    R5[Inexperience on Route] --> ACC
    R6[Inadequate Training] --> ACC
    R7[Poor Health] --> ACC
    R8[Low Motivation and Wage Stress] --> ACC

    R6 --> R1
    R6 --> R3
    R8 --> R1
    R7 --> R2
    R5 --> R1
```

## 4.6 Driver Performance Evaluation Data Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Telematics GPS] --> DB1[Data Lake]
    S2[Fuel Card Logs] --> DB1
    S3[Accident Reports] --> DB1
    S4[Customer Feedback] --> DB1
    S5[Inspection Checklists] --> DB1

    DB1 --> EN[Scoring Engine AI]
    EN --> D1[Driver Dashboard]
    EN --> D2[Fleet Manager Dashboard]
    EN --> D3[Insurance Telematics Report]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap
