```mermaid
        G5["Edge Microcontroller"]
    end
    subgraph layer3["Layer 3 Communication"]
        N1["Cellular 4G 5G"]
        N2["NB IoT"]
        N3["Satellite for Remote Fleets"]
    end
    subgraph layer4["Layer 4 Cloud and Analytics"]
        C1["Time Series Database"]
        C2["ML Predictive Models"]
        C3["Threshold Rule Engine"]
        C4["Digital Twin of Vehicle"]
    end
    subgraph layer5["Layer 5 User Applications"]
        U1["Fleet Manager Web Dashboard"]
        U2["Driver Mobile App"]
        U3["Workshop Tablet Interface"]
        U4["Customer API Access"]
    end
    layer1 --> layer2
    layer2 --> layer3
    layer3 --> layer4
    layer4 --> layer5
```

---

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

### **Q1. Define telematics and list any four of its applications in vehicle maintenance.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Telematics** is the integrated use of telecommunications and information technology for vehicle data acquisition, transmission, and analysis.

**Four applications in vehicle maintenance:**
1. **Remote diagnostics** — Read DTCs without workshop visit.
2. **Predictive maintenance scheduling** — ML-based failure forecasting.
3. **Fuel and idling monitoring** — Reduces fuel wastage and engine wear.
4. **Driver behavior analysis** — Harsh braking/over-revving tied to maintenance alerts.

*(Each application: 0.5 mark; Definition: 1 mark)*

---

### **Q2. What is the difference between Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM) and Predictive Maintenance (PdM)?** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | CBM | PdM |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Trigger | Real-time threshold breach | Statistical / ML-based failure probability |
| Data use | Current snapshot only | Historical + real-time data |
| Approach | Reactive to present condition | Proactive forecast of future failure |
| Complexity | Lower | Higher (requires ML, big data) |

*(Any 3 distinguishing points: 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 2, Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **(a)** Explain the architecture of a telematics-based fleet maintenance system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of OBD-II and CAN bus in this architecture. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** A delivery fleet of **80 vehicles** operates **200 km/day** per vehicle. Average breakdowns/vehicle/year = **4**, MTTR = **10 hours**. After installing telematics, breakdowns reduce to **2/year** and MTTR to **5 hours**. Telematics cost = **₹12,000/vehicle/year**. Downtime cost = **₹600/hour**. Calculate:
> (i) Pre- and post-telematics availability
> (ii) Total downtime hours saved for the fleet
> (iii) ROI of the telematics investment. *(7 Marks)*

### **Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks:**

**1. Five-Layer Architecture (3 marks):**
- **Layer 1 — Data Acquisition:** Sensors (temperature, pressure, vibration, RPM, fuel level, TPMS).
- **Layer 2 — Vehicle Gateway:** OBD-II port reader, CAN bus interface, GPS receiver, GSM modem.
- **Layer 3 — Communication:** 4G/5G/NB-IoT/Satellite networks.
- **Layer 4 — Cloud & Analytics:** Time-series databases, ML models, threshold engines.
- **Layer 5 — Application:** Web dashboard, mobile app, workshop interface.

**2. Role of OBD-II (2 marks):**
- Standardized SAE J1962 connector present in all post-2001 vehicles.
- Provides real-time access to **engine RPM, vehicle speed, coolant temp, DTCs, fuel trim, MAF**.
- Enables plug-and-play telematics without custom ECU integration.

**3. Role of CAN Bus (2 marks):**
- Multi-master serial protocol (**ISO 11898**) connecting all ECUs.
- Allows telematics unit to tap into **brake, steering, transmission, airbag, ADAS** data not exposed via OBD-II.
- High speed (**up to 1 Mbps**) and noise-resistant for in-vehicle use.

### **Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $N = 80$, km/day $= 200$, $B_{\text{pre}} = 4$, $B_{\text{post}} = 2$, $\text{MTTR}_{\text{pre}} = 10$ hr, $\text{MTTR}_{\text{post}} = 5$ hr.

**Step 1 — Annual km per vehicle:**

$$
T_{\text{op}} = 200 \times 365 = 73{,}000 \text{ km/year}
$$

**Step 2 — Pre-Telematics MTBF & Availability:**

$$
\text{MTBF}_{\text{pre}} = \dfrac{73{,}000}{4} = 18{,}250 \text{ km}
$$

$$
A_{\text{pre}} = \dfrac{18{,}250}{18{,}250 + (10 \times 4)} = \dfrac{18{,}250}{18{,}290} = 99.78\%
$$

**Step 3 — Post-Telematics MTBF & Availability:**

$$
\text{MTBF}_{\text{post}} = \dfrac{73{,}000}{2} = 36{,}500 \text{ km}
$$

$$
A_{\text{post}} = \dfrac{36{,}500}{36{,}500 + (5 \times 2)} = \dfrac{36{,}500}{36{,}510} = 99.97\%
$$

**Step 4 — Downtime saved per vehicle:**

$$
\Delta t = (4 \times 10) - (2 \times 5) = 40 - 10 = 30 \text{ hours/vehicle/year}
$$

**Total fleet savings:**

$$
\Delta t_{\text{fleet}} = 30 \times 80 = \mathbf{2{,}400 \text{ hours/year}}
$$

**Step 5 — ROI:**

$$
C_{\text{saved}} = 2{,}400 \times 600 = \text{₹}14{,}40{,}000
$$

$$
C_{\text{telematics}} = 80 \times 12{,}000 = \text{₹}9{,}60{,}000
$$

$$
\text{ROI} = \dfrac{14{,}40{,}000 - 9{,}60{,}000}{9{,}60{,}000} \times 100 = \dfrac{4{,}80{,}000}{9{,}60{,}000} \times 100 = \mathbf{50\%}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

> **(a)** Discuss in detail how telematics enables **Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM)** and **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)**. Compare both approaches with examples. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** A mining fleet operates in a remote area with poor cellular connectivity. The fleet manager wants to deploy telematics for critical equipment monitoring. Discuss the communication options, sensors to deploy, and the ROI framework for this scenario. *(7 Marks)*

### **Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks:**

**CBM via Telematics (3 marks):**
- Sensors stream real-time data (temperature, pressure, vibration).
- Threshold rules trigger alerts → technician inspects.
- **Example:** Coolant temp exceeding **105 °C** triggers an SMS to the workshop before the engine seizes.

**PdM via Telematics (3 marks):**
- Historical data + ML models predict failure probability.
- **Example:** Vibration trend analysis on a rotary crusher predicts bearing failure **21 days in advance**, allowing planned shutdown instead of emergency stoppage.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Parameter | CBM | PdM |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Trigger | Threshold breach | ML probability |
| Data scope | Real-time only | Historical + real-time |
| False positives | Higher | Lower |
| Cost of deployment | Lower | Higher |

### **Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Communication Options for Remote Mining Sites (3 marks):**
- **Satellite communication (Iridium / Inmarsat):** Best for truly remote areas; higher latency.
- **Private LTE / 5G (CBRS):** Suitable for large mine sites; offers local coverage.
- **Mesh radio networks:** Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) relaying — useful in tunnels.
- **Store-and-Forward Telematics:** Data buffered locally and uploaded when connectivity returns.

**Sensors to Deploy (2 marks):**
- Vibration + acoustic emission sensors on crushers.
- Hydraulic pressure sensors on excavators.
- Engine health sensors (oil quality, coolant, exhaust).
- Tire pressure and payload sensors on dump trucks.

**ROI Framework (2 marks):**

$$
\text{ROI} = \dfrac{C_{\text{avoided breakdowns}} + C_{\text{extended asset life}} + C_{\text{fuel saved}} - C_{\text{telematics}}}{C_{\text{telematics}}} \times 100
$$

For mining, **avoided production loss** is the dominant savings component — often **₹1–5 lakh per hour** of crusher downtime.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing CBM with PdM** — Many students write them interchangeably. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Omitting units** in numericals — Always write km, hours, ₹, % alongside numbers.
> 3. **Forgetting the "feedback loop"** in block diagrams — Telematics is **bidirectional**; workshop updates feed back to the cloud.
> 4. **Writing generic "IoT" instead of "telematics"** — Telematics is a **specific subset** of IoT focused on vehicles and fleet mobility.
> 5. **Not mentioning OBD-II / CAN bus explicitly** — These are syllabus-highlighted terms; missing them loses easy marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Bullets — Memorize These:**

- **Telematics = Telecom + Informatics** for vehicle data.
- **Five-layer architecture:** Sensors → Gateway → Communication → Cloud/Analytics → Application.
- **OBD-II** = Standardized diagnostic port; **CAN bus** = In-vehicle network.
- **Maintenance generations:** Reactive → Preventive → CBM → PdM.
- **Telematics enables Gen-3 and Gen-4** maintenance.
- **Availability formula (must memorize):**

$$
A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

- **MTBF = Total Op Time / Failures; MTTR = Total Downtime / Repairs.**
- **ROI = (Savings − Cost) / Cost × 100.**
- **Failure probability (exponential):** $P(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$.
- **Key sensors:** Temperature, pressure, vibration, voltage, TPMS, NOx.
- **Real-world impacts:** Reduces downtime by 30–50%, increases uptime to > 99.9%.
- **Mining/remote fleets:** Use **satellite + store-and-forward** telematics.
- **EV fleets:** Telematics critical for **battery State-of-Health (SoH)** tracking.
- **Driver behavior analytics** feed directly into predictive maintenance triggers.
- **Standard wireless protocols to remember:** 4G/5G, GSM, GPRS, NB-IoT, LTE-M, Satellite.
- **GPS civilian accuracy ≈ ±5 meters.**
- **SAE J1962** = OBD-II connector standard; **ISO 15765** = CAN diagnostic protocol.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Management - Fuel Procurement and Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT633 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuel Management - Fuel Procurement and Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fuel Procurement and Storage** is the strategic subsystem of Fleet Fuel Management that encompasses the entire chain of activities involved in sourcing, purchasing, receiving, handling, storing, dispensing, and accounting for petroleum and alternative fuels used in a transport fleet. It is governed by the principles of *Supply Chain Reliability*, *Quality Assurance*, *Inventory Optimization*, and *Hazardous Material Safety*.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it is classified under the **Logistics Support Pillar** of Fleet Operations and directly contributes to **Course Outcome CO2**: *Analyze fuel procurement strategies and storage practices to optimize fleet operating costs while ensuring safety and regulatory compliance.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Fuel procurement involves *vendor selection, purchase contracts, quality specifications, and pricing mechanisms*. Storage involves *tank farm design, capacity planning, safety regulations, contamination control, and loss accounting*. Together, they form a closed-loop system where procurement efficiency and storage integrity determine fleet fuel economy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your household LPG cylinder supply. You cannot just "buy gas whenever you feel like it" — you depend on a vendor (Indane/Hindustan Petroleum), you must check the cylinder's seal for tampering (quality), you store it upright in a ventilated kitchen (safety), and you keep track of when you last refilled (inventory). If you ran out mid-cooking, your family suffers; if the cylinder leaked, you face danger; if you overstocked, money sat idle.

A transport fleet operates on the **same logic, scaled 1000x**:
- **Vendor** = Oil marketing company (IOC, BPC, HPC) or bulk fuel supplier.
- **Seal check** = Sampling and density testing at delivery.
- **Kitchen** = Underground or above-ground storage tank farm.
- **Refill tracking** = Daily dip-stock readings and fuel register.

The only difference: a fleet consuming **5000 litres per day** cannot tolerate even a 1% mismanagement loss.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Metrics Used:**
> - **Density of Diesel:** **0.85 kg/L at 15°C** (BIS 1460)
> - **Density of Petrol:** **0.74 kg/L at 15°C**
> - **Flash Point of Diesel:** **>55°C** (safe storage class)
> - **Flash Point of Petrol:** **<-40°C** (highly flammable class)
> - **Standard Storage Loss Allowance:** **0.5% – 1.0% per quarter**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fuel Procurement Cycle (Structured Logic)

**Step 1: Demand Forecasting**
- Fleet manager estimates annual fuel demand using: *Annual km × Vehicle-specific fuel economy ÷ 100*.
- Forecasts are adjusted for **seasonal variation** (winter mileage drops, summer AC load rises), **route gradient**, and **load factor**.

**Step 2: Vendor Selection & Contracting**
- Procurement may follow three models:
  - **Direct Bulk Supply (Oil Company Tie-up):** IOC/BPC/HPC deliver via tankers to depot.
  - **Retail Card System:** Driver swipes a fuel card at any PSU pump; bills consolidated.
  - **Open Tender:** Competitive bidding for lowest unit price.
- Selection criteria: *Price stability, delivery reliability, quality certification (BIS mark), credit terms, geographic coverage*.

**Step 3: Quality Specification**
- Fuel must conform to **BIS 1460 (Diesel)** and **BIS 2286 (Petrol)**.
- Critical parameters: *Cetane number, Sulphur content (max 10 ppm for BS-VI), Density, Flash point, Water content, Sediment, Microbial contamination*.

**Step 4: Storage Planning**
- Capacity decided by: *Daily burn × Days-of-cover × Density factor + Reserve margin*.
- Days-of-cover typically ranges **7–21 days** depending on lead time reliability.

**Step 5: Receipt, Inspection & Storage**
- Tanker arrival → Seal verification → Density probe test → Dip measurement → Sampling → Entry into fuel register.
- **FIFO (First-In-First-Out)** is the default unless tank stratification demands LIFO.

**Step 6: Issue & Reconciliation**
- Dispensing records vs. daily dip → Identify shrinkage.
- Reconciliation happens **weekly/monthly** with strict tolerance of **<0.5%**.

### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters (The "Why" Behind the Process)

- **Forecasting accuracy** prevents *stock-out scenarios* that idle the fleet.
- **Vendor vetting** prevents *adulterated fuel* — a major cause of injector and DPF failures costing ₹50,000–₹2,00,000 per vehicle.
- **Quality checks at receipt** protect engines from sulphur, water, and microbial growth in tanks.
- **Proper storage design** prevents evaporation losses, fire hazards, and groundwater contamination (legal liability).
- **Reconciliation** detects pilferage — one of the top three causes of fleet financial loss in India.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Description | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D_a = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\dfrac{K_i \times V_i}{E_i}\right)$ | Annual fuel demand | $D_a$ = Annual demand (L), $K_i$ = Annual km of vehicle $i$, $V_i$ = Vehicle count, $E_i$ = Economy (km/L) | Procurement budgeting |
| $C_{tank} = \dfrac{D_d \times N \times S}{1000}$ | Required storage capacity (m³) | $C_{tank}$ = Capacity (m³), $D_d$ = Daily consumption (L), $N$ = Days of cover, $S$ = Safety factor (1.1–1.25) | Tank sizing |
| $L_e = V \times \rho \times k \times \Delta T$ | Evaporation loss estimation | $L_e$ = Loss (kg), $V$ = Tank volume (m³), $\rho$ = Density, $k$ = Loss coefficient, $\Delta T$ = Temp swing | Storage loss accounting |
| $R_{op} = \bar{u} \times L + S_s$ | Reorder point | $R_{op}$ = Reorder level (L), $\bar{u}$ = Avg daily use (L), $L$ = Lead time (days), $S_s$ = Safety stock (L) | Inventory trigger |
| $S_s = z \times \sigma_L$ | Safety stock | $z$ = Service level z-score, $\sigma_L$ = Std dev of lead time demand | Buffer calculation |
| $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times A \times D}{H}}$ | Economic Order Quantity | $A$ = Order cost (₹), $D$ = Annual demand (L), $H$ = Holding cost per L per year (₹) | Optimal order size |
| $L_{total} = \vert Q_r - Q_d - Q_disp \vert$ | Total storage loss | $Q_r$ = Received quantity, $Q_d$ = Dip-stock quantity, $Q_disp$ = Dispensed quantity | Reconciliation |
| $T_c = \dfrac{C_{avg}}{D_a} \times 365$ | Inventory turnover in days | $C_{avg}$ = Average stock (L), $D_a$ = Annual demand (L) | Storage efficiency |
| $\eta_{storage} = 1 - \dfrac{L_{total}}{Q_r}$ | Storage efficiency | Dimensionless ratio | KPI metric |
| $C_{uc} = \dfrac{C_{total}}{V_{consumed}}$ | Unit cost of fuel delivered | $C_{total}$ = Total fuel expenditure, $V_{consumed}$ = Volume consumed | Cost benchmarking |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are not academic — they appear directly in depot manager KRA sheets and KTU ESE numerical problems. Mastering the **Reorder Point** and **Storage Capacity** calculations guarantees easy marks in Part B.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Optimal Storage Tank Capacity

**Problem Context:** A fleet operates 40 trucks. Each truck averages 60 km/day with an economy of 4 km/L. The depot wants 12 days of fuel cover with a safety factor of 1.20. Determine tank capacity in litres and cubic metres.

**Given:**
- Number of vehicles: $n = 40$
- Daily distance per vehicle: $d = 60$ km
- Fuel economy: $e = 4$ km/L
- Days of cover: $N = 12$ days
- Safety factor: $S = 1.20$
- Density of diesel: $\rho = 0.85$ kg/L

**Step 1 — Compute daily fleet consumption in litres:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_d &= n \times \frac{d}{e} \\
D_d &= 40 \times \frac{60}{4} \\
D_d &= 40 \times 15 \\
D_d &= 600 \text{ L/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute gross demand over the cover period:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{cover} &= D_d \times N \\
D_{cover} &= 600 \times 12 \\
D_{cover} &= 7200 \text{ L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply safety factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{litres} &= D_{cover} \times S \\
C_{litres} &= 7200 \times 1.20 \\
C_{litres} &= 8640 \text{ L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to cubic metres:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{m^3} &= \frac{C_{litres}}{1000} \\
C_{m^3} &= \frac{8640}{1000} \\
C_{m^3} &= 8.64 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Decision:** Round up to the next commercially available tank size — typically **10 m³ (10,000 L)**. The excess provides 16% additional buffer for demand spikes.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Reorder Point with Safety Stock

**Problem Context:** A depot's average daily fuel issue is 800 L/day. Supplier lead time is 5 days with a standard deviation of 1.2 days. Daily demand standard deviation is 50 L. Management wants a 95% service level ($z = 1.65$).

**Step 1 — Compute average lead time demand:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{u} \times L &= 800 \times 5 \\
\bar{u} \times L &= 4000 \text{ L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute combined standard deviation of lead time demand:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_L &= \sqrt{\left(L \times \sigma_d\right)^2 + \left(\bar{u} \times \sigma_{LT}\right)^2} \\
\sigma_L &= \sqrt{\left(5 \times 50\right)^2 + \left(800 \times 1.2\right)^2} \\
\sigma_L &= \sqrt{(250)^2 + (960)^2} \\
\sigma_L &= \sqrt{62500 + 921600} \\
\sigma_L &= \sqrt{984100} \\
\sigma_L &= 992.02 \text{ L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute safety stock:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_s &= z \times \sigma_L \\
S_s &= 1.65 \times 992.02 \\
S_s &= 1636.83 \text{ L} \approx 1637 \text{ L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute reorder point:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{op} &= \bar{u} \times L + S_s \\
R_{op} &= 4000 + 1637 \\
R_{op} &= 5637 \text{ L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** When the dip-stock reading falls to **5637 L**, the procurement officer must raise a Purchase Order. Below this, the fleet risks stock-out during the 5-day supplier window.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Storage Efficiency and Loss Accounting

**Problem Context:** A tank received 20,000 L of diesel on 1st March. After 30 days, dip-stock measured 12,400 L. The dispensing register shows 7,200 L was issued. Compute storage loss, efficiency, and indicate corrective action.

**Step 1 — Total accounted fuel:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{disp} + Q_{dip} &= 7200 + 12400 \\
Q_{disp} + Q_{dip} &= 19600 \text{ L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total loss:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= \vert Q_r - Q_{disp} - Q_{dip} \vert \\
L_{total} &= \vert 20000 - 7200 - 12400 \vert \\
L_{total} &= \vert 20000 - 19600 \vert \\
L_{total} &= 400 \text{ L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Storage efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{storage} &= 1 - \frac{L_{total}}{Q_r} \\
\eta_{storage} &= 1 - \frac{400}{20000} \\
\eta_{storage} &= 1 - 0.02 \\
\eta_{storage} &= 0.98 \text{ or } 98\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Loss as percentage and cost impact:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% loss &= \frac{400}{20000} \times 100 = 2.0\% \\
\text{If diesel price} &= ₹95/\text{L} \\
\text{Monthly loss value} &= 400 \times 95 = ₹38{,}000 \\
\text{Annual loss value} &= 38000 \times 12 = ₹4{,}56{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Diagnosis & Corrective Action:**
- Industry tolerance is **<0.5% per month**.
- 2.0% indicates possible **evaporation, leak, or pilferage**.
- Actions: *Tank calibration check, dispenser calibration audit, CCTV review of dispensing bay, water-drain from tank bottom.*

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Storage Tank Comparison

| Parameter | Underground Tank (UST) | Above-Ground Tank (AST) |
|---|---|---|
| Evaporation loss | **Low (1–2% annually)** | High (3–6% annually) |
| Fire safety risk | Low (insulated by earth) | High (exposed) |
| Installation cost | High (excavation, lining) | Moderate |
| Land footprint | Buried, saves surface space | Requires bund wall (110% capacity) |
| Inspection ease | Difficult (requires gauges) | Easy (visual + manual) |
| Regulatory burden | High (PESO + Pollution Board) | Moderate |
| Best suited for | High-volume city depots | Temporary sites, small fleets |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fuel Procurement & Storage Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fleet Demand Forecast] --> B[Annual Fuel Requirement Calculation]
    B --> C{Vendor Selection Strategy}
    C --> C1[Bulk Oil Company Tie-up]
    C --> C2[Fuel Card System]
    C --> C3[Open Tender]
    C1 --> D[Purchase Order Generation]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Tanker Dispatch to Depot]
    E --> F[Seal Verification at Gate]
    F --> G[Quality Sampling and BIS Testing]
    G --> H{Quality Passed?}
    H -- No --> I[Reject and Return Tanker]
    H -- Yes --> J[Density Probe and Dip Measurement]
    J --> K[Transfer to Storage Tank]
    K --> L[Update Fuel Register FIFO]
    L --> M[Daily Dip Stock Reading]
    M --> N[Dispensing to Vehicles]
    N --> O[Reconciliation Weekly]
    O --> P{Storage Loss within 0.5%?}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Normal Operations]
    P -- No --> R[Audit Trigger and Loss Investigation]
    R --> S[Corrective Action Report]
    S --> M
    Q --> T[Monthly Invoice and Payment]
    T --> U[Management KPI Dashboard]
    U --> V{Reorder Point Reached?}
    V -- Yes --> D
    V -- No --> M
```

### 4.2 Architecture: Storage Tank Subsystem with Safety Layers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inlet Manifold with Filter] --> B[Storage Tank with Level Sensor]
    B --> C[Water Draw-off Valve at Bottom]
    B --> D[Pressure Vacuum Vent at Top]
    B --> E[Sampling Port at Mid Level]
    E --> F[Dispensing Pump]
    F --> G[Flow Meter and Dispenser]
    G --> H[Vehicle Fueling Point]
    B -.Bund Wall 110% Capacity.- B
    B -.Fire Foam System.- B
    B -.Earthing and Lightning Rod.- B
    B -.Spill Containment Sump.- B
```

### 4.3 Architecture: Vendor Evaluation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vendor Bid Received] --> B{Certified BIS Mark}
    B -- No --> X[Disqualified]
    B -- Yes --> C{Delivery Lead Time 7 Days}
    C -- No --> X
    C -- Yes --> D{Credit Period 15 Days}
    D -- No --> X
    D -- Yes --> E{Price Competitive}
    E -- No --> F[Negotiation Round]
    F --> E
    E -- Yes --> G[Vendor Approved]
    G --> H[Contract Signed for 12 Months]
    H --> I[Performance Review Quarterly]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid:** Every box above is an operational node inside a real depot. The first diagram maps the **macro procurement cycle**; the second shows the **safety and instrumentation envelope** around a storage tank; the third is the **vendor gating logic** used during procurement.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Fuel Procurement. List any four quality parameters checked at the time of fuel receipt.**

**Model Answer:**
Fuel procurement is the systematic process of sourcing, contracting, and purchasing fuel in the right quantity, quality, and at the right price to ensure uninterrupted fleet operations.

Four quality parameters checked at receipt:
1. **Density** (compared against BIS 1460 standard for diesel, **0.82–0.86 kg/L at 15°C**).
2. **Water content** (Karl Fischer titration; max **200 ppm**).
3. **Sediment and particulate matter** (visual + lab filtration test).
4. **Flash point** (Pensky-Martens closed cup; minimum **55°C** for diesel).

---

#### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between Above-Ground Storage Tank (AST) and Underground Storage Tank (UST). Mention two merits of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | AST | UST |
|---|---|---|
| Location | On surface with bund wall | Buried below ground |
| Merits | (i) Easy inspection and maintenance (ii) Lower installation cost | (i) Lower evaporation loss (ii) Saves surface land; safer from fire |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **forget to apply the safety factor** in tank sizing — losing 2 marks.
> - In reorder-point problems, do **not** use lead-time alone; always add the **safety stock term**.
> - In reconciliation problems, **never** subtract dip and dispense without using the **absolute value** for total loss.
> - Units must be shown at every step — KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for missing units.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **fuel procurement process** for a medium-scale transport fleet. Discuss vendor selection criteria, contract types, and pricing mechanisms. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The fuel procurement process is a four-stage workflow:

**Stage 1 — Demand Estimation:**
Annual demand is forecasted using:
$$
D_a = \sum_{i} \left( \frac{K_i \times V_i}{E_i} \right)
$$
Demand is segmented by vehicle class (heavy truck, light truck, bus) and adjusted for **seasonality** (winter load, monsoon mileage drop) and **route gradient** (hilly routes consume 10–15% more).

**Stage 2 — Vendor Selection:**
Three procurement models exist:
- **Direct Bulk Supply:** Long-term contract with PSU oil company (IOC/BPC/HPC). Best for fleets consuming >10,000 L/month.
- **Fuel Card System:** Prepaid cards like **IOC SmartFleet, BPC SpeedFleet, HPC FleetCard**. Best for distributed operations.
- **Open Tender:** E-reverse auction for lowest unit price. Used by PSUs and government fleets.

Selection criteria: *BIS certification, lead-time reliability, credit terms, geographic coverage, quality testing facility, past performance.*

**Stage 3 — Contract Type:**
- **Fixed-price contract:** Price locked for 6–12 months; protects against hikes.
- **Floating-price contract:** Linked to international crude; risk-shared.
- **Hybrid contract:** 70% fixed + 30% floating.

**Stage 4 — Pricing Mechanisms:**
- **Platts benchmark + dealer margin** for bulk.
- **Pump price + fleet discount** for retail cards (₹2–₹4/L off).
- **Tender-based rate** for competitive bidding.

**(b)** A fleet of 25 buses operates 200 km/day with an average fuel economy of 3.5 km/L. The depot desires 15 days of fuel cover with a 15% safety margin. Compute: **(i)** daily consumption, **(ii)** storage tank capacity in litres and m³, and **(iii)** the reorder point if supplier lead time is 4 days and daily demand standard deviation is 30 L at 95% service level ($z = 1.65$). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Daily consumption:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_d &= \frac{n \times d}{e} \\
D_d &= \frac{25 \times 200}{3.5} \\
D_d &= \frac{5000}{3.5} \\
D_d &= 1428.57 \text{ L/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Tank capacity:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{cover} &= 1428.57 \times 15 = 21428.57 \text{ L} \\
C_{litres} &= 21428.57 \times 1.15 = 24642.86 \text{ L} \\
C_{m^3} &= \frac{24642.86}{1000} = 24.64 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

Recommend **25 m³ tank** (commercial size).

**(iii) Reorder point:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{u} \times L &= 1428.57 \times 4 = 5714.28 \text{ L} \\
\sigma_L &= \sqrt{(4 \times 30)^2 + 0^2} = 120 \text{ L} \\
S_s &= 1.65 \times 120 = 198 \text{ L} \\
R_{op} &= 5714.28 + 198 = 5912.28 \text{ L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Order must be placed when dip-stock ≈ 5913 L.**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail **fuel storage practices** in a transport depot. Cover tank types, safety features, contamination control, and BIS standards. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Tank Types:**
- **Underground Storage Tank (UST):** Cylindrical steel, double-walled, cathodically protected; preferred for fire safety and land economy.
- **Above-Ground Tank (AST):** Rectangular or cylindrical, mounted on RCC pad with **bund wall of 110% capacity** to contain spills.
- **Mobile Bowser:** For site fuel supply; smaller capacity (2000–5000 L).

**2. Safety Features:**
- **Pressure-Vacuum Vent (PV Valve):** Reduces evaporation by 80%.
- **Float Gauges and ATG probes** for accurate level reading.
- **Fire-fighting foam system** and **earth pit** for static discharge.
- **Spill containment bund** and **lightning arrestor**.

**3. Contamination Control:**
- **Water draw-off at tank bottom** every fortnight.
- **Periodic microbial testing** — bacteria like *Hormoconis resinae* grow in diesel-water interface and clog filters.
- **Filter replacement schedule** at 10,000 L throughput.
- **Tank cleaning** every 3–5 years (sludge removal).

**4. BIS Standards:**
- **BIS 1460:** Automotive diesel fuel specifications (BS-VI norms, max **10 ppm sulphur**).
- **BIS 2286:** Petrol specifications.
- **IS 10987:** Storage and handling of petroleum products — safety code.

**(b)** A depot received 50,000 L of diesel. After 30 days, dispensing records show 42,000 L issued. Dip-stock reads 7,200 L. **(i)** Compute total storage loss and storage efficiency. **(ii)** Comment on whether the loss is acceptable and recommend corrective actions if not. **(iii)** If diesel price is ₹92/L, compute annual financial loss projected from current month's loss. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Loss and efficiency:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= \vert 50000 - 42000 - 7200 \vert \\
L_{total} &= \vert 50000 - 49200 \vert \\
L_{total} &= 800 \text{ L} \\
\eta_{storage} &= 1 - \frac{800}{50000} = 1 - 0.016 = 0.984 \text{ or } 98.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Acceptability:**
Monthly loss of **1.6%** exceeds industry tolerance of **0.5%**. Not acceptable. Possible causes: evaporation (summer), leak in dispensing line, dip-stock measurement error, or pilferage.

**Corrective actions:**
- Recalibrate dispenser flow meter.
- Check tank for leaks using pressure test.
- Verify dip-stick calibration.
- Review CCTV footage of dispensing bay.
- Conduct physical audit of vehicle tank capacities vs. issue records.

**(iii) Annual financial loss:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Monthly loss in ₹} &= 800 \times 92 = ₹73{,}600 \\
\text{Annual loss in ₹} &= 73600 \times 12 = ₹8{,}83{,}200
\end{aligned}
$$

**Critical Alert:** ₹8.83 lakh annual drain is alarming for a depot — must be investigated by the fuel manager within 7 days.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Fuel Procurement** = demand forecast + vendor selection + contract + quality check.
- **Storage** = tank sizing + safety + contamination control + reconciliation.
- Key constant: **Density of diesel = 0.85 kg/L at 15°C**; **diesel flash point >55°C**.
- Formula trio to memorize: **$C_{tank} = \frac{D_d \times N \times S}{1000}$**, **$R_{op} = \bar{u} L + S_s$**, **$\eta_{storage} = 1 - \frac{L_{total}}{Q_r}$**.
- Tolerance benchmark: **storage loss <0.5% per month** is healthy.
- BIS standards: **1460 (diesel), 2286 (petrol), 10987 (storage safety)**.
- Three procurement models: **Bulk supply, Fuel card, Open tender**.
- Always show units — KTU valuations mark down answer sheets without units.
- Safety features mandatory: **bund wall 110%, PV vent, earthing, foam system**.
- Storage efficiency KPI target in industry: **>99.5%**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Consumption Monitoring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT633 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuel Consumption Monitoring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel Consumption Monitoring (FCM)** is the systematic, continuous, and quantitative process of **measuring, recording, analyzing, and controlling** the volume or mass of fuel utilized by a vehicle or an entire fleet over a defined operational distance, time, or work cycle. It is a critical subsystem of **Fleet Management Operations** and directly interfaces with telematics, GPS tracking, CAN-bus diagnostics, and fleet economics (KPI: Cost-per-Kilometer).

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, FCM is treated as a **strategic performance pillar** under Module 2 of *Vehicle Transport and Fleet Management* because fuel typically represents **30% – 45%** of a transporter's total operating cost — second only to driver wages. The goal is to convert a *physical resource burn rate* into *actionable management intelligence*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Household Electricity Meter" Mental Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of FCM exactly like the **electricity meter on your house**. Your KSEB meter measures *kilowatt-hours consumed per month*. Similarly, an FCM system in a truck measures *liters consumed per 100 km driven*. The billing dashboard (your monthly KSEB bill) is the *fleet manager's MIS report* — it tells you whether to switch off the AC, change a habit, or upgrade to a 5-star appliance (i.e., driver training, route optimization, or vehicle retrofitting).

The analogy maps cleanly:

| Household Electricity Meter | Fleet Fuel Consumption Monitor |
|---|---|
| kWh consumed per month | Liters consumed per month |
| "Average units per day" | **Fleet average km/L** |
| Appliance-wise consumption | Vehicle-wise / Trip-wise consumption |
| Bill = Units × Tariff | Cost = Liters × ₹/L |
| Switching to LED | Driver eco-driving training |

## 1.3 Why Fuel Consumption Monitoring is Non-Negotiable in Fleet Operations

> [!NOTE]
> Three mandatory outcomes of any FCM program (per KTU 2024 CO mapping):
> 1. **Detect anomalies** — fuel theft, leakages, unauthorized siphoning, idling losses.
> 2. **Benchmark performance** — inter-vehicle and inter-driver comparison.
> 3. **Justify replacement** — empirical data for vehicle buy-back decisions (TCO logic).

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used (Must Know)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Density of Diesel (at 15 °C):** **ρ ≈ 0.832 – 0.860 kg/L** (standard reference: **0.832 kg/L** per ISO 3675).
> - **Density of Petrol/Gasoline:** **ρ ≈ 0.720 – 0.760 kg/L**.
> - **Standard Calorific Value of Diesel:** **≈ 42.5 MJ/kg** (≈ 35.8 MJ/L).
> - **Standard Calorific Value of Petrol:** **≈ 44.0 MJ/kg** (≈ 32.0 MJ/L).

The two dominant reporting units in India/KTU syllabus:
- **L/100 km** — liters consumed per 100 km (lower is better; preferred in Europe, BS-VI/BS-VII era).
- **km/L** — kilometers per liter (higher is better; preferred in India, especially for two-wheelers and commercial fleets).

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Fuel Consumption Monitoring (Operational Logic)

A complete FCM cycle in a fleet operation consists of **five sequential pillars**. KTU examiners frequently test these in 7-mark sub-questions.

1. **Data Acquisition (Sensors & Telematics)**
   - *How:* Float-type level sensors in the fuel tank, in-line **flow meters** (positive-displacement, Coriolis, turbine), or **CAN-bus OBD-II fuel rate (FR) PID** reading from the ECU.
   - *Why:* Provides the raw volumetric/mass flow rate signal `Q(t)` in L/h.

2. **Data Logging & Storage**
   - *How:* GPS-Telematics units (GTU) store timestamped fuel readings at 1 Hz – 10 Hz.
   - *Why:* Required for trip-level reconstruction (departure, midway, arrival fuel readings).

3. **Data Transmission (Telematics Link)**
   - *How:* GSM/4G packet sent to a centralized **Fleet Management System (FMS)** server (e.g., Trimble, Geotab, Loconav, Mahindra Fleetman).
   - *Why:* Allows real-time dashboarding and exception alerts.

4. **Data Analytics (KPI Computation)**
   - *How:* Server-side algorithms compute fuel efficiency, detect *fuel theft events* (sudden drop with no movement), and idling losses.
   - *Why:* Translates raw bytes into business decisions.

5. **Feedback Loop (Operational Control)**
   - *How:* Driver scorecards, route re-optimization, vehicle maintenance alerts.
   - *Why:* Closes the loop — converts *measurement* into *savings*.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Oriented)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula bank** for any numerical problem on Fuel Consumption Monitoring in PEAUT633. Memorize the conversion logic — examiners love unit-conversion traps.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Volume-based FC (Indian)** | $\text{km/L} = \dfrac{D}{V}$ | km/L | Higher is better |
| 2 | **Volume-based FC (European)** | $\text{L/100 km} = \dfrac{V}{D} \times 100$ | L/100 km | Lower is better |
| 3 | **Fuel Cost per km** | $C_{km} = \dfrac{V \times P_f}{D}$ | ₹/km | Used in route bidding |
| 4 | **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)** | $\text{SFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b}$ | g/kWh | Engine-bench metric |
| 5 | **Mass flow from volumetric** | $\dot{m}_f = Q \times \rho_f$ | kg/h | Multiply by density |
| 6 | **Fleet Average FC** | $\overline{\text{FC}} = \dfrac{\sum V_i}{\sum D_i}$ | km/L | **Weighted avg** — never simple avg |
| 7 | **Idling fuel waste** | $V_{idle} = Q_{idle} \times t_{idle}$ | Liters | Typical diesel idle: **0.8 – 1.2 L/h** |
| 8 | **Theft detection threshold** | $\Delta V_{allowed} = Q_{idle} \times \Delta t$ | Liters | Anything beyond is theft |
| 9 | **CO₂ emission from fuel** | $m_{CO_2} = V \times \rho \times 3.169$ | kg CO₂/L (diesel) | Emission audits |
| 10 | **Energy consumed per km** | $E_{km} = \dfrac{V \times \text{CV}}{D}$ | MJ/km | Useful for EV-comparison |

Where:
- $D$ = distance traveled (km)
- $V$ = volume of fuel consumed (L)
- $P_f$ = unit price of fuel (₹/L)
- $\dot{m}_f$ = mass fuel flow rate (g/h)
- $P_b$ = brake power output (kW)
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (L/h)
- $\rho_f$ = fuel density (kg/L)
- $\text{CV}$ = calorific value (MJ/L)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in engineering practice:** Formula 6 (fleet average) is the most-misused metric in industry. Companies often compute a *simple arithmetic mean* of per-vehicle km/L, which is **mathematically wrong** when vehicles cover unequal distances. KTU examiners test this trap explicitly.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Logistics Sector (Indian context):** A truck fleet running 100 vehicles × 50,000 km/year × improving FC from 3.5 to 4.0 km/L saves roughly **₹35 – 45 lakhs/year** at ₹90/L diesel.
- **Public Transport:** KSRTC / BEST use FCM to compare depot-level efficiency.
- **Emission Compliance:** India BS-VI OBD-II mandates continuous fuel-rate reporting — directly links FCM to regulatory compliance.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below are **exam-grade**. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly with a verbal rationale line (the "**Logic:**" row). No steps are skipped — KTU valuation awards partial credit only when intermediate logic is visible.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Basic L/100 km Calculation (Part A / 3-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement:** A delivery van consumes **48 liters** of diesel over a 5-day period, covering a total distance of **640 km**. Calculate (a) fuel efficiency in km/L, (b) fuel consumption in L/100 km, and (c) the cost per kilometer if diesel costs ₹92.50/L.

### Solution:

**(a) Fuel efficiency in km/L:**

We start from the most fundamental definition. Fuel efficiency is *distance traveled per unit volume of fuel*. This is the reciprocal of the more common European metric.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{km/L} &= \frac{\text{Total Distance } (D)}{\text{Total Volume } (V)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{640 \text{ km}}{48 \text{ L}} \\[4pt]
&= 13.333\ldots \text{ km/L} \\[4pt]
&\approx \mathbf{13.33 \text{ km/L}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** We inverted the *volume-per-distance* definition to get *distance-per-volume* because the question asks for efficiency, which intuitively should grow when fuel becomes "more useful." Hence divide distance by fuel.

**(b) Fuel consumption in L/100 km:**

The European metric asks *how much fuel is needed to cover a standardized 100 km*. It is the reciprocal of (a), scaled by 100.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{L/100 km} &= \frac{V}{D} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{48 \text{ L}}{640 \text{ km}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 0.0750 \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{7.50 \text{ L/100 km}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** A handy verification — the product of km/L and L/100 km must equal exactly **100**. Let us check: $13.33 \times 7.50 = 99.975 \approx 100$ ✓ (minor rounding error). KTU examiners love this cross-check identity.

**(c) Cost per kilometer:**

Cost per km is the *financial shadow* of (b). We multiply L/100 km by unit price and divide by 100 to get ₹/km.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{km} &= \frac{V \times P_f}{D} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{48 \text{ L} \times 92.50 \text{ ₹/L}}{640 \text{ km}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{4440 \text{ ₹}}{640 \text{ km}} \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{6.9375 \text{ ₹/km}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Equivalent identity: $C_{km} = (\text{L/100 km} \times P_f) / 100 = (7.50 \times 92.50)/100 = 6.9375$ ₹/km ✓. Use whichever route feels comfortable.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Fleet Average FC (Part B / 7-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement:** A small fleet has three vehicles A, B, C with the following weekly data:

| Vehicle | Distance (km) | Fuel used (L) |
|---|---|---|
| A | 1500 | 400 |
| B | 800 | 240 |
| C | 2700 | 600 |

(a) Compute the individual km/L for each vehicle.
(b) Compute the **correct fleet average km/L**.
(c) Show that the *simple arithmetic mean* of individual km/L is wrong, and quantify the error.

### Solution:

**(a) Individual km/L:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FC}_A &= \frac{1500}{400} = \mathbf{3.750 \text{ km/L}} \\[4pt]
\text{FC}_B &= \frac{800}{240} = \mathbf{3.333 \text{ km/L}} \\[4pt]
\text{FC}_C &= \frac{2700}{600} = \mathbf{4.500 \text{ km/L}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Apply $D/V$ directly. Notice B is the least efficient — likely an older truck or a heavy-load route.

**(b) Correct fleet average km/L (volume-weighted by distance):**

The fleet average is *total distance covered by the entire fleet* divided by *total fuel burned by the entire fleet*. This is the **distance-weighted harmonic-mean** of per-vehicle FC, not the simple mean.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{\text{FC}}_{fleet} &= \frac{\sum D_i}{\sum V_i} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1500 + 800 + 2700}{400 + 240 + 600} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5000}{1240} \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{4.0323 \text{ km/L}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** We sum distances in the numerator and fuel volumes in the denominator — never mix them. This is the *only* mathematically defensible fleet average because it preserves dimensional consistency.

**(c) Error quantification — Simple Arithmetic Mean Method:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{\text{FC}}_{wrong} &= \frac{3.750 + 3.333 + 4.500}{3} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{11.583}{3} = \mathbf{3.8611 \text{ km/L}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Error} &= 4.0323 - 3.8611 = \mathbf{0.1712 \text{ km/L (underestimate)}} \\[4pt]
\text{Percentage Error} &= \frac{0.1712}{4.0323} \times 100 \approx \mathbf{4.25\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The simple mean **underestimates** efficiency by 4.25% in this case because Vehicle C (the most efficient) covers the longest distance and is therefore underrepresented in a simple mean. In real fleets, this error can reach **8–12%**, causing massive budget miscalculations. This is a **favourite KTU Part B trap**.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Idling & Fuel-Theft Detection (Part B / 7-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement:** A truck's telematics unit logs the following overnight fuel-level readings:

| Time | Fuel level (L) | Engine status |
|---|---|---|
| 22:00 | 220.0 | OFF (parked at depot) |
| 23:00 | 219.5 | OFF |
| 00:00 | 219.2 | OFF |
| 01:00 | 209.0 | OFF |

The manufacturer's spec states that tank evaporation / sensor drift at depot overnight is **0.3 L/h**. If the truck has been stationary the whole time, identify whether fuel theft has occurred and estimate the stolen volume.

### Solution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t &= 01{:}00 - 22{:}00 = 3 \text{ hours} \\[4pt]
\Delta V_{measured} &= 220.0 - 209.0 = 11.0 \text{ L drop} \\[4pt]
\Delta V_{allowed} &= 0.3 \text{ L/h} \times 3 \text{ h} = 0.9 \text{ L} \\[4pt]
V_{theft} &= \Delta V_{measured} - \Delta V_{allowed} \\[4pt]
&= 11.0 - 0.9 \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{10.1 \text{ L (likely stolen)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** A jump of **10 L in a single hour** with no engine running is *physically impossible* for evaporation (which is a slow diffusion process). Telematics systems raise a **"Fuel Theft Alert"** whenever $|\Delta V_{measured}| > \Delta V_{allowed} \times k$, where $k \approx 3$ to 5 is a safety multiplier. In our case, the observed drop is **12× the allowed rate** — a clear-cut theft signal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world extension:** Advanced systems cross-verify with **accelerometer data** (vibration pattern of someone touching the fuel cap) and **geo-fence breach logs** (any non-depot GPS pings during the theft window). KTU Part B questions often ask you to design such a multi-sensor verification logic.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Fuel Consumption Monitoring System

> [!NOTE]
> Below is a **block-level functional architecture** of a modern FCM system, mapped from the fuel tank sensor all the way to the fleet manager's dashboard. This is the canonical diagram a KTU examiner expects when the question says *"With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a fuel consumption monitoring system."*

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[TankFuelReservoir] --> B1[LevelSensorFloatOrUltrasonic]
    B1 --> C1[AnalogSignalConditioningUnit]
    C1 --> D1[TelematicsControlUnitTCU]
    D2[CANBusECUFR_PID] --> D1
    D3[InlineFlowmeterOptional] --> D1
    D1 --> E1[GSM4GTransmitter]
    E1 --> F1[FleetManagementServerCloud]
    F1 --> G1[AnalyticsEngineKPIComputation]
    G1 --> H1[DashboardReports]
    G1 --> I1[AlertModuleTheftOrIdle]
    G1 --> J1[DriverScorecardModule]
    H1 --> K1[FleetManagerDecisionSupport]
    I1 --> K1
    J1 --> K1
    K1 --> L1[OperationalFeedbackEcoDriverTrainingRouteOptimization]
```

**Reading the diagram (left → right):**
- **Sensing layer (A1–B1, D2, D3):** Three independent data sources — tank level sensor, ECU fuel-rate PID, and (optional) in-line flowmeter. Redundancy prevents single-point failure.
- **Acquisition layer (C1, D1):** Signal conditioning + on-board processing.
- **Communication layer (E1):** Cellular uplink to cloud.
- **Server / Analytics (F1, G1):** Cloud-hosted FMS performing KPI math.
- **Output layer (H1, I1, J1):** Dashboards, real-time theft/idle alerts, driver scorecards.
- **Decision layer (K1) → Action (L1):** The closed-loop feedback — eco-driving programs, route optimization, preventive maintenance.

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart for Fuel-Theft vs. Leakage vs. Normal Evaporation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[FuelDropDetected] --> Q1{IsEngineRunning?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> P1[NormalConsumption]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{DropRategreaterThan0_3Lperh}
    Q2 -- No --> P2[NormalEvaporationOrSensorDrift]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{DurationLessthan15min}
    Q3 -- Yes --> A1[LikelyTheft]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{GeoFenceStatus}
    Q4 -- InsideDepot --> A1
    Q4 -- OutsideDepot --> A2[LikelySiphoningAtUnknownLocation]
```

**Logic of the diagram:**
- Any drop while **engine is ON** → counted as normal combustion consumption.
- Engine OFF + drop ≤ 0.3 L/h → noise floor (evaporation or sensor drift).
- Engine OFF + sudden large drop → suspicion branch, further refined by **duration** and **geo-fence**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

> **"Define fuel consumption monitoring. List any four sensors used to measure fuel consumption in a commercial vehicle fleet."**

**Model Answer (KTU Marking Key Pattern):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Fuel consumption monitoring is the systematic measurement, recording and analysis of the quantity of fuel consumed by a vehicle or fleet over a defined distance, time period, or operational cycle, with the objective of controlling operating cost, detecting anomalies, and benchmarking performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sensors (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks — name + brief role):**
> 1. **Float-type fuel level sensor** — gives tank-level reading; derivative yields consumption rate.
> 2. **Ultrasonic / capacitive level sensor** — non-contact alternative; higher accuracy.
> 3. **In-line volumetric flowmeter** (positive-displacement / Coriolis) — direct flow measurement.
> 4. **OBD-II / CAN-bus fuel-rate PID** — ECU-reported instantaneous fuel rate (L/h).
> 5. *(Optional 5th)* **Mass airflow (MAF) sensor** with stoichiometric ratio — infers fuel mass from air mass.

**Examiner's Note:** A definition alone carries 1.5 marks; the remaining 1.5 marks are for naming sensors with a one-line role each. *Do not write essays* — be precise and bullet-driven.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

> **"Distinguish between km/L and L/100 km as fuel-efficiency metrics. Why is L/100 km preferred for fleet reporting?"**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction (1.5 marks):**
> - **km/L** measures *distance per unit fuel* — it is a *fuel-efficiency* metric. Higher is better. Preferred in India for two-wheelers and consumer vehicles.
> - **L/100 km** measures *fuel per unit distance* — it is a *fuel-consumption* metric. Lower is better. Used in Europe and increasingly in Indian BS-VI fleet audits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why L/100 km is preferred for fleet reporting (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Linearity with fuel cost** — cost is directly proportional to L, so L/100 km scales linearly with ₹/100 km, making budgeting trivial.
> 2. **Additivity across trips** — total fleet consumption can be obtained by *simple summation* of L/100 km × km, without unit-conversion errors.
> 3. **Easy cross-vehicle comparison** — regardless of tank size, two trucks can be directly compared on the same axis.
> 4. **Emission regulation alignment** — CO₂ regulations (g/km) scale identically with L/100 km.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**

> **(a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, explain the architecture and working of a fuel consumption monitoring system in a commercial vehicle fleet. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A logistics company operates a fleet of 4 trucks for one week. Data collected is:
>
> | Truck | Distance (km) | Fuel used (L) |
> |---|---|---|
> | T1 | 1800 | 540 |
> | T2 | 1200 | 400 |
> | T3 | 2500 | 625 |
> | T4 | 900 | 270 |
>
> Compute the **fleet average fuel efficiency in km/L and L/100 km**. If diesel costs ₹91.75/L, calculate the **total fuel cost for the week**. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution:

> [!NOTE]
> **Block diagram (3 marks):** Draw the same functional architecture shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes. Label the five layers: Sensing → Acquisition → Communication → Server Analytics → Decision & Feedback. Any equivalent block diagram carrying the same five layers earns full marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Working explanation (4 marks — 1 mark per major layer):**
> 1. **Sensors** (float / ultrasonic / CAN-bus fuel-rate PID) measure instantaneous fuel level or flow rate in the tank/line. The level signal is differentiated to obtain consumption rate `Q(t)`.
> 2. **Telematics Control Unit (TCU)** samples this signal at 1–10 Hz, timestamps it with GPS, and stores it locally.
> 3. **GSM/4G transmitter** uploads packets to the cloud Fleet Management Server (FMS).
> 4. **Analytics engine** on the server computes trip-wise km/L, L/100 km, idling losses, and theft alerts.
> 5. **Output layer** generates dashboards, driver scorecards, and triggers operational feedback — eco-driver training, route re-optimization, maintenance scheduling.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution:

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Fleet total distance and total fuel (2 marks):**
> $$\sum D_i = 1800 + 1200 + 2500 + 900 = 6400 \text{ km}$$
> $$\sum V_i = 540 + 400 + 625 + 270 = 1835 \text{ L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 2 — Fleet average km/L (2 marks):**
> $$\overline{\text{FC}}_{fleet} = \frac{\sum D_i}{\sum V_i} = \frac{6400}{1835} = \mathbf{3.4877 \text{ km/L}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 3 — Fleet average L/100 km (1 mark):**
> $$\text{L/100 km} = \frac{100}{\overline{\text{FC}}_{fleet}} = \frac{100}{3.4877} = \mathbf{28.67 \text{ L/100 km}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 4 — Total weekly fuel cost (2 marks):**
> $$\text{Cost} = \sum V_i \times P_f = 1835 \times 91.75 = \mathbf{₹\ 1{,}68{,}361.25}$$

**Cross-check (optional):** Cost per km = $1,68,361.25 / 6400 = ₹26.31/km. Reasonable for Indian long-haul trucking (typical range: ₹20–35/km in 2024).

---

### **Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**

> **(a)** Discuss the **causes of abnormal fuel consumption** in a fleet. Explain how modern telematics-based FCM systems help in **fuel theft detection and idling-loss reduction**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A bus fleet's telematics records the following readings for one bus on a depot-to-airport route:
>
> - Fuel at departure: **180.0 L**
> - Distance covered (one-way): **42 km**
> - Fuel at arrival (airport): **155.5 L**
> - Idling time at traffic signals: **18 minutes**
> - Idle fuel-rate spec: **1.0 L/h**
>
> Calculate:
> (i) **Total fuel consumed** in the trip.
> (ii) **Fuel consumed in motion only** (excluding idling).
> (iii) **km/L while moving** and **L/100 km**.
> (iv) **Liters wasted due to idling**, and the **percentage of trip fuel lost to idling**. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution:

> [!NOTE]
> **Causes of abnormal fuel consumption (3.5 marks — list with 1-line justification each):**
> 1. **Driver behaviour** — harsh acceleration, overspeeding, excessive idling (typically causes 8–15% extra burn).
> 2. **Vehicle condition** — clogged air filter, faulty injectors, under-inflated tyres, misfiring spark plugs.
> 3. **Load & routing** — overloaded axle, congested routes, frequent stop-go cycles.
> 4. **Fuel quality & theft** — adulterated diesel, unauthorized siphoning at night.
> 5. **Environmental factors** — extreme heat (AC load), high altitude (lower air density), headwinds.
> 6. **Auxiliary loads** — reefer units, telematics charging, in-cabin electrical accessories.

> [!NOTE]
> **Telematics-based detection (3.5 marks):**
> - **Fuel-theft detection:** Telematics compares fuel-level deltas against expected consumption rate derived from GPS distance. Any sudden drop > 3× evaporation threshold with engine OFF raises an instant SMS alert. Geo-fencing corroborates location.
> - **Idling-loss reduction:** The system logs idle duration (engine ON, speed = 0). Reports highlight drivers with > 15% idle time. Combined with **engine auto-cutoff** policies (e.g., idle > 5 min → shutdown), idling waste can drop by 60–70%.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution:

> [!NOTE]
> **Step (i) — Total fuel consumed in trip (1 mark):**
> $$V_{total} = 180.0 - 155.5 = \mathbf{24.5 \text{ L}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step (ii) — Idling fuel (1 mark):**
> $$V_{idle} = Q_{idle} \times t_{idle} = 1.0 \text{ L/h} \times \frac{18}{60} \text{ h} = \mathbf{0.30 \text{ L}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step (iii) — Motion fuel, km/L, L/100 km (2 marks):**
> $$V_{motion} = V_{total} - V_{idle} = 24.5 - 0.30 = \mathbf{24.20 \text{ L}}$$
> $$\text{km/L} = \frac{42}{24.20} = \mathbf{1.735 \text{ km/L}}$$
> $$\text{L/100 km} = \frac{24.20}{42} \times 100 = \mathbf{57.62 \text{ L/100 km}}$$
> *(Note: Low km/L is expected because this is a heavy city bus on a stop-go urban corridor.)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Step (iv) — Idling waste and percentage (3 marks):**
> $$\text{Idling loss (liters)} = \mathbf{0.30 \text{ L}}$$
> $$\% \text{ idling share} = \frac{0.30}{24.5} \times 100 = \mathbf{1.22\%}$$
> **Engineer’s commentary (for full credit):** Although only 1.22% in this single trip, **across an 8-hour shift with ~80 minutes of cumulative idling**, the loss scales to ~4–5%. Scaled to a 50-bus depot running 300 days/year, this is roughly **₹8–10 lakhs/year** of avoidable fuel burn — precisely the kind of insight FCM must surface.

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks on Fuel Consumption Monitoring questions:**
>
> 1. **Wrong fleet average (≈ 4 marks lost):** Computing the *simple arithmetic mean* of individual km/L instead of the *distance-weighted average* $\sum D_i / \sum V_i$. Examiners specifically test this trap. **Always default to the weighted formula unless the question explicitly says "simple mean."**
>
> 2. **Confusing km/L with L/100 km reciprocals:** $1/(\text{km/L}) \neq \text{L/100 km}$. You must **multiply by 100**. Many students forget the factor of 100 and lose 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Ignoring fuel density in mass-flow problems:** If the question gives fuel in **kg**, conversion to **L** requires division by density (≈ 0.832 kg/L for diesel). Skipping this loses 2 marks in conversion-heavy problems.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the units in the final answer:** Always carry units throughout the derivation. A numerically correct answer without units gets only 60% credit on that sub-part.
>
> 5. **Omitting the closing-the-loop step in descriptive answers:** When asked "explain FCM system," many students stop at the dashboard. **Always end with the operational feedback** (eco-driver training, route re-optimization) — that is where the "Management" half of *Fleet Management* is tested.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Definition core:** FCM = systematic measure + record + analyse + control of fuel use, with KPIs km/L and L/100 km.
- **Two reporting metrics:** **km/L (higher better)** vs **L/100 km (lower better)** — they are reciprocals scaled by 100; product = 100.
- **Five operational layers:** Sensing → Acquisition → Communication → Analytics → Feedback (close the loop!).
- **Fleet average = weighted average:** Always $\sum D_i / \sum V_i$. Never the simple mean — examiners test this trap.
- **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)** = g/kWh — used at engine-test bed level; remember to multiply flow (kg/h) by 1000 before dividing by power (kW).
- **Standard constants to memorize:** Diesel density **0.832 kg/L**; diesel CV **42.5 MJ/kg**; idle fuel rate **0.8–1.2 L/h** for medium trucks.
- **Theft detection rule:** $|\Delta V_{measured}| > Q_{idle} \cdot \Delta t \cdot k$ (with $k \approx 3$ to 5) raises an alert.
- **Cost identity:** $C_{km} = (\text{L/100 km} \times P_f) / 100$ — quick mental arithmetic for viva.
- **Emission link:** 1 L of diesel ≈ **3.169 kg CO₂** (use this constant when linking FCM to carbon audits).
- **Block diagram must show closed loop:** Sensor → TCU → Cloud → Dashboard → Manager → Driver training → back to sensor. Half-diagrams lose 3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strategies for Reducing Fuel Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT633 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Strategies for Reducing Fuel Costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Strategies for Reducing Fuel Costs

## 1. Core Definition and Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Fuel cost reduction strategies* in fleet management refer to the **systematic set of operational, technical, managerial, and behavioral interventions** adopted by a transport organization to **minimize the fuel consumed per unit of work output** (e.g., per kilometer, per ton-kilometer, or per trip), while maintaining acceptable levels of service quality, safety, and regulatory compliance.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, fuel cost reduction is treated as a **sub-system of fleet economics**, where fuel is one of the largest variable operating costs — typically accounting for **30% to 60%** of the total operating cost of a commercial fleet. Hence, every percentage point saved on fuel directly improves the **Operating Ratio (OR)** and the **Cost per Kilometer (CPKM)** of the fleet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metric Used by KTU Examiners:**
> **Fuel Efficiency = Distance Travelled (km) / Fuel Consumed (litres)**
> The reciprocal, **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC) = Fuel Consumed / Distance**, is also widely used.
> In heavy commercial vehicles, fuel efficiency in India is commonly benchmarked at **3.5 – 5.5 km/litre** for trucks and **8 – 14 km/litre** for LCVs.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaky Bucket" View

Imagine your fleet's fuel tank is a **bucket of water** with multiple small holes at the bottom. Each hole represents a source of fuel wastage:
- One hole = aggressive acceleration by drivers
- Another hole = idling at signals
- Another hole = under-inflated tyres
- Another hole = poor route planning
- Another hole = unmaintained air filters

Your job as a fleet manager is to **plug every hole systematically**. You cannot fix all holes at once, but identifying each leakage point and addressing it through a structured strategy will keep more fuel in the bucket — and more profit in the company's ledger.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** This topic is mapped to **CO2 — Apply fleet cost control techniques** and aligns with Bloom's Level **Apply / Analyze**.

---

## 2. Classification of Fuel Cost Reduction Strategies

The KTU 2024 module classifies fuel cost reduction strategies into **six major pillars**, which together form the *Fuel Economy Triangle*:

| Pillar | Domain | Approx. Savings Potential |
|--------|--------|---------------------------|
| 1. Vehicle Selection & Right-Sizing | Capital / Engineering | 5% – 15% |
| 2. Route & Trip Planning | Operational | 8% – 20% |
| 3. Driver Behaviour Management | Human Resource | 10% – 25% |
| 4. Preventive Maintenance | Engineering | 5% – 12% |
| 5. Technology & Telematics | Digital | 6% – 18% |
| 6. Fuel Procurement & Storage | Supply Chain | 3% – 10% |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Whenever a 14-mark question asks "Discuss strategies to reduce fuel cost," you must cover **at least four pillars** with one numerical example to score full marks. A purely descriptive answer without a formula or case is usually capped at 8–9 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Six Pillars of Fuel Cost Reduction

## Pillar 1 — Vehicle Selection and Right-Sizing

**Concept:** Match the vehicle capacity to the typical load requirement. An over-powered truck carrying 40% load wastes fuel disproportionately.

**Why it works:** Engines operate most efficiently near **60% – 75% of their rated load**. Below this, the engine still consumes substantial fuel to overcome rolling resistance and internal friction.

**How to apply:**
- Conduct a **load-utilization audit** every quarter.
- Downsize LCVs to handle last-mile delivery where trucks underperform.
- Choose **diesel over petrol** for high-utilization commercial vehicles due to higher energy density (**≈ 35.8 MJ/litre** for diesel vs. **32.0 MJ/litre** for petrol).

---

## Pillar 2 — Route and Trip Planning

**Concept:** Replace ad-hoc routing with **algorithm-optimized routing** that minimizes distance, congestion, and elevation gain.

**Why it works:** A 10% reduction in distance travelled produces almost a 10% reduction in fuel (linear relationship for steady-speed driving).

**How to apply:**
- Use **GIS-based route optimization software**.
- Avoid **urban congestion zones** during peak hours.
- Consolidate shipments to maintain high load factor.

---

## Pillar 3 — Driver Behaviour Management

**Concept:** Train drivers in **eco-driving techniques** and monitor their style via scorecards.

**Why it works:** Driver behaviour can cause a **fuel variation of up to 25%** between two drivers using identical vehicles on identical routes.

**Sub-strategies:**
- Smooth acceleration and braking
- Maintaining optimum speed (typically **45–60 km/h** for trucks)
- Avoiding unnecessary idling
- Anticipating traffic using horizon scanning

---

## Pillar 4 — Preventive Maintenance

**Concept:** Keep the vehicle in **factory-tuned condition** so that no mechanical degradation increases fuel burn.

**Why it works:** A clogged air filter alone can increase fuel consumption by **up to 10%**. Misaligned wheels increase rolling resistance.

**Key Maintenance Levers:**
- Clean air filters
- Correct tyre pressure (every 0.5 bar under-inflation ≈ 1% extra fuel)
- Timely engine tuning
- Quality engine oil (low-viscosity synthetic)
- Wheel alignment and balancing

---

## Pillar 5 — Technology and Telematics

**Concept:** Deploy **GPS, IoT sensors, and fleet management software** to monitor and control fuel use in real time.

**Why it works:** Visibility alone drives accountability; drivers and dispatchers behave differently when metrics are visible.

**Tools:**
- **GPS trackers** for route deviation alerts
- **Fuel level sensors** to detect theft and abnormal consumption
- **CAN-bus data loggers** to capture RPM, throttle, and brake events
- **ADAS** (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems) for speed limiting

---

## Pillar 6 — Fuel Procurement and Storage

**Concept:** Reduce the **per-litre purchase cost** and **storage losses**.

**Levers:**
- Bulk procurement contracts
- On-site fuel bowsers
- Quality checks to avoid adulterated fuel
- Anti-theft locking systems on tanks
- Bunker management and leak detection

---

## KTU Formula Sheet — Fuel Cost Reduction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **high-yield formulas** that KTU examiners expect in 14-mark derivations.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SFC} &= \frac{\text{Fuel Used (L)}}{\text{Distance (km)}} \quad &[\text{L/km}] \\[4pt]
\text{Fuel Efficiency (FE)} &= \frac{\text{Distance (km)}}{\text{Fuel Used (L)}} \quad &[\text{km/L}] \\[4pt]
\text{Total Fuel Cost} &= \text{Fuel Used (L)} \times \text{Rate per Litre} \quad &[\text{INR}] \\[4pt]
\% \text{ Savings} &= \frac{\text{Old Cost} - \text{New Cost}}{\text{Old Cost}} \times 100 \quad &[\%] \\[4pt]
\text{CPKM (Cost per km)} &= \frac{\text{Total Operating Cost}}{\text{Total km Run}} \quad &[\text{INR/km}] \\[4pt]
\text{Load Factor} &= \frac{\text{Actual Load}}{\text{Rated Payload Capacity}} \times 100 \quad &[\%] \\[4pt]
\text{Fuel Saved by Tyre Pressure} &= \text{Base FE} \times (0.02 \times \Delta P) \quad &[\text{km/L}] \\[4pt]
\text{Idling Fuel Loss} &= \text{Idle Hour} \times \text{Idle SFC} \quad &[\text{L}]
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Units Legend:**
> L = Litres, km = kilometres, INR = Indian Rupees, ΔP = Pressure deviation in bar, SFC = Specific Fuel Consumption.

---

## Real-World Engineering Utility

In real fleet operations, these strategies are deployed together in what is called a **"Fuel Management System (FMS)"**. Major logistics companies such as DHL, FedEx, and Indian firms like **TCI** and **Delhivery** report combined fuel savings of **12% – 18%** annually after full FMS deployment. The same principles are taught in KTU to sensitize future engineers to the **economic + environmental** double dividend of fuel economy — lower operating cost **and** lower CO₂ emissions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Environmental Linkage:** Every litre of diesel saved avoids approximately **2.68 kg of CO₂** being released into the atmosphere. This connects Module 2 directly to **Sustainable Fleet Operations**, often asked as a 3-mark KTU question.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations and Numerical Analysis

## Worked Example 1 — Effect of Tyre Pressure on Fuel Economy

**Problem Statement:**
A truck runs **50,000 km/year** at a baseline fuel efficiency of **4.0 km/litre**. If the tyres are under-inflated by **1.0 bar** on average, estimate the annual extra fuel cost. Assume diesel price = **₹95/litre**. Use the rule: every 0.5 bar under-inflation = **1% increase in fuel consumption**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert under-inflation to percentage increase.**
Pressure deviation is given as $\Delta P = 1.0$ bar. Per the rule:

$$
\text{Increase in consumption} = \frac{\Delta P}{0.5} \times 1\% = \frac{1.0}{0.5} \times 1\% = 2\%
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate baseline fuel consumption.**

$$
F_{\text{base}} = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{FE}} = \frac{50{,}000}{4.0} = 12{,}500 \text{ litres}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the extra fuel due to under-inflation.**

$$
F_{\text{extra}} = F_{\text{base}} \times 0.02 = 12{,}500 \times 0.02 = 250 \text{ litres}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to monetary loss.**

$$
\text{Loss} = F_{\text{extra}} \times \text{Rate} = 250 \times 95 = \text{₹}23{,}750
$$

**Final Answer:** The fleet loses **₹23,750 per truck per year** due to a 1 bar tyre pressure drop — purely from fuel cost.

> [!TIP]
> **Insight for students:** For a fleet of 100 such trucks, the annual loss becomes **₹23.75 lakh**. This is precisely why KTU examiners love this numerical.

---

## Worked Example 2 — Idling Fuel Cost Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A delivery van idles for an average of **2 hours/day** at customer unloading points. The idle specific fuel consumption is **1.2 litres/hour**. The van operates **300 days/year**. Calculate annual fuel wasted and cost (diesel @ ₹95/L).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Daily idle fuel.**

$$
F_{\text{daily}} = \text{Idle hours} \times \text{Idle SFC} = 2 \times 1.2 = 2.4 \text{ L/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual idle fuel.**

$$
F_{\text{annual}} = 2.4 \times 300 = 720 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual cost of idling.**

$$
\text{Cost} = 720 \times 95 = \text{₹}68{,}400
$$

**Conclusion:** By simply training drivers to switch off the engine during long idles (>1 minute), the fleet can recover **₹68,400 per van per year**.

---

## Worked Example 3 — Driver Behaviour Impact (Comparative Analysis)

**Problem Statement:**
Two drivers, A and B, drive identical trucks on identical routes. Driver A averages **3.5 km/L** while Driver B averages **4.5 km/L**. Each runs **60,000 km/year**. Calculate the annual fuel cost difference at ₹95/L.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Fuel used by Driver A.**

$$
F_A = \frac{60{,}000}{3.5} = 17{,}142.86 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 2 — Fuel used by Driver B.**

$$
F_B = \frac{60{,}000}{4.5} = 13{,}333.33 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 3 — Difference in fuel.**

$$
\Delta F = F_A - F_B = 17{,}142.86 - 13{,}333.33 = 3{,}809.52 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 4 — Monetary impact.**

$$
\text{Difference} = 3{,}809.52 \times 95 = \text{₹}3{,}61{,}904
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Training Driver A to drive like Driver B saves **≈ ₹3.62 lakh per year per truck**. This is the financial case for **eco-driving programmes**.

---

## Worked Example 4 — Route Optimization Savings

**Problem Statement:**
A fleet runs **2,000 km/day** at **5 km/L**. After route optimization software, the daily distance reduces by **8%**, and average speed increases from 35 to 45 km/h, lowering SFC from 0.20 L/km to 0.18 L/km. Compute the **% reduction in fuel cost**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Baseline daily fuel.**

$$
F_1 = 2{,}000 \times 0.20 = 400 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 2 — New daily distance.**

$$
D_2 = 2{,}000 \times (1 - 0.08) = 1{,}840 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 3 — New daily fuel.**

$$
F_2 = 1{,}840 \times 0.18 = 331.2 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage reduction.**

$$
\% \text{ Saving} = \frac{400 - 331.2}{400} \times 100 = \frac{68.8}{400} \times 100 = 17.2\%
$$

**Conclusion:** A combination of shorter route + better speed profile reduces fuel use by **17.2%**, even larger than either intervention alone — demonstrating the **multiplier effect** of stacked strategies.

---

## Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Best Strategy

| Strategy | Implementation Cost | Payback Period | Skill Required | KTU Exam Frequency |
|----------|--------------------|-----------------|----------------|--------------------|
| Driver Training | Low | 3–6 months | Soft skills | **Very High** |
| Tyre Pressure Mgmt | Very Low | < 1 month | Mechanical basics | High |
| Route Optimization | Medium | 6–12 months | GIS / IT | High |
| Telematics Deployment | High | 12–24 months | Electronics, Data | Medium |
| Preventive Maintenance | Medium | Ongoing | Mechanical | Very High |
| Vehicle Right-Sizing | Very High (CAPEX) | 36–60 months | Strategic | Medium |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
