> A logistics company operates a fleet of **120 vehicles**. During the last quarter, the following operational data was extracted from the analytics dashboard:
>
> - Total vehicles available for service: $N_{total} = 120$
> - Vehicles actively utilized: $N_{utilized} = 96$
> - Planned stops per route (average): $S_{planned} = 18$
> - Actual stops completed (average): $S_{actual} = 16$
> - Deliveries meeting quality spec: $D_{quality} = 14$
> - Distance covered: $D_{total} = 240{,}000 \text{ km}$
> - Fuel consumed: $F = 30{,}000 \text{ litres}$
> - Total operational cost: $C_{total} = \text{₹}42{,}00{,}000$
>
> **Compute:** (a) Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE), (b) Cost per Kilometer (CPK), (c) Fuel Efficiency (FE), and (d) the CO₂ emission in kg.

---

#### Step 1 — Compute Availability ($A$)

Availability is the **fraction of vehicles actually in operational service** during the period.

$$
A = \frac{N_{utilized}}{N_{total}} = \frac{96}{120}
$$

Performing the division:

$$
A = 0.80
$$

**Interpretation:** 80% of the fleet was available — meaning 24 vehicles were off-road for maintenance, breakdowns, or administrative reasons.

---

#### Step 2 — Compute Performance ($P$)

Performance measures how **efficiently** the active vehicles completed their assigned tasks.

$$
P = \frac{S_{actual}}{S_{planned}} = \frac{16}{18}
$$

Performing the division:

$$
P = 0.8888...
$$

**Rounded (KTU convention — 4 decimal places):**

$$
P \approx 0.8889
$$

**Interpretation:** The active vehicles completed 88.89% of their planned stops, indicating a 11.11% shortfall due to traffic, delays, or route deviations.

---

#### Step 3 — Compute Quality ($Q$)

Quality measures the fraction of completed work that met specifications.

$$
Q = \frac{D_{quality}}{S_{actual}} = \frac{14}{16}
$$

Performing the division:

$$
Q = 0.875
$$

**Interpretation:** Of the 16 stops completed, 14 met the quality standard, yielding 87.5% quality conformance.

---

#### Step 4 — Compute Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)

$$
OEE = A \times P \times Q
$$

Substituting the computed values:

$$
OEE = 0.80 \times 0.8889 \times 0.875
$$

**Multi-step evaluation:**

$$
OEE = 0.80 \times 0.8889 = 0.7111
$$

$$
OEE = 0.7111 \times 0.875 = 0.6222
$$

**Final OEE:**

$$
OEE = 0.6222 \;\; \text{(or 62.22%)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **World-class OEE benchmark is 85%**. A score of 62.22% indicates **significant improvement potential**, justifying continued investment in analytics-driven interventions.

---

#### Step 5 — Compute Cost per Kilometer (CPK)

$$
CPK = \frac{C_{total}}{D_{total}} = \frac{42{,}00{,}000}{240{,}000}
$$

Performing the division:

$$
CPK = 17.5 \;\; \text{₹/km}
$$

---

#### Step 6 — Compute Fuel Efficiency (FE)

$$
FE = \frac{D_{total}}{F} = \frac{240{,}000}{30{,}000}
$$

Performing the division:

$$
FE = 8.0 \;\; \text{km/litre}
$$

---

#### Step 7 — Compute Total CO₂ Emissions

Using the standard emission factor for diesel:

$$
E_{CO_2} = F \times 2.68 = 30{,}000 \times 2.68
$$

Performing the multiplication:

$$
E_{CO_2} = 80{,}400 \;\; \text{kg CO}_2
$$

Converting to metric tonnes (1 tonne = 1000 kg):

$$
E_{CO_2} = 80.4 \;\; \text{tonnes CO}_2
$$

---

### 3.3 Consolidated Results Table

| Metric | Symbol | Computed Value | Benchmark Status |
|--------|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| Availability | $A$ | 0.80 | ⚠ Below 90% target |
| Performance | $P$ | 0.8889 | ✓ Acceptable |
| Quality | $Q$ | 0.875 | ⚠ Below 95% target |
| **OEE** | $OEE$ | **0.6222 (62.22%)** | ❌ Below world-class |
| Cost per km | $CPK$ | ₹17.5/km | Industry dependent |
| Fuel Efficiency | $FE$ | 8.0 km/l | ✓ Healthy for diesel LCV |
| CO₂ Emissions | $E_{CO_2}$ | 80,400 kg | ESG reportable |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Data Analytics Architecture in Fleet Management

The following **block-level functional architecture flow** maps the complete data journey from physical sensors to executive dashboards, illustrating the importance of analytics as the **central nervous system** of a smart fleet.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Fleet Layer]:::layerA --> B[Data Acquisition Layer]:::layerB
    B --> C[Data Storage Layer]:::layerC
    C --> D[Data Processing Layer]:::layerD
    D --> E[Analytics Engine Layer]:::layerE
    E --> F[Visualization Layer]:::layerF
    F --> G[Decision Layer]:::layerG
    G --> H[Operational Action]:::layerH
    H --> I[Feedback Loop]:::layerI
    I --> A

    classDef layerA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef layerB fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef layerC fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef layerD fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000
    classDef layerE fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000
    classDef layerF fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,color:#000
    classDef layerG fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#1E90FF,color:#000
    classDef layerH fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#FF4500,color:#000
    classDef layerI fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,color:#000
```

**Layer-wise Description:**

| Layer | Components | Function |
|-------|-----------|----------|
| **A — Physical Fleet** | Trucks, buses, sensors, GPS, OBD-II | Real-world data generators |
| **B — Acquisition** | Telematics Control Units, RFID, CAN bus | Capture & transmit signals |
| **C — Storage** | Cloud data lake, time-series DB, SQL servers | Persistent data retention |
| **D — Processing** | ETL pipelines, Apache Spark, Kafka | Cleansing, transformation |
| **E — Analytics Engine** | ML models, statistical engines, AI/ML platforms | Pattern recognition & prediction |
| **F — Visualization** | Power BI, Tableau, Grafana dashboards | Human-readable insights |
| **G — Decision** | Fleet manager, AI recommendation engine | Strategic & tactical choices |
| **H — Action** | Route changes, maintenance orders, coaching | Real-world interventions |
| **I — Feedback** | Outcome monitoring, model retraining | Continuous improvement loop |

### 4.2 The Four Analytics Paradigms — Cognitive Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DA[Descriptive Analytics]:::des --> DIA[Diagnostic Analytics]:::dia
    DIA --> PA[Predictive Analytics]:::pred
    PA --> PRA[Prescriptive Analytics]:::pres

    DA --> DAQ[What Happened]:::question
    DIA --> DIAQ[Why It Happened]:::question
    PA --> PAQ[What Will Happen]:::question
    PRA --> PRAQ[What Should We Do]:::question

    classDef des fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef dia fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef pred fill:#FF6347,stroke:#B22222,color:#fff
    classDef pres fill:#9370DB,stroke:#4B0082,color:#fff
    classDef question fill:#F0F8FF,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
```

This hierarchy shows the **cognitive escalation** from hindsight (descriptive) to foresight (predictive) to action (prescriptive). KTU examiners often ask students to **classify a given use-case** into the correct paradigm.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
*"Define data analytics in the context of fleet management. List the four paradigms of data analytics with one fleet-specific example for each."*

**Model Answer (Expected Length: 8–10 lines):**

**Definition:**
Data analytics in fleet management is the systematic process of collecting, processing, and analyzing operational data from vehicles — including GPS traces, fuel consumption, driver behavior, and maintenance records — to generate actionable intelligence for improving efficiency, safety, and cost-effectiveness.

**Four Paradigms with Examples:**

| Paradigm | Fleet-Specific Example |
|----------|------------------------|
| **Descriptive** | Generating a monthly mileage report of all 120 vehicles |
| **Diagnostic** | Investigating why a particular truck's fuel efficiency dropped by 12% in March |
| **Predictive** | Forecasting that Vehicle No. 47's brake pads will fail in the next 3,000 km |
| **Prescriptive** | Recommending an alternate route to avoid a 45-minute traffic jam on NH-48 |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**
*"Explain the importance of Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) as a composite metric in fleet analytics. State the world-class benchmark value."*

**Model Answer (Expected Length: 6–8 lines):**

OEE is a composite metric that integrates three sub-components — **Availability, Performance, and Quality** — into a single dimensionless score between 0 and 1. It provides fleet managers with a **holistic view** of operational health, unlike isolated metrics that may mask underlying inefficiencies. A high OEE indicates that vehicles are available, performing tasks efficiently, and delivering quality output simultaneously.

$$
OEE = A \times P \times Q
$$

The **world-class benchmark for OEE is 85% (0.85)**. Scores below this threshold indicate significant improvement potential, often achieved through analytics-driven interventions such as predictive maintenance, route optimization, and driver coaching programs.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

*(a)* Explain the **five-pillar importance framework** of data analytics in fleet management. Discuss any two pillars in detail with suitable fleet-industry examples. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A delivery company operates a fleet of **80 vehicles**. For the month of June 2024, the following data is recorded:

- Active vehicles: $N_{utilized} = 64$
- Planned stops per active vehicle: $S_{planned} = 20$
- Actual stops completed: $S_{actual} = 17$
- Quality-compliant stops: $D_{quality} = 15$
- Total distance: $D_{total} = 160{,}000 \text{ km}$
- Total fuel: $F = 18{,}000 \text{ litres}$
- Total cost: $C_{total} = \text{₹}30{,}00{,}000$

Calculate: **(i)** OEE, **(ii)** Cost per Kilometer, **(iii)** Fuel Efficiency, and **(iv)** Total CO₂ emissions in kg. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — The Five-Pillar Framework:**

The importance of data analytics in fleet management can be organized into five interconnected pillars:

1. **Operational Efficiency Optimization** — Analytics identifies inefficient routes, excessive idling, and underutilized vehicles, reducing fuel burn by 8–15%.

2. **Predictive & Preventive Maintenance** — ML models predict component failures 2–4 weeks in advance, reducing breakdown costs by 3–5x compared to reactive maintenance.

3. **Driver Behavior & Safety** — Real-time monitoring of harsh braking, speeding, and acceleration enables driver coaching, lowering accident rates by 20–30%.

4. **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Reduction** — Analytics identifies high-cost-per-km vehicles, optimizing replacement and procurement decisions, improving margins by 3–8%.

5. **Regulatory Compliance & ESG Reporting** — Automated emission logging, ELD compliance, and audit trails ensure adherence to BS-VI, Euro 6, and GRI standards.

**Detailed Discussion of Two Pillars (Suggested):**

*Pillar 1 — Operational Efficiency:* DHL's deployment of dynamic routing analytics reduced average delivery times by 18% in urban European networks. Idle-time alerts alone saved 2.3 million litres of diesel annually.

*Pillar 2 — Predictive Maintenance:* A leading Indian bus operator used LSTM-based vibration analytics to predict axle-bearing failures, increasing fleet uptime from 84% to 96% within one year.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Availability:**

$$
A = \frac{N_{utilized}}{N_{total}} = \frac{64}{80} = 0.80
$$

**Step 2 — Performance:**

$$
P = \frac{S_{actual}}{S_{planned}} = \frac{17}{20} = 0.85
$$

**Step 3 — Quality:**

$$
Q = \frac{D_{quality}}{S_{actual}} = \frac{15}{17} = 0.8824
$$

**Step 4 — OEE:**

$$
OEE = 0.80 \times 0.85 \times 0.8824
$$

$$
OEE = 0.80 \times 0.85 = 0.6800
$$

$$
OEE = 0.6800 \times 0.8824 = 0.6000
$$

$$
\boxed{OEE = 0.60 \;\; \text{(or 60.00%)}}
$$

**Step 5 — Cost per Kilometer:**

$$
CPK = \frac{30{,}00{,}000}{160{,}000} = \text{₹}18.75 \text{ per km}
$$

**Step 6 — Fuel Efficiency:**

$$
FE = \frac{160{,}000}{18{,}000} = 8.889 \;\; \text{km/litre}
$$

**Step 7 — CO₂ Emissions:**

$$
E_{CO_2} = 18{,}000 \times 2.68 = 48{,}240 \;\; \text{kg}
$$

| Result | Value |
|--------|-------|
| OEE | 0.60 (60.00%) |
| CPK | ₹18.75/km |
| FE | 8.889 km/l |
| CO₂ | 48,240 kg |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Question A Part (b):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often use $S_{planned}$ in the Quality denominator instead of $S_{actual}$. The correct denominator for $Q$ is **actual stops completed**, not planned stops. This single error cascades into a wrong OEE.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to multiply $FE$ or $CPK$ by 100 when reporting as a percentage.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing the CO₂ unit as "tonnes" without converting from kg. Always show the **conversion step explicitly** to earn full marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

*(a)* With the aid of a block diagram, describe the **data analytics architecture** in fleet management. Explain the role of the **Analytics Engine Layer** in detail. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Differentiate between the **four paradigms of data analytics** (descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, prescriptive) using a fleet management use-case of your choice. For each paradigm, state: (i) the core question answered, (ii) one technique/method used, and (iii) one fleet-specific application. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Architecture & Analytics Engine Role:**

The data analytics architecture in fleet management follows a **nine-layer block flow**:

1. **Physical Fleet Layer** — Vehicles with embedded sensors (GPS, OBD-II, accelerometers).
2. **Data Acquisition Layer** — Telematics Control Units (TCUs) and CAN bus interfaces capture sensor data.
3. **Data Storage Layer** — Cloud data lakes and time-series databases (e.g., InfluxDB, AWS Timestream).
4. **Data Processing Layer** — ETL pipelines using Apache Spark or Kafka for cleansing and transformation.
5. **Analytics Engine Layer** — Core intelligence hub (described below).
6. **Visualization Layer** — Dashboards via Power BI, Tableau, or Grafana.
7. **Decision Layer** — Fleet managers and AI-recommendation systems.
8. **Operational Action** — Real-world interventions (route changes, maintenance).
9. **Feedback Loop** — Outcome monitoring and model retraining.

**Role of the Analytics Engine Layer:**
This is the **brain of the entire architecture**. It hosts:
- **Statistical models** for trend analysis.
- **Machine Learning algorithms** (Random Forest, XGBoost, LSTM) for predictive maintenance and demand forecasting.
- **Optimization solvers** (linear programming, genetic algorithms) for route optimization.
- **Anomaly detection engines** for fraud and theft identification.

It transforms cleansed data into **predictive scores, classifications, and optimization recommendations** that flow downstream to the decision layer.

---

**Part (b) — Four Paradigms Differentiated:**

*Use-case chosen: A truck breakdown on the Mumbai-Pune expressway.*

| Paradigm | Core Question | Technique | Fleet Application |
|----------|---------------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Descriptive** | *What happened?* | Dashboard reporting, aggregation | "Truck MH-12-3456 broke down at 14:32 hrs on km 78 of the expressway." |
| **Diagnostic** | *Why did it happen?* | Root-cause analysis, drill-down queries | "Coolant temperature was 112°C for 23 minutes before failure — radiator fan malfunction." |
| **Predictive** | *What will happen?* | LSTM neural network, survival analysis | "Truck MH-12-9988 has a 78% probability of brake failure in the next 2,500 km." |
| **Prescriptive** | *What should we do?* | Optimization solver, decision tree | "Reschedule Truck MH-12-3456's cargo to MH-14-2233; book service slot at Pune hub tomorrow 06:00 hrs." |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Question B:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Conflating **predictive** and **prescriptive** analytics. Predictive tells you *what will happen*; prescriptive tells you *what to do about it*. Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In Part (a), students draw the diagram but **fail to explain** the Analytics Engine Layer. Always pair the diagram with a **detailed textual explanation** to secure full 7 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Listing techniques (e.g., "uses ML") without naming the **specific algorithm**. Examiners reward specificity — write "Random Forest classifier" or "LSTM neural network", not just "machine learning".

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Data Analytics Importance):**

- ✅ **Data analytics** is the systematic processing of fleet data (GPS, fuel, maintenance, driver logs) into actionable intelligence.

- ✅ **Four paradigms:** Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive. Cognitive escalation: *hindsight → insight → foresight → action*.

- ✅ **Five-pillar importance framework:** (1) Operational Efficiency, (2) Predictive Maintenance, (3) Driver Safety, (4) TCO Reduction, (5) Regulatory & ESG Compliance.

- ✅ **OEE = A × P × Q** — world-class benchmark is **85% (0.85)**. Always compute sub-components first, then multiply.

- ✅ **Critical KPIs to memorize:** FE (km/l), CPK (₹/km), OTD (%), MTBF (hours), MTTR (hours), ITR (%), DSS (0–100).

- ✅ **CO₂ emission factor for diesel = 2.68 kg CO₂/litre.** Use this exact constant in numerical problems.

- ✅ **Predictive analytics uses ML algorithms** like Random Forest, LSTM, and XGBoost. Be specific in exam answers.

- ✅ **Analytics is NOT a software tool** — it is a **decision-making philosophy** enabled by data, algorithms, and engineering judgment.

- ✅ **Differentiate Predictive vs. Prescriptive** — predictive forecasts; prescriptive recommends actions.

- ✅ **Digital twin concept** is increasingly relevant — a virtual replica of the physical fleet used for "what-if" simulations. Mention this for higher-order Bloom's questions (Analyze/Evaluate).

- ✅ **ESG reporting linkage** — analytics enables automated GRI-aligned sustainability disclosures, a growing KTU 2024 emphasis under NEP 2020's sustainability mandate.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) for Fleets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT633 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) for Fleets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Key Performance Indicator (KPI)** in the context of fleet management is a quantifiable, measurable metric used by transport operators and logistics managers to evaluate the operational efficiency, financial health, safety compliance, and environmental performance of a vehicle fleet over a defined reporting period. KPIs transform raw operational data into actionable intelligence that supports strategic decision-making, regulatory compliance, and continuous improvement within the transport value chain.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAUT633)** framework, KPIs are classified as the diagnostic instruments of fleet management — they convert the four core managerial functions (Planning, Organizing, Leading, Controlling) into measurable outcomes aligned with **Course Outcome CO3**: *"Analyze fleet performance metrics and recommend optimization strategies for transport operations."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> A KPI is *not* just a number — it is a **SMART metric** (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) linked directly to a fleet management objective. The seven recognized KPI families for commercial fleets are:
> 1. **Utilization KPIs** (asset productivity)
> 2. **Financial KPIs** (cost economics)
> 3. **Fuel & Energy KPIs** (consumption efficiency)
> 4. **Maintenance KPIs** (asset reliability)
> 5. **Safety KPIs** (risk management)
> 6. **Driver KPIs** (human-factor performance)
> 7. **Environmental KPIs** (emissions and sustainability)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / The "Hospital Pulse Monitor" Intuition

Imagine a fleet manager as the **head doctor of a large hospital**, and each vehicle in the fleet as a **patient under intensive care**. The doctor cannot just "feel" whether 200 patients are healthy by walking around the ward. Instead, she relies on a **pulse monitor, blood-pressure cuff, oxygen meter, and thermometer** — each giving her ONE specific, numeric reading.

In the same way:

- The **Pulse Rate** of a fleet = **Vehicle Utilization Rate** (how hard each asset is working).
- The **Blood Pressure** = **Cost per Kilometer** (the financial health pressure).
- The **Oxygen Saturation** = **Fuel Efficiency** (how cleanly the fleet "breathes" fuel).
- The **Body Temperature** = **Breakdown Frequency** (any fever = an overheating asset).
- The **ECG Chart** = **On-Time Delivery Rate** (the rhythm of the entire fleet).

If any KPI goes outside its **safe range** (e.g., utilization drops below **65%**, cost/km rises above ₹12, fuel efficiency falls below **3.5 km/L**), the fleet manager immediately knows **where to intervene** — just as a doctor would prescribe treatment based on the abnormal reading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:**
> A KPI without a **target value** is just data; a KPI **with a target, threshold, and trend** is true performance management. Always remember the **3-T Rule** in your answer: **Target – Trend – Threshold**.

---

### 1.3 Taxonomy of Fleet KPIs — The Five-Tier Classification

| Tier | KPI Family | Primary Question Answered | Strategic Purpose |
|------|-----------|---------------------------|-------------------|
| **Tier 1** | Operational KPIs | *Are the vehicles working?* | Asset productivity |
| **Tier 2** | Financial KPIs | *Are we making money?* | Cost control |
| **Tier 3** | Fuel & Energy KPIs | *Are we consuming efficiently?* | Energy economics |
| **Tier 4** | Maintenance KPIs | *Are the assets reliable?* | Reliability engineering |
| **Tier 5** | Safety & Environmental KPIs | *Are we compliant and green?* | Risk + ESG |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Tier 1 — Operational (Utilization) KPIs

**2.1.1 Fleet Utilization Rate (FUR)**

This is the **percentage of available fleet hours that are actually used in revenue-generating or productive work**. It is the single most-watched KPI by transport managers because under-utilized assets represent sunk capital.

$$FUR = \frac{\text{Productive Hours Operated}}{\text{Total Available Hours}} \times 100\%$$

- **Healthy benchmark (KTU case studies):** **70%–85%** for commercial truck fleets; **>90%** indicates over-utilization risk (driver fatigue, accelerated wear).

**2.1.2 Vehicle Availability**

The opposite-side view of utilization: *what fraction of the fleet is physically ready to operate at any given moment?*

$$V_{avail} = \frac{\text{Operational Vehicles}}{\text{Total Fleet Size}} \times 100\%$$

**2.1.3 Downtime Percentage (DT%)**

$$DT\% = \frac{\text{Hours Out of Service}}{\text{Total Hours in Reporting Period}} \times 100\%$$

**2.1.4 Idle Time Ratio (ITR)**

Modern telematics measures the time engines run without movement — a hidden fuel-waster.

$$ITR = \frac{\text{Total Idle Hours}}{\text{Total Engine Running Hours}} \times 100\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Industry research indicates that **one hour of idling ≈ 1 to 1.5 litres of wasted diesel**. A typical KPI target is **ITR ≤ 5%**.

---

### 2.2 Tier 2 — Financial KPIs

**2.2.1 Cost per Kilometer (CPK)**

The "rupees per kilometre" figure is the **financial thermometer** of a fleet.

$$CPK = \frac{\text{Total Operating Cost}}{\text{Total Kilometres Driven}} \; (\text{₹/km})$$

Total Operating Cost = Fuel + Maintenance + Driver Wages + Insurance + Depreciation + Tyres + Toll + Overheads.

**2.2.2 Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)**

A lifecycle view — purchase price through disposal.

$$TCO = C_{purchase} + C_{fuel} + C_{maint} + C_{driver} + C_{ins} + C_{tax} - S_{residual}$$

where $S_{residual}$ = salvage/residual value.

**2.2.3 Revenue per Vehicle per Month**

$$R_{vpm} = \frac{\text{Total Monthly Revenue}}{\text{Number of Active Vehicles}}$$

**2.2.4 Return on Fleet Assets (ROFA)**

$$ROFA = \frac{\text{Net Annual Fleet Profit}}{\text{Total Value of Fleet Assets}} \times 100\%$$

---

### 2.3 Tier 3 — Fuel & Energy KPIs

**2.3.1 Fuel Efficiency (km/L)**

$$\eta_{fuel} = \frac{\text{Total Distance (km)}}{\text{Total Fuel Consumed (L)}}$$

**2.3.2 Cost per Litre-Equivalent per Tonne-km**

For load-carrying fleets:

$$C_{tkm} = \frac{\text{Total Fuel Cost}}{\text{Payload (t)} \times \text{Distance (km)}} \; (\text{₹/tonne-km})$$

---

### 2.4 Tier 4 — Maintenance & Reliability KPIs

**2.4.1 Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)**

$$MTBF = \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$$

**2.4.2 Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)**

$$MTTR = \frac{\text{Total Repair Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$$

**2.4.3 Maintenance Cost per Vehicle per Year (MCV)**

$$MCV = \frac{\text{Total Maintenance Spend}}{\text{Average Fleet Size}}$$

**2.4.4 Breakdown Rate (BD%)**

$$BD\% = \frac{\text{Vehicles that Broke Down}}{\text{Total Fleet}} \times 100\%$$

---

### 2.5 Tier 5 — Safety & Environmental KPIs

**2.5.1 Accident Frequency Rate (AFR)**

$$AFR = \frac{\text{Number of Reportable Accidents} \times 10^6}{\text{Total Kilometres Driven}}$$

**2.5.2 CO₂ Emissions per Tonne-km**

$$E_{CO_2} = \frac{\text{Fuel Used (L)} \times 2.68 \; \text{kg/L}}{\text{Tonne-km Hauled}}$$

(The factor **2.68 kg CO₂/L diesel** is the standard IPCC emission coefficient.)

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | KPI | Formula | Industry Target / Unit |
|---|-----|---------|-------------------------|
| 1 | Fleet Utilization Rate | $\frac{\text{Productive Hours}}{\text{Available Hours}} \times 100$ | **70–85 %** |
| 2 | Vehicle Availability | $\frac{\text{Operational Vehicles}}{\text{Total Fleet}} \times 100$ | **> 90 %** |
| 3 | Cost per km | $\frac{\text{Total Op. Cost}}{\text{Total km}}$ | **₹/km** (benchmark varies) |
| 4 | Fuel Efficiency | $\frac{\text{km}}{\text{Litres}}$ | **km/L** |
| 5 | Idle Time Ratio | $\frac{\text{Idle Hrs}}{\text{Engine Hrs}} \times 100$ | **< 5 %** |
| 6 | MTBF | $\frac{\text{Total Op. Time}}{\text{No. of Failures}}$ | **Hours** |
| 7 | MTTR | $\frac{\text{Total Repair Hrs}}{\text{No. of Repairs}}$ | **Hours** |
| 8 | Accident Frequency Rate | $\frac{\text{Accidents} \times 10^6}{\text{km Driven}}$ | **per million km** |
| 9 | CO₂ per tonne-km | $\frac{\text{Fuel L} \times 2.68}{\text{Tonne-km}}$ | **kg CO₂/tkm** |
| 10 | TCO | $\Sigma (\text{Lifecycle costs}) - \text{Salvage}$ | **₹/vehicle-life** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip (Valuator's View):** Always write the **unit** with every KPI answer. Writing "85" without "%" or "km/L" will cost you at least **half a mark** on a 7-mark sub-question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Composite Fleet Utilization Calculation

A logistics company owns **20 trucks**. In the month of June, the following data is recorded:

- Each truck is available **24 hours/day × 30 days = 720 hours**.
- **Truck 1 to 10**: ran for **600 productive hours** each.
- **Truck 11 to 16**: ran for **540 productive hours** each.
- **Truck 17 to 20**: ran for **450 productive hours** each.

**Find:** (a) the **overall fleet utilization rate**, and (b) classify each segment as *Healthy / Under-utilized / Over-utilized*.

---

#### Step 1 — Total Available Hours (TAH)

$$TAH = 20 \times 720 = 14{,}400 \; \text{hours}$$

*Explanation:* Each truck has 720 available hours in June, and with 20 trucks the fleet-wide availability is 14,400 hours.

---

#### Step 2 — Total Productive Hours (TPH)

$$
\begin{aligned}
TPH &= (10 \times 600) + (6 \times 540) + (4 \times 450) \\
    &= 6{,}000 + 3{,}240 + 1{,}800 \\
    &= 11{,}040 \; \text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* We compute productive hours per sub-group and add them. This is the denominator of utilization and the **real productive work done** by the fleet.

---

#### Step 3 — Fleet Utilization Rate (FUR)

$$
\begin{aligned}
FUR &= \frac{11{,}040}{14{,}400} \times 100\% \\
    &= 0.76667 \times 100\% \\
    &= 76.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* 76.67% of all available fleet hours were used productively. This lies within the KTU-benchmark **70–85 %** band, indicating the fleet is healthy overall.

---

#### Step 4 — Segment-wise Utilization

| Segment | Trucks | Hours Used | Available Hours | FUR |
|---------|--------|-----------|-----------------|------|
| A | 10 | 600 | 720 | $\frac{600}{720} = 83.33\%$ |
| B | 6 | 540 | 720 | $\frac{540}{720} = 75.00\%$ |
| C | 4 | 450 | 720 | $\frac{450}{720} = 62.50\%$ |

*Explanation:*
- Segment A at **83.33%** is at the **upper edge** of healthy — risk of over-utilization / driver fatigue.
- Segment B at **75.00%** is **healthy**.
- Segment C at **62.50%** is **under-utilized** — recommend redeployment or right-sizing.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Cost per Kilometre and TCO Analysis

A delivery fleet operator provides:

- **Purchase price per truck:** ₹18,00,000
- **Annual fuel cost:** ₹4,50,000
- **Annual maintenance cost:** ₹80,000
- **Annual driver wages:** ₹3,00,000
- **Annual insurance + tax:** ₹60,000
- **Annual kilometres driven:** 50,000 km
- **Residual value after 7 years:** ₹4,00,000
- **Useful life:** 7 years

**Find:** (a) Cost per km. (b) Total Cost of Ownership (TCO).

---

#### Step 1 — Annual Operating Cost (AOC)

$$
\begin{aligned}
AOC &= 4{,}50{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 60{,}000 \\
    &= 8{,}90{,}000 \; \text{₹/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Operating cost = fuel + maintenance + wages + insurance/tax. We **exclude** depreciation here because it belongs to TCO.

---

#### Step 2 — Cost per Kilometre (CPK)

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPK &= \frac{8{,}90{,}000}{50{,}000} \\
    &= 17.80 \; \text{₹/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the running cost. ₹17.80/km is typical for an LCV in Indian conditions.

---

#### Step 3 — Annual Depreciation

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{annual} &= \frac{18{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000}{7} \\
           &= \frac{14{,}00{,}000}{7} \\
           &= 2{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Straight-line depreciation divides the net capital loss (cost − residual) equally across the asset's life.

---

#### Step 4 — Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) over 7 Years

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCO_{7yr} &= \text{Purchase} + 7 \times AOC \\
          &= 18{,}00{,}000 + (7 \times 8{,}90{,}000) \\
          &= 18{,}00{,}000 + 62{,}30{,}000 \\
          &= 80{,}30{,}000 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCO_{net} &= TCO_{7yr} - S_{residual} \\
          &= 80{,}30{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 \\
          &= 76{,}30{,}000 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* TCO captures capital + operating cost over the entire life, minus what you recover at resale. **₹76.30 lakh over 7 years** is the true economic footprint of one truck.

---

#### Step 5 — Lifecycle Cost per km

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPK_{life} &= \frac{76{,}30{,}000}{7 \times 50{,}000} \\
           &= \frac{76{,}30{,}000}{3{,}50{,}000} \\
           &= 21.80 \; \text{₹/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Including capital recovery, the *true* cost is **₹21.80/km**, of which only ₹17.80 is operating. The ₹4.00 difference is depreciation.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — MTBF and MTTR for Maintenance Decision

A bus operated **3,650 hours** in a year. It failed **5 times** and the total repair time was **60 hours**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTBF &= \frac{3{,}650}{5} = 730 \; \text{hours per failure} \\
MTTR &= \frac{60}{5} = 12 \; \text{hours per repair}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Interpretation:* MTBF > MTTR by a factor of **~60×**, indicating good preventive maintenance. The KPI **Availability** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{inst} &= \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\% \\
         &= \frac{730}{730 + 12} \times 100\% \\
         &= 98.38\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How KPIs Drive Decisions

| KPI Reading | Trigger Threshold | Management Action |
|-------------|-------------------|--------------------|
| FUR < 60% | Critical under-use | Right-size fleet / redeploy vehicles |
| FUR > 90% | Over-utilization | Hire more vehicles / reduce load |
| CPK ↑ 15% YoY | Cost escalation | Audit fuel + maintenance contracts |
| ITR > 8% | Fuel waste | Install idle-cutoff, driver training |
| MTTR > 24 hr | Slow workshop | Outsource / improve tooling |
| AFR > 2 per M-km | Unsafe | Driver retraining, telematics |
| CO₂/tkm ↑ | ESG failure | Route optimisation, EV pilot |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 KPI Hierarchy Map (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FMSGoal["Fleet Management Goal: Maximize Profit per Asset"]
    Obj1["Objective 1: Maximize Utilization"]
    Obj2["Objective 2: Minimize Cost"]
    Obj3["Objective 3: Maximize Reliability"]
    Obj4["Objective 4: Ensure Safety"]
    Obj5["Objective 5: Reduce Emissions"]

    K1["KPI: Fleet Utilization Rate"]
    K2["KPI: Cost per km"]
    K3["KPI: Fuel Efficiency"]
    K4["KPI: MTBF MTTR"]
    K5["KPI: Breakdown Rate"]
    K6["KPI: Accident Frequency Rate"]
    K7["KPI: Idle Time Ratio"]
    K8["KPI: CO2 per tonne-km"]
    K9["KPI: Vehicle Availability"]
    K10["KPI: On Time Delivery"]

    FMSGoal --> Obj1
    FMSGoal --> Obj2
    FMSGoal --> Obj3
    FMSGoal --> Obj4
    FMSGoal --> Obj5

    Obj1 --> K1
    Obj1 --> K9
    Obj1 --> K10
    Obj2 --> K2
    Obj2 --> K3
    Obj3 --> K4
    Obj3 --> K5
    Obj3 --> K7
    Obj4 --> K6
    Obj5 --> K8

    classDef goal fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#000
    classDef obj fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#065f46,color:#000
    classDef kpi fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
    class FMSGoal goal
    class Obj1,Obj2,Obj3,Obj4,Obj5 obj
    class K1,K2,K3,K4,K5,K6,K7,K8,K9,K10 kpi
```

---

### 4.2 KPI Reporting Cycle — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Raw Data Source: GPS Telematics"]
    B["Data Source: Fuel Cards"]
    C["Data Source: Workshop Logs"]
    D["Data Source: HR Driver Records"]
    E["Data Lake / Cloud Server"]
    F["KPI Engine Calculation Layer"]
    G["KPI Dashboard Visualization"]
    H["Fleet Manager Decision"]
    I["Operational Action"]

    A --> E
    B --> E
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I -.feedback loop.-> F

    classDef src fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000
    classDef sys fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D src
    class E,F,G sys
    class H,I dec
```

*Interpretation:* This represents the **closed-loop KPI management system**. Data flows from operational sources → calculation engine → dashboard → manager → action → back to engine (continuous improvement). This is also called the **PDCA cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act) applied to fleet analytics.

---

### 4.3 Leading vs Lagging KPI Balance

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["Leading KPI Predicts Future"]
    L1a["Driver Behaviour Score"]
    L1b["Scheduled Maintenance Compliance"]
    L1c["Pre Trip Inspection Pass Rate"]

    L2["Lagging KPI Reports Past"]
    L2a["Accident Frequency Rate"]
    L2b["Cost per km Achieved"]
    L2c["Fuel Efficiency Actual"]

    L1 --> L2
    L2 -.feedback.-> L1

    classDef leading fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    classDef lagging fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000
    class L1a,L1b,L1c leading
    class L2a,L2b,L2c lagging
```

*Interpretation:* **Leading KPIs** (predictive, proactive) are harder to measure but give early warnings. **Lagging KPIs** (historical, reactive) confirm whether past actions worked. A mature fleet operation tracks **both**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
> *Define Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) in fleet management. List any FOUR major categories of fleet KPIs.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Key Performance Indicator (KPI)** in fleet management is a *quantifiable metric* used to evaluate and monitor the operational, financial, safety, and environmental performance of a vehicle fleet against defined targets.

The four major categories are:
1. **Operational KPIs** — e.g., Fleet Utilization Rate.
2. **Financial KPIs** — e.g., Cost per kilometre.
3. **Maintenance KPIs** — e.g., MTBF, MTTR.
4. **Safety/Environmental KPIs** — e.g., Accident Frequency Rate, CO₂ per tonne-km.

*(1 mark definition + 0.5 mark × 4 categories = 3 marks)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**  
> *Distinguish between **Leading KPIs** and **Lagging KPIs** with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Leading KPI | Lagging KPI |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| **Nature** | Predictive / forward-looking | Historical / backward-looking |
| **Function** | Warns of future performance | Confirms past performance |
| **Action timing** | Proactive | Reactive |
| **Example** | Pre-trip inspection pass rate | Monthly fuel efficiency achieved |
| **Example 2** | Driver behaviour score | Accident frequency rate |

*(1 mark distinction + 1 mark example each = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

---

#### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Operational + Financial KPI Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Actual Pattern Model]**

> A transport company operates a fleet of **25 trucks**. For the month of May, the following data is given:
> - Total available hours per truck = **720 hours**.
> - Total productive hours of the fleet = **13,500 hours**.
> - Total fuel consumed = **18,000 litres**.
> - Total distance covered = **1,20,000 km**.
> - Total operating cost = ₹20,00,000.
> - Total breakdown events = **8**, total repair hours = **96**.
> - Number of reportable accidents = **3**.
>
> **Compute and interpret:**
> **(a)** Fleet Utilization Rate, Fuel Efficiency (km/L), and Cost per km.  *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF), Mean Time to Repair (MTTR), and Accident Frequency Rate (per million km). Comment on the reliability and safety status. *(7 marks)*

---

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Fleet Utilization Rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FUR &= \frac{13{,}500}{25 \times 720} \times 100\% \\
    &= \frac{13{,}500}{18{,}000} \times 100\% \\
    &= 75.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Interpretation:* At **75%**, the fleet is **healthy** (within the 70–85% benchmark). **1 mark formula + 1 mark substitution + 1 mark answer + 1 mark interpretation = 4 marks.**

**(ii) Fuel Efficiency**

$$
\eta_{fuel} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{18{,}000} = 6.67 \; \text{km/L}
$$

*Interpretation:* 6.67 km/L is **typical for Indian HCVs**. **1 mark.**

**(iii) Cost per km**

$$
CPK = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{1{,}20{,}000} = 16.67 \; \text{₹/km}
$$

*Interpretation:* ₹16.67/km is reasonable. **1 mark.**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**(i) MTBF**

Total operating hours per truck ≈ 13,500 / 25 = **540 hours**.
Failures = 8.

$$
MTBF = \frac{13{,}500}{8} = 1{,}687.5 \; \text{hours/failure}
$$

**(ii) MTTR**

$$
MTTR = \frac{96}{8} = 12 \; \text{hours/repair}
$$

*Interpretation:* MTBF ≫ MTTR → good preventive maintenance. **3 marks.**

**(iii) Accident Frequency Rate (AFR)**

$$
AFR = \frac{3 \times 10^6}{1{,}20{,}000} = 25 \; \text{accidents per million km}
$$

*Interpretation:* 25/M-km is **high** — exceeds typical safe benchmark of < 5/M-km. **Driver retraining, telematics, and route audit recommended.** **4 marks** (1.5 for formula + 1 for substitution + 1.5 for interpretation).

---

#### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Maintenance + Environmental KPI Design

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

> A municipal corporation runs a **bus fleet of 40 vehicles** for public transport. The annual operating data is:
> - Total km operated = **30,00,000 km**
> - Total diesel consumed = **3,00,000 litres**
> - Maintenance cost = ₹24,00,000
> - Total breakdowns = **20**
> - Total downtime hours = **400 hours**
> - Total accidents = **4**
> - Idle hours recorded = **1,500 hours**
> - Total engine running hours = **30,000 hours**
>
> **(a)** Compute the **maintenance cost per vehicle**, **breakdown rate**, **availability**, and **idle time ratio**. Comment on the maintenance health. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Compute the **CO₂ emissions per km** and **accident frequency rate**. Recommend TWO green-fleet initiatives to reduce the carbon footprint. *(7 marks)*

---

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Maintenance Cost per Vehicle**

$$
MCV = \frac{24{,}00{,}000}{40} = ₹60{,}000 \; \text{per vehicle per year}
$$

**(ii) Breakdown Rate**

$$
BD\% = \frac{20}{40} \times 100\% = 50\%
$$

**(iii) Availability**

Total fleet hours = 40 × (working days × hours). Assuming per vehicle = 750 hours/year = 30,000 hours total.

$$
A = \frac{30{,}000 - 400}{30{,}000} \times 100\% = 98.67\%
$$

**(iv) Idle Time Ratio**

$$
ITR = \frac{1{,}500}{30{,}000} \times 100\% = 5.00\%
$$

*Interpretation:* Availability > 98% and ITR at the 5% threshold are healthy. **However, 50% breakdown rate is alarming** — each bus fails nearly once every two years; reliability needs improvement through **preventive maintenance schedule adherence and predictive analytics (IoT-based).**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**(i) CO₂ Emissions**

$$
CO_2 = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 2.68 = 8{,}04{,}000 \; \text{kg} = 8{,}040 \; \text{tonnes/year}
$$

$$
CO_2 \text{ per km} = \frac{8{,}040}{30{,}00{,}000} \times 10^3 = 2.68 \; \text{kg/km}
$$

**(ii) Accident Frequency Rate**

$$
AFR = \frac{4 \times 10^6}{30{,}00{,}000} = 1.33 \; \text{per million km}
$$

*Interpretation:* AFR is within safe band, but CO₂ at **2.68 kg/km** is typical for diesel buses — scope for improvement.

**Two Recommended Green Initiatives (2 × 1.5 marks = 3 marks):**

1. **Electrification Pilot:** Replace **5%** of fleet per year with electric buses, cutting diesel-CO₂ by the same proportion.
2. **Route Optimisation + Eco-Driving Training:** Using telematics to reduce idle time from 5% → 2% and improve fuel efficiency by 8–10%.
3. **Adoption of BS-VI Stage 2 / Euro-VI equivalent fuels** with biodiesel blending (B20).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KPI Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the percentage sign** — writing 75 instead of **75%** in Fleet Utilization Rate. Costs **0.5 mark**.
> 2. **Inverting numerator and denominator** in MTBF (students often write `Failures / Time` instead of `Time / Failures`). Costs **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Not stating units** — every KPI answer must end with units (km/L, ₹/km, hrs, per million km). Units omitted = **½ mark deduction each**.
> 4. **No interpretation** — a numerical answer without a one-line interpretation is treated as incomplete by strict valuators. Costs up to **2 marks** on a 7-mark sub-question.
> 5. **Misapplication of AFR factor** — forgetting the `× 10⁶` multiplier for Accident Frequency Rate. Costs **1 mark**.
> 6. **Confusing TCO with Annual Cost** — TCO is *lifetime*, not yearly. Always state the time period.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **KPI** is a *SMART*, *quantifiable* metric tied to a fleet objective.
- ✅ The **5 KPI families**: Operational, Financial, Fuel, Maintenance, Safety/Environmental.
- ✅ **Fleet Utilization Rate** = Productive Hours / Available Hours × 100; healthy = **70–85%**.
- ✅ **Cost per km** = Total Operating Cost / Total km; benchmark depends on vehicle class.
- ✅ **TCO** = Lifecycle cost – Salvage value; includes depreciation + operating cost.
- ✅ **MTBF** = Total Op. Time / Number of Failures.
- ✅ **MTTR** = Total Repair Hours / Number of Repairs.
- ✅ **Availability** = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR) × 100.
- ✅ **Idle Time Ratio** < 5% is the KTU-benchmark target.
- ✅ **AFR** uses a `× 10⁶` multiplier per million km.
- ✅ **CO₂ emission factor** for diesel = **2.68 kg/L** (IPCC standard).
- ✅ **Leading vs Lagging KPIs**: predict vs report. Use both.
- ✅ Always write **units**, **target value**, and a one-line **interpretation**.
- ✅ Use **PDCA + Telematics feedback loop** for continuous KPI improvement.
- ✅ For exam: structure every KPI answer as **Formula → Substitution → Final Value with Unit → Interpretation**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using Data for Decision Making
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT633 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Using Data for Decision Making

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Data-Driven Decision Making (DDDM)** in the context of vehicle transport and fleet management is defined as the systematic, evidence-based process of collecting, processing, analyzing, and interpreting operational, financial, and performance data of a vehicle fleet to support, justify, and optimize strategic, tactical, and operational decisions. It replaces intuition-based or experience-only judgments with quantifiable insights derived from telematics, maintenance logs, fuel records, driver behavior logs, and financial ledgers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Paraphrase):** *"Using data for decision making involves leveraging operational metrics, key performance indicators (KPIs), and analytical tools to evaluate fleet efficiency, control costs, improve safety, and align transport operations with business objectives."*

In a B.Tech automotive/transport engineering context, this topic sits at the intersection of **Operations Management**, **Business Analytics**, and **Automotive Engineering**, providing the quantitative backbone for all other modules in PEAUT633.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Doctor and the Patient

Imagine a fleet of 50 trucks is a **patient**, and the fleet manager is a **doctor**. A doctor who treats without checking blood pressure, sugar levels, or X-rays is dangerous — they would be guessing. Similarly, a fleet manager who replaces vehicles, schedules maintenance, or assigns drivers without consulting fuel logs, telematics data, or utilization reports is making **blind decisions**.

- **Symptoms** of the fleet = Telematics alerts, breakdown frequency
- **Medical history** = Maintenance records, past incident logs
- **Lab reports** = Fuel consumption data, cost sheets
- **Diagnosis** = KPI dashboards
- **Prescription** = Strategic decision (e.g., replace 5 vehicles, retrain 3 drivers)

Just as evidence-based medicine is the gold standard in healthcare, **evidence-based fleet management** is the gold standard in modern transport operations.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in PEAUT633

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** This topic directly supports the course outcomes related to:
> - **CO3:** Apply data analytics for fleet performance evaluation
> - **CO4:** Optimize transport operations using quantitative techniques
> - **CO5:** Develop cost-control and replacement strategies using operational data

Without mastering this module, a transport engineer cannot justify purchasing decisions, defend budget proposals, or implement ISO 39001 (road traffic safety) compliance — all of which require hard numbers.

### 1.4 Hierarchy of Decisions Supported by Data

| Decision Level | Time Horizon | Data Required | Example |
|----------------|--------------|---------------|---------|
| **Strategic** | 3–10 years | TCO, market trends, depreciation curves | Replace entire fleet with EVs |
| **Tactical** | 6 months – 2 years | Utilization rates, cost/km, route profitability | Add 3 vehicles to a high-demand lane |
| **Operational** | Daily–Weekly | Telematics, driver logs, fuel slips | Reroute truck #14 to avoid traffic |

### 1.5 Key Industry Metrics at a Glance (Bold for Emphasis)

The most universally accepted fleet industry metrics — all derived from data — are:

- **Cost per Kilometer (CPK)**
- **Vehicle Utilization Rate (VUR)**
- **Fuel Efficiency (km/L or L/100km)**
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)**
- **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)**
- **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)**
- **Return on Investment (ROI)**
- **Net Present Value (NPV)**
- **Break-Even Point (BEP)**
- **Driver Score / Safety Index**

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Stage DDDM Framework for Fleet Management

The theoretical backbone of "Using Data for Decision Making" follows a universal five-stage analytical pipeline. Each stage must be rigorously executed for the decision to be defensible.

**Stage 1 — Data Collection (The "Gather" Stage)**
- **Sources:** GPS/telematics units, electronic logging devices (ELDs), fuel cards, workshop job cards, driver inspection reports, ERP systems, accident reports, toll data, customs/port logs.
- **Data Types Collected:**
  - *Structured:* Numerical (odometer reading, fuel filled in liters, cost in INR)
  - *Unstructured:* Driver notes, photos of damage, voice recordings
  - *Semi-structured:* JSON outputs from telematics APIs, XML from ERP exports

**Stage 2 — Data Cleaning & Validation (The "Purify" Stage)**
- Remove duplicate fuel entries
- Flag outliers (e.g., a single fuel fill of 500L on a small truck is likely a data error)
- Standardize units (km vs miles, INR vs USD)
- Reconcile workshop invoices with maintenance schedules

**Stage 3 — Data Analysis (The "Compute" Stage)**
- Descriptive analytics: What happened last quarter? (e.g., average fuel consumption was 4.2 km/L)
- Diagnostic analytics: Why did it happen? (e.g., consumption rose because of congested routes)
- Predictive analytics: What will happen? (e.g., vehicle X will need brake replacement in 8,000 km)
- Prescriptive analytics: What should we do? (e.g., reroute via NH-48 to save ₹12,000/month)

**Stage 4 — Insight Generation (The "Interpret" Stage)**
- Translate numbers into business language
- E.g., "Our VUR dropped from 78% to 61% because 3 vehicles are underutilized on the Bengaluru-Chennai lane; consolidating loads could save ₹18 lakh annually."

**Stage 5 — Decision Execution & Feedback (The "Act & Learn" Stage)**
- Implement the decision
- Monitor KPIs post-implementation
- Close the feedback loop

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The vertical pipe symbol `$\vert$` is used instead of `$\mid$` or raw `|` inside table cells to prevent markdown parsing errors.

| # | Metric | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|--------|---------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Cost per Kilometer (CPK)** | $CPK = \dfrac{TC}{D}$ | $TC$ = Total Cost (₹), $D$ = Distance (km) | Compare vehicles; set pricing |
| 2 | **Vehicle Utilization Rate (VUR)** | $VUR = \dfrac{AH}{AH + IH} \times 100$ | $AH$ = Active Hours, $IH$ = Idle Hours | Decide if fleet is over/under-sized |
| 3 | **Fuel Efficiency (FE)** | $FE = \dfrac{D_{km}}{F_{L}}$ | $D_{km}$ = Distance in km, $F_{L}$ = Fuel in L | Driver training, vehicle selection |
| 4 | **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | $MTBF = \dfrac{T_{op}}{N_{f}}$ | $T_{op}$ = Total operational period (hrs), $N_{f}$ = Number of failures | Reliability benchmarking |
| 5 | **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** | $MTTR = \dfrac{T_{down}}{N_{f}}$ | $T_{down}$ = Total downtime (hrs), $N_{f}$ = Number of failures | Workshop efficiency measurement |
| 6 | **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $TCO = C_{acq} + C_{op} + C_{maint} + C_{fuel} + C_{lab} - S_{res}$ | $C_{acq}$ = Acquisition, $C_{op}$ = Operating, $C_{maint}$ = Maintenance, $C_{fuel}$ = Fuel, $C_{lab}$ = Labor, $S_{res}$ = Salvage | Vehicle replacement decisions |
| 7 | **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $ROI = \dfrac{(G - I)}{I} \times 100$ | $G$ = Gain, $I$ = Investment | Justify telematics purchases |
| 8 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}$ | $CF_{t}$ = Cash flow at time $t$, $r$ = Discount rate, $n$ = Years | Long-term fleet expansion |
| 9 | **Break-Even Point (BEP)** | $BEP = \dfrac{FC}{P - VC}$ | $FC$ = Fixed Cost, $P$ = Price/revenue per unit, $VC$ = Variable cost per unit | Route profitability analysis |
| 10 | **Payload Efficiency (PE)** | $PE = \dfrac{AL}{ML} \times 100$ | $AL$ = Actual load (tons), $ML$ = Max legal load (tons) | Compliance with axle-load norms |
| 11 | **Cost per Ton-Kilometer (CPTK)** | $CPTK = \dfrac{TC}{W \times D}$ | $TC$ = Trip cost (₹), $W$ = Weight (tons), $D$ = Distance (km) | Logistics tendering |
| 12 | **Driver Safety Score (DSS)** | $DSS = 100 - (w_1 H + w_2 S + w_3 B + w_4 O)$ | $H$ = Harsh braking, $S$ = Speeding, $B$ = Bumps, $O$ = Over-revving, $w_i$ = Weights | Insurance premium reduction |
| 13 | **Downtime Percentage (DTP)** | $DTP = \dfrac{T_{down}}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | $T_{down}$ = Downtime, $T_{total}$ = Total time | Asset availability for SLAs |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Application Map:**
> - **Logistics Companies** (e.g., DHL, Blue Dart, Delhivery) → Use CPTK and VUR to optimize hub-and-spoke networks.
> - **Public Transport** (KSRTC, BMTC) → Use MTBF and DTP to justify depot expansions.
> - **Construction Fleets** → Use CPK and PE to bid competitively in tenders.
> - **Ride-Hailing** (Ola, Uber) → Use ROI and NPV for electric vehicle transition.
> - **Mining & Heavy Earthmoving** → Use TCO over 15-year life to choose between Caterpillar, Komatsu, and Tata Hitachi.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Over Vehicle Life

This is a high-yield derivation that frequently appears in KTU Part B questions (14 marks). We will derive the **TCO per kilometer** formula in complete algebraic detail.

**Given:**
- $P_0$ = Purchase price of vehicle (₹)
- $n$ = Useful life in years
- $A_k$ = Annual kilometers driven in year $k$ (km)
- $C_{f,k}$ = Fuel cost per km in year $k$ (₹/km)
- $C_{m,k}$ = Maintenance cost per km in year $k$ (₹/km)
- $C_{l,k}$ = Labor/driver cost per km in year $k$ (₹/km)
- $C_{o,k}$ = Overhead cost per km in year $k$ (₹/km) — insurance, taxes, depreciation
- $S$ = Salvage value at end of year $n$ (₹)
- $r$ = Discount rate (annual, decimal)

**Step 1:** Write the annual cost of ownership for year $k$.

$$
C_{annual,k} = A_k \times (C_{f,k} + C_{m,k} + C_{l,k} + C_{o,k})
$$

**Step 2:** Discount the annual cost to present value (Year 0). This accounts for the time value of money — a rupee today is worth more than a rupee in 5 years.

$$
PV(C_{annual,k}) = \frac{A_k \times (C_{f,k} + C_{m,k} + C_{l,k} + C_{o,k})}{(1 + r)^k}
$$

**Step 3:** Sum the present values of all annual costs from year 1 to year $n$.

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{n} PV(C_{annual,k}) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{A_k \times (C_{f,k} + C_{m,k} + C_{l,k} + C_{o,k})}{(1 + r)^k}
$$

**Step 4:** Add the initial purchase price at year 0 (no discounting needed).

$$
TCO_{undiscounted} = P_0 + \sum_{k=1}^{n} A_k \times (C_{f,k} + C_{m,k} + C_{l,k} + C_{o,k})
$$

**Step 5:** Subtract the present value of the salvage value (it is a negative cost — a return).

$$
TCO = P_0 + \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{A_k \times (C_{f,k} + C_{m,k} + C_{l,k} + C_{o,k})}{(1 + r)^k} - \frac{S}{(1 + r)^n}
$$

**Step 6:** Derive the **TCO per kilometer** by dividing by the total distance traveled over the life.

$$
D_{total} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} A_k
$$

$$
TCO_{per\ km} = \frac{TCO}{D_{total}} = \frac{P_0 - \frac{S}{(1+r)^n} + \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{A_k \times (C_{f,k} + C_{m,k} + C_{l,k} + C_{o,k})}{(1+r)^k}}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} A_k}
$$

**Step 7 (Special Case — Constant Annual Cost):** If $A_k = A$ (constant annual km) and cost per km is constant, this simplifies dramatically.

$$
TCO = P_0 + A \times (C_f + C_m + C_l + C_o) \times \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r} - \frac{S}{(1+r)^n}
$$

Here, the factor $\dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$ is the **present value annuity factor (PVAF)**, a standard financial time-value multiplier.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Vehicle Replacement Decision

**Problem Statement (KTU Exam Style):**

A logistics company operates a Tata LPT 1109 truck. Data for the last 12 months is as follows:

- Purchase price: ₹18,00,000
- Annual kilometers: 60,000 km
- Fuel cost: ₹6.50 per km
- Maintenance cost: ₹1.20 per km
- Driver/cleaner cost: ₹1.80 per km
- Insurance + tax + overhead: ₹0.90 per km
- Salvage value after 7 years: ₹3,50,000
- Discount rate: 8% per annum

**Calculate:**
- (a) The Total Cost of Ownership over 7 years (undiscounted).
- (b) The discounted TCO.
- (c) The TCO per kilometer.
- (d) The decision: should the company replace the truck now or continue for 7 years if a competing new truck offers TCO per km of ₹14.50?

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Part (a) — Undiscounted TCO:**

Annual cost per km (sum of all operating costs):

$$
C_{per\ km} = 6.50 + 1.20 + 1.80 + 0.90 = 10.40\ \text{₹/km}
$$

Annual cost:

$$
C_{annual} = 60{,}000 \times 10.40 = ₹6{,}24{,}000
$$

Total over 7 years (excluding salvage):

$$
C_{7yr} = 6{,}24{,}000 \times 7 = ₹43{,}68{,}000
$$

Undiscounted TCO:

$$
TCO_{undisc} = 18{,}00{,}000 + 43{,}68{,}000 - 3{,}50{,}000 = ₹58{,}18{,}000
$$

**Part (b) — Discounted TCO using PVAF at $r = 8\%$, $n = 7$:**

Compute the Present Value Annuity Factor:

$$
PVAF = \frac{1 - (1.08)^{-7}}{0.08}
$$

$$
(1.08)^7 = 1.71382
$$

$$
(1.08)^{-7} = 0.58349
$$

$$
PVAF = \frac{1 - 0.58349}{0.08} = \frac{0.41651}{0.08} = 5.2064
$$

Discounted operating cost:

$$
PV(C_{7yr}) = 6{,}24{,}000 \times 5.2064 = ₹32{,}48{,}794
$$

Discounted salvage (returned to us at year 7):

$$
PV(S) = \frac{3{,}50{,}000}{1.71382} = ₹2{,}04{,}221
$$

Discounted TCO:

$$
TCO_{disc} = 18{,}00{,}000 + 32{,}48{,}794 - 2{,}04{,}221 = ₹48{,}44{,}573
$$

**Part (c) — TCO per kilometer:**

$$
D_{total} = 60{,}000 \times 7 = 4{,}20{,}000\ \text{km}
$$

$$
TCO_{per\ km} = \frac{48{,}44{,}573}{4{,}20{,}000} = ₹11.53\ \text{per km}
$$

**Part (d) — Decision:**

- Current truck (Tata LPT 1109): ₹11.53/km
- Competing new truck: ₹14.50/km

**Decision: RETAIN the Tata LPT 1109.** The current truck is ₹2.97/km cheaper. The data clearly justifies not replacing it.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always show the comparison in a clean two-column table. KTU examiners award marks for **decision justification** (typically 2–3 marks out of 14) — not just the calculation.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Break-Even Point for Route Profitability

**Given:**
- $F$ = Fixed cost of operating the route (₹/month) — e.g., depot rent, insurance, driver salary
- $v$ = Variable cost per km (₹/km) — fuel, wear, toll
- $p$ = Revenue per km (₹/km) — freight rate
- $x$ = Distance traveled per month (km)

**Step 1:** Total cost function.

$$
TC(x) = F + v \cdot x
$$

**Step 2:** Total revenue function.

$$
TR(x) = p \cdot x
$$

**Step 3:** Profit function.

$$
\pi(x) = TR(x) - TC(x) = p \cdot x - F - v \cdot x = (p - v) \cdot x - F
$$

**Step 4:** Set profit to zero for break-even.

$$
(p - v) \cdot x_{BEP} - F = 0
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for break-even distance.

$$
x_{BEP} = \frac{F}{p - v}
$$

This is the **minimum km/month** the route must generate to avoid losses.

**Engineering interpretation:** If $p \le v$, the route is structurally unprofitable — no amount of distance will recover costs. The company should **close the route**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Decision Mapping Table

| Decision Required | Data Inputs | Analytical Tool | Output Metric | KTU Weightage |
|-------------------|-------------|-----------------|---------------|---------------|
| Replace a vehicle? | Maintenance history, age, km, cost | TCO, NPV, Equivalent Annual Cost | TCO/km vs new | High (14-mark Q) |
| Add a new vehicle? | Demand, route profitability | BEP, ROI | BEP months, ROI % | High |
| Reroute? | Telematics GPS, traffic data | Travel time analysis | Time saved (hrs) | Medium |
| Retrain driver? | DSS, harsh event count | Statistical comparison | DSS before/after | Medium |
| Close a route? | Revenue, cost, volume | Contribution margin | Margin % | High |
| Buy EV vs Diesel? | Total ownership cost over 10 yrs | TCO with battery replacement | TCO EV vs TCO Diesel | High |
| Reduce fleet size? | VUR by vehicle | Right-sizing model | Optimal fleet size | Medium |
| Negotiate insurance? | Accident data, DSS | Loss ratio | Premium reduction | Low-Medium |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Bank]**
*Define "Data-Driven Decision Making" in the context of vehicle fleet management. List any four key performance indicators (KPIs) used to evaluate fleet performance.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Data-Driven Decision Making (DDDM) in vehicle fleet management is the systematic process of collecting, validating, analyzing, and interpreting operational, financial, and performance data of a fleet to make informed strategic, tactical, and operational decisions. It replaces intuition-based judgments with evidence-based insights derived from telematics, fuel records, maintenance logs, and financial data.

**Four key KPIs (½ mark each, 2 marks total):**

1. **Cost per Kilometer (CPK):** Total operating cost divided by distance traveled. Indicates cost efficiency.
2. **Vehicle Utilization Rate (VUR):** Active hours divided by total available hours. Indicates asset productivity.
3. **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** Total operational hours divided by number of failures. Indicates reliability.
4. **Fuel Efficiency (FE):** Kilometers traveled per liter of fuel. Indicates energy economy and driver behavior.

*(Distribution: 1 mark definition + 2 marks KPIs)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
*What is Vehicle Utilization Rate (VUR)? Why is it considered a critical metric in fleet right-sizing decisions?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Vehicle Utilization Rate (VUR) is the percentage of total available time during which a vehicle is actively used for revenue-generating operations, calculated as:

$$
VUR = \frac{AH}{AH + IH} \times 100
$$

where $AH$ = Active Hours and $IH$ = Idle Hours.

**Why Critical for Right-Sizing (1.5 marks):**

A low VUR (typically below 60%) indicates that the fleet is over-sized — too many vehicles are sitting idle, inflating fixed costs such as insurance, depreciation, and parking. A consistently high VUR (above 85%) suggests the fleet is under-sized, leading to driver fatigue, missed deliveries, and SLA violations. Thus, VUR is the **primary diagnostic metric** to decide whether to add, retain, or remove vehicles from the fleet.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 3]**

**Question A:**
*(a)* Define Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) of a vehicle. Derive the discounted TCO formula for a vehicle with a useful life of *n* years, including the present value annuity factor. (7 marks)

*(b)* A construction company is evaluating two options for transporting 80,000 tonnes of aggregates annually over a distance of 50 km per day:

| Parameter | Option 1: Buy 5 New Tipper Trucks | Option 2: Outsource to Contractor |
|-----------|-----------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Capital Investment | ₹2,00,00,000 | Nil |
| Annual Operating Cost | ₹45,00,000 | ₹72,00,000 |
| Useful Life | 10 years | — |
| Salvage Value | ₹25,00,000 | — |
| Discount Rate | 9% per annum | 9% per annum |

Using the NPV method, recommend which option the company should choose. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

*(1 mark for definition)* Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) is the comprehensive sum of all costs associated with acquiring, operating, maintaining, and disposing of a vehicle over its entire useful life, expressed in present value terms to account for the time value of money.

*(6 marks for derivation — refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the full algebraic derivation. Key steps:)*

Step 1: Annual cost $C_{annual,k} = A_k \times (C_f + C_m + C_l + C_o)$

Step 2: Discount to present value $\dfrac{C_{annual,k}}{(1+r)^k}$

Step 3: Sum from $k = 1$ to $n$

Step 4: Add $P_0$, subtract discounted salvage $\dfrac{S}{(1+r)^n}$

Step 5: For constant $A$ and cost per km, the summation reduces to a present value annuity factor $PVAF = \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$

**Final Formula (must be quoted in the answer):**

$$
TCO = P_0 + A \cdot C_{per\ km} \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r} - \frac{S}{(1+r)^n}
$$

---

**Part (b) — NPV Comparison (7 marks):**

**Option 1: Buy 5 New Trucks**

Compute PVAF at $r = 9\%$, $n = 10$:

$$
(1.09)^{10} = 2.36736
$$

$$
PVAF = \frac{1 - (1/2.36736)}{0.09} = \frac{1 - 0.42241}{0.09} = \frac{0.57759}{0.09} = 6.4177
$$

*(1 mark for correct PVAF calculation)*

Present value of operating cost:

$$
PV(\text{operating}) = 45{,}00{,}000 \times 6.4177 = ₹2,88,79{,}650
$$

Present value of salvage (a benefit, so subtracted from cost):

$$
PV(\text{salvage}) = \frac{25{,}00{,}000}{2.36736} = ₹10{,}55{,}910
$$

Total NPV of cost (Option 1):

$$
NPV_1 = 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}88{,}79{,}650 - 10{,}55{,}910 = ₹4,78{,}23{,}740
$$

*(1 mark for correct NPV)*

**Option 2: Outsource**

Present value of annual outsourcing cost (treated as a perpetuity-like annuity over 10 years — assumption: same cost structure for the comparison period):

$$
NPV_2 = 72{,}00{,}000 \times 6.4177 = ₹4,62{,}07{,}440
$$

*(1 mark for correct NPV)*

**Comparison Table:**

| Option | NPV of Cost | Decision |
|--------|-------------|----------|
| Option 1: Buy Trucks | ₹4,78,23,740 | Higher cost |
| Option 2: Outsource | ₹4,62,07,440 | **Lower cost by ₹16,16,300** |

*(2 marks for comparison table and decision justification)*

**Final Recommendation:** The company should **outsource** the transport to the contractor, saving approximately ₹16.16 lakh in present value terms over 10 years. However, this recommendation should be qualified with non-financial considerations: control over fleet, availability during peak demand, and quality of service.

*(1 mark for qualified recommendation)*

---

### 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks)

**Question B:**
*(a)* Explain the five-stage Data-Driven Decision Making (DDDM) framework with specific examples from fleet management operations. (7 marks)

*(b)* A delivery fleet recorded the following operational data for the last quarter:
- Total distance covered: 3,60,000 km
- Total fuel consumed: 60,000 L
- Total number of breakdowns: 12
- Total downtime: 240 hours
- Total operational period: 7,200 hours
- Total cost (fuel + maintenance + driver): ₹32,40,000

Calculate: (i) Fuel Efficiency, (ii) Cost per Kilometer, (iii) MTBF, (iv) MTTR, (v) Vehicle Utilization Rate (VUR) if idle hours were 1,800 hours. Interpret each metric. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Five-Stage DDDM Framework (7 marks):**

*(Marks distributed: 1 mark per stage + 1 mark for example integration)*

1. **Data Collection:** Gathering raw data from GPS, fuel cards, OBD-II, workshop job cards, and driver logs. *Example:* Telematics devices record 100+ data points per second per vehicle.

2. **Data Cleaning & Validation:** Removing duplicates, flagging outliers (e.g., a 500 L fuel fill entry), standardizing units. *Example:* Discarding 27 erroneous fuel entries out of 4,200 to ensure 99.36% data integrity.

3. **Data Analysis:** Applying descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, and prescriptive analytics. *Example:* Running a regression to find that harsh braking increases fuel consumption by 0.3 L per 100 km.

4. **Insight Generation:** Translating numbers into business recommendations. *Example:* "Reducing harsh braking by 50% will save ₹4.2 lakh per quarter in fuel costs."

5. **Decision Execution & Feedback:** Implementing the decision and closing the loop. *Example:* Rolling out a driver training program, then measuring DSS improvement from 72 to 84 in 90 days.

*(1 mark for clear linkage of all five stages to fleet operations)*

---

**Part (b) — Five KPI Calculations (7 marks):**

*(1.4 marks each: 1 for formula, 0.4 for numerical answer)*

**(i) Fuel Efficiency:**

$$
FE = \frac{D_{km}}{F_L} = \frac{3{,}60{,}000}{60{,}000} = 6.0\ \text{km/L}
$$

*Interpretation:* The fleet achieves 6 km per liter, which is reasonable for heavy commercial vehicles in India. Industry benchmark is 5.5–7 km/L for this segment.

**(ii) Cost per Kilometer:**

$$
CPK = \frac{TC}{D} = \frac{32{,}40{,}000}{3{,}60{,}000} = ₹9.00\ \text{per km}
$$

*Interpretation:* The cost per km is competitive for long-haul operations (typical range ₹8–₹12).

**(iii) Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):**

$$
MTBF = \frac{T_{op}}{N_f} = \frac{7{,}200}{12} = 600\ \text{hours per failure}
$$

*Interpretation:* On average, the fleet experiences a failure every 600 hours. Industry standard is 800+ hours, indicating that preventive maintenance needs improvement.

**(iv) Mean Time To Repair (MTTR):**

$$
MTTR = \frac{T_{down}}{N_f} = \frac{240}{12} = 20\ \text{hours per failure}
$$

*Interpretation:* Repairs take 20 hours on average. Industry best-in-class is below 8 hours. Workshop efficiency, parts availability, and mechanic training should be reviewed.

**(v) Vehicle Utilization Rate (VUR):**

$$
VUR = \frac{AH}{AH + IH} \times 100 = \frac{7{,}200}{7{,}200 + 1{,}800} \times 100
$$

$$
VUR = \frac{7{,}200}{9{,}000} \times 100 = 80\%
$$

*Interpretation:* The fleet is utilized 80% of the time, which is healthy. It indicates neither over-sizing nor excessive over-utilization. A target band of 75–85% is ideal.

*(1 bonus mark for cross-referencing MTBF and MTTR to derive overall availability: $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100 = \dfrac{600}{620} \times 100 = 96.77\%$)*

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Points (Read Carefully):**
> 1. **Skipping the discounting step:** In TCO/NPV questions, students often compute undiscounted values and lose 2–3 marks. Always apply $\dfrac{1}{(1+r)^n}$ to future values.
> 2. **No units in the final answer:** Writing "9" instead of "₹9.00/km" — examiners deduct 0.5 marks per instance.
> 3. **No interpretation:** Calculating five KPIs but writing zero interpretation — at least 1 mark per metric is reserved for **engineering interpretation** in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Wrong BEP denominator:** For break-even, the denominator is $(p - v)$, not just $p$.
> 5. **Confusing CPK and CPTK:** Cost per ton-km is a different and more specific metric — only use it when weight is involved.
> 6. **Forgetting salvage value in TCO:** The salvage is a benefit, so it should be **subtracted** in the TCO cost equation, not added.
> 7. **VUR × 100 omission:** VUR must always be expressed as a percentage; raw ratios get partial credit only.

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam Day:**
> - [ ] **DDDM =** 5 stages: Collect → Clean → Analyze → Insight → Act & Feedback
> - [ ] **CPK** = Total Cost ÷ Distance
> - [ ] **VUR** = Active Hours ÷ (Active + Idle) × 100
> - [ ] **MTBF** = Total Op Hours ÷ Number of Failures
> - [ ] **MTTR** = Total Downtime ÷ Number of Failures
> - [ ] **TCO** = $P_0$ + PV(operating costs) − PV(salvage)
> - [ ] **PVAF** = $\dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$ — used in annuity-style fleet cost problems
> - [ ] **BEP** distance = $F \div (p - v)$
> - [ ] **CPTK** = Cost ÷ (Weight × Distance) — used in logistics tendering
> - [ ] **DSS** = 100 − weighted sum of harsh events
> - [ ] **GIGO principle:** Always validate data before analyzing
> - [ ] **Discount rate $r$** is always applied to future cash flows, never to year 0
> - [ ] **Salvage value** is a **benefit** (subtracted from cost) in TCO calculations
> - [ ] **Three decision levels:** Strategic (years), Tactical (months), Operational (days)
> - [ ] **Data sources:** GPS, OBD-II, ELD, fuel cards, workshop tablets, Fastag, ERP
> - [ ] **Industry tools to mention:** SAP TM, Oracle TMS, Trimble, Geotab, Verizon Connect, Locus.sh
> - [ ] **Funnel principle:** Raw data → Information → Knowledge → Wisdom → Decision (value increases as volume decreases)

**Final Exam Mantra:** *"A fleet manager without data is a pilot without instruments. Quantitative justification is non-negotiable in PEAUT633."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automation and Future Trends - Electric and Hybrid Vehicles in Fleets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT633 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Automation and Future Trends - Electric and Hybrid Vehicles in Fleets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 | Automation and Future Trends: Electric and Hybrid Vehicles in Fleets

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Electric Vehicle (EV)** is a road vehicle whose propulsion energy is derived wholly or substantially from an external electricity supply, stored in a rechargeable electrochemical battery pack and delivered to one or more electric traction motors. A **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)** combines a conventional internal combustion engine (ICE) with an electric propulsion system, using an energy storage device (battery + ultracapacitor) to enable regenerative braking, engine shut-off at idle, and optimised operating-point operation. In a **fleet context**, these vehicles are deployed en-masse under a single ownership or management structure (corporate, rental, last-mile delivery, public transit, or government), where the procurement, charging/refuelling, maintenance, telemetry, and replacement are governed by a centralised **Fleet Electrification Strategy**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For PEAUT633, the focus is **not** on powertrain thermodynamics or cell chemistry, but on the **fleet management implications** — Total Cost of Ownership (TCO), charging infrastructure planning, route optimisation under range constraints, driver behaviour telematics, residual value, and the regulatory/automation overlay (autonomous driving, V2X, smart grids).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **corporate office canteen** that has been run on LPG cylinders for 20 years. Suddenly the management decides to switch to electric induction stoves powered by rooftop solar + grid backup. What changes?

- The **fuel delivery** model changes (no more weekly cylinder trucks, instead daily electricity bills).
- The **refuelling time** structure changes (overnight slow charging vs. 2-hour fast refills).
- The **maintenance crew** changes (no more gas-line plumbers, instead electricians + battery technicians).
- The **upfront cost** is higher, but the **per-unit-running cost** drops sharply.
- The **range / downtime** model becomes the new bottleneck.

An EV/Hybrid fleet behaves **identically** — the "fuel" changes from diesel/petrol to kilowatt-hours, the "fuel station" becomes a charging depot, and the fleet manager's KPIs shift from litres/100 km to **kWh/km, State-of-Charge (SOC), charging-window utilisation, and battery health (State-of-Health, SOH)**.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for KTU Recall)

- **kWh/100 km** — Energy consumption rate (electrical equivalent of litres/100 km).
- **MPGe** — Miles Per Gallon equivalent (33.7 ÷ kWh per mile).
- **SOC (State of Charge, %)** — Instantaneous battery charge level, analogous to a fuel gauge.
- **SOH (State of Health, %)** — Battery degradation indicator; SOH < 80 % typically triggers fleet retirement.
- **BEV / HEV / PHEV / FCEV** — Battery EV / Hybrid EV / Plug-in Hybrid EV / Fuel-Cell EV.
- **Level 1 / Level 2 / DCFC** — Charging speeds (~2 kW / ~7–22 kW / 50–350 kW).
- **V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid)** — Bi-directional energy flow, allowing the fleet battery to discharge back to the grid during peak demand.
- **TCO (Total Cost of Ownership)** — Lifetime cost metric used to compare ICE vs. EV in fleets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorisation Anchor:** For KTU board answers, always structure an EV-fleet discussion around **four pillars** — (1) Powertrain architecture, (2) Charging & energy infrastructure, (3) TCO & financing, (4) Automation & telematics integration.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Powertrain Architecture Classification

The fleet manager must first classify the vehicle platform because each class has a distinct duty-cycle, range, and total-cost profile:

- **BEV (Battery Electric Vehicle):** Single energy source = grid electricity stored in Li-ion/NMC/LFP pack. Zero tailpipe emission. Best for urban delivery, intra-city buses, last-mile 2W/3W, corporate cars with predictable routes.
- **HEV (Hybrid Electric Vehicle):** ICE + battery + motor. Battery recharged **only by the engine and regenerative braking** (no plug). Best for high-utilisation ICE fleets transitioning gradually (e.g., taxi aggregators, intercity coaches).
- **PHEV (Plug-in Hybrid EV):** Larger battery (typically 10–20 kWh) that can be externally charged + ICE range-extender. Best for mixed duty cycles (urban + intercity, e.g., sales-force vehicles).
- **FCEV (Fuel-Cell EV):** Hydrogen fuel cell + small buffer battery. Currently limited to buses, heavy trucks, pilot fleets. Zero emission at tailpipe, water vapour only.
- **REEV (Range-Extended EV):** BEV with a small ICE generator (e.g., BMW i3 REx). Generator never drives wheels — pure EV drive with range anxiety relief.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Architectural Choice in Fleets

| Architecture | Fleet Use-Case | Why This Choice |
|---|---|---|
| BEV (LFP) | City logistics, e-commerce vans | Lowest per-km cost, depot-chargable, predictable urban routes |
| BEV (NMC) | Premium corporate, ride-hailing | Higher energy density, longer range for varied trips |
| HEV | Long-haul trucks, intercity taxis | No charging downtime, gradual electrification |
| PHEV | Sales cars, mixed-route executives | Flexibility when charging infra is sparse |
| FCEV | Heavy buses, mining trucks | High energy density by mass, fast refuel |

## 2.3 Charging Infrastructure Tiers for Fleets

- **Level 1 (AC, ~2 kW, single-phase 230 V / 15 A):** Overnight "trickle" charge, suitable for small 2W/3W fleets.
- **Level 2 (AC, 7–22 kW, three-phase 415 V):** Standard depot charger, typical for 4W corporate fleets, full charge in 4–8 hours.
- **DC Fast Charger / DCFC (50–350 kW DC):** Opportunity charging for high-utilisation fleets (delivery vans between shifts, buses at terminus).
- **Megawatt Charging System (MCS, up to 3.75 MW):** Emerging standard for Class-8 heavy trucks and electric aviation ground support.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below contains every quantitative formula a KTU 2024 examiner can ask under this topic. **No `|` (vertical bar) is used inside the table to prevent markdown-rendering breakage** — the absolute-value / divide symbol is written as `\vert` or `\mid`.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Engineering Use in Fleet |
