|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Battery stored energy | $E_{bat} = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}$ | Wh | Sizing battery pack for route energy demand |
| 2 | Energy in kWh | $E_{kWh} = E_{bat} \div 1000$ | kWh | Comparing with electricity bills |
| 3 | Real-world range | $R = E_{bat} \div EC_{unit}$ | km | Route feasibility check |
| 4 | Energy consumption rate | $EC = E_{bat} \div D_{trip}$ | kWh/km | Standard fleet KPI |
| 5 | Per-100-km energy | $EC_{100} = EC \times 100$ | kWh/100 km | Benchmarking similar vehicles |
| 6 | MPGe (US EPA) | $MPGe = 33.7 \div EC_{mile}$ | miles/gal-eq | Cross-comparison with ICE MPG |
| 7 | AC charging time | $t = E_{kWh} \div P_{ch}$ | hours | Depot charging schedule |
| 8 | DC fast-charge time (80 %) | $t_{80} = 0.8 \times E_{kWh} \div P_{DCFC}$ | hours | Mid-route turnaround |
| 9 | Regenerative energy recaptured | $E_{regen} = \eta_{reg} \times \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | Wh | Energy audit on hilly routes |
| 10 | State of Charge (SOC) | $SOC = \frac{E_{rem}}{E_{bat,max}} \times 100$ | % | Telemetry dashboard metric |
| 11 | State of Health (SOH) | $SOH = \frac{E_{bat,actual}}{E_{bat,new}} \times 100$ | % | Battery retirement decision |
| 12 | Total Cost of Ownership | $TCO = C_{cap} + C_{fuel} + C_{maint} + C_{ins} - S_{res}$ | ₹ / ₹-Lakh | EV vs. ICE comparison |
| 13 | Cost per km | $C_{km} = TCO \div L_{total}$ | ₹/km | Operational KPI |
| 14 | Payback period | $PP = \frac{\Delta C_{cap}}{\Delta C_{km,ICE} - \Delta C_{km,EV}}$ | years | Investment justification |
| 15 | CO₂ avoided (well-to-wheel) | $CO_{2,saved} = D \times EF_{grid} \times N_{veh} \times Y$ | kg/year | Sustainability reporting |
| 16 | Equivalent grid emission factor | $EF_{grid} = 0.7\text{–}0.9$ (India, kg CO₂/kWh) | kg/kWh | ESG claim verification |
| 17 | Diesel CO₂ factor | $EF_{diesel} = 2.68$ | kg CO₂/L | Baseline ICE emission |
| 18 | Charging power from V/I | $P = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I \times \cos\phi$ | W (3-phase AC) | Charger selection |
| 19 | Charge cycles to degradation | $N_{80} \approx 1500\text{–}3000$ (LFP), $800\text{–}1500$ (NMC) | cycles | Warranty & replacement |
| 20 | Depot load demand | $P_{depot} = N_{veh} \times P_{ch} \times DF$ | kW | Transformer sizing |

**Legend:** $V_{nom}$ = nominal voltage; $C_{Ah}$ = ampere-hour capacity; $EC$ = energy consumption; $D$ = annual distance; $EF$ = emission factor; $N_{veh}$ = fleet size; $Y$ = years; $\eta_{reg}$ = regen efficiency (typically 0.6–0.8); $m$ = vehicle mass; $v$ = velocity; $S_{res}$ = residual salvage value; $C_{cap}$ = capital cost; $C_{fuel}$ = lifetime fuel/electricity cost; $C_{maint}$ = maintenance cost; $C_{ins}$ = insurance; $L_{total}$ = lifetime km; $DF$ = demand diversity factor (0.6–0.8).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- A **logistics fleet operator** in Kerala (e.g., a spice exporter running 50 mini-trucks from Kochi) uses **Formula 3** to verify that a 30 kWh LFP BEV with an EC of 0.18 kWh/km covers the 165 km Kochi–Kozhikode corridor with a 20 % safety margin.
- A **public-transport bus depot** in Thiruvananthapuram uses **Formula 20** to size the incoming 11 kV/433 V transformer so that simultaneous overnight charging of 40 buses (each 22 kW AC) does not exceed sanctioned load after diversity correction.
- A **CSR/ESG team** uses **Formula 15** to claim carbon abatement in annual sustainability reports — this is increasingly a tender pre-qualification for corporate B2B contracts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** KTU 2024 evaluators award full marks only when the student shows **every algebraic transition** with a one-line justification. The three derivations below are the **highest-frequency 14-mark problems** expected on this topic.

---

## 3.1 Derivation I — Real-World EV Range from Battery Pack Specification

**Problem Statement (Typical 7-mark sub-question):**
A fleet operator plans to procure an electric delivery van with a **300 V nominal battery pack rated at 100 Ah**. The vehicle's measured energy consumption under loaded urban-delivery conditions is **0.20 kWh/km**. The fleet manager wants a **20 % range buffer** to account for detours, HVAC load, and battery aging. Compute the **real-world usable range** and the **energy needed to fully recharge** from 10 % SOC to 100 % SOC assuming a charger efficiency of 92 %.

### Step 1 — Compute total stored energy in the battery pack.

We use the fundamental relation between voltage, charge, and energy:
$$E_{bat} = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}$$

Substituting the given values:
$$E_{bat} = 300 \text{ V} \times 100 \text{ Ah}$$

$$E_{bat} = 30{,}000 \text{ Wh}$$

Converting to the standard fleet-management unit of kilowatt-hours:
$$E_{kWh} = \frac{E_{bat}}{1000} = \frac{30{,}000}{1000}$$

$$E_{kWh} = 30 \text{ kWh}$$

**Justification (text row):** A 300 V × 100 Ah pack is a *series-string Li-ion configuration* typical of NMC packs; the nominal voltage is the average operating voltage, not the open-circuit voltage, and is the correct value to use for fleet energy-budgeting.

### Step 2 — Compute the ideal (no-buffer) range.

We use the inverse of the energy-consumption rate:
$$R_{ideal} = \frac{E_{kWh}}{EC}$$

$$R_{ideal} = \frac{30 \text{ kWh}}{0.20 \text{ kWh/km}}$$

$$R_{ideal} = 150 \text{ km}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the safety buffer.

A 20 % safety buffer means the operator should *plan* for only 80 % of the ideal range. Therefore:
$$R_{usable} = R_{ideal} \times (1 - 0.20)$$

$$R_{usable} = 150 \times 0.80$$

$$R_{usable} = 120 \text{ km}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the actual energy needed to recharge from 10 % to 100 % SOC.

The energy drawn from the pack to refill this SOC delta is:
$$\Delta E_{pack} = 0.90 \times E_{kWh}$$

$$\Delta E_{pack} = 0.90 \times 30$$

$$\Delta E_{pack} = 27 \text{ kWh}$$

Because the charger is not 100 % efficient, the **energy drawn from the grid (the amount billed)** is:
$$E_{grid} = \frac{\Delta E_{pack}}{\eta_{ch}}$$

$$E_{grid} = \frac{27}{0.92}$$

$$E_{grid} \approx 29.35 \text{ kWh}$$

**Final Result Box:** Real-world usable range = **120 km**; Energy billed per full refill from 10 % SOC = **≈ 29.35 kWh**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Comparison: EV vs. ICE

**Problem Statement (7-mark sub-question):**
A delivery company compares an **Electric Van (₹18,00,000 capital, ₹1.20/km electricity, ₹0.30/km maintenance)** with a **Diesel Van (₹12,00,000 capital, ₹6.50/km diesel, ₹1.10/km maintenance)**. Both run **60,000 km/year for 8 years**. Salvage values are **40 % of capital for EV** and **30 % for ICE**. Insurance is **₹25,000/yr for EV, ₹18,000/yr for ICE**. Compute the **8-year TCO** for each and the **payback period** of the EV premium.

### Step 1 — Set up the TCO framework.

The standard KTU-level TCO identity is:
$$TCO = C_{cap} + C_{fuel} + C_{maint} + C_{ins} - S_{res}$$

### Step 2 — Compute EV TCO.

- $C_{cap,EV} = 18{,}00{,}000$
- $C_{fuel,EV} = 1.20 \times 60{,}000 \times 8 = 5{,}76{,}000$
- $C_{maint,EV} = 0.30 \times 60{,}000 \times 8 = 1{,}44{,}000$
- $C_{ins,EV} = 25{,}000 \times 8 = 2{,}00{,}000$
- $S_{res,EV} = 0.40 \times 18{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}20{,}000$

Substituting:
$$TCO_{EV} = 18{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}76{,}000 + 1{,}44{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 - 7{,}20{,}000$$

$$TCO_{EV} = 20{,}00{,}000$$

### Step 3 — Compute ICE TCO.

- $C_{cap,ICE} = 12{,}00{,}000$
- $C_{fuel,ICE} = 6.50 \times 60{,}000 \times 8 = 31{,}20{,}000$
- $C_{maint,ICE} = 1.10 \times 60{,}000 \times 8 = 5{,}28{,}000$
- $C_{ins,ICE} = 18{,}000 \times 8 = 1{,}44{,}000$
- $S_{res,ICE} = 0.30 \times 12{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}60{,}000$

Substituting:
$$TCO_{ICE} = 12{,}00{,}000 + 31{,}20{,}000 + 5{,}28{,}000 + 1{,}44{,}000 - 3{,}60{,}000$$

$$TCO_{ICE} = 46{,}32{,}000$$

### Step 4 — Compute the per-km operating cost (TCO/km).

$$C_{km,EV} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{4{,}80{,}000} \approx 4.17 \text{ ₹/km}$$

$$C_{km,ICE} = \frac{46{,}32{,}000}{4{,}80{,}000} \approx 9.65 \text{ ₹/km}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the EV premium (capital difference) and annual km-cost saving.

$$\Delta C_{cap} = 18{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}00{,}000$$

$$\Delta C_{km} = 9.65 - 4.17 = 5.48 \text{ ₹/km}$$

$$\text{Annual Saving} = 5.48 \times 60{,}000 = 3{,}28{,}800 \text{ ₹/yr}$$

### Step 6 — Compute the simple payback period.

$$PP = \frac{\Delta C_{cap}}{\Delta C_{km,ICE} - \Delta C_{km,EV}}$$

$$PP = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{3{,}28{,}800}$$

$$PP \approx 1.83 \text{ years}$$

**Final Result Box:** EV TCO = **₹20,00,000**; ICE TCO = **₹46,32,000**; Payback = **≈ 1.83 years**. Conclusion — *The EV is decisively cheaper over 8 years despite higher capital cost.*

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Annual CO₂ Avoided by Fleet Electrification

**Problem Statement (7-mark sub-question):**
A 100-vehicle corporate EV fleet replaces an equivalent ICE fleet. Each EV consumes **0.15 kWh/km** and runs **25,000 km/yr**. The grid emission factor is **0.82 kg CO₂/kWh** and the diesel emission factor is **2.68 kg CO₂/L**, with ICE vehicles consuming **5.5 L/100 km**. Compute the **net annual CO₂ saved**.

### Step 1 — Compute ICE fleet annual diesel consumption and emissions.

$$D_{ICE} = 5.5 \text{ L/100 km} \times 25{,}000 \text{ km/yr} = 1{,}375 \text{ L/yr (per vehicle)}$$

$$CO_{2,ICE,per\,veh} = 1{,}375 \times 2.68 = 3{,}685 \text{ kg/yr}$$

For 100 vehicles:
$$CO_{2,ICE,fleet} = 3{,}685 \times 100 = 3{,}68{,}500 \text{ kg/yr}$$

### Step 2 — Compute EV fleet annual electricity consumption and emissions.

$$E_{EV,per\,veh} = 0.15 \text{ kWh/km} \times 25{,}000 \text{ km/yr} = 3{,}750 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

$$CO_{2,EV,per\,veh} = 3{,}750 \times 0.82 = 3{,}075 \text{ kg/yr}$$

For 100 vehicles:
$$CO_{2,EV,fleet} = 3{,}075 \times 100 = 3{,}07{,}500 \text{ kg/yr}$$

### Step 3 — Compute net annual CO₂ avoided.

$$CO_{2,saved} = CO_{2,ICE,fleet} - CO_{2,EV,fleet}$$

$$CO_{2,saved} = 3{,}68{,}500 - 3{,}07{,}500$$

$$CO_{2,saved} = 61{,}000 \text{ kg/yr} = 61 \text{ tonnes CO₂/yr}$$

**Justification (text row):** This is a *well-to-wheel* comparison; a *tank-to-wheel* comparison would show 100 % saving because EVs have zero tailpipe emissions. The 61 t/yr figure accounts for the Indian grid mix, which still has ~75 % fossil share, hence the modest per-vehicle saving of 0.61 t/yr.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EV Fleet Electrification Decision-Block Architecture (Mermaid)

The following block diagram maps the **end-to-end decision flow** a fleet manager must traverse when evaluating EV/HEV adoption. The diagram follows the Mermaid safeguards — all node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters, and labels contain only clean uppercase text.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[START FLEET USE CASE] --> A2{ROUTE PATTERN}
    A2 -->|PREDICTABLE URBAN| A3[BEV LFP PLATFORM]
    A2 -->|MIXED URBAN INTERCITY| A4[PHEV PLATFORM]
    A2 -->|LONG HAUL| A5[HEV OR FCEV PLATFORM]

    A3 --> B1[BATTERY SIZING]
    A4 --> B1
    A5 --> B1

    B1 --> B2[ENERGY PER KM EC]
    B2 --> B3[ROUTE RANGE R]
    B3 --> C1{BUFFER MORE THAN 20 PERCENT}

    C1 -->|YES| C2[APPROVE VEHICLE PLATFORM]
    C1 -->|NO| C3[UPSIZE BATTERY OR ADD RANGE EXTENDER]

    C2 --> D1[CHARGING INFRASTRUCTURE TIER]
    D1 -->|DEPOT OVERNIGHT| D2[LEVEL 2 AC 22 KW]
    D1 -->|OPPORTUNITY FAST| D3[DCFC 50 TO 150 KW]
    D1 -->|HEAVY TRUCK| D4[DCFC 350 KW OR MCS]

    D2 --> E1[TRANSFORMER LOAD CALC]
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1

    E1 --> F1[TCO MODELING]
    F1 --> F2{PAYBACK LESS THAN 4 YEARS}

    F2 -->|YES| G1[PROCUREMENT APPROVED]
    F2 -->|NO| G2[REVISIT FUEL OR USAGE PATTERN]

    G1 --> H1[DEPLOYMENT AND TELEMATICS]
    H1 --> H2[SOC MONITORING]
    H1 --> H3[SOH TRACKING]
    H1 --> H4[DRIVER BEHAVIOUR SCORE]
    H1 --> H5[V2G READINESS]

    H2 --> I1[CONTINUOUS OPTIMIZATION LOOP]
    H3 --> I1
    H4 --> I1
    H5 --> I1
    I1 --> A1
```

**Reading Guide for the Student:** Trace the path A1 → A2 → A3 → B1 → B2 → B3 → C1 → C2 → D1 → D2 → E1 → F1 → F2 → G1 → H1 → I1 → A1. This is the *closed-loop* fleet electrification cycle: a decision is never "fire-and-forget" — telemetry continually refines the next replacement cycle.

## 4.2 BEV Powertrain Functional Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[GRID AC SUPPLY] --> P2[AC DC CHARGER]
    P2 --> P3[HIGH VOLTAGE BATTERY PACK]
    P3 --> P4[DC DC CONVERTER]
    P4 --> P5[12 V AUXILIARY BATTERY]

    P3 --> P6[POWER ELECTRONICS CONTROLLER]
    P6 --> P7[TRACTION MOTOR INVERTER]
    P7 --> P8[TRACTION MOTOR]
    P8 --> P9[TRANSMISSION SINGLE SPEED]
    P9 --> P10[WHEELS]

    P10 --> P11[REGENERATIVE BRAKING KINETIC ENERGY]
    P11 --> P6
    P6 --> P3

    P3 --> P12[BATTERY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM BMS]
    P12 --> P13[TELEMATICS CLOUD]
    P13 --> P14[FLEET MANAGEMENT DASHBOARD]
```

**Reading Guide:** Energy flows from grid → charger → battery → inverter → motor → wheels. During braking, the motor becomes a generator, returning energy to the battery. The BMS continuously publishes SOC/SOH to the cloud, where the fleet manager sees the live state of every vehicle.

## 4.3 Comparative Powertrain Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HEV [HEV ARCHITECTURE]
        H1[FUEL TANK] --> H2[ICE ENGINE]
        H2 --> H3[PLANETARY GEAR POWER SPLIT]
        H3 --> H4[WHEELS]
        H5[MG1 MOTOR GENERATOR] --> H3
        H6[MG2 TRACTION MOTOR] --> H4
        H7[HIGH VOLTAGE BATTERY] --> H5
        H7 --> H6
    end

    subgraph BEV [BEV ARCHITECTURE]
        B1[BATTERY PACK] --> B2[INVERTER]
        B2 --> B3[MOTOR]
        B3 --> B4[REDUCTION GEAR]
        B4 --> B5[WHEELS]
    end

    subgraph PHEV [PHEV ARCHITECTURE]
        Q1[BATTERY] --> Q2[MOTOR]
        Q2 --> Q3[CLUTCH]
        Q3 --> Q4[WHEELS]
        Q5[ICE ENGINE] --> Q3
        Q6[FUEL TANK] --> Q5
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**Define the term "Total Cost of Ownership" as applied to electric vehicle fleet management. List any four cost components included in TCO.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
TCO is the comprehensive lifetime cost incurred by a fleet operator in owning and operating a vehicle, expressed as the sum of all capital and operational expenses minus the residual salvage value at end-of-life. The four cost components are:
1. **Capital cost** — purchase price including taxes.
2. **Fuel / Energy cost** — diesel cost (ICE) or electricity cost (EV) over vehicle life.
3. **Maintenance & repair cost** — periodic service, tyres, brake wear, battery replacement.
4. **Insurance and overhead cost** — annual premium, registration, depot overhead, telematics subscription.
TCO is then reduced by the **residual / salvage value** at the time of disposal. (1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the four components.)

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**Differentiate between State of Charge (SOC) and State of Health (SOH) of an EV battery. Why is SOH critical for fleet managers?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**SOC** is the *instantaneous* charge level of the battery expressed as a percentage of its current maximum capacity (analogous to a fuel gauge). **SOH** is the *degradation* indicator, defined as the ratio of the battery's present full-charge capacity to its original rated capacity, expressed as a percentage.
SOH is critical for fleet managers because (i) it determines when a battery should be **second-life-retired** (typically at SOH < 80 %), (ii) it governs the **residual value** of the vehicle, (iii) it impacts the **warranty claims** and **insurance premium**, and (iv) it influences **route feasibility** because an aged battery delivers less range. (1 mark each for definitions + 1 mark for the four SOH implications.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B carries 14 marks. Standard pattern: sub-part (a) = 7 marks, sub-part (b) = 7 marks. The two choices below are **independent alternatives** — students answer EITHER Choice A OR Choice B in full.

---

### Choice A — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**Question:** A logistics company is evaluating the replacement of 50 diesel delivery vans (capital ₹10,00,000 each; mileage 12 km/L; diesel ₹90/L) with 50 electric vans (capital ₹16,00,000 each; energy 0.18 kWh/km; electricity ₹8/kWh). Both vehicle types run 40,000 km/year for 10 years. Maintenance cost is ₹0.90/km for diesel and ₹0.25/km for electric. Salvage value is 25 % of capital for both.

**(a)** Compute the **8-year TCO** for each platform and the **per-km cost**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **simple payback period** of the EV premium and the **annual CO₂ saved** (assume diesel emission factor = 2.68 kg/L, grid factor = 0.82 kg/kWh). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

- Diesel TCO: Capital = 50 × 10,00,000 = ₹5,00,00,000; Fuel = (40,000/12) × 90 × 10 × 50 = ₹15,00,00,000; Maintenance = 0.90 × 40,000 × 10 × 50 = ₹1,80,00,000; Salvage = 0.25 × 5,00,00,000 = ₹1,25,00,000.
$$TCO_{diesel} = 5{,}00{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}25{,}00{,}000 = ₹20{,}55{,}00{,}000$$

- EV TCO: Capital = 50 × 16,00,000 = ₹8,00,00,000; Energy = 0.18 × 40,000 × 10 × 50 = ₹2,88,00,000 (in ₹ terms at ₹8/kWh = 0.18 × 8 × 40,000 × 10 × 50); Maintenance = 0.25 × 40,000 × 10 × 50 = ₹50,00,000; Salvage = 0.25 × 8,00,00,000 = ₹2,00,00,000.
$$TCO_{EV} = 8{,}00{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}88{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 = ₹9{,}38{,}00{,}000$$

- Per-km cost:
$$C_{km,diesel} = \frac{20{,}55{,}00{,}000}{50 \times 40{,}000 \times 10} = \frac{20{,}55{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}00{,}000} \approx 10.28 \text{ ₹/km}$$
$$C_{km,EV} = \frac{9{,}38{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}00{,}000} \approx 4.69 \text{ ₹/km}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

- EV premium = 8,00,00,000 − 5,00,00,000 = ₹3,00,00,000.
- Annual km-cost saving = (10.28 − 4.69) × (50 × 40,000) = 5.59 × 20,00,000 = ₹1,11,80,000/yr.
- Payback:
$$PP = \frac{3{,}00{,}00{,}000}{1{,}11{,}80{,}000} \approx 2.68 \text{ years}$$

- Annual CO₂ saved (per vehicle): Diesel CO₂ = (40,000/12) × 2.68 ≈ 8,933 kg/yr; EV CO₂ = (0.18 × 40,000) × 0.82 = 5,904 kg/yr; Saving per vehicle = 3,029 kg/yr ≈ 3.03 t/yr.
- Fleet total: 50 × 3,029 = 1,51,450 kg/yr = **≈ 151.45 tonnes CO₂/yr**.

---

### Choice B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**Question:** A metropolitan bus operator is transitioning 40 city buses from diesel to BEV. Each BEV bus has a **250 kWh LFP battery**, a measured energy consumption of **1.1 kWh/km**, and a depot Level-2 AC charger rated **60 kW**.

**(a)** Compute the **daily operating range** if the bus leaves the depot at 100 % SOC and returns at **20 % SOC** (assume 90 % charger efficiency). Also compute the **overnight charging time** required to refill from 20 % back to 100 %. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the depot charges **all 40 buses simultaneously overnight between 22:00 and 06:00**, compute the **peak electrical demand** and discuss two **load-management strategies** to avoid exceeding grid-sanctioned load. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

- Usable energy per cycle = (100 % − 20 %) × 250 kWh = 200 kWh.
- Daily range:
$$R = \frac{200}{1.1} \approx 181.8 \text{ km}$$

- Energy to be restored = 200 kWh. Considering charger efficiency, grid energy required = 200 / 0.90 ≈ 222.2 kWh.
- Overnight charging time:
$$t = \frac{222.2}{60} \approx 3.70 \text{ hours}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

- Peak demand if all 40 charge simultaneously: 40 × 60 = **2,400 kW (2.4 MW)**.
- **Strategy 1 — Time-of-Use Staggered Charging:** Split the fleet into 4 batches of 10 buses each, starting at 22:00, 00:00, 02:00, and 04:00. Each batch needs 3.7 hours, so the last batch finishes around 07:40 — acceptable for a 22:00-to-06:00 *partial* window. Demand drops to 10 × 60 = 600 kW.
- **Strategy 2 — Demand Diversity Factor + Smart Curtailment:** Apply a diversity factor of 0.7 (since not all chargers pull peak simultaneously) → effective load ≈ 2,400 × 0.7 = 1,680 kW. Combine with a battery-energy-storage system (BESS) at the depot to shave the morning peak, charging the BESS at night from grid and discharging during the 06:00 simultaneous departure spike.
- *(Any equivalent strategy — V2G, solar-plus-storage, dynamic load balancing — receives full marks.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Avoid These!):**
> 1. **Forgetting the charger efficiency.** A 90 %-efficient charger means you must divide, not multiply. Showing 222 kWh instead of 200 kWh costs you 1 mark in any TCO/energy problem.
> 2. **Confusing V2G with V2H.** V2G = discharge to *grid* (utility); V2H = discharge to *home/building*. Examiners will reject generic "battery gives back electricity" answers.
> 3. **Mixing tank-to-wheel and well-to-wheel.** EV emissions are *zero tank-to-wheel* but *non-zero well-to-wheel*. A blanket statement "EVs have zero emissions" loses 1 mark; the correct phrasing is "zero tailpipe emissions".
> 4. **Ignoring the demand diversity factor** in depot-load problems. Computing 40 × 60 kW = 2,400 kW without discussing diversity will be marked incomplete.
> 5. **Using the wrong salvage-value base.** Salvage = percentage of *capital cost*, not TCO. Students who write "20 % of TCO" lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Forgetting battery-replacement cost** in long-horizon TCO (>8 years). At year 8, an LFP pack may need replacement (₹15–20 Lakh for a bus) — this must be itemised separately.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **EV classifications:** BEV / HEV / PHEV / FCEV / REEV — know the energy source and use-case for each.
- ✅ **Core metrics:** kWh/100 km, MPGe, SOC (instantaneous), SOH (degradation), kWh/km (energy consumption rate).
- ✅ **Charging tiers:** Level 1 (~2 kW) → Level 2 (7–22 kW) → DCFC (50–350 kW) → MCS (up to 3.75 MW).
- ✅ **Key formulas to memorise:** Range, TCO, Payback period, CO₂ saved, Depot demand, Charging time.
- ✅ **TCO always has 5 components:** Capital + Fuel + Maintenance + Insurance − Salvage. Battery replacement is a *separate* line item for >8-year horizons.
- ✅ **Grid emission factor for India:** 0.7–0.9 kg CO₂/kWh (Central Electricity Authority, 2023–24 range).
- ✅ **Diesel emission factor:** 2.68 kg CO₂/L (universal chemistry-based constant).
- ✅ **Fleet KPIs shift from** "litres/100 km, MPG, downtime hours" **to** "kWh/100 km, MPGe, charging-window utilisation, SOH, energy/tonne-km".
- ✅ **V2G** is bi-directional; **V2H** is uni-directional to a building; both unlock demand-response revenue.
- ✅ **Range buffer rule of thumb:** Always design routes for **80 % of nominal range** to allow for HVAC, detours, and battery aging.
- ✅ **Closed-loop fleet management:** Procurement → Deployment → Telemetry → Optimisation → Replacement. The loop never ends.
- ✅ **Well-to-wheel vs. tank-to-wheel:** Use the former for honest ESG reporting; the latter overstates EV benefit.
- ✅ **Demand Diversity Factor (DF):** 0.6–0.8 for EV depot planning. Never size the transformer at N × P_max.
- ✅ **LFP vs. NMC trade-off:** LFP = safer, longer cycle life (3000+), lower energy density. NMC = higher energy density, shorter life (800–1500 cycles), higher thermal-runaway risk.
- ✅ **Automation overlay:** Connected fleet + telematics + ADAS + autonomous L1–L5 is the *next* module but begins with EV data infrastructure (every EV is a data node).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autonomous Vehicles in Fleet Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT633 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Autonomous Vehicles in Fleet Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
An **Autonomous Vehicle (AV)** in a fleet management context is a commercially deployed road vehicle equipped with a layered suite of sensors, embedded computing, artificial intelligence (AI) decision stacks, and vehicle-to-everything (V2X) communication modules that allow it to perform longitudinal and lateral driving tasks — including perception, planning, and actuation — without continuous human input. When integrated into a fleet, these vehicles become nodes inside a centralized **Fleet Management System (FMS)**, which orchestrates routing, dispatch, charging, and maintenance decisions in real time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The course treats AVs not as a stand-alone robotics topic, but as a *fleet resource* whose autonomy levels (SAE J3016) directly influence **fleet capacity planning, TCO (Total Cost of Ownership), utilization rate, and dispatcher workload**.

### 1.2 Levels of Driving Automation (SAE J3016 Standard)
The Society of Automotive Engineers has defined **six levels** of driving automation, which KTU examiners expect students to memorize. The transition from **Level 0** (no automation) to **Level 5** (full automation) governs how much human intervention is required:

| Level | Name | Steering / Acceleration | Monitoring of Environment | Fallback Responsibility |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | No Automation | Human | Human | Human |
| 1 | Driver Assistance | Human + System | Human | Human |
| 2 | Partial Automation | System | Human | Human |
| 3 | Conditional Automation | System | System | Human (must be ready) |
| 4 | High Automation | System | System | System (within ODD) |
| 5 | Full Automation | System | System | System (all conditions) |

> [!NOTE]
> **ODD (Operational Design Domain)** is a critical term in the KTU syllabus. It defines the specific operating conditions (weather, road type, speed range, geofence) under which an AV is certified to operate. Fleet managers must ensure every dispatched trip remains **inside the ODD** of the assigned vehicle.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy
Think of an autonomous fleet vehicle as a **newly hired delivery driver with a strict instruction manual**. A Level 5 autonomous truck is like a driver who:
- **Sees the world** through multiple senses (cameras, radar, LiDAR) — just like your eyes and ears.
- **Thinks ahead** using an AI brain that plans routes, predicts pedestrian behavior, and obeys traffic rules — like your logical reasoning.
- **Communicates with a central office** (the Fleet Management System) that decides which deliveries to assign, when to refuel, and which garage to visit for service — like a dispatcher calling the driver on the radio.

The key difference is that this "driver" never gets tired, never texts while driving, and follows the dispatcher's algorithm with 100% compliance — which is why fleet operators are investing heavily in this technology.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics
> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU numerical problems, the following standard metrics are used in fleet AV studies:
> - **Fleet Utilization Rate (η):** typically **65% – 85%** for AVs vs. **40% – 60%** for human-driven fleets.
> - **Fuel / Energy Reduction:** **10% – 20%** due to eco-driving algorithms.
> - **Accident Rate Reduction Target:** **40% – 90%** depending on SAE level.
> - **TCO Payback Period:** **3 – 7 years** for Level 4 commercial deployment.
>
> All percentage symbols use the standard notation `%` — never confuse with logical operators in your exam scripts.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 System Architecture of an Autonomous Fleet Vehicle
The architecture follows a classic **Sense → Plan → Act** pipeline, expanded for fleet integration:

1. **Perception Layer (Sensors):**
   - **LiDAR** (Light Detection and Ranging) – 3D point cloud of surroundings, range up to **200 m**, accuracy ± **2 cm**.
   - **Radar** – Robust in rain/fog, measures relative velocity via Doppler shift.
   - **Cameras (Mono / Stereo)** – Lane detection, traffic sign recognition, object classification via CNNs.
   - **Ultrasonic Sensors** – Short-range (< **5 m**), used for low-speed parking maneuvers.
   - **GNSS + IMU** – Provides absolute position (latitude, longitude) fused at **100 Hz**.

2. **Decision Layer (Compute & AI):**
   - **Localization:** Particle filter or graph-SLAM with sub-decimeter accuracy.
   - **Object Tracking:** Kalman Filter / DeepSORT assigns persistent IDs to surrounding vehicles and pedestrians.
   - **Behavior Planning:** Finite State Machine + Reinforcement Learning for merging, overtaking, and yielding.
   - **Trajectory Planning:** Generates smooth polynomial paths respecting jerk limits (typically **jerk ≤ 2 m/s³** for passenger comfort).

3. **Actuation Layer (Drive-by-Wire):**
   - Electronic Control Units (ECUs) translate planned commands into steering angle, throttle, and brake pressure via CAN bus or Ethernet (100BASE-T1 automotive Ethernet).

4. **Fleet Integration Layer (V2X):**
   - **V2I (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure):** Receives signal phase and timing (SPaT) data from smart traffic lights.
   - **V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle):** Shares trajectory intentions within a **300 m** radius.
   - **V2N (Vehicle-to-Network):** Streams telemetry to the FMS cloud every **100 ms** to **1 s**.

### 2.2 Mathematical Model for Fleet Productivity with AVs
The **effective fleet capacity (EFC)** of a fleet with a mix of human-driven and autonomous vehicles is given by:

$$
EFC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( V_i \cdot \eta_i \cdot H_i \cdot \left( 1 - D_i \right) \right)
$$

Where:
- $V_i$ = number of vehicles of type $i$ (human, L3, L4, L5)
- $\eta_i$ = utilization rate of vehicle class $i$ (fraction of active time)
- $H_i$ = average operating hours per day (typically **16 h** for AVs vs. **8 h** for human drivers due to duty-hour limits)
- $D_i$ = downtime fraction (maintenance, charging, cleaning)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for KTU?** The examiner often asks students to compute how many human-driven trucks an autonomous truck can replace. The simple substitution ratio is:
>
> **Substitution Ratio =** $\dfrac{H_{AV} \cdot \eta_{AV}}{H_{Human} \cdot \eta_{Human}}$
>
> A typical answer in case studies is **2.5 : 1** to **3 : 1**, meaning one Level 4 truck can replace 2.5 to 3 human-driven trucks in long-haul logistics.

### 2.3 Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) — KTU Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Human-Driven Fleet | Autonomous Fleet |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Vehicle Capital Cost | $C_v$ | INR / vehicle | $C_v^{H}$ | $C_v^{A} = 1.5 C_v^{H}$ to $3 C_v^{H}$ |
| Sensor / Compute Premium | $C_s$ | INR / vehicle | **0** | $0.4 C_v^{H}$ to $0.8 C_v^{H}$ |
| Driver Wages | $C_d$ | INR / year | High | **0** (Level 4+) |
| Insurance | $C_i$ | INR / year | $0.03 C_v$ | $0.05 C_v$ (initial) |
| Energy / Fuel | $C_e$ | INR / km | Baseline | $0.85$ to $0.90 \times$ Baseline |
| Maintenance | $C_m$ | INR / km | Baseline | $0.70$ to $0.80 \times$ Baseline (initially higher in early years) |
| Downtime Loss | $C_l$ | INR / hour | $25 \times$ Baseline | $10 \times$ Baseline |

$$
TCO = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{(C_v + C_s + C_d + C_i + C_e + C_m + C_l)}{(1+r)^t}
$$

Where $T$ is the vehicle life (typically **8 – 10 years**) and $r$ is the discount rate (typically **8% – 12%** in Indian fleet studies).

### 2.4 Eco-Driving Energy Savings
Autonomous eco-driving reduces energy consumption through:
- **Predictive Cruise Control (PCC):** Uses HD map + V2I to anticipate terrain and signal phases.
- **Platooning:** Maintains inter-vehicle gap of **10 m** at highway speeds, reducing aerodynamic drag by **8% – 15%**.

$$
E_{saved} = E_{baseline} \cdot \left(1 - f_{p} \cdot g \cdot k_{aero}\right)
$$

Where:
- $f_p$ = platooning fraction of total km (e.g., **0.6**)
- $g$ = gap reduction factor (e.g., **0.5** for half-gap)
- $k_{aero}$ = aerodynamic drag reduction coefficient (**0.10 – 0.15**)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
Autonomous fleet vehicles are being deployed today in:
- **Mining haulage** (Komatsu, Caterpillar) — closed ODD, highest commercial maturity.
- **Port logistics** (Kalmar, Konecranes) — yard tractors in container terminals.
- **Highway platooning** (TuSimple, Embark, Kodiak) — long-haul trucking trials in the US and China.
- **Last-mile delivery robots** (Starship, Nuro) — urban geofenced routes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU examiner expects students to identify **why closed ODD applications succeed first**: there are no pedestrians, no weather extremes, and no complex intersections — which removes the hardest AI problems and allows fleet managers to realize ROI quickly.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Break-Even Mileage for an Autonomous Truck

A standard KTU-style problem asks: *At what annual mileage does the higher capital cost of an AV get offset by lower operating costs?*

Let:
- $C_c^A$ = capital cost of autonomous truck
- $C_c^H$ = capital cost of human-driven truck
- $C_{op}^A$ = operating cost per km of AV
- $C_{op}^H$ = operating cost per km of human truck
- $M$ = annual mileage in km

**Step 1: Total annual cost of human-driven truck**

$$
TC_H(M) = C_c^H + M \cdot C_{op}^H
$$

**Step 2: Total annual cost of autonomous truck**

$$
TC_A(M) = C_c^A + M \cdot C_{op}^A
$$

**Step 3: Set them equal to find break-even mileage $M^*$**

$$
C_c^H + M^* \cdot C_{op}^H = C_c^A + M^* \cdot C_{op}^A
$$

**Step 4: Rearrange to isolate $M^*$**

$$
M^* \cdot C_{op}^H - M^* \cdot C_{op}^A = C_c^A - C_c^H
$$

**Step 5: Factor out $M^*$**

$$
M^* \cdot \left( C_{op}^H - C_{op}^A \right) = \Delta C_c
$$

**Step 6: Solve for $M^*$**

$$
M^* = \frac{\Delta C_c}{C_{op}^H - C_{op}^A} = \frac{C_c^A - C_c^H}{C_{op}^H - C_{op}^A}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Since $C_c^A > C_c^H$ and $C_{op}^A < C_{op}^H$, the numerator is positive and the denominator is positive, giving a positive $M^*$. If $M < M^*$, the human-driven truck is cheaper per year. If $M > M^*$, the AV is cheaper per year. This is the classic fleet manager's decision rule.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU 2019 Pattern adapted):**
A fleet manager compares two options:
- Human-driven truck: Capital = **₹ 30,00,000**, Operating cost = **₹ 45 / km**.
- Autonomous truck (Level 4): Capital = **₹ 60,00,000**, Operating cost = **₹ 25 / km** (includes energy, insurance, R&M, but no driver).

Find the **break-even annual mileage**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute capital difference**

$$
\Delta C_c = 60{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}00{,}000 = 30{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 2: Compute operating cost difference per km**

$$
\Delta C_{op} = 45 - 25 = 20 \text{ INR / km}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the break-even formula**

$$
M^* = \frac{30{,}00{,}000}{20} = 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ km / year}
$$

**Step 4: Interpret**

Since the truck runs about **2,00,000 km / year** on long-haul Indian highway routes, the AV breaks even before year 1 ends, and from year 2 onwards saves **₹ 20 per km = ₹ 40,00,000 per year per truck** compared to a human-driven equivalent.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Automation Levels for Fleet Use

| Criterion | Level 2 (ADAS) | Level 4 (High Auto) | Level 5 (Full Auto) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Driver still required? | Yes | No (in ODD) | No (all conditions) |
| ODD restrictions | None | Strict (geo + weather) | None |
| Sensor cost as % of vehicle | 5% – 10% | 25% – 40% | 40% – 60% |
| Maturity for commercial fleets | Available now | Pilots / mines / ports | Research only |
| KTU exam weightage | Low | **Highest** | Medium |
| Typical fleet use case | Highway assist | Hub-to-hub trucking | Concept / future |

### 3.4 Risk-Adjusted Fleet Sizing Formula
Fleet managers do not deploy 100% AVs due to edge-case risks. The **risk-adjusted fleet size** is:

$$
N_{AR} = N_{ideal} \cdot \left(1 + \alpha \cdot \sigma_{tech} + \beta \cdot \sigma_{reg}\right)
$$

Where:
- $N_{ideal}$ = theoretically required number of AVs
- $\sigma_{tech}$ = technology failure standard deviation (e.g., **0.05** for L4, **0.15** for L3)
- $\sigma_{reg}$ = regulatory uncertainty factor (e.g., **0.10**)
- $\alpha, \beta$ = weighting coefficients (commonly **0.6** and **0.4**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This explains why KTU case studies always show a **mixed fleet** of human-driven + autonomous vehicles in the transition period of **2025 – 2035**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System Architecture of an Autonomous Fleet Vehicle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SensorSuite["Sensor Suite: LiDAR, Radar, Camera, GNSS, IMU"]
    Perception["Perception Layer: Object Detection, Tracking, SLAM"]
    Planning["Planning Layer: Behavior Planner and Trajectory Planner"]
    Control["Control Layer: Steer, Throttle, Brake ECUs"]
    V2X["V2X Module: V2V, V2I, V2N Communication"]
    FMS["Central Fleet Management System Cloud"]
    Dispatcher["Dispatcher Dashboard and KPIs"]
    Maintenance["Predictive Maintenance Engine"]
    DriverUI["HMI for Safety Driver or Remote Operator"]
    SensorSuite --> Perception
    Perception --> Planning
    Planning --> Control
    Planning --> V2X
    V2X --> FMS
    FMS --> Dispatcher
    FMS --> Maintenance
    V2X --> DriverUI
```

### 4.2 Fleet Integration Flow: AV Inside a Logistics Company

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CustomerOrder["Customer Order Received in ERP"]
    OrderToFMS["Order Routed to Fleet Management System"]
    AVSelection["Algorithm Selects Best AV Based on Load, Battery, ODD"]
    TaskAssign["Task Assigned to AV via Mobile Network"]
    EnRoute["AV Executes Mission: Highway Driving and Platooning"]
    Telemetry["Real Time Telemetry Streamed to Cloud Every 1 Second"]
    IncidentCheck{"Incident or ODD Violation Detected"}
    RemoteOp["Remote Operator Takes Control"]
    NormalReturn["AV Completes Trip and Returns to Depot"]
    BatteryCheck["Battery and Maintenance Status Updated in FMS"]
    Billing["Trip Completed and Invoice Generated"]
    CustomerOrder --> OrderToFMS
    OrderToFMS --> AVSelection
    AVSelection --> TaskAssign
    TaskAssign --> EnRoute
    EnRoute --> Telemetry
    Telemetry --> IncidentCheck
    IncidentCheck -- Yes --> RemoteOp
    IncidentCheck -- No --> NormalReturn
    RemoteOp --> NormalReturn
    NormalReturn --> BatteryCheck
    BatteryCheck --> Billing
```

### 4.3 SAE Level Decision Matrix for Fleet Manager

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Fleet Manager Decides Automation Level"]
    RouteType{"Route Type"}
    RouteType -- Closed Site Mining or Port --> L4Depot["Deploy Level 4: Highest ROI"]
    RouteType -- Highway Long Haul --> L3Highway["Deploy Level 3 or 4: Hub to Hub"]
    RouteType -- Urban Mixed Traffic --> L2Assist["Deploy Level 2 ADAS: Driver Still Mandatory"]
    BudgetCheck{"Annual Mileage Above 150000 km"}
    BudgetCheck -- Yes --> AVJustified["AV Justified: High Utilization"]
    BudgetCheck -- No --> HumanFleet["Human Driven Fleet More Economical"]
    L4Depot --> AVJustified
    L3Highway --> AVJustified
    Start --> RouteType
    Start --> BudgetCheck
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Operational Design Domain (ODD)" as used in autonomous vehicle fleet management. Why is it critical for a fleet manager to enforce ODD restrictions during dispatch?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Operational Design Domain (ODD)** is the complete set of operating conditions — including road type, weather, time of day, geofence, and speed range — under which an autonomous vehicle is certified to function safely without human intervention. It is critical because **(1 mark)** if a vehicle is dispatched outside its ODD, its perception and planning algorithms may fail, leading to accidents; **(1 mark)** and **(2)** from a legal and insurance perspective, ODD violations void the manufacturer's safety certification, exposing the fleet operator to unlimited liability. Hence, the FMS must hard-code geofences and weather checks into the dispatch algorithm.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three on-board sensors used in autonomous vehicles and state the primary purpose of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **LiDAR** – Generates a 3D point cloud of surroundings up to **200 m** for object detection and localization. **(1 mark)**
2. **Radar** – Measures relative velocity of objects using Doppler shift; robust in rain, fog, and dust. **(1 mark)**
3. **Camera (Mono / Stereo)** – Performs lane detection, traffic sign recognition, and object classification using convolutional neural networks. **(1 mark)**

*(Ultrasonic for parking and GNSS/IMU for position are also acceptable.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Adapted]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the four functional layers of an autonomous vehicle integrated with a fleet management system. State two key responsibilities of the Fleet Management System in this architecture. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The four functional layers are:

1. **Perception Layer (2 marks):** This layer fuses data from LiDAR, radar, cameras, ultrasonic sensors, and GNSS/IMU. It produces a real-time model of the vehicle's surroundings and its position within that model. The output is a list of tracked objects with position, velocity, and classification labels (car, pedestrian, cyclist).

2. **Planning Layer (2 marks):** This layer takes the perceived world model and decides *what to do next* (behavior planning) and *how to do it* (trajectory planning). The behavior planner selects maneuvers such as lane change, yield, or overtake, while the trajectory planner generates a smooth, jerk-limited path.

3. **Control / Actuation Layer (1 mark):** Converts the planned trajectory into steering angle, throttle, and brake commands sent over the CAN bus or automotive Ethernet to the drive-by-wire ECUs.

4. **Fleet Integration Layer (V2X) (1 mark):** This is the unique layer in fleet AVs. It exchanges data with the central FMS cloud, other vehicles (V2V), and infrastructure (V2I) such as smart traffic lights.

**Two responsibilities of the FMS (1 mark):**
- **Dispatch and Routing:** Assigns the right AV to the right load based on ODD compatibility, battery state, and traffic conditions.
- **Predictive Maintenance:** Uses telemetry data to predict component failures and schedule depot visits before breakdown occurs.

**(b)** A logistics company is evaluating two trucks for a **2,00,000 km / year** long-haul route:
- **Human-Driven Truck:** Capital cost = **₹ 25,00,000**, Operating cost = **₹ 40 / km** (includes driver wages).
- **Autonomous Truck (Level 4):** Capital cost = **₹ 55,00,000**, Operating cost = **₹ 22 / km** (no driver, lower fuel via eco-driving).

Calculate: **(i)** the annual cost of each option, and **(ii)** the break-even annual mileage. Comment on which option the fleet manager should choose. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Annual cost calculations (3 marks):**

For the human-driven truck:

$$
TC_H = 25{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 \times 40 = 25{,}00{,}000 + 80{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}05{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

For the autonomous truck:

$$
TC_A = 55{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 \times 22 = 55{,}00{,}000 + 44{,}00{,}000 = 99{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**(ii) Break-even mileage (3 marks):**

$$
M^* = \frac{C_c^A - C_c^H}{C_{op}^H - C_{op}^A} = \frac{55{,}00{,}000 - 25{,}00{,}000}{40 - 22} = \frac{30{,}00{,}000}{18}
$$

$$
M^* = 1{,}66{,}667 \text{ km / year}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):**
Since the planned annual mileage of **2,00,000 km** exceeds the break-even point of **1,66,667 km**, the fleet manager should choose the **Autonomous Truck**, saving approximately **₹ 6,00,000 per year per truck** over the human-driven alternative.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Adapted]*

**(a)** Explain the SAE J3016 levels of driving automation. Identify which level is most appropriate for **closed-domain commercial fleet deployment** in 2024–2025 and justify your answer with two engineering reasons. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**SAE J3016 Levels (4 marks):**
- **Level 0 – No Automation:** Driver performs all tasks.
- **Level 1 – Driver Assistance:** Either steering OR acceleration is automated (e.g., adaptive cruise control).
- **Level 2 – Partial Automation:** Both steering AND acceleration automated, but driver must monitor constantly (e.g., Tesla Autopilot).
- **Level 3 – Conditional Automation:** Vehicle monitors environment; driver can look away but must be ready to intervene.
- **Level 4 – High Automation:** Vehicle handles all driving within a defined ODD; no driver needed inside ODD.
- **Level 5 – Full Automation:** Vehicle handles all driving in all conditions; no steering wheel needed.

**Most appropriate level for closed-domain fleets: Level 4 (2 marks).**
**Justification:**
1. **Engineering Reason:** Level 4 vehicles are already commercially deployed in mines (Rio Tinto, BHP) and container ports (Kalmar), where the ODD is well-defined and free of pedestrians, providing reliable revenue.
2. **Engineering Reason:** Level 5 requires solving perception in unstructured environments (heavy rain, unmarked rural roads), which current sensor and AI stacks cannot guarantee. Level 4 avoids this by *restricting* the operating domain, which is acceptable inside a private fleet.

**(b)** Discuss three operational benefits and two key challenges of integrating autonomous vehicles into a commercial fleet. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Operational Benefits (3 × 2 = 6 marks):**

1. **Higher Utilization and 24×7 Operation:** Autonomous trucks do not need rest breaks and are not bound by driver-hour regulations. This allows continuous operation, effectively **doubling** the productive hours of a fleet asset.

2. **Lower Variable Cost per km:** Elimination of driver wages (₹ 15–25 / km savings), combined with eco-driving algorithms that reduce fuel consumption by **10% – 20%**, lowers the operating cost per kilometer significantly.

3. **Improved Safety and Lower Insurance Premiums:** Human error causes **~94%** of road accidents (WHO data). AVs do not get distracted or drowsy, reducing accident rates and, over time, insurance premiums.

**Key Challenges (1 mark each, choose any 2):**

1. **High Upfront Capital Cost:** Sensor suite, compute platform, and software integration add **40% – 80%** to the base vehicle cost, raising the break-even mileage requirement.

2. **Regulatory and Legal Uncertainty:** Indian Motor Vehicle rules (1989) do not yet grant Level 4 vehicles unrestricted road access. Permit-by-permit approvals delay scale-up.

3. **Cybersecurity Risk:** Connected AVs are vulnerable to ransomware and GPS spoofing, requiring the FMS to invest in intrusion-detection systems and over-the-air patch management.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **NOT** confuse *Driver Assistance* (Level 1) with *Partial Automation* (Level 2). At L1, only ONE function is automated; at L2, BOTH steering and acceleration are automated, but human monitoring is **mandatory**. Marks are commonly lost here.
> - In break-even calculations, always specify the **unit** of the answer (km / year). Writing just a number will cost you one mark.
> - Do not claim AVs are "100% accident-free." Use the qualified phrase "*potentially reduce accidents by up to 90%*." Examiners penalize absolute statements in engineering exams.
> - Always mention the **ODD** when describing an AV deployment. A description that omits ODD loses one full mark on a 7-mark sub-question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Autonomous Vehicle (AV):** A vehicle capable of sensing its environment and operating without human input, classified by **SAE J3016** into **6 levels (0 – 5)**.
- **Operational Design Domain (ODD):** The specific conditions (geofence, weather, time, road type) under which an AV can operate. **Every fleet dispatch must respect ODD.**
- **Core sensor suite:** **LiDAR, Radar, Camera, Ultrasonic, GNSS + IMU** — each covers a weakness of the others.
- **System pipeline:** **Sense → Plan → Act**, with an added **V2X Fleet Integration Layer** for commercial fleets.
- **Key performance metrics:** Utilization rate **65% – 85%**, energy savings **10% – 20%**, accident reduction target **40% – 90%**, TCO payback **3 – 7 years**.
- **Break-even mileage formula:** $M^* = \dfrac{\Delta C_c}{\Delta C_{op}}$ — used in KTU numerical problems to decide AV vs. human-driven truck.
- **Substitution ratio:** **2.5 : 1 to 3 : 1** — one Level 4 truck replaces 2.5 – 3 human-driven trucks due to extended operating hours and higher utilization.
- **Best early use case:** Closed ODD environments (mines, ports, agricultural fields) before open-road deployment.
- **Risk-adjusted sizing:** A pure AV fleet is not deployed in transition years; a **mixed fleet** is the practical KTU answer.
- **Highest exam weightage:** Level 4 (L4) — understand its definition, ODD, sensor needs, and economics thoroughly.
- **Eco-driving gains:** Predictive cruise control + platooning can cut fuel use by **8% – 15%** on highways.
- **Cybersecurity and regulation:** Always mention as challenges; do not ignore them in long-answer questions.
- **TCO components to remember:** $C_v$ (vehicle), $C_s$ (sensors), $C_d$ (driver), $C_i$ (insurance), $C_e$ (energy), $C_m$ (maintenance), $C_l$ (downtime loss).
- **Discount rate convention:** Indian fleet TCO studies use $r = 8\% - 12\%$; write this explicitly in derivations.
- **Substitution rule of thumb for KTU essays:** AVs succeed first where the route is **repetitive, geofenced, and weather-controlled** — keep this sentence in your answer bank.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emerging Trends in Fleet Management Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT633 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Emerging Trends in Fleet Management Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Emerging Trends in Fleet Management Technology** refer to the convergence of advanced digital, electronic, and computational innovations that are fundamentally transforming how commercial vehicle fleets are monitored, controlled, optimized, and maintained. According to the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus for *PEAUT633 – Vehicle Transport and Fleet Management*, this topic encompasses the integration of **Internet of Things (IoT)**, **Artificial Intelligence (AI)**, **Big Data Analytics**, **Autonomous Driving Systems**, **Electric and Alternative Fuel Vehicles**, **Blockchain-based logistics**, **5G Connectivity**, and **Cloud-based Fleet Platforms** into conventional transport operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Definition:** Emerging trends are not merely upgrades to existing telematics; they represent a *paradigm shift* from reactive fleet supervision to **predictive, prescriptive, and autonomous** fleet ecosystems.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a traditional fleet manager sitting in a control room with a two-way radio, calling each driver individually to ask: *"Where are you? How much fuel is left? Is the engine okay?"*

Now imagine upgrading that control room into a **smart command center** — like the bridge of a Star Trek starship — where:

- Every vehicle is a *connected node* streaming live data (**IoT**).
- The system *predicts* a tire failure **3 days before it happens** (**Predictive AI**).
- Routes automatically re-optimize when traffic jams appear (**Smart Routing**).
- Trucks literally *drive themselves* on highways (**Autonomy**).
- Payments and shipment records are *tamper-proof digital ledgers* (**Blockchain**).
- The entire fleet runs on **clean electric motors** instead of diesel (**EVs**).

That transformation is exactly what *Emerging Trends in Fleet Management Technology* describes — the migration from **manual, reactive supervision** to **intelligent, autonomous, data-driven orchestration**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Anchors:** Companies like **Tesla, Volvo Autonomous Solutions, Uber Freight, Amazon Logistics, DHL, and FedEx** are already deploying these emerging technologies at industrial scale. Indian examples include **Tata Motors' connected vehicle platform** and **Mahindra's electric last-mile delivery fleets**.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Performance Indicators

The following **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)** quantify the impact of emerging fleet technologies:

| KPI | Definition | Typical Target Value |
|-----|-----------|----------------------|
| **Fleet Utilization Rate** | Active hours of vehicles vs. available hours | **>85%** |
| **Fuel Efficiency (km/l or kWh/km)** | Distance per unit energy consumed | **EV: 5–7 km/kWh** |
| **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | Average operational time between breakdowns | **>10,000 km** |
| **CO₂ Emission Reduction** | Percentage drop in greenhouse gases vs. baseline | **30–60%** for EVs |
| **On-Time Delivery (OTD) %** | Shipments delivered within promised window | **>95%** |
| **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | Lifecycle cost per vehicle | **Reduced 15–25%** via AI |

---

### 1.4 The Eight Pillars of Emerging Fleet Technology

The KTU 2024 scheme organizes the emerging trends into **eight interconnected pillars**:

1. **Internet of Things (IoT) and Advanced Telematics**
2. **Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML)**
3. **Big Data and Cloud Computing**
4. **Autonomous and Semi-Autonomous Vehicles**
5. **Electric Vehicles (EVs) and Alternative Powertrains**
6. **Blockchain for Logistics Security**
7. **5G and Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) Communication**
8. **Predictive Maintenance and Digital Twins**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pillar 1 — Internet of Things (IoT) and Advanced Telematics

**Theory:** IoT in fleet management refers to a network of physical vehicles embedded with sensors, GPS modules, accelerometers, fuel-flow meters, and onboard diagnostics (OBD-II) ports that continuously transmit operational data to a centralized cloud platform.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Sensing:** Onboard sensors capture vehicle speed, location, fuel level, engine RPM, tire pressure, brake temperature, driver behavior (harsh braking, acceleration, cornering).
- **Step 2 — Transmission:** Data is transmitted via **GPS satellites, GSM/4G/5G cellular networks**, or **Dedicated Short-Range Communication (DSRC)**.
- **Step 3 — Edge Processing:** Data is pre-filtered at the vehicle's **Edge Gateway** to reduce bandwidth consumption.
- **Step 4 — Cloud Aggregation:** Data is stored in cloud databases (AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub).
- **Step 5 — Visualization:** Fleet managers view dashboards showing real-time maps, KPIs, and alerts.

**Why and How It Matters:**

- Enables **real-time visibility** — the foundation for every other emerging trend.
- Reduces **vehicle theft** through geo-fencing.
- Supports **insurance telematics** (usage-based premiums).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Amazon's delivery vans are equipped with IoT sensors that monitor 200+ parameters per second, allowing the system to flag anomalies in real-time.

---

### 2.2 Pillar 2 — Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML)

**Theory:** AI/ML algorithms process historical and real-time fleet data to identify patterns, make predictions, and optimize decisions without human intervention.

**Core Application Areas:**

- **Demand Forecasting:** Predicting shipment volume per region per day using **time-series models** (ARIMA, LSTM neural networks).
- **Route Optimization:** Solving the **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)** — a combinatorial optimization challenge.
- **Driver Scoring:** Classifying drivers as safe, moderate, or risky using **classification algorithms**.
- **Anomaly Detection:** Identifying fraud (fuel siphoning, route deviation) using **unsupervised learning**.

**The VRP Objective Function** (foundational concept):

The classical *Vehicle Routing Problem* seeks to minimize the total distance traveled while serving all customers exactly once:

$$\text{Minimize} \quad Z = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \sum_{j=0}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{m} c_{ij} \cdot x_{ijk}$$

where:
- $c_{ij}$ = travel cost (distance, time, or fuel) from node $i$ to node $j$.
- $x_{ijk}$ = 1 if vehicle $k$ travels from $i$ to $j$, else 0.
- $m$ = total available vehicles.
- $n$ = number of customer locations.

**Subject to constraints:**

$$\sum_{k=1}^{m} x_{ijk} = 1 \quad \forall \, j \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$$

$$\sum_{j=0}^{n} x_{ijk} - \sum_{j=0}^{n} x_{jik} = 0 \quad \forall \, i, \, \forall \, k$$

---

### 2.3 Pillar 3 — Big Data and Cloud Computing

**Theory:** Modern fleets generate **terabytes of data daily**. Big Data technologies (Hadoop, Spark) and Cloud platforms (AWS, Azure, GCP) enable storage, processing, and analytics at scale.

**The Five V's of Big Data in Fleet Management:**

| Dimension | Meaning | Fleet Example |
|-----------|---------|---------------|
| **Volume** | Magnitude of data | **TB/day** from sensor streams |
| **Velocity** | Speed of generation | **milliseconds** between telemetry packets |
| **Variety** | Types of data | GPS, video, CAN bus, weather |
| **Veracity** | Trustworthiness | Sensor noise, GPS drift |
| **Value** | Business insight | Cost savings, safety gains |

---

### 2.4 Pillar 4 — Autonomous and Semi-Autonomous Vehicles

**Theory:** Autonomous vehicles use a sensor fusion stack comprising **LiDAR, RADAR, cameras, ultrasonic sensors, and GPS** to perceive the environment and navigate without human input.

**SAE Levels of Driving Automation** *(memorize this for KTU exams!)*:

| SAE Level | Name | Human Role | Fleet Relevance |
|-----------|------|-----------|-----------------|
| **0** | No Automation | Full control | Legacy trucks |
| **1** | Driver Assistance | Active | Adaptive cruise |
| **2** | Partial Automation | Supervises | Lane-keep + ACC |
| **3** | Conditional Automation | Backup-ready | Traffic jam pilot |
| **4** | High Automation | Optional | **Geofenced robotrucks** |
| **5** | Full Automation | None | Theoretic only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** Levels 3 and 4 are commercially deployed for fleet use (e.g., **Waymo Via, TuSimple, Aurora, Volvo Vera**).

---

### 2.5 Pillar 5 — Electric Vehicles (EVs) and Alternative Powertrains

**Theory:** EVs replace internal combustion engines (ICE) with electric motors powered by lithium-ion battery packs. Fleet electrification is driven by **Net-Zero commitments**, **fuel cost volatility**, and **government incentives**.

**Battery State of Charge (SoC) Equation:**

$$SoC(t) = SoC(t_0) - \frac{1}{E_{cap}} \int_{t_0}^{t} P_{load}(\tau) \, d\tau$$

where:
- $E_{cap}$ = total battery energy capacity (kWh).
- $P_{load}(\tau)$ = instantaneous power draw (kW).
- $SoC(t_0)$ = initial state of charge (typically **100%**).

**Alternative Powertrains:**

- **Battery Electric Vehicles (BEV):** Pure electric.
- **Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEV):** ICE + electric (no plug).
- **Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV):** Larger battery, external charging.
- **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEV):** Hydrogen → electricity.
- **Compressed Natural Gas (CNG) / Bio-CNG:** Cleaner fossil fuel.

---

### 2.6 Pillar 6 — Blockchain for Logistics Security

**Theory:** Blockchain is a **distributed, immutable ledger** where transactions are grouped into cryptographically linked blocks.

**Smart Contract Equation** (conceptual trust guarantee):

$$T_{valid} = H(\text{prev\_block}) \parallel T_{shipment} \parallel H(\text{next\_block})$$

where $H(\cdot)$ is a cryptographic hash function (e.g., **SHA-256**) and $\parallel$ denotes concatenation. Any tampering breaks the chain.

**Fleet Use Cases:**

- Tamper-proof *Proof of Delivery* (PoD).
- Smart contracts for *automatic freight payment*.
- End-to-end *cold-chain traceability* for pharmaceuticals.

---

### 2.7 Pillar 7 — 5G and Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) Communication

**Theory:** 5G offers **<10 ms latency**, **1 Gbps peak speeds**, and **1 million devices/km²** density — ideal for real-time fleet coordination.

**V2X Sub-modes:**

- **V2V** — Vehicle-to-Vehicle (platooning, collision avoidance).
- **V2I** — Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (smart traffic signals).
- **V2P** — Vehicle-to-Pedestrian (safety alerts).
- **V2N** — Vehicle-to-Network (cloud services).
- **V2G** — Vehicle-to-Grid (bidirectional EV charging).

---

### 2.8 Pillar 8 — Predictive Maintenance and Digital Twins

**Theory:** Predictive Maintenance (PdM) uses ML on sensor streams to forecast component failures before they occur. A **Digital Twin** is a virtual replica of a physical vehicle that simulates its behavior in real-time.

**Remaining Useful Life (RUL) Prediction Formula:**

$$RUL(t) = T_{failure} - t_{current}$$

A common ML-based prediction uses a **degradation index** $D(t) \in [0, 1]$:

$$RUL_{predicted} = \frac{1 - D(t)}{\lambda}$$

where $\lambda$ is the degradation rate constant fitted from historical failure data.

---

### 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables / Units | Application |
|---|------------------|-----------|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | VRP Objective | $Z = \sum c_{ij} \cdot x_{ijk}$ | Cost in ₹ or km | Route optimization |
| 2 | SoC of EV battery | $SoC(t) = SoC(t_0) - \frac{1}{E_{cap}}\int P_{load}\, dt$ | Dimensionless %, E in kWh | EV range planning |
| 3 | RUL Prediction | $RUL = \frac{1 - D(t)}{\lambda}$ | Time in days | Predictive maintenance |
| 4 | MTBF | $MTBF = \frac{T_{total}}{N_{failures}}$ | Hours or km | Reliability |
| 5 | Fleet Utilization | $U = \frac{T_{active}}{T_{available}} \times 100\%$ | Percent | Asset productivity |
| 6 | Fuel Cost Savings | $\Delta C = C_{diesel} - C_{EV}$ | ₹ per km | EV TCO comparison |
| 7 | CO₂ Reduction | $\Delta CO_2 = \frac{CO_{2,ICE} - CO_{2,EV}}{CO_{2,ICE}} \times 100\%$ | Percent | Sustainability KPI |
| 8 | Blockchain Hash | $H_{new} = H_{prev} \parallel T \parallel nonce$ | SHA-256 | Logistics integrity |
| 9 | Platooning Gap | $d_{gap} = v \cdot t_{reaction}$ | Meters | Autonomous trucks |
| 10 | 5G Latency Budget | $\tau_{total} = \tau_{sensor} + \tau_{net} + \tau_{compute}$ | ms | V2X safety |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always **state units** in your final numerical answers. Marks are routinely deducted for missing units like "kWh", "₹/km", or "ms".

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Comparison — Diesel vs. Electric Fleet

This is the **most commonly asked 14-mark derivation** in KTU 2024 scheme exams under Module 3.

**Step 1 — Define the TCO components**

The total cost of owning a vehicle over its lifecycle $T$ (years) includes:

$$TCO = C_{purchase} + C_{fuel} + C_{maintenance} + C_{insurance} + C_{depreciation} + C_{infrastructure}$$

**Step 2 — Express the fuel cost term**

For a **diesel vehicle** traveling $D$ km/year at fuel economy $\eta_d$ km/l with diesel price $P_d$ ₹/l:

$$C_{fuel}^{diesel} = \frac{D \cdot T}{\eta_d} \cdot P_d$$

For an **electric vehicle** consuming $\eta_e$ kWh/100 km with electricity price $P_e$ ₹/kWh:

$$C_{fuel}^{EV} = \frac{D \cdot T \cdot \eta_e}{100} \cdot P_e$$

**Step 3 — Express the maintenance cost term**

Empirically, EV maintenance is roughly **40–60%** of diesel maintenance because of fewer moving parts:

$$C_{maint}^{EV} = \alpha \cdot C_{maint}^{diesel}, \quad \alpha \approx 0.5$$

**Step 4 — Express the battery replacement cost** (for EVs)

If the battery costs $C_{bat}$ ₹ and must be replaced once during $T$ years:

$$C_{bat} = \frac{C_{bat,initial}}{T}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute all terms into TCO**

$$TCO_{diesel} = P_{veh,d} + \frac{D \cdot T \cdot P_d}{\eta_d} + M_d + I_d + Dep_d$$

$$TCO_{EV} = P_{veh,e} + \frac{D \cdot T \cdot \eta_e \cdot P_e}{100} + \alpha M_d + I_e + \frac{C_{bat}}{T} + C_{charger}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the savings**

$$\Delta TCO = TCO_{diesel} - TCO_{EV}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta TCO = (P_{veh,d} - P_{veh,e}) + \frac{D \cdot T}{\eta_d}\left(P_d - \frac{\eta_d \cdot \eta_e \cdot P_e}{100}\right) + M_d(1 - \alpha) + \frac{C_{bat}}{T} + C_{charger}}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical Example for KTU Practice**

**Given:**
- $D = 50{,}000$ km/year
- $T = 8$ years
- $\eta_d = 4$ km/l
- $\eta_e = 20$ kWh/100 km
- $P_d = ₹95$/l
- $P_e = ₹8$/kWh
- $P_{veh,d} = ₹25$ lakh
- $P_{veh,e} = ₹38$ lakh
- $M_d = ₹1.5$ lakh/year
- $C_{bat} = ₹10$ lakh (replaced once)
- $C_{charger} = ₹2$ lakh (one-time)

**Compute diesel fuel cost:**

$$C_{fuel}^{diesel} = \frac{50{,}000 \times 8}{4} \times 95 = 100{,}000 \times 95 = ₹95{,}00{,}000$$

**Compute EV electricity cost:**

$$C_{fuel}^{EV} = \frac{50{,}000 \times 8 \times 20}{100} \times 8 = 80{,}000 \times 8 = ₹6{,}40{,}000$$

**Compute maintenance:**

$$M_d(1 - \alpha) = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 8 \times (1 - 0.5) = ₹6{,}00{,}000$$

**Compute battery annualized:**

$$\frac{C_{bat}}{T} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{8} = ₹1{,}25{,}000$$

**Compute net savings (ignoring fixed purchase differential for parity):**

$$\Delta TCO = (25{,}00{,}000 - 38{,}00{,}000) + (95{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}40{,}000) + 6{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}25{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000$$

$$\Delta TCO = -13{,}00{,}000 + 88{,}60{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}25{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\Delta TCO = ₹84{,}85{,}000 \text{ (favoring EV over 8 years)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Despite a higher purchase price, the EV saves **₹84.85 lakh** over 8 years due to massive fuel and maintenance savings. This is why fleet operators are electrifying aggressively.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Technology Adoption Impact

| Technology | Cost Saving | Safety Gain | Emissions Cut | KTU Marks Weight |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|---------------|------------------|
| IoT Telematics | **15–25%** | **+30%** | **5–10%** | High |
| AI Route Optimization | **10–20%** | **+15%** | **8–15%** | Very High |
| Big Data Analytics | **5–15%** | **+20%** | **3–8%** | Medium |
| Autonomous Trucks | **30–45%** | **+50%** | **20–30%** | Very High |
| Electric Vehicles | **40–60% (fuel)** | **+10%** | **50–100%** | Very High |
| Blockchain | **2–5%** | **+5%** | **0%** | Medium |
| 5G / V2X | **8–12%** | **+40%** | **10–15%** | High |
| Predictive Maintenance | **10–18%** | **+25%** | **5–10%** | High |

---

### 3.3 Platooning Aerodynamic Drag Reduction Derivation

When two trucks platoon at close gap $d$, the lead truck's drag coefficient $C_d$ is reduced.

$$F_{drag} = \frac{1}{2} \rho \cdot v^2 \cdot A \cdot C_d$$

The drag reduction factor $\beta$ is empirically:

$$\beta = 1 - 0.55 \cdot e^{-d/L}$$

where $L$ is the truck length. At $d = 5$ m and $L = 12$ m:

$$\beta = 1 - 0.55 \cdot e^{-5/12} = 1 - 0.55 \times 0.659 = 1 - 0.362 = 0.638$$

Thus the following truck experiences only **63.8%** of its solo drag — saving **~36%** fuel.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Emerging Fleet Management System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PhysicalLayer [PHYSICAL FLEET LAYER]
        V1[Truck A]
        V2[Truck B]
        V3[Van C]
        V4[EV Fleet]
    end

    subgraph SensorStack [ONBOARD SENSOR STACK]
        S1[GPS Module]
        S2[OBD II Reader]
        S3[LiDAR and Camera]
        S4[Fuel Cell Meter]
        S5[Tire Pressure Sensor]
    end

    subgraph EdgeGateway [EDGE GATEWAY ECU]
        E1[Data Pre-Processing]
        E2[Local ML Inference]
        E3[5G Modem]
    end

    subgraph ConnectivityLayer [CONNECTIVITY LAYER]
        C1[4G and 5G Cellular]
        C2[Satellite Link]
        C3[DSRC and V2X]
        C4[WiFi Hotspot at Depot]
    end

    subgraph CloudLayer [CLOUD and BIG DATA LAYER]
        CL1[AWS IoT Core]
        CL2[Data Lake Hadoop]
        CL3[Spark Streaming]
        CL4[Time Series DB]
    end

    subgraph IntelligenceLayer [AI and ML LAYER]
        AI1[Route Optimizer]
        AI2[Demand Forecaster]
        AI3[Predictive Maintenance]
        AI4[Driver Behavior CNN]
        AI5[Digital Twin Engine]
    end

    subgraph ApplicationLayer [APPLICATION and DASHBOARD LAYER]
        AP1[Fleet Manager Dashboard]
        AP2[Driver Mobile App]
        AP3[Customer Tracking Portal]
        AP4[Regulatory Compliance Module]
    end

    subgraph SecurityLayer [SECURITY and BLOCKCHAIN]
        SE1[Smart Contracts]
        SE2[Identity Management]
        SE3[Tamper Proof Ledger]
    end

    V1 --> S1
    V1 --> S2
    V2 --> S3
    V3 --> S4
    V4 --> S5
    S1 --> E1
    S2 --> E1
    S3 --> E2
    S4 --> E1
    S5 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> E3
    E3 --> C1
    E3 --> C2
    E3 --> C3
    C1 --> CL1
    C2 --> CL1
    C3 --> CL1
    C4 --> CL1
    CL1 --> CL2
    CL2 --> CL3
    CL3 --> CL4
    CL4 --> AI1
    CL4 --> AI2
    CL4 --> AI3
    CL4 --> AI4
    CL4 --> AI5
    AI1 --> AP1
    AI2 --> AP1
    AI3 --> AP1
    AI4 --> AP2
    AI5 --> AP1
    AP1 --> AP3
    AP1 --> AP4
    AP1 --> SE1
    AP3 --> SE2
    AP4 --> SE3
```

**Interpretation of the Diagram:**

- The **Physical Fleet Layer** comprises the actual vehicles (trucks, vans, EVs).
- The **Sensor Stack** collects real-time operational data.
- The **Edge Gateway** performs local filtering and immediate decisions (e.g., emergency braking).
- The **Connectivity Layer** transmits data via cellular, satellite, or short-range links.
- The **Cloud Layer** aggregates and stores data at scale.
- The **AI/ML Layer** transforms raw data into actionable intelligence.
- The **Application Layer** delivers this intelligence to human users.
- The **Security Layer** ensures trust, identity, and tamper-proof records.

---

### 4.2 V2X Communication Topology Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V2V1[Truck 1] -- V2V --> V2V2[Truck 2]
    V2V2 -- V2V --> V2V3[Truck 3]
    V2V1 -- V2I --> Signal[Smart Traffic Signal]
    V2V2 -- V2I --> Signal
    Signal -- V2N --> Cloud[Cloud Traffic Mgmt]
    Cloud -- V2N --> V2V3
    V2V1 -- V2P --> Ped[Pedestrian Smartphone]
    V2V3 -- V2G --> Grid[Smart Power Grid]
```

This depicts **platooning** (V2V), **smart signal priority** (V2I), **pedestrian safety alerts** (V2P), and **bidirectional EV charging** (V2G) — all enabled by 5G.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define **Internet of Things (IoT)** in the context of fleet management. List any **four sensor types** used in IoT-enabled commercial vehicles.

**Model Answer:**
IoT in fleet management refers to a network of internet-connected sensors, devices, and vehicles that continuously collect, transmit, and exchange operational data with a centralized cloud platform to enable real-time visibility and control of the fleet. Four sensor types commonly used are:

1. **GPS receiver** — for location tracking.
2. **OBD-II reader** — for engine diagnostics (RPM, coolant temp, fault codes).
3. **Accelerometer** — for detecting harsh braking, acceleration, and cornering.
4. **Fuel-level sensor / flow meter** — for monitoring fuel consumption and detecting theft.

*(Any four valid sensors acceptable. Max 3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for sensors.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State the **SAE Levels of Driving Automation**. Which level is currently commercially deployed for highway freight platooning?

**Model Answer:**
The SAE classifies driving automation into **6 levels (0 to 5)**: Level 0 — No Automation; Level 1 — Driver Assistance; Level 2 — Partial Automation; Level 3 — Conditional Automation; Level 4 — High Automation; Level 5 — Full Automation. Commercial highway freight platooning is deployed at **SAE Level 4 (High Automation)**, where the vehicle operates autonomously within a geofenced operational design domain (ODD).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **omit units** in numerical answers — leading to a 0.5–1 mark deduction.
> - Drawing the **SAE Levels table or IoT architecture diagram** adds 1–2 marks even in theory questions.
> - In derivations, **skipping algebraic steps** costs heavily; always show substitutions clearly.
> - Writing only definitions without **real-world fleet examples** (Tata, Ashok Leyland, Volvo, DHL) is penalized as "lack of application context."

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **eight emerging trends** in fleet management technology with relevant examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** comparison between a diesel truck and an electric truck. Given the following data, compute the **net savings over 8 years**:

- Annual distance: **60,000 km**
- Diesel price: **₹92/litre**, Electric price: **₹7/kWh**
- Diesel efficiency: **3.5 km/l**, EV efficiency: **22 kWh/100 km**
- Purchase price: Diesel ₹22 lakh, EV ₹36 lakh
- Annual maintenance: Diesel ₹1.2 lakh, EV ₹0.6 lakh
- Battery replacement: ₹9 lakh once in 8 years
- Charging infrastructure: ₹2 lakh one-time *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

The eight emerging trends are:

1. **IoT and Telematics** — Real-time vehicle tracking via GPS, OBD, and sensors. Example: *Tata Motors' Fleet Edge platform*.
2. **AI / ML** — Predictive analytics, route optimization, demand forecasting. Example: *Uber Freight's ML dispatch system*.
3. **Big Data and Cloud** — Hadoop/Spark-based fleet data lakes. Example: *Amazon's cloud logistics platform*.
4. **Autonomous Vehicles** — Level 4 trucks for highway platooning. Example: *TuSimple and Aurora*.
5. **Electric Vehicles** — Battery-powered commercial fleets. Example: *Mahindra Electric last-mile vans*.
6. **Blockchain** — Tamper-proof proof-of-delivery. Example: *TradeLens (Maersk + IBM)*.
7. **5G / V2X** — Sub-10 ms latency communication. Example: *5GAA consortium trials*.
8. **Predictive Maintenance + Digital Twins** — Forecast failures. Example: *Siemens digital twin for trucks*.

*(1 mark per trend with example. 8 × 0.875 ≈ 7 marks.)*

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Diesel fuel cost over 8 years:**

$$C_{fuel}^{diesel} = \frac{60{,}000 \times 8}{3.5} \times 92 = 137142.86 \times 92 = ₹1{,}26{,}17{,}143$$

**Step 2 — EV electricity cost over 8 years:**

$$C_{fuel}^{EV} = \frac{60{,}000 \times 8 \times 22}{100} \times 7 = 105600 \times 7 = ₹7{,}39{,}200$$

**Step 3 — Maintenance cost over 8 years:**

$$M_{diesel} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 8 = ₹9{,}60{,}000$$

$$M_{EV} = 60{,}000 \times 8 = ₹4{,}80{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Battery annualized:**

$$\frac{C_{bat}}{T} = \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{8} = ₹1{,}12{,}500$$

**Step 5 — Apply TCO formula:**

$$\Delta TCO = (P_{veh,d} - P_{veh,e}) + (C_{fuel}^{diesel} - C_{fuel}^{EV}) + (M_{diesel} - M_{EV}) + \frac{C_{bat}}{T} + C_{charger}$$

$$\Delta TCO = (22{,}00{,}000 - 36{,}00{,}000) + (1{,}26{,}17{,}143 - 7{,}39{,}200) + (9{,}60{,}000 - 4{,}80{,}000) + 1{,}12{,}500 + 2{,}00{,}000$$

$$\Delta TCO = -14{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}18{,}77{,}943 + 4{,}80{,}000 + 1{,}12{,}500 + 2{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\Delta TCO = ₹1{,}12{,}70{,}443 \text{ savings favoring EV}}$$

**Conclusion:** Despite a ₹14 lakh higher purchase price, the EV delivers **~₹1.13 crore savings** over 8 years due to lower fuel and maintenance costs — a compelling economic case for fleet electrification.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of an **IoT-based fleet management system** with a neat block diagram. Discuss how **AI/ML** is used in route optimization and predictive maintenance. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A fleet operator has **20 diesel trucks** running **80,000 km/year** each. Average fuel efficiency is **4 km/l** and diesel costs **₹90/litre**. If the operator replaces **half the fleet with EVs** having an efficiency of **18 kWh/100 km** and electricity cost of **₹8/kWh**, calculate the **annual fuel cost savings**. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

The IoT-based fleet architecture consists of four layers:

**Layer 1 — Perception (Sensors):** GPS, OBD-II, accelerometer, fuel sensor, dash-cam.

**Layer 2 — Network (Connectivity):** 4G/5G cellular, satellite, DSRC for short-range V2V.

**Layer 3 — Processing (Cloud + AI):** AWS IoT Core receives data → Hadoop data lake → Spark streaming → ML models (route optimizer, RUL predictor).

**Layer 4 — Application (User):** Web dashboards, driver mobile apps, customer tracking portals.

**AI in Route Optimization:** AI solves the Vehicle Routing Problem using heuristics (e.g., Clarke-Wright savings) and meta-heuristics (Genetic Algorithm, Ant Colony Optimization). It accounts for **dynamic constraints** like traffic, weather, delivery windows, and vehicle capacity — outperforming static manual planning by **10–20%**.

**AI in Predictive Maintenance:** ML models (Random Forest, XGBoost, LSTM) are trained on historical sensor-failure data. They predict the **Remaining Useful Life (RUL)** of components like brakes, batteries, and engines. Trucks are serviced *just before* failure — reducing downtime by **30–50%** and emergency breakdowns by **70%**.

*(7 marks: 3 for diagram + 2 for AI in routing + 2 for AI in PdM.)*

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Total annual km of original fleet:**

$$D_{total} = 20 \times 80{,}000 = 16{,}00{,}000 \text{ km/year}$$

**Step 2 — Annual diesel cost (original 20 diesel trucks):**

$$C_{diesel} = \frac{16{,}00{,}000}{4} \times 90 = 4{,}00{,}000 \times 90 = ₹3{,}60{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3 — After replacing 10 trucks with EVs:**

- Remaining diesel km = $10 \times 80{,}000 = 8{,}00{,}000$ km.
- New EV km = $10 \times 80{,}000 = 8{,}00{,}000$ km.

**Diesel cost (10 trucks remaining):**

$$C_{diesel}^{new} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{4} \times 90 = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 90 = ₹1{,}80{,}00{,}000$$

**EV electricity cost (10 trucks):**

$$C_{EV} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000 \times 18}{100} \times 8 = 1{,}44{,}000 \times 8 = ₹11{,}52{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Annual savings:**

$$\Delta C = C_{diesel} - (C_{diesel}^{new} + C_{EV})$$

$$\Delta C = 3{,}60{,}00{,}000 - (1{,}80{,}00{,}000 + 11{,}52{,}000)$$

$$\Delta C = 3{,}60{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}91{,}52{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\Delta C = ₹1{,}68{,}48{,}000 \text{ annual savings} \approx ₹1.68 \text{ crore/year}}$$

**Conclusion:** Replacing half the diesel fleet with EVs saves **₹1.68 crore annually** in fuel costs — a transformative economic benefit. Over a typical 8-year truck life, cumulative savings exceed **₹13 crore**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 3**

- **Eight pillars** to memorize: IoT, AI/ML, Big Data, Autonomy, EVs, Blockchain, 5G/V2X, Predictive Maintenance + Digital Twins.
- **SAE Levels 0–5** — Level 4 is the current commercial deployment level for highway freight.
- **VRP objective function:** Minimize $\sum c_{ij} x_{ijk}$ subject to assignment and flow conservation constraints.
- **EV SoC equation:** Always track integral of power draw over time divided by battery capacity.
