- **Predictive Maintenance RUL:** $RUL = (1 - D(t)) / \lambda$ where $D(t)$ is the degradation index.
- **Big Data 5 V's:** Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value.
- **V2X modes:** V2V, V2I, V2P, V2N, V2G — memorize the abbreviations and one example each.
- **TCO comparison** is the **most important numerical problem** in Module 3 — practice the derivation at least thrice.
- **Blockchain formula:** Hash links previous block + transaction + nonce via SHA-256.
- **Platooning drag reduction:** $\beta = 1 - 0.55 \cdot e^{-d/L}$ — save in mind for short derivations.
- **Always mention units** in numerical answers (kWh, ₹/km, %, ms).
- **Use real-world fleet examples** (Tata, Ashok Leyland, Volvo, DHL, FedEx, Amazon) wherever applicable — this scores extra application marks.
- **Draw a labeled block diagram** whenever the question mentions "explain with diagram" — marks are explicitly reserved for diagrams.
- **KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Focus:** Link every answer to **COs** (e.g., CO3: "Apply emerging technologies for fleet optimization").
- **Avoid vague statements** like "AI is useful" — instead write "AI-driven route optimization reduces distance traveled by 10–20% as demonstrated by DHL's ORION system."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT633 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Case Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition: Case Studies in Fleet Management

In the context of **PEAUT633 – Vehicle Transport and Fleet Management**, a **Case Study** is a structured, real-world or simulated business scenario that requires the student to apply the theoretical principles of vehicle procurement, operation, maintenance, route planning, costing, driver management, and fleet replacement decisions to arrive at a justified engineering or managerial solution. Case studies are the **integration tool** that combines Module 1 (Vehicle Selection & Specifications), Module 2 (Operating Costs & Maintenance), and Module 3 (Fleet Operations & Management) into a single decision-making exercise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examiner-Verbatim):**
> A *Fleet Management Case Study* is a problem statement, usually framed around a transport company, public transport operator, logistics service provider, or government undertaking, in which multiple variables (fleet size, vehicle mix, route structure, operating hours, fuel cost, maintenance cost, depreciation, driver policy, and service level requirements) are presented. The candidate must diagnose the operational issues, identify the cost drivers, apply quantitative models (NPV, EUAC, Payback, breakeven, optimization), and recommend the most economically and operationally viable alternative.

## 1.2 Classification of Case Studies in the KTU Syllabus

Case studies in this course are conventionally classified along three axes:

| **Axis** | **Type 1** | **Type 2** | **Type 3** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Decision Domain | Vehicle Procurement / Replacement | Route & Schedule Optimization | Maintenance Policy & Cost Control |
| Data Density | Quantitative (numbers-driven) | Mixed (semi-quantitative) | Qualitative + Diagnostic |
| Typical Mark Weight | 14 Marks (full ESE question) | 14 Marks | 7 Marks sub-part |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Imagine a fleet manager as a doctor. The "symptoms" are the operational complaints — late deliveries, high fuel bills, frequent breakdowns, low driver retention. The "medical tests" are the data tables — vehicle age distribution, km/litre, cost per km, downtime hours. The "diagnosis" is identifying whether the disease is an ageing fleet, a poor route mix, or weak preventive maintenance. The "prescription" is the recommended solution — replace X vehicles, reschedule Y routes, increase PM frequency. **That entire diagnostic flow IS a fleet management case study.**

## 1.3 Standard Fleet Management Case Study — Template Anatomy

Every case study presented in this module follows a predictable anatomy:

1. **Background / Context** — Who is the operator, what is the service profile (city bus, intercity transport, last-mile logistics, mining haulage, school transport, taxi aggregator)?
2. **Present Data Set** — Fleet composition (vehicle type, age, capacity, km run per day/year), financial parameters (purchase price, salvage value, fuel cost, tyre cost, crew cost, insurance, taxes).
3. **Operating Environment** — Terrain, road condition, average speed, load factor, trip length, regulatory framework.
4. **Decision Trigger** — Why has the case arisen? (Cost escalation, fleet ageing, new contract, expansion plan, route rationalization).
5. **Alternatives Available** — Typically 2 to 4 options (continue with current fleet / replace / augment / outsource / change policy).
6. **Evaluation Criteria** — NPV, EUAC, Payback Period, IRR, breakeven load factor, utilization rate, ROI.

## 1.4 Physical & Financial Constants Used

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants and metrics recur across every fleet case study and **must be memorized**:
> - **Working days per year:** **300 days** (commercial) / **365 days** (continuous operations like buses)
> - **Effective working hours per shift:** **8 hours** (single shift) / up to **24 hours** (multi-shift operations)
> - **Depreciation methods:** Straight Line Method (SLM) and Written Down Value (WDV)
> - **Discount rate (typical KTU assumption):** **10 % – 12 % p.a.**
> - **Service life of a commercial vehicle:** **8 – 10 years** or **8,00,000 – 10,00,000 km**, whichever is earlier
> - **Standard accounting salvage value:** **10 %** of purchase price (unless stated otherwise)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 5-Phase Case Study Solution Framework (KTU Standard)

Every 14-mark fleet management case study in the KTU 2024 scheme is best answered using the following 5-phase framework. Examiners award marks for **systematic methodology** as much as for the final numerical answer.

### Phase 1 — Problem Identification & Data Tabulation
- Read the case **twice**. First pass: understand the business. Second pass: extract numerical data.
- Tabulate all given parameters under three heads: **Vehicle Data, Cost Data, Operating Data**.
- Identify the **unknown** that needs to be found (replacement year, optimal fleet size, lowest EUAC, breakeven km, etc.).

### Phase 2 — Identify the Governing Decision Model
- If the question involves **time-value of money** → use NPV / EUAC / IRR.
- If the question involves **cost comparison** at different operating km → use cost-per-km curves.
- If the question involves **fleet sizing** → use load factor × demand formula.
- If the question involves **route economics** → use revenue per trip − cost per trip.
- If the question involves **replacement vs. overhaul** → compare marginal cost of overhaul with cost of new equivalent.

### Phase 3 — Quantitative Computation
- Apply the formula chosen. Show all intermediate steps.
- Use consistent units (lakhs, lakhs/year, Rs/km).
- Round only at the final answer.

### Phase 4 — Sensitivity Check (KTU Favourite!)
- Re-compute the answer by changing **one parameter** (usually fuel price, km run, or discount rate) by ±10 % to 20 %.
- Comment on whether the decision reverses.

### Phase 5 — Recommendation & Justification
- State the recommendation in one sentence.
- Justify using the computed metric AND at least one qualitative factor (driver comfort, brand reliability, after-sales service, regulatory compliance, environmental footprint).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Formula Name** | **Equation** | **Where Used** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost | $EUAC = (P-S) \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1} + S \cdot i$ | Replace-vs-keep decision | Rs/year |
| 2 | Net Present Value | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_{t}}{(1+i)^{t}}$ | Compare two investment options | Rs (lakhs) |
| 3 | Present Worth of Annual Cost | $PW = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$ | Convert annual cost to lump-sum equivalent | Rs |
| 4 | Capital Recovery Factor | $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | Converts P to A | dimensionless |
| 5 | Sinking Fund Factor | $SFF = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | Salvage accumulation | dimensionless |
| 6 | Single Payment Compound Amount | $F = P(1+i)^{n}$ | Future value of capital | Rs |
| 7 | Single Payment Present Worth | $P = F(1+i)^{-n}$ | Discount future cost to today | Rs |
| 8 | Cost per km (Total) | $C_{km} = C_{fuel} + C_{tyre} + C_{maint} + C_{crew} + C_{depr} + C_{overhead}$ | Operating cost analysis | Rs/km |
| 9 | Breakeven km | $KM_{BE} = \dfrac{Fixed\ Cost\ (Rs/year)}{Revenue\ per\ km - Variable\ cost\ per\ km}$ | Fleet sizing decision | km/year |
| 10 | Payback Period | $PP = \dfrac{Initial\ Investment}{Annual\ Net\ Cash\ Inflow}$ | Quick screening tool | years |
| 11 | Optimal Replacement (Minimum EUAC) | Find $n^{\ast}$ such that $EUAC(n)$ is minimum | When to replace vehicle | years |
| 12 | Load Factor | $LF = \dfrac{Passengers\ Carried}{Seating\ Capacity} \times 100$ | Bus utilization | % |
| 13 | Fleet Size Required | $N = \lceil \dfrac{Total\ Passengers\ \times\ Trip\ Length}{Capacity \times Trips\ per\ day \times LF} \rceil$ | Public transport planning | vehicles |
| 14 | Driver Cost per km | $C_{driver} = \dfrac{Salary\ \times\ Number\ of\ Crew}{km\ run\ per\ year}$ | Crew cost allocation | Rs/km |
| 15 | Tyre Life (km) | $L_{tyre} = \dfrac{Tyre\ Price\ \times\ Retread\ allowance}{Cost\ per\ mm\ wear\ \times\ Tread\ depth}$ | Tyre cost per km | km |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention used throughout this sheet:** $P$ = Present value (Rs), $S$ = Salvage value (Rs), $i$ = Annual discount rate (decimal), $n$ = Life in years, $A$ = Annual uniform amount (Rs/year), $CF_{t}$ = Cash flow in year $t$.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of Case Study Analysis

The case study technique is the **bridge between theory and the transport industry**. In real engineering practice:

- **Public Transport Undertakings** (KSRTC, BMTC, BEST, DTC) use the EUAC model to decide when to scrap a bus.
- **Logistics Companies** (Blue Dart, Delhivery, DHL) use NPV to choose between owning and leasing.
- **Mining Operators** (Coal India, Singareni) use load factor × demand to size dumper fleets.
- **Corporate Fleet Operators** (Ola, Uber, company cabs) use breakeven km to set target km/day per driver.
- **School Transport** operators use cost-per-km to compute student fee.

Thus, **every case study trains the student to think like a transport manager**, not just an examiner.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Three representative case studies are solved below in full KTU-board style. **No step is skipped**; every algebraic transition is explicitly stated.

---

## 3.1 Case Study 1 — Optimal Replacement Decision (EUAC Method)

### Problem Statement (KTU Standard Pattern)

> *"KSRTC has a bus that was purchased 5 years ago for **Rs. 30,00,000**. Its current resale value is **Rs. 18,00,000**. The annual operating cost of the bus in its present condition is **Rs. 4,50,000** and is expected to increase by **Rs. 50,000 every year** due to ageing. A new equivalent bus of the same make costs **Rs. 35,00,000**, has a useful life of **10 years**, a salvage value of **Rs. 5,00,000**, and an annual operating cost of **Rs. 2,80,000** that will increase by **Rs. 25,000 per year**. Assuming an interest rate of **10 % p.a.**, advise KSRTC whether to keep the old bus or replace it now."*

### Solution Framework

We need to compute the **Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC)** of:
- **Option A:** Keep the existing bus (over its remaining life).
- **Option B:** Replace with new bus.

The option with the **lower EUAC** is recommended.

---

### Step 1 — Estimate the Remaining Life of the Old Bus

The old bus is **5 years old**. Its total useful life is **10 years** (same as new bus, since both are same make). Therefore,

$$n_{old} = 10 - 5 = 5 \text{ years remaining}$$

The resale value of Rs. 18,00,000 today effectively acts as the **initial investment recovered** if we keep the bus.

---

### Step 2 — Compute the Capital Recovery Cost for the Old Bus

Since the old bus is already paid for, the "P" in the EUAC formula is the **forgone resale value** (i.e., the opportunity cost of not selling it now). If we keep it, we forgo Rs. 18,00,000 today, so:

$$P_{old} = 18{,}00{,}000$$
$$S_{old} = \text{Negligible at year 5 (assume } 0 \text{ for simplicity)}$$

The **Capital Recovery Factor** for $n = 5$, $i = 10 \% = 0.10$:

$$CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$$

$$CRF = \dfrac{0.10 \times (1.10)^{5}}{(1.10)^{5}-1}$$

Computing $(1.10)^{5}$ step-by-step:

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.10)^{1} &= 1.1000 \\
(1.10)^{2} &= 1.2100 \\
(1.10)^{3} &= 1.3310 \\
(1.10)^{4} &= 1.4641 \\
(1.10)^{5} &= 1.6105
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting:

$$CRF = \dfrac{0.10 \times 1.6105}{1.6105 - 1.0} = \dfrac{0.16105}{0.6105}$$

$$CRF = 0.2638 \text{ per year}$$

Therefore, the **Annual Capital Recovery Cost** of the old bus:

$$A_{cr,old} = P_{old} \times CRF = 18{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2638$$

$$A_{cr,old} = \text{Rs. } 4{,}74{,}840 \text{ / year}$$

---

### Step 3 — Compute the Geometrically Increasing Operating Cost as an Equivalent Annual Amount

The old bus has an operating cost that starts at **Rs. 4,50,000 in year 1** and increases by **Rs. 50,000 every year**. This is an **arithmetic gradient series**, not a uniform series.

Total operating cost in year $k$:

$$OC_{k} = 4{,}50{,}000 + (k-1) \times 50{,}000$$

The **equivalent uniform annual cost of an arithmetic gradient series** is given by:

$$A_{total} = A_{1} + G \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - i \cdot n - 1}{i \cdot \left[(1+i)^{n} - 1\right]}$$

Where:
- $A_{1} = 4{,}50{,}000$ (first-year operating cost)
- $G = 50{,}000$ (annual gradient)
- $n = 5$, $i = 0.10$

Compute the gradient-to-uniform conversion factor:

$$\text{Factor} = \dfrac{(1.10)^{5} - (0.10)(5) - 1}{0.10 \times \left[(1.10)^{5} - 1\right]}$$

$$\text{Factor} = \dfrac{1.6105 - 0.50 - 1.0}{0.10 \times 0.6105}$$

$$\text{Factor} = \dfrac{0.1105}{0.06105} = 1.8102$$

Therefore:

$$A_{oc,old} = 4{,}50{,}000 + 50{,}000 \times 1.8102$$

$$A_{oc,old} = 4{,}50{,}000 + 90{,}510 = \text{Rs. } 5{,}40{,}510 \text{ / year}$$

---

### Step 4 — Total EUAC of Old Bus

$$EUAC_{old} = A_{cr,old} + A_{oc,old}$$

$$EUAC_{old} = 4{,}74{,}840 + 5{,}40{,}510$$

$$\boxed{EUAC_{old} = \text{Rs. } 10{,}15{,}350 \text{ per year}}$$

---

### Step 5 — Compute EUAC for the New Bus

For the new bus:
- $P_{new} = 35{,}00{,}000$
- $S_{new} = 5{,}00{,}000$
- $n_{new} = 10$ years
- $i = 0.10$

**Capital Recovery Cost (with salvage):**

$$A_{cr,new} = (P - S) \times CRF_{10} + S \cdot i$$

First, compute $CRF$ for $n = 10$, $i = 0.10$:

$$(1.10)^{10} = 2.5937$$

$$CRF_{10} = \dfrac{0.10 \times 2.5937}{2.5937 - 1.0} = \dfrac{0.25937}{1.5937} = 0.16275$$

Now:

$$A_{cr,new} = (35{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000) \times 0.16275 + 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10$$

$$A_{cr,new} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.16275 + 50{,}000$$

$$A_{cr,new} = 4{,}88{,}250 + 50{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 5{,}38{,}250 \text{ / year}$$

**Operating Cost (gradient):** $A_{1} = 2{,}80{,}000$, $G = 25{,}000$, $n = 10$, $i = 0.10$.

Gradient factor for $n = 10$:

$$\text{Factor} = \dfrac{(1.10)^{10} - (0.10)(10) - 1}{0.10 \times \left[(1.10)^{10} - 1\right]}$$

$$\text{Factor} = \dfrac{2.5937 - 1.0 - 1.0}{0.10 \times 1.5937} = \dfrac{0.5937}{0.15937} = 3.7255$$

Therefore:

$$A_{oc,new} = 2{,}80{,}000 + 25{,}000 \times 3.7255 = 2{,}80{,}000 + 93{,}138$$

$$A_{oc,new} = \text{Rs. } 3{,}73{,}138 \text{ / year}$$

**Total EUAC of new bus:**

$$EUAC_{new} = 5{,}38{,}250 + 3{,}73{,}138$$

$$\boxed{EUAC_{new} = \text{Rs. } 9{,}11{,}388 \text{ per year}}$$

---

### Step 6 — Comparison & Recommendation

$$EUAC_{new} \ (9{,}11{,}388) < EUAC_{old} \ (10{,}15{,}350)$$

**Savings by replacement:** $10{,}15{,}350 - 9{,}11{,}388 =$ **Rs. 1,03,962 per year**.

> **Recommendation:** KSRTC **should replace** the old bus immediately. The new bus yields an annual saving of approximately Rs. 1.04 lakh.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to **subtract the resale value of the old bus** from the cost of the new bus when computing net investment. Always state clearly that the **effective P for the new option is (Price of new − Resale of old)** if the purchase decision is being made today. In this case, since we are comparing "keep" vs "replace", we kept them as two independent streams — both approaches are valid as long as you state the assumption.

---

## 3.2 Case Study 2 — Fleet Sizing for a City Bus Route

### Problem Statement

> *"A city bus route has a one-way length of **12 km**. The scheduled round-trip time including layover is **90 minutes**. Peak-hour demand is **900 passengers/hour** in each direction. The buses have a seating capacity of **50** with a maximum permissible **standing-to-seated ratio of 1:1** (i.e., crush capacity = 100). The desired **load factor** is **85 %** of seated capacity. Determine the **number of buses required** to operate the route at a frequency of **one bus every 6 minutes**."*

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the maximum capacity per bus under load factor constraint:**

$$\text{Effective load per bus} = 50 \times 0.85 = 42.5 \approx 42 \text{ passengers}$$

**Step 2 — Cycle time of one bus:**

$$T_{cycle} = 90 \text{ minutes} = 1.5 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3 — Headway desired:**

$$h = 6 \text{ minutes} = 0.1 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4 — Number of buses required:**

Using the universal fleet formula:

$$N = \dfrac{T_{cycle}}{h}$$

$$N = \dfrac{90}{6}$$

$$\boxed{N = 15 \text{ buses}}$$

**Step 5 — Verify that capacity meets demand:**

Passengers carried per hour per direction = (Number of buses per hour) × (Capacity per bus) × (Load factor)

$$\text{Buses per hour} = \dfrac{60}{6} = 10 \text{ buses/hour/direction}$$

$$\text{Capacity} = 10 \times 42 = 420 \text{ passengers/hour/direction}$$

**Step 6 — Compare against demand:**

Demand = 900 passengers/hour. Capacity = 420 passengers/hour.

$$\text{Deficit} = 900 - 420 = 480 \text{ passengers/hour}$$

The 15-bus fleet is **inadequate**. To meet demand, we need:

$$N_{required} = \lceil \dfrac{900}{42} \rceil = \lceil 21.43 \rceil = 22 \text{ buses per hour per direction}$$

$$\text{Buses on route} = 22 \text{ (using one-way) but total cycle needs 2 directions}$$

Actually, since round-trip covers both directions, **22 buses per hour in service on the loop**:

$$\text{Buses required} = N = \dfrac{22 \times 1.5}{1} = 33 \text{ buses (on the route continuously)}$$

> **Recommendation:** A minimum of **33 buses** is needed to serve peak demand at the desired 85 % load factor.

---

## 3.3 Case Study 3 — Breakeven Analysis for Owning vs Leasing

### Problem Statement

> *"A logistics company must decide between **owning a truck** (purchase price Rs. 25,00,000, life 8 years, salvage Rs. 3,00,000, annual operating cost Rs. 3,00,000, km run 40,000/year) and **leasing an equivalent truck** at Rs. 65/km (all-inclusive). Which option is cheaper if the company wants a return of 12 % on its capital?"*

### Solution

**Step 1 — Capital Recovery Cost per year for Owning:**

$$A_{cr,own} = (P - S) \times CRF_{8,\,12\%} + S \times i$$

Compute $CRF$ for $n = 8$, $i = 0.12$:

$$(1.12)^{8} = 2.4760$$

$$CRF = \dfrac{0.12 \times 2.4760}{2.4760 - 1.0} = \dfrac{0.2971}{1.4760} = 0.20130$$

$$A_{cr,own} = (25{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000) \times 0.20130 + 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.12$$

$$A_{cr,own} = 22{,}00{,}000 \times 0.20130 + 36{,}000$$

$$A_{cr,own} = 4{,}42{,}860 + 36{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 4{,}78{,}860 \text{ / year}$$

**Step 2 — Total Annual Cost of Owning:**

$$TC_{own} = A_{cr,own} + OC_{own} = 4{,}78{,}860 + 3{,}00{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 7{,}78{,}860 \text{ / year}$$

**Step 3 — Cost per km of Owning:**

$$C_{km,own} = \dfrac{7{,}78{,}860}{40{,}000} = \text{Rs. } 19.47 \text{ / km}$$

**Step 4 — Cost per km of Leasing:**

$$C_{km,lease} = \text{Rs. } 65.00 \text{ / km}$$

**Step 5 — Breakeven km per year:**

$$KM_{BE} = \dfrac{A_{cr,own} + \text{Fixed OC}}{Lease\ Rate - Variable\ OC\ per\ km}$$

Variable OC per km for owning = $3{,}00{,}000 / 40{,}000$ = Rs. 7.50/km. Lease has no separate variable OC (already in Rs. 65/km).

$$KM_{BE} = \dfrac{4{,}78{,}860}{65.00 - 7.50} = \dfrac{4{,}78{,}860}{57.50}$$

$$\boxed{KM_{BE} \approx 8{,}328 \text{ km / year}}$$

**Step 6 — Comparison at the planned 40,000 km/year:**

Since the planned km (40,000) is **far greater** than breakeven (8,328), **owning is decisively cheaper**.

> **Recommendation:** The company should **purchase** the truck. Cost per km drops from Rs. 65 (lease) to Rs. 19.47 (own) — a saving of Rs. 45.53/km, or Rs. 18.21 lakh per year.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Master Case Study Solution Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read Case Study] --> B[Phase 1: Data Tabulation]
    B --> C{Identify Decision Type}
    C -->|Replacement| D[Apply EUAC / NPV Model]
    C -->|Fleet Sizing| E[Apply N = T_cycle / Headway]
    C -->|Own vs Lease| F[Apply Breakeven km Analysis]
    C -->|Cost per km| G[Apply Cost Decomposition]
    D --> H[Phase 3: Numerical Computation]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Phase 4: Sensitivity Check +/- 10 to 20 percent]
    I --> J{Decision Reverses?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Refine Assumptions and Re-solve]
    J -->|No| L[Phase 5: Recommendation]
    K --> H
    L --> M[End: Justify with Quantitative + Qualitative Factors]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Tree for Vehicle Replacement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Reaches Age X years] --> B[Compute EUAC keep at age X]
    A --> C[Compute EUAC replace today]
    B --> D{EUAC keep > EUAC replace?}
    C --> D
    D -->|Yes| E[Replace the Vehicle Now]
    D -->|No| F[Keep Vehicle, Re-evaluate Next Year]
    E --> G[End Decision]
    F --> G
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Cost Stack Architecture of a Fleet Vehicle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Cost per km] --> B[Fixed Costs]
    A --> C[Variable Costs]
    B --> B1[Depreciation]
    B --> B2[Insurance and Taxes]
    B --> B3[Crew / Driver Salary]
    B --> B4[Garage Overhead]
    C --> C1[Fuel]
    C --> C2[Tyres]
    C --> C3[Maintenance and Spares]
    C --> C4[Lubricants]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Fleet Sizing Logic Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Route Length L km] --> B[Cycle Time T minutes]
    A --> C[Desired Headway h minutes]
    B --> D[Number of Buses N = T divided by h]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Capacity per Bus times Load Factor]
    E --> F{Supply >= Demand per Hour?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Fleet Size OK]
    F -->|No| H[Increase N or Reduce Headway h]
    H --> D
    G --> I[End]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Own vs Lease Case Study

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OptionA [Option A: Own]
        A1[Capital Recovery Cost per year] --> A3
        A2[Operating Cost per year] --> A3[Total Annual Cost Own]

    end

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define "Load Factor" as applied to public transport fleet operation. State its typical design range for urban bus services in India.**

**Model Answer:**
Load factor is the ratio of the **actual number of passengers carried** to the **seating capacity** of the vehicle, usually expressed as a percentage.

$$LF = \dfrac{\text{Passengers Carried}}{\text{Seating Capacity}} \times 100 \ \%$$

For Indian urban bus services under **peak-hour planning**, the standard design load factor adopted by **UITP and MoHUA** guidelines is **85 % to 100 %** of seated capacity. During off-peak hours, LF may fall to **40 % – 50 %**. A load factor consistently above 100 % indicates overcrowding, requiring fleet augmentation.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**List any four key financial metrics used in fleet replacement case studies. State one limitation of the Payback Period method.**

**Model Answer:**
The four key financial metrics used in fleet replacement analysis are:
1. **Net Present Value (NPV)** — measures total discounted profit.
2. **Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC)** — normalizes unequal-life alternatives.
3. **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** — discount rate at which NPV = 0.
4. **Payback Period (PP)** — time taken to recover initial investment.

**Limitation of Payback Period:** It **ignores the time value of money** (in its simple form) and **disregards all cash flows occurring after the payback period**, which is a serious drawback for vehicles with long economic lives (8–10 years). A truck may pay back in 3 years but incur huge maintenance costs in years 7–10 — Payback Period will not capture this.

---

## Part B Question (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Module 3 Choice]`

> **A logistics company owns 40 trucks that run an average of 60,000 km/year each. The fleet manager has noticed that maintenance cost has been rising steeply. The following data are provided for a typical truck:**
>
> | **Parameter** | **Value** |
> |---|---|
> | Purchase price (4 years ago) | Rs. 22,00,000 |
> | Current resale value | Rs. 12,00,000 |
> | Annual operating cost (current) | Rs. 4,20,000 |
> | Annual increase in operating cost | Rs. 60,000 |
> | Price of equivalent new truck | Rs. 28,00,000 |
> | Life of new truck | 8 years |
> | Salvage of new truck | Rs. 4,00,000 |
> | Annual operating cost (new) | Rs. 2,40,000 |
> | Annual increase in operating cost (new) | Rs. 25,000 |
> | Interest rate | 10 % p.a. |
>
> **(a)** Compute the **EUAC of keeping the existing truck** for the rest of its useful life, given that the truck has a total useful life of 10 years. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compute the **EUAC of replacing with a new truck** and **advise the company** whether to replace. State any one **qualitative factor** that may influence the decision. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — EUAC of Keeping:**

- Remaining life: $n = 10 - 4 = 6$ years.
- Forgone resale (P): Rs. 12,00,000; salvage of old bus at year 6: negligible (assume 0).

Compute $(1.10)^{6}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.10)^{5} &= 1.6105 \\
(1.10)^{6} &= 1.6105 \times 1.10 = 1.7716
\end{aligned}$$

$$CRF_{6} = \dfrac{0.10 \times 1.7716}{1.7716 - 1.0} = \dfrac{0.17716}{0.7716} = 0.22961$$

Capital recovery cost:

$$A_{cr,old} = 12{,}00{,}000 \times 0.22961 = \text{Rs. } 2{,}75{,}532 \text{ / year}$$

Gradient factor for $n = 6$:

$$\text{Factor} = \dfrac{(1.10)^{6} - 0.10 \times 6 - 1}{0.10 \times \left[(1.10)^{6} - 1\right]} = \dfrac{1.7716 - 0.6 - 1.0}{0.10 \times 0.7716} = \dfrac{0.1716}{0.07716} = 2.2238$$

Operating cost equivalent annual:

$$A_{oc,old} = 4{,}20{,}000 + 60{,}000 \times 2.2238 = 4{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}33{,}428 = \text{Rs. } 5{,}53{,}428 \text{ / year}$$

$$\boxed{EUAC_{old} = 2{,}75{,}532 + 5{,}53{,}428 = \text{Rs. } 8{,}28{,}960 \text{ / year}}$$

**Part (b) — EUAC of New Truck:**

- $P = 28{,}00{,}000$, $S = 4{,}00{,}000$, $n = 8$, $i = 0.10$.

Compute $CRF_{8}$:

$$(1.10)^{8} = 2.1436$$

$$CRF_{8} = \dfrac{0.10 \times 2.1436}{2.1436 - 1.0} = \dfrac{0.21436}{1.1436} = 0.18744$$

$$A_{cr,new} = (28{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000) \times 0.18744 + 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10$$

$$A_{cr,new} = 24{,}00{,}000 \times 0.18744 + 40{,}000 = 4{,}49{,}856 + 40{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 4{,}89{,}856 \text{ / year}$$

Gradient factor for $n = 8$:

$$\text{Factor} = \dfrac{(1.10)^{8} - 0.10 \times 8 - 1}{0.10 \times \left[(1.10)^{8} - 1\right]} = \dfrac{2.1436 - 0.8 - 1.0}{0.10 \times 1.1436} = \dfrac{0.3436}{0.11436} = 3.0045$$

$$A_{oc,new} = 2{,}40{,}000 + 25{,}000 \times 3.0045 = 2{,}40{,}000 + 75{,}113 = \text{Rs. } 3{,}15{,}113 \text{ / year}$$

$$\boxed{EUAC_{new} = 4{,}89{,}856 + 3{,}15{,}113 = \text{Rs. } 8{,}04{,}969 \text{ / year}}$$

**Comparison:** $EUAC_{new}$ = Rs. 8,04,969 < $EUAC_{old}$ = Rs. 8,28,960.

**Savings:** Rs. 23,991 per year per truck. Across 40 trucks, the annual saving = **Rs. 9,59,640**.

> **Recommendation:** **Replace** the trucks. The new trucks yield a net annual saving of approximately Rs. 9.6 lakhs for the entire fleet.
>
> **Qualitative Factor:** *Reliability of after-sales service network* of the new vehicle brand. Even if EUAC favours replacement, a weak service network in remote operating routes may tip the decision toward keeping the old fleet.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Module 3 Choice]`

> **A city bus operator wishes to introduce a new route of length 18 km one-way. The scheduled round-trip time including terminal layover is 75 minutes. The expected peak-hour passenger demand is 1,200 passengers per hour in each direction. The buses have a seating capacity of 45 and a maximum crush load (with standees) of 75. The operator desires an average load factor of 80 % of seating capacity and a service frequency of one bus every 5 minutes.**
>
> **(a)** Calculate the **number of buses required** to operate the route and verify whether the **frequency condition is satisfied**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Calculate the **effective passenger carrying capacity per hour per direction** and determine if **the fleet can meet peak demand**. If not, recommend the revised fleet size. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

Effective load per bus = $45 \times 0.80 = 36$ passengers.

Number of buses on route:

$$N = \dfrac{T_{cycle}}{h} = \dfrac{75 \text{ min}}{5 \text{ min}} = \boxed{15 \text{ buses}}$$

Frequency check: Number of buses dispatched per hour per direction = $60 / 5 = 12$ buses/hour. ✓ (Frequency condition satisfied.)

**Part (b):**

Capacity per hour per direction = $12 \text{ buses} \times 36 \text{ passengers} = 432 \text{ passengers/hour}$.

Required capacity = 1,200 passengers/hour.

**Deficit** = $1{,}200 - 432 = 768$ passengers/hour.

To meet demand, required buses per hour per direction:

$$n_{required} = \lceil 1{,}200 / 36 \rceil = \lceil 33.33 \rceil = 34 \text{ buses/hour}$$

$$\text{Total fleet size} = 34 \times (75/60) = 34 \times 1.25 = 42.5 \implies \boxed{43 \text{ buses}}$$

> **Recommendation:** The original 15 buses are **grossly inadequate**. The fleet must be expanded to **43 buses** to meet peak demand at 80 % load factor, OR the headway must be tightened to **2 minutes 12 seconds** to keep fleet at 15.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing one-way vs round-trip demand** — Some students double-count or halve demand. Always clarify that a route typically has **two directions of equal demand**.
> 2. **Forgetting the load factor** — Treating capacity as 45 (seated) instead of $45 \times LF$ inflates the answer and the examiner deducts 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring the rounding-up convention** — Always show the ceiling function explicitly: $\lceil x \rceil$.
> 4. **Skipping the qualitative justification** — Even in a quantitative 14-mark question, a one-line qualitative remark (reliability, comfort, environment) earns the final 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Case Study Framework:** Every fleet case study follows a 5-phase solution structure — Identify → Model → Compute → Sensitize → Recommend.
- **EUAC is the universal tool** for replacement decisions when alternatives have **unequal lives** — never use simple cost comparison alone.
- **Gradient series arithmetic** is required whenever operating cost is stated to increase by a fixed Rs/year (very common in KTU cases).
- **Fleet Sizing Formula:** $N = T_{cycle} / h$, with $h$ derived from demand and capacity.
- **Breakeven km** is the metric that decides Own vs Lease — always compute and compare against planned km.
- **Sensitivity Analysis** (+/− 10 % to 20 %) is **mandatory** in any 14-mark question — it carries 2 marks separately in KTU valuation.
- **Discount rate of 10 % – 12 %** is the standard KTU assumption unless specified.
- **Working days = 300/year** for commercial fleets, **365/year** for buses and continuous operations.
- **Always state qualitative factors** in the recommendation — driver comfort, brand reliability, after-sales network, regulatory compliance, environmental impact.
- **Round only the final answer** to two decimals; keep intermediate steps precise.
- **Tables must use ASCII characters only** — never embed a `|` inside a cell; substitute with `\vert` or `\mid`.
- **Mermaid node IDs** must be alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`); never use reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `style` as node IDs.
- **Show all intermediate algebraic steps** — KTU examiners reward the **method**, not just the final number.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fleet Planning and Optimization - Strategic Fleet Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT633 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fleet Planning and Optimization - Strategic Fleet Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STRATEGIC FLEET PLANNING (SFP)** is the long-horizon, top-level management process of determining the *optimal composition, size, acquisition timing, replacement cycles, and operational utilization* of a vehicle fleet, so that it consistently and cost-effectively supports the organization's core business mission, regulatory obligations, and total-cost-of-ownership (TCO) targets over a multi-year planning horizon (typically **3 to 10 years**).

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, Strategic Fleet Planning sits at the apex of the **Fleet Management Hierarchy (FMH)** and is treated as a *strategic-level decision* — distinct from tactical decisions (route planning, daily dispatch) and operational decisions (driver assignment, fuel refilling). It interfaces directly with corporate finance, procurement, HR, sustainability, and risk management functions.

The four pillars of Strategic Fleet Planning as recognized by KTU Module-4 syllabus are:

| Pillar | Strategic Question Answered |
|---|---|
| **1. Fleet Sizing** | *How many vehicles do we need?* |
| **2. Vehicle Selection** | *Which vehicle type / brand / technology?* |
| **3. Replacement Policy** | *When to retire, replace, or extend life?* |
| **4. Utilization Optimization** | *How to maximize asset productivity?* |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Chess Grandmaster" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine you are a chess grandmaster. A *tactical* player thinks one move ahead ("move my knight to capture that pawn"). An *operational* player thinks about the next few moves. But a **strategic** grandmaster thinks 15 moves ahead, anticipates the opponent's responses, sacrifices a bishop to win a queen 10 moves later, and constantly asks: *"Given the entire remaining game, what is the optimal opening, mid-game, and endgame structure of my pieces?"*

Strategic Fleet Planning is **exactly this**, but applied to vehicles:

- **Opening Game** = Initial acquisition and fleet composition
- **Mid-Game** = Replacement timing, technology upgrades, right-sizing
- **End-Game** = Disposal, second-life reuse, residual value recovery
- **Opponent** = Rising fuel costs, emissions regulations, market volatility, demand fluctuations

A fleet manager who only thinks tactically will be reactive, always firefighting. A *strategic* fleet planner, like the grandmaster, designs an end-state vision and reverse-engineers today's decisions to reach it.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics in Strategic Fleet Planning (Industry-Recognized KTU Values):**
> - **Planning Horizon:** **5 years (typical)**, range 3–10 years
> - **Discount Rate (WACC-based):** **8% – 12%** for Indian corporate fleets
> - **Vehicle Useful Life:** **8–10 years** (commercial), **5–7 years** (passenger)
> - **Target Utilization Rate:** **80% – 85%** (industry benchmark)
> - **Minimum Cost Point (Replacement Age):** Determined via Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC) method
> - **Target Fleet Availability:** **≥ 95%** (mission-critical fleets)

## 1.4 Why Strategic Fleet Planning Matters — The KTU Perspective

Most transport businesses lose money not because of bad drivers or bad routes, but because of **strategic mistakes made years earlier**: over-sized fleets, wrong vehicle class, delayed replacements, misaligned acquisition financing. A 2024 industry study (NAFA, AFTC-aligned) shows that companies with formal SFP processes achieve **15–25% lower Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** than those that plan tactically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:** When answering Module-4 questions, always remember to position any tactical decision (e.g., "should we replace 5 trucks this year?") within a *strategic framework* (e.g., "based on our 5-year SFP plan, our EAC analysis shows…"). This demonstrates **CO4: Apply fleet optimization techniques** at the strategic level.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Four-Phase Strategic Fleet Planning Process

Strategic Fleet Planning is not a single decision — it is a **structured, cyclical, four-phase process**:

### Phase 1 — Situation Analysis (Where are we today?)
- Audit current fleet: age, mileage, condition, utilization
- Compute current **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)**
- Benchmark against industry peers
- Identify **gaps** between current state and business objectives

### Phase 2 — Demand Forecasting (What will we need?)
- Forecast transport demand (tonnage-km, passenger-km) for the planning horizon
- Account for **business growth, seasonality, market expansion**
- Use techniques: **Time-series analysis, regression modeling, scenario planning**
- Translate demand into vehicle-hours and vehicle-kilometers

### Phase 3 — Fleet Design (How do we meet the demand?)
- Determine **optimal fleet size** (minimum vehicles to meet service level)
- Select **vehicle type, capacity, technology** (diesel / EV / CNG / hybrid)
- Decide on **ownership vs. leasing vs. rental mix**
- Plan **acquisition schedule** year-by-year

### Phase 4 — Replacement & Optimization Strategy (How do we sustain it?)
- Define **replacement policy** (fixed-age, fixed-mileage, condition-based, EAC-based)
- Establish **disposal strategy** (resale, auction, second-life)
- Set up **continuous monitoring KPIs**
- Build **feedback loop** back to Phase 1 (annual review)

## 2.2 The Strategic Fleet Planning Logic Chain

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ CORPORATE BUSINESS STRATEGY (Vision, Mission, Goals)    │
└────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘
                     ↓
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ TRANSPORT DEMAND FORECAST (5-year horizon)               │
│ → Tonne-km / Passenger-km / Vehicle-hours                │
└────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘
                     ↓
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ FLEET SIZING DECISION                                    │
│ N = (Annual Demand × (1 + Reserve%)) / (Veh × Util × Hrs)│
└────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘
                     ↓
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ VEHICLE SELECTION (TCO-minimizing configuration)         │
│ → Owned / Leased / Rental mix                            │
└────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘
                     ↓
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ REPLACEMENT POLICY (EAC-based optimum age)               │
│ → Buy / Hold / Retire decision matrix                    │
└────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘
                     ↓
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ MONITORING & FEEDBACK (KPI dashboard)                    │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE FOLLOWING TABLE IS A HIGH-YIELD REVISION TOOL — MASTER THESE EQUATIONS FOR THE KTU BOARD EXAM.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fleet Size (Deterministic)** | $N = \dfrac{D \cdot (1 + R)}{C \cdot U \cdot H}$ | Minimum vehicles to meet demand |
| 2 | **Vehicle Utilization Rate** | $U = \dfrac{\text{Productive Hours}}{\text{Available Hours}} \times 100$ | KPI for asset productivity |
| 3 | **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $TCO = C_{cap} + C_{fuel} + C_{maint} + C_{lab} + C_{ins} + C_{admin} - S_{res}$ | Per-vehicle life-cycle cost |
| 4 | **Net Present Value (NPV) of TCO** | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+i)^t} - S_n(1+i)^{-n}$ | Discounted life-cycle cost |
| 5 | **Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC)** | $EAC = \dfrac{NPV \cdot i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Identify optimal replacement age |
| 6 | **Optimal Replacement Age Rule** | Replace when $EAC_{new} < EAC_{keep}$ | EAC-minimizing decision |
| 7 | **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** | $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Used inside EAC formula |
| 8 | **Sinking Fund Factor (SFF)** | $SFF = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Residual value to annual equivalent |
| 9 | **Break-even Replacement Mileage** | $M^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot C_{replace}}{C_{marginal}}}$ | Marginal-cost vs. average-cost intersection |
| 10 | **Vehicle Productivity Index** | $VPI = \dfrac{\text{Revenue Generated}}{\text{Vehicle}} \div \text{Industry Average}$ | Relative fleet performance |
| 11 | **Cost per Kilometer** | $CPK = \dfrac{TCO_{annual}}{\text{Annual km traveled}}$ | Operational efficiency metric |
| 12 | **Ownership vs. Lease Break-even** | $C_{own} = C_{lease} \cdot n + S_{res}(1+i)^{-n}$ | Make-or-buy decision |

**Notation Key** (for all formulas above):
- $N$ = Fleet size (number of vehicles) | $D$ = Annual transport demand | $R$ = Reserve capacity fraction (typically 10–20%) | $C$ = Vehicle capacity per trip | $U$ = Utilization rate | $H$ = Annual operating hours per vehicle
- $C_{cap}$ = Capital cost | $C_{fuel}$ = Lifetime fuel cost | $C_{maint}$ = Lifetime maintenance cost | $C_{lab}$ = Lifetime driver/labor cost | $C_{ins}$ = Insurance | $C_{admin}$ = Admin overhead | $S_{res}$ = Residual/salvage value
- $C_t$ = Cost in year $t$ | $i$ = Discount rate | $n$ = Life in years | $C_{marginal}$ = Marginal cost per km | $M^*$ = Optimum mileage at replacement

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where these formulas are used in industry:**
> - **Logistics Companies** (TCS, Delhivery, DHL) use Fleet Size + TCO formulas to plan 5-year capex
> - **Public Bus Operators** (KSRTC, BMTC) use EAC to decide bus replacement age
> - **Ride-sharing Firms** (Ola, Uber) use CPK and VPI for driver/vehicle incentive design
> - **Mining & Construction** use the Break-even Replacement formula for heavy equipment
> - **Corporate Fleets** use Ownership-vs-Lease break-even to optimize balance sheet

The EAC (Equivalent Annual Cost) method is **the single most-tested formula** in KTU Module-4 board exams. Master it thoroughly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Optimal Fleet Size Formula

We begin with the **fundamental transport demand equation**. A vehicle of capacity $C$ (tonnes or passengers) operating for $H$ hours/year at utilization $U$ provides the following annual capacity:

$$
\text{Annual Capacity per Vehicle} = C \cdot U \cdot H
$$

If the annual transport demand is $D$ (in the same units as $C$), the **minimum number of vehicles required to meet demand** is:

$$
N_{min} = \frac{D}{C \cdot U \cdot H}
$$

In practice, we **never** size a fleet to operate at 100% — we add a **reserve capacity $R$** to absorb breakdowns, demand surges, and seasonal peaks. Thus:

$$
\boxed{N = \frac{D \cdot (1 + R)}{C \cdot U \cdot H}}
$$

> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style):**
> A logistics company needs to transport **200,000 tonnes/year**. Each truck carries 10 tonnes, operates 2,500 hours/year, has 80% utilization, and the company wants a **15% reserve margin**. Find the optimal fleet size.

**Step 1 — Identify values:** $D = 200{,}000$; $C = 10$; $U = 0.80$; $H = 2{,}500$; $R = 0.15$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the formula:**

$$
N = \frac{200{,}000 \times (1 + 0.15)}{10 \times 0.80 \times 2{,}500}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator:**

$$
10 \times 0.80 \times 2{,}500 = 20{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator:**

$$
200{,}000 \times 1.15 = 230{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Final result:**

$$
N = \frac{230{,}000}{20{,}000} = 11.5 \rightarrow 12 \text{ vehicles (round up)}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The company needs **12 trucks**, not 10. The 2 extra trucks (≈20% reserve) protect against breakdowns, peak demand, and scheduled maintenance.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC) Method for Replacement Decision

The EAC method converts all cash flows (capital cost, operating costs, residual value) over a vehicle's life into a **single equivalent annual cost**, enabling apples-to-apples comparison of "keep the old vehicle" vs. "buy a new one."

### Step 1 — Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) Derivation

Consider an annuity $A$ paid at the end of each year for $n$ years at discount rate $i$. Its present value must equal the initial loan/investment $P$:

$$
P = A \cdot \left[\frac{1}{1+i} + \frac{1}{(1+i)^2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{(1+i)^n}\right]
$$

The bracketed term is a geometric series summing to:

$$
P = A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}
$$

Solving for $A$ (the annual equivalent of $P$):

$$
A = P \cdot \frac{i}{1 - (1+i)^{-n}} = P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}
$$

The multiplier is the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**:

$$
\boxed{CRF = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}}
$$

### Step 2 — Sinking Fund Factor (SFF) Derivation

If a future sum $F$ is to be accumulated by equal annual deposits $A$ at rate $i$:

$$
F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}
$$

Solving for $A$:

$$
A = F \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}
$$

The multiplier is the **Sinking Fund Factor (SFF)**:

$$
\boxed{SFF = \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}}
$$

### Step 3 — EAC Formula Assembly

The Equivalent Annual Cost of owning a vehicle for $n$ years is:

$$
EAC(n) = \underbrace{P \cdot CRF(n)}_{\text{Annualized capital}} + \underbrace{C_{op}}_{\text{Annual operating cost}} - \underbrace{S_{res} \cdot SFF(n)}_{\text{Annualized residual credit}}
$$

Substituting CRF and SFF:

$$
\boxed{EAC(n) = P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1} + C_{op} - S_{res} \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}}
$$

> **Worked Example (KTU Board Style — 14 marks):**
> A truck costs **₹25,00,000** new, has annual operating cost of **₹6,00,000** (rising with age), a salvage value of **₹5,00,000**, and discount rate is **10%**. Compute the EAC for keeping it **5 years** vs. **7 years**, and recommend the optimal replacement age.

**Step 1 — Calculate CRF for n = 5, i = 0.10:**

$$
CRF(5) = \frac{0.10(1.10)^5}{(1.10)^5 - 1} = \frac{0.10 \times 1.6105}{0.6105} = \frac{0.16105}{0.6105} = 0.2638
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate SFF for n = 5:**

$$
SFF(5) = \frac{0.10}{(1.10)^5 - 1} = \frac{0.10}{0.6105} = 0.1638
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate EAC for n = 5:**

$$
EAC(5) = 25{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2638 + 6{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1638
$$

$$
EAC(5) = 6{,}59{,}500 + 6{,}00{,}000 - 81{,}900 = 11{,}77{,}600 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate CRF for n = 7:**

$$
CRF(7) = \frac{0.10(1.10)^7}{(1.10)^7 - 1} = \frac{0.10 \times 1.9487}{0.9487} = 0.2054
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate SFF for n = 7:**

$$
SFF(7) = \frac{0.10}{(1.10)^7 - 1} = \frac{0.10}{0.9487} = 0.1054
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate EAC for n = 7:**

$$
EAC(7) = 25{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2054 + 6{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1054
$$

$$
EAC(7) = 5{,}13{,}500 + 6{,}00{,}000 - 52{,}700 = 10{,}60{,}800 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 7 — Decision:**

Since $EAC(7) = 10{,}60{,}800 < EAC(5) = 11{,}77{,}600$, **keep the truck for 7 years** (lower annual cost). We would continue computing EAC for n = 8, 9, 10… until EAC starts to rise, then take the minimum.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Marginal Cost vs. Average Cost (Break-Even Replacement Mileage)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a classic KTU question pattern. We derive the **break-even replacement mileage** $M^*$ at which it becomes cheaper to buy a new vehicle than to keep the old one running.

### Setup
- Replacement cost of a new vehicle: $C_{replace}$ (capital only)
- Marginal cost per km of running the old vehicle (rising with age): $C_{marg}$ (fuel + maintenance that *increase* with mileage)
- Cumulative operating cost grows roughly linearly with mileage: $C_{op}(M) = C_{marg} \cdot M$

### Derivation
**Average cost per km** (over the vehicle's life to date, $M$ km):

$$
AC(M) = \frac{C_{replace} + C_{marg} \cdot M}{M} = \frac{C_{replace}}{M} + C_{marg}
$$

**Marginal cost per km** (cost of the *next* km):

$$
MC(M) = C_{marg}
$$

Setting $AC = MC$ to find the minimum point (the optimal replacement point):

$$
\frac{C_{replace}}{M} + C_{marg} = C_{marg}
$$

This gives $C_{replace}/M = 0$, which is degenerate. The correct formulation is:

- Total cost of running old vehicle for the *next* $M$ km: $C_{next} = C_{marg} \cdot M$
- Total cost of buying a new vehicle (amortized over its expected life of $M_{new}$ km): $C_{new} = C_{replace} + C_{marg} \cdot M_{new}$

The **break-even point** is found by minimizing:

$$
\text{TC}(M) = \frac{C_{replace}}{M_{new}} \cdot M + C_{marg} \cdot M
$$

Differentiating with respect to $M$ and setting to zero (using a quadratic model $C_{marg} = aM + b$ for rising marginal cost):

$$
\frac{d}{dM}\left[\frac{C_{replace}}{M_{new}} M + aM^2 + bM\right] = 0
$$

$$
\frac{C_{replace}}{M_{new}} + 2aM^* + b = 0
$$

Solving for $M^*$:

$$
\boxed{M^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot C_{replace}}{2a}}}
$$

In the simplified form (where $C_{marg}$ is the constant increase in cost per km per year):

$$
\boxed{M^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot C_{replace}}{C_{marg}}}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The optimal mileage at which to replace is where the **declining average cost curve** intersects the **rising marginal cost curve**. Before $M^*$, the old vehicle is cheaper per km; after $M^*$, the new vehicle wins.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Strategic Decisions Mapped to Tools

| Strategic Decision | Primary Tool | Key Formula | KTU Board Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **How many vehicles to buy?** | Fleet Sizing Equation | $N = D(1+R)/(C \cdot U \cdot H)$ | **High** (3–7 marks) |
| **Buy vs. Lease?** | Cost-of-Ownership Comparison | $C_{own} = C_{lease} \cdot n + S_{res}(1+i)^{-n}$ | **Medium** (3 marks) |
| **Diesel vs. EV?** | TCO Comparison | $TCO_{diesel}$ vs. $TCO_{EV}$ over 8 years | **High** (7–14 marks) |
| **When to replace?** | Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC) | $EAC(n) = P \cdot CRF + C_{op} - S_{res} \cdot SFF$ | **Very High** (7–14 marks) |
| **Right-sizing the fleet?** | Utilization Analysis | $U$ vs. benchmark; $VPI$ | **Medium** (3–7 marks) |
| **Outsource transport?** | Make-or-Buy Analysis | $CPK_{own}$ vs. $CPK_{outsource}$ | **Medium** (3–7 marks) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Strategic Fleet Planning Master Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Corporate Business Strategy] --> A2[Transport Demand Forecast 3 to 10 years]
    A2 --> A3[Current Fleet Audit and Gap Analysis]
    A3 --> A4[Compute Baseline TCO]
    A4 --> A5{Strategic Choice Point}
    A5 -->|Expand| B1[Calculate Optimal Fleet Size N]
    A5 -->|Maintain| B2[Run EAC for Replacement Analysis]
    A5 -->|Right Size| B3[Utilization and VPI Benchmarking]
    B1 --> C1[Vehicle Type and Technology Selection]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[Ownership vs Lease vs Rental Mix]
    C2 --> C3[Acquisition Schedule and Financing]
    C3 --> D1[Implementation and Procurement]
    D1 --> D2[Monitor KPIs: TCO, U, CPK, VPI]
    D2 --> D3{Annual Strategic Review}
    D3 -->|Gap Found| A2
    D3 -->|On Track| D4[Continue Execution]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — EAC Decision Block (Module 4 Favorite)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start: Vehicle Age n years] --> B[Compute P, Cop, Sres, i]
    B --> C[Compute CRF and SFF for age n]
    C --> D[EAC_keep = P x CRF + Cop - Sres x SFF]
    D --> E{Compare with EAC of New Vehicle}
    E -->|EAC_keep lower| F[Keep the Old Vehicle for 1 more year]
    E -->|EAC_new lower| G[Replace Now: Dispose Old and Buy New]
    F --> H[Increment n by 1 and Re-evaluate Next Year]
    H --> A
    G --> I[Update Fleet Plan and Procurement]
```

```mermaid
    end
    subgraph PROCESSING_LAYER["PROCESSING LAYER - OPTIMIZATION ENGINE"]

    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS_LAYER["OUTPUTS LAYER"]

    end

```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Vehicle Selection Decision Tree (Diesel vs. EV vs. CNG vs. Hybrid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Vehicle Selection Decision] --> B{Daily Route Distance km}
    B -->|Under 80 km| C{Cargo Weight tonnes}
    B -->|80 to 250 km| D[Short Haul: Evaluate EV with moderate battery]
    B -->|Above 250 km| E[Long Haul: Diesel or CNG dominant]
    C -->|Under 2 tonnes| F[Last Mile: EV with small battery recommended]
    C -->|Above 2 tonnes| G{Mid Haul Light Commercial}

    G -->|Mixed Use| I[Diesel still cost effective in 2024]
    F --> J[End: Select EV]
    D --> J

    E --> L
    J --> M[Run TCO Confirmation]

    L --> M
    M --> N{Emission Zone in City}
```
