```mermaid
    N -->|No| P[Confirm TCO-based Selection]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define Strategic Fleet Planning. List its four pillars.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Strategic Fleet Planning (SFP) is the long-horizon, top-level process of determining the optimal *composition, size, acquisition timing, replacement cycle, and utilization* of a vehicle fleet to support an organization's business mission, regulatory obligations, and TCO targets over a 3–10 year planning horizon.

> **Four Pillars (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Fleet Sizing** — determining the optimal number of vehicles
> 2. **Vehicle Selection** — choosing the right vehicle type/technology
> 3. **Replacement Policy** — deciding when to retire and replace
> 4. **Utilization Optimization** — maximizing asset productivity

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**
**What is the Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC) method? Why is it preferred over simple NPV for vehicle replacement decisions?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **EAC Definition (1.5 marks):** The Equivalent Annual Cost method converts all cash flows (capital cost, operating costs, residual value) over a vehicle's life into a *single equivalent annual cost*, using the Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) and Sinking Fund Factor (SFF). It is computed as:
> 
> $$EAC = P \cdot CRF + C_{op} - S_{res} \cdot SFF$$
> 
> **Why EAC is preferred over NPV (1.5 marks):** NPV gives a single total value, making it hard to compare vehicles with *different life spans*. EAC expresses cost on an *annual basis*, enabling direct comparison of vehicles of unequal age, unequal residual values, and unequal operating life. The vehicle with the **lowest EAC** is the optimal replacement choice.

---

## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted Model — Module 4]**

**A transport company is evaluating whether to replace its delivery vans. The relevant data is:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Purchase price (P) | ₹18,00,000 |
| Annual operating cost (C_op) | ₹4,50,000 |
| Salvage value (S_res) | ₹4,00,000 |
| Discount rate (i) | 10% |

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Compute the Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC) if the van is kept for 5 years. Show all steps.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate CRF for n = 5, i = 0.10:**

$$
CRF(5) = \frac{0.10(1.10)^5}{(1.10)^5 - 1}
$$

We compute $(1.10)^5 = 1.61051$ and $(1.10)^5 - 1 = 0.61051$.

$$
CRF(5) = \frac{0.10 \times 1.61051}{0.61051} = \frac{0.16105}{0.61051} = 0.2638
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate SFF for n = 5:**

$$
SFF(5) = \frac{0.10}{0.61051} = 0.1638
$$

**Step 3 — Apply EAC formula:**

$$
EAC(5) = P \cdot CRF + C_{op} - S_{res} \cdot SFF
$$

$$
EAC(5) = 18{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2638 + 4{,}50{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1638
$$

$$
EAC(5) = 4{,}74{,}840 + 4{,}50{,}000 - 65{,}520 = 8{,}59{,}320 \text{ per year}
$$

> **Final Answer (7 marks):** $EAC(5) = \text{₹}8{,}59{,}320$ per year.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Now compute EAC for n = 7 years and n = 10 years. Recommend the optimal replacement age based on minimum EAC. Justify with strategic reasoning.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate CRF and SFF for n = 7:**

$(1.10)^7 = 1.94872$; $(1.10)^7 - 1 = 0.94872$.

$$
CRF(7) = \frac{0.10 \times 1.94872}{0.94872} = \frac{0.19487}{0.94872} = 0.2054
$$

$$
SFF(7) = \frac{0.10}{0.94872} = 0.1054
$$

**Step 2 — EAC for n = 7:**

$$
EAC(7) = 18{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2054 + 4{,}50{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1054
$$

$$
EAC(7) = 3{,}69{,}720 + 4{,}50{,}000 - 42{,}160 = 7{,}77{,}560 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate CRF and SFF for n = 10:**

$(1.10)^{10} = 2.59374$; $(1.10)^{10} - 1 = 1.59374$.

$$
CRF(10) = \frac{0.10 \times 2.59374}{1.59374} = \frac{0.25937}{1.59374} = 0.1627
$$

$$
SFF(10) = \frac{0.10}{1.59374} = 0.0627
$$

**Step 4 — EAC for n = 10:**

$$
EAC(10) = 18{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1627 + 4{,}50{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.0627
$$

$$
EAC(10) = 2{,}92{,}860 + 4{,}50{,}000 - 25{,}080 = 7{,}17{,}780 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison Table:**

| n (years) | EAC (₹/year) |
|---|---|
| 5 | 8,59,320 |
| 7 | 7,77,560 |
| **10** | **7,17,780** ← Minimum |

**Step 6 — Recommendation:**

Since **EAC(10) is the lowest**, the company should keep the van for **10 years** from a pure cost-minimization standpoint. However, a **strategic recommendation** must also consider:
- *Reliability degradation after year 8* (rising breakdowns, increased downtime cost not captured in $C_{op}$)
- *Emission norm changes* (BS-VII may force retirement at year 7–8)
- *Technology obsolescence* (EV alternatives may make diesel vans uneconomical by year 8)

> **Final Strategic Answer (7 marks):** Pure EAC says replace at year 10, but **strategically, replace between years 7 and 8**, balancing cost minimization with reliability, regulatory, and technology risk.

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted Model — Module 4]**

**A logistics firm needs 2,00,000 tonne-km of transport per year. Each truck has a capacity of 10 tonnes, operates 2,000 hours/year, with 75% utilization. The firm wants a 20% reserve margin.**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Calculate the optimal fleet size. Show all steps clearly.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**
- Annual demand $D = 2{,}00{,}000$ tonne-km
- Capacity per trip $C = 10$ tonnes
- Annual hours $H = 2{,}000$
- Utilization $U = 0.75$
- Reserve $R = 0.20$

**Step 2 — Apply fleet sizing formula:**

$$
N = \frac{D \cdot (1 + R)}{C \cdot U \cdot H}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator:**

$$
C \cdot U \cdot H = 10 \times 0.75 \times 2{,}000 = 15{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator:**

$$
D \cdot (1 + R) = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 1.20 = 2{,}40{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Final calculation:**

$$
N = \frac{2{,}40{,}000}{15{,}000} = 16 \text{ vehicles}
$$

> **Final Answer (7 marks):** $N = 16$ trucks required to meet demand with 20% reserve.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Discuss the strategic implications of this fleet size. What happens if (i) demand grows by 25% next year, (ii) the firm leases 4 trucks instead of owning, (iii) utilization drops to 60%? Use strategic fleet planning concepts to analyze.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Demand grows 25% (1 mark):** New demand = $2{,}00{,}000 \times 1.25 = 2{,}50{,}000$ tonne-km. New required fleet size:

$$
N_{new} = \frac{2{,}50{,}000 \times 1.20}{15{,}000} = 20 \text{ vehicles}
$$

**Strategic insight:** Rather than buying 4 more trucks (heavy capex), consider **leasing 4 vehicles for 12 months** while studying whether demand growth is sustained. This is a *tactical response within a strategic framework*.

**(ii) Lease 4 trucks (3 marks):** Strategic implications:
- **Balance Sheet Impact:** Owned trucks are assets; leased trucks are off-balance-sheet operating expenses. Leasing improves *Return on Assets* (ROA).
- **Flexibility:** Leasing allows easy fleet scaling up/down — a strategic advantage in volatile markets.
- **Cost Trade-off:** Leasing is typically 15–25% more expensive over the full life, but preserves liquidity. KTU students should compute:
  
$$
\text{Lease cost over 5 years} = 4 \times \text{Annual lease} \times 5
$$

$$
\text{Own cost over 5 years} = 4 \times \text{Purchase} + 4 \times C_{op} \times 5 - 4 \times S_{res}
$$

- **Strategic recommendation:** Lease when (a) technology is changing rapidly (e.g., EV transition), (b) demand is uncertain, (c) capital is constrained.

**(iii) Utilization drops to 60% (3 marks):** New effective capacity per truck:

$$
C \cdot U' \cdot H = 10 \times 0.60 \times 2{,}000 = 12{,}000
$$

Required fleet:

$$
N' = \frac{2{,}40{,}000}{12{,}000} = 20 \text{ vehicles}
$$

**Strategic insight:** With lower utilization, the *same demand now requires 20 vehicles instead of 16*. This is a **major red flag** indicating:
- **Over-capacity risk** — fleet is now too large for current demand
- **Cost per km rises** — fixed costs spread over fewer productive hours
- **Strategic response:** Right-size the fleet by *disposing 3–4 under-utilized vehicles* (use EAC analysis to find the optimal disposal point) and route-optimize to push utilization back to 75%+.

> **Final Strategic Answer (7 marks):** The 16-truck figure is sensitive to all three assumptions. A robust Strategic Fleet Plan must *stress-test* fleet size under multiple scenarios (sensitivity analysis) and build in **leasing flexibility** to handle demand uncertainty.

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON PITFALLS — WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — $D$ must be in the same unit as $C$ (e.g., tonne-km vs. tonnes). Mixing units is the #1 cause of wrong answers.
> 2. **Skipping the CRF/SFF derivation** — Examiners award 2–3 marks for showing the formula derivation, not just the substitution. **Always write out $CRF = i(1+i)^n/[(1+i)^n - 1]$ before using it.**
> 3. **Ignoring salvage value** — A common error is to forget the $S_{res} \cdot SFF$ term. Salvage is a *credit*, not a cost; subtracting it is correct.
> 4. **Not rounding up fleet size** — You cannot have 11.5 trucks. Always round *up* to the next whole number. Examiners will deduct marks for 11.5 as the final answer.
> 5. **Confusing EAC with NPV** — EAC is *annual*, NPV is *total*. In a 14-mark question, students often answer with NPV when EAC was asked, leading to 4–5 mark loss.
> 6. **Failing to add strategic commentary** — A purely numerical answer scores 8–9 out of 14. To score 12+, you **must** discuss strategic implications (regulatory risk, technology obsolescence, demand uncertainty, lease vs. buy).
> 7. **Using the wrong discount rate** — Corporate fleets use the firm's **WACC**, not the loan interest rate. Use 8–12% as a typical KTU range.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> **Must Remember Before the KTU Board Exam:**

- ☐ **Strategic Fleet Planning = long-horizon (3–10 yrs)**, top-level decision on size, mix, replacement, utilization
- ☐ **Four Pillars:** Sizing, Selection, Replacement, Optimization
- ☐ **Fleet Size Formula:** $N = D(1+R) / (C \cdot U \cdot H)$ — **round UP** to integer
- ☐ **TCO includes:** Capital + Fuel + Maintenance + Labor + Insurance + Admin − Salvage
- ☐ **NPV = discounted life-cycle cost**; **EAC = NPV converted to annual** (preferred for replacement decisions)
- ☐ **CRF = $i(1+i)^n/[(1+i)^n - 1]$** — annualizes capital
- ☐ **SFF = $i/[(1+i)^n - 1]$** — annualizes residual/salvage
- ☐ **Optimal Replacement Rule:** Choose $n$ that **minimizes EAC**; cross-check with reliability, regulatory, technology factors
- ☐ **Sensitivity Analysis:** Always stress-test demand, utilization, and discount rate assumptions
- ☐ **Strategic vs. Tactical:** Tactical = day-to-day; Strategic = 3–10 year horizon, capex, TCO, business alignment
- ☐ **Reserve Margin:** Typically **10–20%** of base demand
- ☐ **Target Utilization:** **80–85%** (industry benchmark for KTU questions)
- ☐ **Lease vs. Own:** Lease when (a) technology is changing, (b) demand uncertain, (c) capital constrained
- ☐ **Vehicle Productivity Index (VPI):** Measures how fleet vehicles perform vs. industry average
- ☐ **Cost per km (CPK):** $CPK = TCO_{annual} / \text{Annual km}$ — fundamental operational KPI
- ☐ **Decision Tip:** Every SFP decision should be backed by **TCO + EAC + Sensitivity**, not intuition
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fleet Optimization Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT633 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fleet Optimization Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🚛 Fleet Optimization Techniques — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fleet Optimization** is the systematic application of mathematical, statistical, and operations research methodologies to determine the **ideal number, type, allocation, routing, and utilization** of vehicles in a transport fleet such that total system cost is minimized (or profit maximized) while satisfying demand, service-level, and operational constraints.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **PEAUT633 — Vehicle Transport and Fleet Management**, the optimization is studied across **four inter-locking decision layers**:

1. **Strategic layer** — *How many vehicles? What type? Owned or hired?* (Long horizon, capital-intensive)
2. **Tactical layer** — *Where to position empty vehicles? How to schedule drivers?* (Medium horizon)
3. **Operational layer** — *Which vehicle serves which customer in which sequence?* (Daily, real-time)
4. **Performance layer** — *Load factor, dead-mileage ratio, cost per ton-km* (Continuous monitoring)

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The KSRTC Depot Manager

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you are the **depot manager of a state road transport corporation**. You have 40 buses, 12 routes, peak-hour rush, idle buses in the depot, breakdown-prone vehicles, fuel price shocks, and a fixed daily budget. Fleet optimization is the *scientific equivalent of the experienced manager's gut feel*, but expressed through equations, integer variables, and constraint matrices — so that the answer is reproducible, defensible, and provably optimal.

## 1.3 Why This Topic is a KTU High-Yield Module

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test the ability of students to:
> - Formulate a **Linear Programming (LP) / Integer Programming (IP) model** for fleet sizing or trip assignment.
> - Compute the **optimum fleet size** using demand-capacity equations.
> - Interpret **utilization ratios** and their cost implications.
> - Compare alternative **routing strategies** (direct delivery vs. consolidated hub-and-spoke).

## 1.4 Core Performance Metrics Used in Optimization

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Benchmark |
|---|---|---|
| **Vehicle Utilization** | $\eta_v$ | **> 85 %** for healthy fleet |
| **Load Factor** | $\lambda$ | **> 80 %** for goods carriers |
| **Dead Mileage Ratio** | $\rho_d$ | **< 15 %** of total km |
| **Cost per Ton-Km** | $C_{tk}$ | Fleet-specific KPI |
| **Trip Completion Rate** | $\tau_c$ | **> 95 %** |
| **Empty Running Ratio** | $r_e$ | **< 20 %** |

All these metrics appear inside the **objective function** and **constraints** of any rigorous fleet optimization model.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of Fleet Optimization — The Three Building Blocks

### Block A — Demand Forecasting
- Translates **forecasted freight/passenger demand** into *origin-destination (O-D) trip matrices*.
- Input to optimization: $D_{ij}$ = demand between node $i$ and node $j$ (tons/day or passengers/day).

### Block B — Vehicle Routing & Scheduling
- Allocates specific vehicles to specific trips, decides **sequence of customer visits**, and decides **load consolidation** decisions.

### Block C — Cost Modeling
- Builds the **objective function** that the optimizer minimizes:
  - Fixed cost of vehicle ownership
  - Variable cost per km
  - Loading/unloading cost
  - Penalty for un-served demand
  - Penalty for late delivery

## 2.2 Cost Decomposition of a Fleet

The **Total Cost of Operation (TCO)** of a fleet over a planning horizon $T$ is decomposed as:

$$
TCO = \underbrace{C_f}_{\text{fixed}} + \underbrace{C_v \cdot D}_{\text{km-based}} + \underbrace{C_t \cdot T_h}_{\text{time-based}} + \underbrace{C_l \cdot Q}_{\text{loading}} + \underbrace{C_p \cdot U}_{\text{penalty}}
$$

Where:
- $C_f$ = Fixed cost per vehicle per period (insurance, depreciation, salary)
- $C_v$ = Variable cost per km (fuel, tyres, maintenance)
- $D$ = Total distance travelled (km)
- $C_t$ = Cost per hour (driver wages, idle time)
- $T_h$ = Total hours of operation
- $C_l$ = Loading/unloading cost per ton
- $Q$ = Total tonnage handled
- $C_p$ = Penalty cost per unit of un-served demand
- $U$ = Un-served demand

## 2.3 Fleet Utilization and Load Factor

### Vehicle Utilization Ratio
$$
\eta_v = \frac{\text{Loaded Distance} + \text{Passenger-Km Earned}}{\text{Total Possible Distance} \times \text{Number of Vehicles}} \times 100 \%
$$

### Load Factor (Goods Fleet)
$$
\lambda = \frac{\text{Actual Payload}}{\text{Rated Payload Capacity}} \times 100 \%
$$

### Empty Running Ratio
$$
r_e = \frac{\text{Empty Distance}}{\text{Total Distance}} \times 100 \%
$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below contains **all high-yield formulas** examiners expect for Fleet Optimization. The vertical bar symbol has been written using $\vert$ to preserve markdown rendering.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Transport Cost | $TC = n C_f + D C_v$ | ₹ | $n \geq 1$, $D \geq 0$ |
| 2 | Cost per Ton-Km | $C_{tk} = TC \div (Q \cdot D)$ | ₹ / ton-km | Lower is better |
| 3 | Vehicle Utilization | $\eta_v = \text{Loaded km} \div \text{Total km}$ | % | Target $\geq 85\%$ |
| 4 | Load Factor | $\lambda = Q_a \div Q_r$ | % | $0 \leq \lambda \leq 1$ |
| 5 | Empty Running Ratio | $r_e = D_e \div D_t$ | % | Target $\leq 20\%$ |
| 6 | Optimum Fleet Size | $N^* = \lceil D_p \div (C_v \cdot \mu) \rceil$ | vehicles | $D_p$ = peak demand |
| 7 | Trip Frequency | $f_t = D_d \div (C_v \cdot \lambda)$ | trips/day | integer $\geq 1$ |
| 8 | Break-Even Distance | $D_{be} = C_f \div (C_h - C_v)$ | km | Hire vs Own decision |
| 9 | Vehicle Productivity | $P_v = Q \cdot D \div n$ | ton-km/vehicle | KPI |
| 10 | Dead Mileage Penalty | $P_d = \alpha \cdot D_e$ | ₹ | $\alpha$ = penalty/km |
| 11 | LP Objective (Assignment) | $\min Z = \sum_{i,j} c_{ij} x_{ij}$ | ₹ | $x_{ij} \in \{0,1\}$ |
| 12 | Constraint — Demand | $\sum_j x_{ij} = 1 \; \forall i$ | — | Each customer once |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Fleet optimization is the *mathematical backbone* of:
> - **Amazon / Flipkart** last-mile delivery routing
> - **Indian Railways** coach and loco scheduling
> - **KSRTC / BEST** bus deployment
> - **Container shipping lines** (empty container repositioning)
> - **Ola / Uber** dynamic ride-matching
> - **Cold-chain logistics** (temperature-constrained VRP)
> - **Mining & construction** heavy equipment dispatching

Every KTU graduate working in **logistics, supply chain, public transport, or e-commerce** will encounter these techniques within the first two years of employment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimum Fleet Size from Peak Demand

### Problem Statement
A goods carrier handles a **peak daily demand** $D_p$ tons. Each vehicle has **rated capacity** $C_v$ tons and operates an average of **$\mu$ trips per day**. Determine the minimum number of vehicles $N^*$ required.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Daily tonnage capacity of ONE vehicle**

$$
\text{Capacity}_{1} = C_v \cdot \mu \quad \text{[tons/day]}
$$

**Step 2 — Total capacity needed across fleet of $N$ vehicles**

$$
\text{Capacity}_{N} = N \cdot C_v \cdot \mu
$$

**Step 3 — Apply feasibility constraint** — total capacity must equal or exceed peak demand

$$
N \cdot C_v \cdot \mu \geq D_p
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $N$**

$$
N \geq \frac{D_p}{C_v \cdot \mu}
$$

**Step 5 — Take the ceiling function** because $N$ must be a positive integer

$$
N^* = \left\lceil \frac{D_p}{C_v \cdot \mu} \right\rceil
$$

**Interpretation:** The optimum fleet size is the **smallest integer** that satisfies the demand constraint. Adding one extra vehicle beyond $N^*$ adds **fixed cost without productivity gain** — a classic example of *marginal cost = marginal benefit*.

### Worked Numerical Example

Given:
- $D_p = 480$ tons/day
- $C_v = 12$ tons/vehicle
- $\mu = 4$ trips/day

$$
N^* = \left\lceil \frac{480}{12 \times 4} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{480}{48} \right\rceil = \lceil 10 \rceil = 10 \text{ vehicles}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cost Minimization for Hire vs Own Decision

### Setup
A logistics manager must choose between:
- **Option A** — Own a vehicle: fixed cost $C_f$ per period, variable cost $C_v$ per km.
- **Option B** — Hire a vehicle: cost $C_h$ per km (all-inclusive, no fixed component).

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Total cost of own vehicle over distance $D$**

$$
TC_{own} = C_f + C_v \cdot D
$$

**Step 2 — Total cost of hired vehicle over distance $D$**

$$
TC_{hire} = C_h \cdot D
$$

**Step 3 — Set both costs equal** to find the indifference (break-even) point

$$
C_f + C_v \cdot D_{be} = C_h \cdot D_{be}
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange**

$$
C_f = (C_h - C_v) \cdot D_{be}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for break-even distance**

$$
D_{be} = \frac{C_f}{C_h - C_v}
$$

**Decision Rule:**
- If $D > D_{be}$ → **Own** the vehicle.
- If $D < D_{be}$ → **Hire** the vehicle.

### Numerical Illustration

Given $C_f = 1{,}50{,}000$ ₹/month, $C_v = 8$ ₹/km, $C_h = 22$ ₹/km:

$$
D_{be} = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{22 - 8} = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{14} \approx 10{,}714 \text{ km/month}
$$

So if the monthly run exceeds **~10,714 km**, owning is more economical.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Linear Programming Model for Trip Assignment

This is the **most frequently asked 14-mark derivation** in KTU papers.

### Model Formulation

**Sets**
- $I$ = set of origins (depots / cities) = $\{1, 2, \dots, m\}$
- $J$ = set of destinations = $\{1, 2, \dots, n\}$

**Parameters**
- $c_{ij}$ = cost of transporting one unit from $i$ to $j$ (₹/ton)
- $a_i$ = supply at origin $i$ (tons)
- $b_j$ = demand at destination $j$ (tons)

**Decision Variables**
- $x_{ij}$ = quantity transported from $i$ to $j$ (tons), $x_{ij} \geq 0$

**Objective Function — Minimize Total Transportation Cost**

$$
\min Z = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij} \, x_{ij}
$$

**Constraint 1 — Supply Constraint**

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} = a_i \quad \forall i \in I
$$

**Constraint 2 — Demand Constraint**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{ij} = b_j \quad \forall j \in J
$$

**Constraint 3 — Non-Negativity**

$$
x_{ij} \geq 0 \quad \forall i, j
$$

**Constraint 4 — Balanced Problem**

$$
\sum_{i} a_i = \sum_{j} b_j
$$

### Step-by-Step Solution Approach (KTU Pattern)

**Step 1** — Check **balanced transport problem**: total supply must equal total demand. If not, add a *dummy* row or column.

**Step 2** — Use **North-West Corner Rule** to find initial basic feasible solution (IBFS).

**Step 3** — Compute $u_i + v_j - c_{ij}$ for all unoccupied cells to find optimality index.

**Step 4** — If all indices are non-negative, solution is optimal. Otherwise, find the most negative cell, draw a closed loop, and re-allocate.

**Step 5** — Iterate until optimality achieved.

### Numerical Illustration

| | D1 (100) | D2 (150) | D3 (50) | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| O1 (80) | 4 | 8 | 8 | 80 |
| O2 (120) | 6 | 6 | 3 | 120 |
| O3 (100) | 10 | 12 | 9 | 100 |
| Demand | 100 | 150 | 50 | 300 |

Total supply = 300 = Total demand → **Balanced**.

**IBFS via North-West Corner:**

$$
x_{11} = \min(80, 100) = 80 \quad \Rightarrow \text{O1 exhausted}
$$

$$
x_{21} = \min(120, 20) = 20 \quad \Rightarrow \text{D1 satisfied}
$$

$$
x_{22} = \min(100, 150) = 100 \quad \Rightarrow \text{O2 exhausted}
$$

$$
x_{32} = \min(100, 50) = 50 \quad \Rightarrow \text{D2 satisfied}
$$

$$
x_{33} = \min(50, 50) = 50 \quad \Rightarrow \text{All done}
$$

**Total Cost:**

$$
Z = 80(4) + 20(6) + 100(6) + 50(12) + 50(9)
$$

$$
Z = 320 + 120 + 600 + 600 + 450 = 2{,}090 \; ₹
$$

This IBFS is the **starting point**; the MODI method (or Stepping-Stone) is then applied to test optimality.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) — Simplified Mathematical Formulation

The **VRP** is the central combinatorial optimization problem in fleet management.

$$
\min \sum_{k \in K} \sum_{(i,j) \in A} c_{ij} \, x_{ijk}
$$

Subject to:

$$
\sum_{k \in K} \sum_{j \in \Delta^+(i)} x_{ijk} = 1 \quad \forall i \in V \setminus \{0\}
$$

$$
\sum_{j \in \Delta^+(0)} x_{0jk} = 1 \quad \forall k \in K
$$

$$
\sum_{i \in V} q_i \cdot \sum_{j \in \Delta^+(i)} x_{ijk} \leq Q \quad \forall k \in K
$$

$$
x_{ijk} \in \{0, 1\} \quad \forall (i,j) \in A, \; k \in K
$$

Where:
- $V$ = set of nodes (customers + depot 0)
- $A$ = set of arcs
- $K$ = set of vehicles
- $c_{ij}$ = cost of arc $(i,j)$
- $q_i$ = demand at node $i$
- $Q$ = vehicle capacity
- $\Delta^+(i)$ = set of arcs leaving node $i$
- $x_{ijk} = 1$ if vehicle $k$ traverses arc $(i,j)$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **binary integrality** $x_{ijk} \in \{0,1\}$ is what makes the VRP an **NP-hard problem** — meaning exact solutions become computationally intractable for large fleets (> 50 vehicles), which is why **heuristics** (Clarke-Wright, Sweep, Tabu Search, Genetic Algorithms) are widely used in industry.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Fleet Optimization Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Demand Data O-D Matrix] --> B[Forecasting Module]
    B --> C[Demand Matrix Dij]
    C --> D[Fleet Sizing Optimizer]
    D --> E{Vehicle Type Selection}
    E --> F[Capacity Check]
    F --> G[Vehicle Routing Module]
    G --> H[Cost Computation Engine]
    H --> I[Utilization Metrics]
    I --> J{KPI Threshold Met?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Final Schedule]
    J -- No --> L[Feedback Re-optimize]
    L --> D
    K --> M[Dispatch Execution]
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram — Hire vs Own Decision

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Start Decision] --> X[Compute Monthly Distance D]
    X --> Y[Compute Break-Even D_be]
    Y --> Z{D greater than D_be?}
    Z -- Yes --> P1[Own Vehicle Recommended]
    Z -- No --> P2[Hire Vehicle Recommended]
    P1 --> END[End]
    P2 --> END
```

## 4.3 Process Diagram — Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Customer Locations Input] --> R2[Distance Matrix Computation]
    R2 --> R3[Demand Aggregation]
    R3 --> R4[Fleet Size Check]

    R5 --> R6[Route Construction]
    R6 --> R7[Capacity Validation]
    R7 --> R8{All Customers Served?}
    R8 -- No --> R9[Insert Additional Vehicle]
    R9 --> R5
    R8 -- Yes --> R10[Output Optimized Routes]
```

## 4.4 Layered Architecture — Strategic, Tactical, Operational Optimization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[STRATEGIC LAYER Long Term] --> L1A[Fleet Size]
    L1 --> L1B[Vehicle Type Mix]
    L1 --> L1C[Depot Location]

    L3[OPERATIONAL LAYER Short Term] --> L3A[Daily Trip Assignment]

    L2 --> L3
```

```mermaid
    TCO --> A5[Penalty Cost Cp]
    A1 --> A1a[Depreciation]
    A1 --> A1b[Insurance]
    A1 --> A1c[Driver Salary]

```

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define fleet optimization. List any four key performance indicators used to measure fleet performance.**

**Model Answer:**
Fleet optimization is the systematic application of operations research techniques to determine the **ideal number, type, allocation, and routing** of vehicles in a transport fleet, subject to demand and service-level constraints, such that the total system cost is minimized.

Four KPIs:
1. **Vehicle Utilization Ratio** ($\eta_v$) — proportion of time/distance vehicle is productively employed.
2. **Load Factor** ($\lambda$) — actual payload / rated payload.
3. **Cost per Ton-Km** ($C_{tk}$) — total cost / (payload × distance).
4. **Empty Running Ratio** ($r_e$) — empty km / total km.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Write the mathematical formulation of a Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP).**

**Model Answer:**
Given a depot node $0$, set of customers $V \setminus \{0\}$, vehicle capacity $Q$, demand $q_i$, and cost $c_{ij}$ per arc, the CVRP is:

$$
\min \sum_{k \in K} \sum_{(i,j) \in A} c_{ij} \, x_{ijk}
$$

Subject to:

$$
\sum_{k} \sum_{j} x_{ijk} = 1 \; \forall i \in V \setminus \{0\}
$$

$$
\sum_{i} q_i \sum_{j} x_{ijk} \leq Q \; \forall k
$$

$$
x_{ijk} \in \{0, 1\}
$$

The binary integrality reflects the *either-or* nature of route selection.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Option 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Explain the **linear programming formulation of the transportation problem** as applied to fleet trip assignment. Define all variables and constraints clearly. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define Sets:**
- $I = \{1, 2, \dots, m\}$ — origins (depots, warehouses)
- $J = \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$ — destinations (customers, zones)

**Step 2 — Parameters:**
- $c_{ij}$ — unit transport cost from $i$ to $j$ (₹/ton)
- $a_i$ — supply at origin $i$ (tons)
- $b_j$ — demand at destination $j$ (tons)

**Step 3 — Decision Variable:**
- $x_{ij}$ — quantity transported from $i$ to $j$ (tons), $x_{ij} \geq 0$

**Step 4 — Objective Function:**

$$
\min Z = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij} \, x_{ij}
$$

**Step 5 — Supply Constraint:**

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} = a_i \quad \forall i
$$

**Step 6 — Demand Constraint:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{ij} = b_j \quad \forall j
$$

**Step 7 — Non-Negativity:**

$$
x_{ij} \geq 0 \quad \forall i, j
$$

**Application Context:**
The model assigns freight trips from multiple depots to multiple destinations such that the **total transport cost** is minimized while **all supply is dispatched** and **all demand is satisfied**.

#### (b) A transport company operates 3 depots supplying 4 destinations. The cost matrix (₹/ton), supply, and demand are given below. Find the **initial basic feasible solution** using the **North-West Corner Rule** and compute the total cost. **(7 marks)**

| | D1 (50) | D2 (80) | D3 (70) | D4 (100) | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| O1 (60) | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 | 60 |
| O2 (100) | 2 | 7 | 6 | 5 | 100 |
| O3 (140) | 9 | 3 | 5 | 4 | 140 |
| Demand | 50 | 80 | 70 | 100 | 300 |

**Model Solution:**

Total supply = Total demand = **300** → Balanced.

**North-West Corner Allocation:**

$$
x_{11} = \min(60, 50) = 50 \quad \Rightarrow \text{D1 done, O1 left } 10
$$

$$
x_{12} = \min(10, 80) = 10 \quad \Rightarrow \text{O1 done, D2 left } 70
$$

$$
x_{22} = \min(100, 70) = 70 \quad \Rightarrow \text{D2 done, O2 left } 30
$$

$$
x_{23} = \min(30, 70) = 30 \quad \Rightarrow \text{O2 done, D3 left } 40
$$

$$
x_{33} = \min(140, 40) = 40 \quad \Rightarrow \text{D3 done, O3 left } 100
$$

$$
x_{34} = \min(100, 100) = 100 \quad \Rightarrow \text{All done}
$$

**Allocations:**

| Cell | Value | Cost/ton | Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| O1-D1 | 50 | 4 | 200 |
| O1-D2 | 10 | 6 | 60 |
| O2-D2 | 70 | 7 | 490 |
| O2-D3 | 30 | 6 | 180 |
| O3-D3 | 40 | 5 | 200 |
| O3-D4 | 100 | 4 | 400 |
| **Total** | | | **1530** |

$$
Z_{IBFS} = ₹ \, 1{,}530
$$

**Note:** This is the *initial* solution; optimality tested via MODI/Stepping-Stone.

---

### **Question B — Option 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Derive the formula for **optimum fleet size** from peak demand. Explain with a numerical example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given peak daily demand $D_p$, vehicle capacity $C_v$, and trips per vehicle per day $\mu$.

**Step 1:** Per-vehicle daily capacity = $C_v \cdot \mu$

**Step 2:** Total capacity of $N$ vehicles = $N \cdot C_v \cdot \mu$

**Step 3:** Feasibility requires $N \cdot C_v \cdot \mu \geq D_p$

**Step 4:** Solving:

$$
N \geq \frac{D_p}{C_v \cdot \mu}
$$

**Step 5:** Since $N$ must be a positive integer:

$$
N^* = \left\lceil \frac{D_p}{C_v \cdot \mu} \right\rceil
$$

**Numerical Example:**
A carrier handles $D_p = 750$ tons/day peak freight. Each truck has capacity $C_v = 15$ tons and operates $\mu = 5$ trips/day. Find $N^*$.

$$
N^* = \left\lceil \frac{750}{15 \times 5} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{750}{75} \right\rceil = \lceil 10 \rceil = 10 \text{ trucks}
$$

**Interpretation:** A fleet of **10 trucks** is the minimum to handle peak demand. Owning an 11th vehicle would add fixed cost without proportional benefit.

#### (b) A logistics firm is comparing **owning vs hiring** a vehicle. Fixed cost of ownership = ₹ 2,00,000/month. Variable cost = ₹ 10/km. Hiring cost = ₹ 25/km. Determine the **break-even distance**. If monthly expected run = 15,000 km, recommend. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cost of Owning:**

$$
TC_{own} = 2{,}00{,}000 + 10 \cdot D
$$

**Step 2 — Cost of Hiring:**

$$
TC_{hire} = 25 \cdot D
$$

**Step 3 — Equate:**

$$
2{,}00{,}000 + 10 D = 25 D
$$

$$
2{,}00{,}000 = 15 D
$$

**Step 4 — Break-Even:**

$$
D_{be} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{15} = 13{,}333.33 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**

Since expected run **15,000 km > 13,333 km**, the recommendation is to **OWN** the vehicle.

**Verification:**
- $TC_{own} = 2{,}00{,}000 + 10(15{,}000) = ₹ 3{,}50{,}000$
- $TC_{hire} = 25 \times 15{,}000 = ₹ 3{,}75{,}000$
- **Saving by owning = ₹ 25,000/month** ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the ceiling function** in fleet sizing — writing $N = 5.4$ instead of $N^* = 6$ loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing units** — using ton-km and tonne-km interchangeably confuses the examiner. Be consistent.
> 3. **Not stating "balanced problem"** explicitly before solving a transportation problem — examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 4. **Ignoring integrality** in VRP — students often write $x_{ij} \geq 0$ when $x_{ij} \in \{0,1\}$ is required.
> 5. **Skipping economic interpretation** after numerical solution — always end with the *managerial decision*.
> 6. **Confusing fixed cost with variable cost** in the hire-vs-own derivation — list every component explicitly.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 15 Things to Remember for Fleet Optimization (Module 4)**
>
> 1. Fleet optimization has **4 layers**: Strategic → Tactical → Operational → Performance.
> 2. **Optimum fleet size** = $\lceil D_p \div (C_v \cdot \mu) \rceil$.
> 3. **Break-even distance** = $C_f \div (C_h - C_v)$ — used in hire-vs-own.
> 4. **Load factor** $\lambda$ = actual payload / rated capacity.
> 5. **Vehicle utilization** $\eta_v$ = loaded km / total km.
> 6. **Empty running ratio** $r_e$ should be below **20 %** for a healthy fleet.
> 7. **TCO** = fixed + variable + time + loading + penalty cost.
> 8. **Cost per ton-km** $C_{tk}$ is the master KPI for goods fleets.
> 9. **Transportation LP** is the canonical tool for trip assignment.
> 10. **North-West Corner Rule** generates IBFS for transport problems.
> 11. **MODI / Stepping-Stone** tests optimality.
> 12. **VRP** is **NP-hard**; binary variables $x_{ijk} \in \{0,1\}$.
> 13. **Heuristics** — Clarke-Wright, Sweep, Nearest Neighbor — used in real-world routing.
> 14. **Capacity constraint** in CVRP: $\sum_i q_i y_{ik} \leq Q$ for each vehicle $k$.
> 15. Always interpret the **economic decision** (own vs hire, add/remove vehicle, consolidate route) after solving.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Route Optimization and Load Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT633 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Route Optimization and Load Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Route Optimization — Formal Academic Definition

**Route Optimization** in fleet management is the process of determining the most cost-effective, time-efficient, and operationally feasible sequence of stops (nodes/customer locations) for a fleet of vehicles operating from one or more depots, subject to a defined set of operational constraints (vehicle capacity, time windows, service durations, driver hours, and regulatory limits). Mathematically, it is a constrained combinatorial optimization problem most often formulated as a **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)** — a generalization of the classical **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Route Optimization is the application of mathematical and heuristic algorithms to determine the optimal allocation of delivery or pickup requests among available vehicles and the sequence in which these requests should be serviced, minimizing total distance, time, fuel consumption, and vehicle wear while respecting real-world constraints.

## 1.2 Load Planning — Formal Academic Definition

**Load Planning** (also termed *Load Optimization* or *Cargo Planning*) is the engineering and operational discipline of arranging cargo, freight, or passengers within a vehicle's usable volume and payload envelope such that (a) the **Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW)** and individual **axle load limits** are not violated, (b) the **center of gravity** of the load remains within prescribed stability limits, and (c) **load utilization** (both weight-based and volumetric) is maximized.

The load planning objective function is commonly expressed as:

$$\text{Maximize } U = w_1 \cdot U_w + w_2 \cdot U_v$$

where $U_w$ is the weight utilization ratio, $U_v$ is the volumetric (cube) utilization ratio, and $w_1, w_2$ are normalized weighting factors ($w_1 + w_2 = 1$).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **delivery dispatch manager in Kochi** who must dispatch **8 delivery trucks** to serve **25 customers** spread across the city by **6 PM**. If you do this manually — like drawing lines on a map — you will likely waste hours, burn excess diesel, miss delivery slots, and overload some trucks while under-loading others.

**Route Optimization** is the GPS-brain that says: *"For each of your 8 trucks, here is the mathematically shortest tour that visits each assigned customer exactly once and returns to the depot, never exceeding 2 hours of driving per route."*

**Load Planning** is the loading supervisor who says: *"On Truck 3, place the 850 kg washing machine at the bottom-center (lowest CG point), then stack the lighter 40 kg carton boxes symmetrically on top. Total weight = 1,800 kg; well within the 2,500 kg payload limit. Floor space used = 92%."*

Together, they convert a chaotic logistics problem into a streamlined, predictable, and economical operation — saving **15–25% in total distribution cost**, a benchmark widely cited in KTU-aligned logistics literature.

## 1.4 KTU Key Terms and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are **high-frequency in KTU examinations**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential Route Optimization & Load Planning Metrics (Memorize for KTU)**
> - **Total Distance Travelled (TDT):** Sum of all km driven by the fleet.
> - **Vehicle Fill Rate (VFR):** Percentage of payload capacity utilized.
> - **Drop Size (DS):** Number of customers serviced per route.
> - **Time Window (TW):** Customer-specified delivery/pickup interval.
> - **Service Time (ST):** Fixed time spent at each stop.
> - **Payload:** Maximum allowable cargo weight = GVW − Kerb Weight.
> - **Axle Load Limit (ALL):** Regulatory maximum per axle.
> - **Cube Utilization (CU):** Volume of cargo ÷ Usable cargo volume.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Routing Problems

The Vehicle Routing Problem family is a hierarchy of NP-hard combinatorial optimization problems. KTU frequently expects students to identify the correct VRP variant from a problem statement.

| Variant | Acronym | Defining Constraint | KTU Real-World Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| Travelling Salesman Problem | **TSP** | Single vehicle, visit all nodes once | Single courier, multiple deliveries |
| Capacitated VRP | **CVRP** | Vehicle capacity (weight/volume) constraint | Bakery distribution vans |
| VRP with Time Windows | **VRPTW** | Each customer has a TW | E-commerce same-day delivery |
| VRP with Pickup and Delivery | **VRPPD** | Goods picked up and delivered | Reverse logistics / rentals |
| Open VRP | **OVRP** | Vehicles do NOT return to depot | One-way rentals, taxi drop-offs |
| VRP with Backhauls | **VRPB** | Delivery on outbound, pickup on return | Grocery replenishment cycles |

## 2.2 Mathematical Formulation of the Classic CVRP

The Capacitated VRP is the foundation of almost all real-world fleet routing. Let us define the sets, parameters, and decision variables precisely.

### 2.2.1 Sets and Indices

- $N = \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ — set of all nodes, where $0$ is the depot and $\{1, \ldots, n\}$ are customers.
- $K = \{1, 2, \ldots, m\}$ — set of available vehicles.
- $A = \{(i, j) \mid i, j \in N, i \ne j\}$ — set of all possible arcs (directed edges).

### 2.2.2 Parameters

- $d_{ij}$ — distance (or cost) from node $i$ to node $j$.
- $q_i$ — demand of customer $i$ (in kg or units).
- $Q$ — homogeneous vehicle capacity.
- $t_{ij}$ — travel time from node $i$ to node $j$.

### 2.2.3 Decision Variables

$$x_{ijk} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if vehicle } k \text{ travels arc } (i, j) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

$$y_{ik} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if customer } i \text{ is served by vehicle } k \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### 2.2.4 Objective Function and Constraints

$$\text{Minimize } Z = \sum_{k \in K} \sum_{(i, j) \in A} d_{ij} \cdot x_{ijk}$$

Subject to:

$$\sum_{k \in K} y_{ik} = 1 \quad \forall i \in \{1, \ldots, n\} \quad \text{(Each customer served exactly once)}$$

$$\sum_{i \in N} q_i \cdot y_{ik} \le Q \quad \forall k \in K \quad \text{(Vehicle capacity)}$$

$$\sum_{j \in N} x_{ijk} = \sum_{j \in N} x_{jik} = y_{ik} \quad \forall i \in N, \forall k \in K \quad \text{(Flow conservation)}$$

$$\sum_{j \in N} x_{0jk} = 1 \quad \forall k \in K \quad \text{(Each vehicle leaves depot once)}$$

$$x_{ijk} \in \{0, 1\}, \quad y_{ik} \in \{0, 1\}$$

## 2.3 Load Planning Engineering Framework

Load planning is governed by three interacting constraints that KTU examiners frequently test:

### 2.3.1 Weight Constraint

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \le W_{payload} = W_{GVW} - W_{kerb}$$

where $W_i$ is the weight of item $i$, $W_{GVW}$ is the gross vehicle weight rating, and $W_{kerb}$ is the unladen vehicle weight.

### 2.3.2 Axle Load Constraint

$$W_{front\,axle} \le ALL_{front}, \quad W_{rear\,axle} \le ALL_{rear}$$

### 2.3.3 Volumetric Constraint

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i \le V_{cargo\,bay}$$

### 2.3.4 Center-of-Gravity (Stability) Constraint

The longitudinal center of gravity $x_{cg}$ from the front axle must lie within a stability envelope. For a truck with two axles, wheelbase $L$:

$$x_{cg} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot x_i}{W_{total}}$$

For stability:

$$0.45 \cdot L \le x_{cg} \le 0.55 \cdot L$$

(Industry rule: CG should be near the geometric center of the wheelbase.)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning / Use | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z = \sum d_{ij} \cdot x_{ijk}$ | CVRP objective — minimize total distance | km |
| 2 | $S_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$ | Clarke-Wright Savings (merging two routes) | km |
| 3 | $VFR = \dfrac{W_{loaded}}{W_{payload}} \times 100\%$ | Vehicle Fill Rate (weight) | % |
| 4 | $VFR_v = \dfrac{V_{loaded}}{V_{cargo}} \times 100\%$ | Volumetric Fill Rate | % |
| 5 | $W_{payload} = W_{GVW} - W_{kerb}$ | Maximum legal payload | kg |
| 6 | $x_{cg} = \dfrac{\sum W_i x_i}{\sum W_i}$ | Longitudinal CG position | m |
| 7 | $d_{ij} = \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2}$ | Euclidean distance | km |
| 8 | $T_{route} = \sum t_{ij} \cdot x_{ijk} + \sum ST_i$ | Total route time | hours |
| 9 | $\text{Fuel} = \alpha \cdot TDT + \beta \cdot \text{idle}$ | Approximate fuel use | litres |
| 10 | $n_{vehicles} \ge \left\lceil \dfrac{\sum q_i}{Q} \right\rceil$ | Minimum fleet size lower bound | vehicles |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The Clarke-Wright savings value $S_{ij}$ can be **negative**. When $S_{ij} \le 0$, merging the two routes $0 \to i$ and $0 \to j$ into $i \to j$ would **increase** the total distance — never merge in this case.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Route Optimization and Load Planning are foundational to:
- **Last-mile delivery** (Amazon, Flipkart, Swiggy Instamart)
- **Cold-chain logistics** (vaccine distribution, dairy, seafood)
- **Public transit scheduling** (KSRTC bus route rationalization)
- **Waste collection** (Municipal Corporation of Kochi/Trivandrum)
- **Military logistics** (convoy planning under threat)
- **Ride-sharing dispatch** (Ola, Uber, Rapido dynamic routing)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Clarke-Wright Savings Algorithm (Manual Solution)

This is the **most heavily tested** algorithm in KTU's Vehicle Transport module. We will solve a complete problem step-by-step.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A depot (Node 0) serves 4 customers (Nodes 1, 2, 3, 4). The following symmetric distance matrix $d_{ij}$ (in km) is given. Each vehicle has capacity $Q = 100$ units. Customer demands are $q_1 = 20$, $q_2 = 30$, $q_3 = 25$, $q_4 = 15$. Find the optimal set of routes using the Clarke-Wright Savings Method.

| From \ To | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | 0 | 40 | 60 | 75 | 90 |
| **1** | 40 | 0 | 20 | 30 | 50 |
| **2** | 60 | 20 | 0 | 25 | 40 |
| **3** | 75 | 30 | 25 | 0 | 35 |
| **4** | 90 | 50 | 40 | 35 | 0 |

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Initial Solution (One Route Per Customer)

Without optimization, each customer is served by a dedicated round trip:

| Route | Path | Distance (km) | Load |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 → 1 → 0 | 40 + 40 = 80 | 20 |
| B | 0 → 2 → 0 | 60 + 60 = 120 | 30 |
| C | 0 → 3 → 0 | 75 + 75 = 150 | 25 |
| D | 0 → 4 → 0 | 90 + 90 = 180 | 15 |

**Total Initial Distance = 80 + 120 + 150 + 180 = 530 km**

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Compute All Pairwise Savings

The savings formula is:

$$S_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$$

We compute all $\binom{4}{2} = 6$ pair savings:

$$S_{12} = d_{01} + d_{02} - d_{12} = 40 + 60 - 20 = 80 \text{ km}$$

$$S_{13} = 40 + 75 - 30 = 85 \text{ km}$$

$$S_{14} = 40 + 90 - 50 = 80 \text{ km}$$

$$S_{23} = 60 + 75 - 25 = 110 \text{ km}$$

$$S_{24} = 60 + 90 - 40 = 110 \text{ km}$$

$$S_{34} = 75 + 90 - 35 = 130 \text{ km}$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Sort Savings in Descending Order

| Rank | Pair | Savings $S_{ij}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | (3, 4) | 130 |
| 2 | (2, 3) | 110 |
| 3 | (2, 4) | 110 |
| 4 | (1, 3) | 85 |
| 5 | (1, 2) | 80 |
| 6 | (1, 4) | 80 |

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Iteratively Merge Routes (Capacity Check)

**Merging Rule:** Two customers $i$ and $j$ can be linked on the same route only if:
(a) Neither $i$ nor $j$ is interior to an existing route (only endpoints can be merged).
(b) The combined load does not exceed $Q = 100$.
(c) The resulting route does not contain a sub-tour.

**Iteration 1:** Merge $(3, 4)$ with savings 130.
- Current load = $q_3 + q_4 = 25 + 15 = 40 \le 100$ ✓
- Neither is interior yet ✓
- New route: $0 \to 3 \to 4 \to 0$, distance = $75 + 35 + 90 = 200$ km
- Remaining routes: $A(1)$, $B(2)$, $C(3\text{-}4)$. Total distance so far = $80 + 120 + 200 = 400$ km

**Iteration 2:** Try $(2, 3)$ with savings 110.
- Customer 3 is now interior to route C — **cannot merge** as endpoint.
- Skip.

**Iteration 3:** Try $(2, 4)$ with savings 110.
- Customer 4 is an endpoint of route C ✓
- Customer 2 is isolated ✓
- Combined load = $q_2 + q_3 + q_4 = 30 + 25 + 15 = 70 \le 100$ ✓
- Merge → New route: $0 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 0$
- Distance = $60 + 25 + 35 + 90 = 210$ km
- Remaining: $A(1)$, $C(2\text{-}3\text{-}4)$. Total = $80 + 210 = 290$ km

**Iteration 4:** Try $(1, 3)$ with savings 85.
- Customer 3 is interior — **cannot merge**.

**Iteration 5:** Try $(1, 2)$ with savings 80.
- Customer 2 is endpoint of route C ✓
- Customer 1 is isolated ✓
- Combined load = $q_1 + q_2 + q_3 + q_4 = 20 + 30 + 25 + 15 = 90 \le 100$ ✓
- Merge → New route: $0 \to 1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 0$
- Distance = $40 + 20 + 25 + 35 + 90 = 210$ km
- **Final: Single route, total distance = 210 km**

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Result Summary

| Metric | Before Optimization | After Optimization | Savings |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Distance | 530 km | 210 km | **320 km (60.4%)** |
| Number of Routes | 4 | 1 | — |
| Vehicle Utilization | — | 90% | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When two savings values are equal (e.g., $S_{23} = S_{24} = 110$ in this example), the tie-breaking rule is **not standardized**. State your chosen convention explicitly. Common conventions: alphabetical order of node index, larger combined demand first, or smallest marginal cost.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Load Planning and Center of Gravity

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A delivery truck has the following specifications:
- Kerb weight = 3,500 kg
- GVW = 8,500 kg
- Wheelbase $L = 4.0$ m
- Front axle load limit = 4,000 kg
- Rear axle load limit = 6,000 kg

Four cargo items must be loaded onto the flatbed. Determine if the loading arrangement satisfies all constraints.

| Item | Mass (kg) | Distance from front axle (m) |
|---|---|---|
| Pallet A | 1,200 | 1.0 |
| Pallet B | 800 | 2.0 |
| Pallet C | 1,500 | 3.0 |
| Pallet D | 600 | 3.5 |

### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Total Weight Check

$$W_{total} = W_{kerb} + \sum W_i = 3500 + (1200 + 800 + 1500 + 600) = 7600 \text{ kg}$$

$$W_{total} = 7600 \text{ kg} \le GVW = 8500 \text{ kg} \quad \checkmark$$

Payload used = $4100$ kg, payload capacity = $5000$ kg → **Vehicle Fill Rate** = $4100/5000 = 82\%$.

### 3.2.3 Step 2 — Center of Gravity Calculation

$$x_{cg,total} = \frac{W_{kerb} \cdot x_{kerb} + \sum W_i \cdot x_i}{W_{total}}$$

Assuming the kerb weight CG is at the wheelbase center ($x_{kerb} = 2.0$ m):

$$x_{cg,total} = \frac{3500(2.0) + 1200(1.0) + 800(2.0) + 1500(3.0) + 600(3.5)}{7600}$$

Numerator:

$$\begin{aligned}
3500 \times 2.0 &= 7000 \\
1200 \times 1.0 &= 1200 \\
800 \times 2.0 &= 1600 \\
1500 \times 3.0 &= 4500 \\
600 \times 3.5 &= 2100 \\
\text{Sum} &= 16400
\end{aligned}$$

$$x_{cg,total} = \frac{16400}{7600} = 2.158 \text{ m}$$

### 3.2.4 Step 3 — Stability Check

Acceptable range: $0.45L \le x_{cg} \le 0.55L$

$$\begin{aligned}
0.45 \times 4.0 &= 1.80 \text{ m} \\
0.55 \times 4.0 &= 2.20 \text{ m} \\
1.80 \le 2.158 \le 2.20 \quad &\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2.5 Step 4 — Axle Load Distribution

Assuming $x_{cg} = 2.158$ m and $L = 4.0$ m, and ignoring the small effect of CG height:

$$W_{rear} = \frac{W_{total} \cdot x_{cg}}{L} = \frac{7600 \times 2.158}{4.0} = 4100.2 \text{ kg}$$

$$W_{front} = W_{total} - W_{rear} = 7600 - 4100.2 = 3499.8 \text{ kg}$$

**Verification:**
- $W_{front} = 3499.8 \le 4000$ kg ✓
- $W_{rear} = 4100.2 \le 6000$ kg ✓

### 3.2.6 Conclusion

All three constraints (weight, stability, axle load) are satisfied. The load plan is **feasible** with **82% weight utilization**. For even better stability, Pallet C (1500 kg) could be moved slightly toward $x = 2.5$ m, shifting the CG closer to 2.0 m.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Algorithm | Type | Time Complexity | Best Use Case | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Clarke-Wright Savings** | Heuristic | $O(n^2 \log n)$ | Single depot, balanced loads | Very High |
| **Nearest Neighbor** | Constructive heuristic | $O(n^2)$ | Quick baseline TSP solution | High |
| **2-Opt / 3-Opt** | Local search improvement | $O(n^2)$ to $O(n^3)$ | Route polishing | Medium |
| **Dijkstra's Algorithm** | Exact shortest path | $O(V^2)$ or $O(E \log V)$ | Single-source shortest path | High |
| **Floyd-Warshall** | Exact all-pairs | $O(V^3)$ | All-pairs shortest path | Medium |
| **Genetic Algorithm** | Metaheuristic | Problem-dependent | Large-scale, near-optimal | Medium |
| **Sweep Algorithm** | Geometric heuristic | $O(n \log n)$ | Clustered customer geography | Low-Medium |

## 4.1 Route Optimization Process Flow

The following flowchart captures the canonical KTU-aligned route optimization pipeline from data ingestion to dispatch.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G{Choose Solution Method}
    G -->|Exact| H[Branch and Bound or Integer Programming]
    G -->|Heuristic| I[Clarke-Wright Savings]
    G -->|Metaheuristic| J[Genetic Algorithm or Tabu Search]
    H --> K[Validate Constraints]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Capacity Check]

    K --> N[Driver Hours Check]
    L --> O[Generate Optimized Route Plan]

    N --> O

```

## 4.2 Clarke-Wright Savings Algorithm Block Architecture

This block diagram depicts the internal logic of the savings-based heuristic from initialization to convergence.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Initialize Read Distance Matrix and Demands] --> B0[Create Initial Routes One Customer per Trip]
    B0 --> C0[Compute All Pairwise Savings Sij]
    C0 --> D0[Sort Savings in Descending Order]
    D0 --> E0[Pick Highest Unused Saving Sij]
    E0 --> F0{Check Merge Feasibility}
    F0 -->|Capacity Violated| E0
    F0 -->|Interior Node Conflict| E0
    F0 -->|Subtour Risk| E0
    F0 -->|Feasible| G0[Merge Routes i and j]
    G0 --> H0[Update Route Distance and Load]
    H0 --> I0{More Savings to Test}
    I0 -->|Yes| E0
    I0 -->|No| J0[Output Final Route Set]
```

The load planning subsystem interfaces with the routing module and vehicle telemetry to ensure both operational feasibility and regulatory compliance.


# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP). List any four variants of VRP with one-line descriptions.**

**Model Answer:**

The Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) is a combinatorial optimization problem that seeks to determine the optimal set of routes for a fleet of vehicles to service a set of customers from one or more depots, minimizing total transportation cost while satisfying operational constraints such as vehicle capacity, time windows, and service durations.

**Four Variants:**
1. **TSP (Travelling Salesman Problem):** Single vehicle visits all customers once and returns to depot — no capacity constraint.
2. **CVRP (Capacitated VRP):** Each vehicle has a maximum carrying capacity constraint.
3. **VRPTW (VRP with Time Windows):** Each customer must be serviced within a specified time interval.
4. **VRPB (VRP with Backhauls):** Outbound route delivers goods; return route picks up goods from customers.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the concept of Vehicle Fill Rate (VFR). Why is it considered a key KPI in fleet load planning?**

**Model Answer:**

**Vehicle Fill Rate (VFR)** is the ratio of the actual load carried by a vehicle to its maximum allowable payload (weight or volume), expressed as a percentage.

$$VFR = \frac{W_{loaded}}{W_{payload}} \times 100\% \quad \text{or} \quad VFR_v = \frac{V_{loaded}}{V_{cargo}} \times 100\%$$

**Why VFR is a Key KPI:**
- **Cost Efficiency:** Low VFR means more trips and higher per-unit transportation cost.
- **Emissions Tracking:** Poorly loaded trucks waste fuel per ton-km, increasing carbon footprint.
- **Asset Utilization:** Higher VFR means better return on fleet capital investment.
- **Operational Benchmarking:** Allows comparison across routes, drivers, and depots.
- **Load Planning Feedback:** Identifies opportunities to consolidate shipments or right-size vehicles.

A healthy VFR target in the Indian trucking sector is **75–90%** by weight and **80–95%** by volume.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In Part B numerical problems, students frequently lose marks by (1) failing to explicitly state the Clarke-Wright savings formula before substitution, (2) not verifying the **capacity constraint** before merging routes, (3) not mentioning that endpoints alone can be merged (interior nodes cannot), and (4) failing to compute the **percentage savings vs. initial solution**. Always show the formula, the substitution, the numerical result, and a one-line interpretation.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A depot must serve 5 customers with demands $q_1 = 10, q_2 = 15, q_3 = 20, q_4 = 25, q_5 = 30$ kg. The distance matrix from depot (0) is:

| From \ To | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | 0 | 20 | 30 | 50 | 35 | 45 |
| **1** | 20 | 0 | 15 | 25 | 40 | 30 |
| **2** | 30 | 15 | 0 | 20 | 25 | 35 |
| **3** | 50 | 25 | 20 | 0 | 30 | 40 |
| **4** | 35 | 40 | 25 | 30 | 0 | 15 |
| **5** | 45 | 30 | 35 | 40 | 15 | 0 |

Vehicle capacity $Q = 50$ kg. Solve using the **Clarke-Wright Savings Method** and report the optimized routes with total distance.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Step 1: Initial Solution (Dedicated Trips)**

| Route | Path | Distance (km) | Load (kg) |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 → 1 → 0 | 20 + 20 = 40 | 10 |
| B | 0 → 2 → 0 | 30 + 30 = 60 | 15 |
| C | 0 → 3 → 0 | 50 + 50 = 100 | 20 |
| D | 0 → 4 → 0 | 35 + 35 = 70 | 25 |
| E | 0 → 5 → 0 | 45 + 45 = 90 | 30 |

**Total Initial Distance = 360 km**

**Step 2: Compute Savings $S_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$**

$$S_{12} = 20 + 30 - 15 = 35 \text{ km}$$
$$S_{13} = 20 + 50 - 25 = 45 \text{ km}$$
$$S_{14} = 20 + 35 - 40 = 15 \text{ km}$$
$$S_{15} = 20 + 45 - 30 = 35 \text{ km}$$
$$S_{23} = 30 + 50 - 20 = 60 \text{ km}$$
$$S_{24} = 30 + 35 - 25 = 40 \text{ km}$$
$$S_{25} = 30 + 45 - 35 = 40 \text{ km}$$
$$S_{34} = 50 + 35 - 30 = 55 \text{ km}$$
$$S_{35} = 50 + 45 - 40 = 55 \text{ km}$$
$$S_{45} = 35 + 45 - 15 = 65 \text{ km}$$

**Step 3: Rank Savings (Descending)**

| Rank | Pair | $S_{ij}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | (4,5) | 65 |
| 2 | (2,3) | 60 |
| 3 | (3,4) | 55 |
| 4 | (3,5) | 55 |
| 5 | (1,3) | 45 |
| 6 | (2,4) | 40 |
| 7 | (2,5) | 40 |
| 8 | (1,2) | 35 |
| 9 | (1,5) | 35 |
| 10 | (1,4) | 15 |

**Step 4: Iterative Merging**

**Iteration 1:** Merge (4,5), savings 65.
- Load = $25 + 30 = 55 > Q = 50$ ✗ **Capacity Violation**
- **Skip.**

**Iteration 2:** Merge (2,3), savings 60.
- Load = $15 + 20 = 35 \le 50$ ✓
- New Route: $0 \to 2 \to 3 \to 0$, distance = $30 + 20 + 50 = 100$ km.

**Iteration 3:** Merge (3,4), savings 55.
- 3 is now interior to route (2,3) — cannot merge as endpoint.
- **Skip.**

**Iteration 4:** Merge (3,5), savings 55.
- 3 is interior — **Skip.**

**Iteration 5:** Merge (1,3), savings 45.
- 3 is interior — **Skip.**

**Iteration 6:** Merge (2,4), savings 40.
- 2 is endpoint of route (2,3); 4 is isolated.
- Combined load = $15 + 20 + 25 = 60 > 50$ ✗ **Capacity Violation**
- **Skip.**

**Iteration 7:** Merge (2,5), savings 40.
- 2 is endpoint of route (2,3); 5 is isolated.
- Combined load = $15 + 20 + 30 = 65 > 50$ ✗ **Capacity Violation**
- **Skip.**

**Iteration 8:** Merge (1,2), savings 35.
- 2 is endpoint of route (2,3); 1 is isolated.
- Combined load = $15 + 20 + 10 = 45 \le 50$ ✓
- New Route: $0 \to 1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 0$, distance = $20 + 15 + 20 + 50 = 105$ km.

**Iteration 9:** Merge (1,5), savings 35.
- 1 is now interior to route (1,2,3) — **Skip.**

**Iteration 10:** Merge (1,4), savings 15.
- 1 is interior — **Skip.**

**Final Routes:**

| Route | Path | Distance (km) | Load (kg) | VFR |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 → 1 → 2 → 3 → 0 | 105 | 45 | 90% |
| 2 | 0 → 4 → 0 | 70 | 25 | 50% |
| 3 | 0 → 5 → 0 | 90 | 30 | 60% |

**Total Optimized Distance = 105 + 70 + 90 = 265 km**
**Savings vs. Initial = 360 − 265 = 95 km (26.4% reduction)**

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of **Load Planning** in ensuring vehicle stability. With a neat sketch, describe the concept of the **center of gravity (CG)** and explain how improper load distribution affects handling, braking efficiency, and tyre wear.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Role of Load Planning in Vehicle Stability:**

Load planning ensures that cargo is positioned such that the vehicle's **center of gravity (CG)** remains within prescribed longitudinal and lateral limits. A well-executed load plan directly impacts:

1. **Handling and Steering Response:**
   - A CG positioned too far forward reduces steering feedback and causes understeer.
   - A CG positioned too far rearward makes the rear axle light, causing oversteer (rear-wheel skid) — particularly dangerous in trucks on wet Kerala monsoon roads.

2. **Braking Efficiency:**
   - During emergency braking, weight transfers forward. A high rearward CG exacerbates this, lifting the rear axle and risking loss of rear-wheel braking and jackknifing (in articulated vehicles).
   - Optimal CG allows balanced front-rear braking force utilization.

3. **Tyre Wear:**
   - Uneven axle loading (e.g., 70% on one axle) causes premature and asymmetric tyre wear.
   - Reduces tyre life by 20–40% under chronic overload conditions.

4. **Rollover Risk:**
   - The Static Stability Factor (SSF) of a vehicle is $SSF = T/2h$, where $T$ is the track width and $h$ is the CG height. Higher CG = lower SSF = higher rollover probability.

**Concept of Center of Gravity (CG):**

The center of gravity of a loaded vehicle is the point at which the entire weight of the vehicle plus cargo can be assumed to act vertically downward.

**Sketch (ASCII representation — KTU students should reproduce as a clean labelled diagram):**

```
    Front Axle                                Rear Axle
        |                                          |
        ●—————————————————L=4.0m——————————————●
        |                                          |
        |     ↓ W_kerb @ x = 2.0m                  |
        |     ↓ W_cargo @ x = various              |
        |                                          |
        |          ● x_cg = 2.16 m                 |
        |          |                               |
        |          ↓ W_total                       |
        |__________________________________________|
                        Chassis
```

**Equations:**

$$x_{cg} = \frac{\sum W_i x_i}{\sum W_i}$$

**Acceptable CG Envelope:**

$$0.45L \le x_{cg} \le 0.55L$$

**Practical Recommendations:**
- Place the **heaviest items lowest and nearest to the longitudinal center** of the wheelbase.
- Distribute cargo **symmetrically** about the vehicle centerline to prevent lateral instability.
- **Secure all items** to prevent shifting during braking or cornering (kinetic CG migration).
- **Re-plan the load** whenever partial unloading occurs at intermediate stops.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a clear mathematical formulation, explain the **Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP)**. Define all sets, parameters, and decision variables used in your model. Discuss why CVRP is classified as an NP-hard problem.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Mathematical Formulation of CVRP:**

**Sets:**
- $N = \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, n\}$: set of nodes, where node 0 is the depot and nodes $\{1, \ldots, n\}$ are customers.
- $K = \{1, 2, \ldots, m\}$: set of homogeneous vehicles, each with capacity $Q$.
- $A = \{(i, j) \mid i, j \in N, i \ne j\}$: set of arcs (directions between nodes).

**Parameters:**
- $d_{ij}$: distance (or travel cost) from node $i$ to node $j$.
- $q_i$: demand at customer $i$ (assumed $\le Q$).
- $Q$: vehicle capacity.
- $t_{ij}$: travel time from node $i$ to node $j$.

**Decision Variables:**

$$x_{ijk} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if vehicle } k \text{ traverses arc } (i, j) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

$$y_{ik} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if customer } i \text{ is assigned to vehicle } k \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Objective Function:**

$$\text{Minimize } Z = \sum_{k \in K} \sum_{(i, j) \in A} d_{ij} \cdot x_{ijk}$$

**Constraints:**

1. **Each customer served exactly once:**
$$\sum_{k \in K} y_{ik} = 1 \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$$

2. **Vehicle capacity not exceeded:**
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i \cdot y_{ik} \le Q \quad \forall k \in K$$

3. **Flow conservation at each customer node:**
$$\sum_{j \in N} x_{ijk} = \sum_{j \in N} x_{jik} = y_{ik} \quad \forall i \in \{1, \ldots, n\}, \forall k \in K$$

4. **Each vehicle leaves the depot exactly once:**
$$\sum_{j \in N} x_{0jk} = 1 \quad \forall k \in K$$

5. **Each vehicle returns to the depot exactly once:**
$$\sum_{i \in N} x_{i0k} = 1 \quad \forall k \in K$$

6. **Binary restrictions:**
$$x_{ijk} \in \{0, 1\}, \quad y_{ik} \in \{0, 1\}$$

7. **Subtour elimination (MTZ formulation):**
$$u_i - u_j + n \cdot x_{ijk} \le n - 1 \quad \forall i, j \in \{1, \ldots, n\}, i \ne j, \forall k \in K$$

where $u_i$ are auxiliary variables representing the visit order.

**Why CVRP is NP-Hard:**

CVRP is classified as **NP-hard** for the following reasons:

1. **Generalization of TSP:** When $m = 1$ (single vehicle) and $Q = \infty$, CVRP reduces to the classical TSP, which is a well-known NP-hard problem. Since CVRP is a strict generalization, it inherits (and in fact exceeds) the complexity of TSP.

2. **Combinatorial Explosion:** The number of possible assignments is $\frac{(n-1)!}{(n/m-1)! \cdot m!} \cdot \text{permutations}$, which grows factorially. For $n = 20$ customers and $m = 4$ vehicles, this exceeds $10^{15}$ feasible solutions.

3. **No known polynomial-time exact algorithm:** All known exact algorithms (Branch-and-Bound, Branch-and-Cut, Dynamic Programming) have exponential worst-case complexity.

4. **Practical resolution uses heuristics/metaheuristics:** Clarke-Wright Savings, Sweep Algorithm, Genetic Algorithms, Tabu Search, and Simulated Annealing are employed because exact methods are intractable for $n > 50$ customers.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Nearest Neighbor Heuristic** for solving the TSP. Apply it to the following 5-city distance matrix (city 1 is the start/depot) and determine the route and total distance:

| From \ To | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | 0 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 |
| **2** | 10 | 0 | 12 | 18 | 22 |
| **3** | 15 | 12 | 0 | 8 | 14 |
| **4** | 20 | 18 | 8 | 0 | 6 |
| **5** | 25 | 22 | 14 | 6 | 0 |

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Nearest Neighbor Heuristic — Algorithm Description:**

The Nearest Neighbor (NN) heuristic is a simple constructive procedure for the TSP:
1. Start at the depot (starting city).
2. From the current city, visit the unvisited city with the shortest distance.
3. Repeat step 2 until all cities are visited.
4. Return to the depot from the last visited city.

**Step-by-Step Application:**

**Step 1:** At city 1 (depot), the distances to unvisited cities are:
- $d_{12} = 10$, $d_{13} = 15$, $d_{14} = 20$, $d_{15} = 25$
- **Nearest = City 2** (distance 10)

**Step 2:** At city 2, distances to unvisited cities:
- $d_{23} = 12$, $d_{24} = 18$, $d_{25} = 22$
- **Nearest = City 3** (distance 12)

**Step 3:** At city 3, distances to unvisited cities:
- $d_{34} = 8$, $d_{35} = 14$
- **Nearest = City 4** (distance 8)

**Step 4:** At city 4, only one unvisited city remains:
- $d_{45} = 6$
- **Move to City 5** (distance 6)

**Step 5:** All cities visited; return to depot:
- $d_{51} = 25$

**Resulting Tour:**

$$1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 5 \to 1$$

**Total Distance Calculation:**

$$Z = d_{12} + d_{23} + d_{34} + d_{45} + d_{51}$$

$$Z = 10 + 12 + 8 + 6 + 25 = 61 \text{ units}$$

**Discussion:**

The Nearest Neighbor heuristic is **fast** ($O(n^2)$) and produces a feasible tour quickly. However, it is a **greedy** algorithm and does not guarantee optimality. In this example, the greedy choice from city 4 to city 5 (cost 6) was excellent, but the return to city 1 (cost 25) is expensive because the tour ends far from the depot.

**Improvement Possibility:**
- Running NN from each of the 5 cities as a starting point and selecting the best tour.
- Applying a **2-opt local search** post-hoc to remove crossing edges.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse the **return arc** distance with the outbound distance. In a symmetric TSP, $d_{ij} = d_{ji}$, so this matters less, but in an **asymmetric TSP (ATSP)**, $d_{ij} \ne d_{ji}$ and you must use the correct directional value. Always check problem symmetry.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and review before every KTU exam on this module.**

**Core Concepts (Memorize Definitions):**
- ☐ Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) — generalization of TSP with multiple vehicles.
- ☐ Capacitated VRP (CVRP) — adds vehicle capacity constraint.
- ☐ Time Windows (VRPTW) — each customer has a service time interval.
- ☐ Backhauls (VRPB) — pickup on return leg.
- ☐ Open VRP (OVRP) — vehicles do not return to depot.
- ☐ Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) — single-vehicle VRP special case.

**Key Algorithms (Know Procedure + When to Use):**
- ☐ Clarke-Wright Savings Method — most common KTU heuristic.
- ☐ Nearest Neighbor Heuristic — fast baseline TSP solver.
- ☐ Sweep Algorithm — geometric clustering for geographically clustered customers.
- ☐ 2-Opt / 3-Opt — local search improvement of an existing tour.
- ☐ Dijkstra's Algorithm — single-source shortest path.
- ☐ Floyd-Warshall — all-pairs shortest path.

**Essential Formulas (Be Able to Write From Memory):**
- ☐ Clarke-Wright Savings: $S_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$
- ☐ Center of Gravity: $x_{cg} = \dfrac{\sum W_i x_i}{\sum W_i}$
- ☐ Payload: $W_{payload} = W_{GVW} - W_{kerb}$
- ☐ Vehicle Fill Rate: $VFR = \dfrac{W_{loaded}}{W_{payload}} \times 100\%$
- ☐ Axle Load: $W_{rear} = \dfrac{W_{total} \cdot x_{cg}}{L}$
- ☐ Euclidean Distance: $d_{ij} = \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2}$
- ☐ Min Fleet Size Bound: $n_{vehicles} \ge \lceil \sum q_i / Q \rceil$

**Load Planning Checklist (Memorize the 5 Constraints):**
- ☐ Total weight $\le$ GVW
- ☐ Per-axle load $\le$ regulatory limit
- ☐ Volume $\le$ cargo bay capacity
- ☐ Center of gravity within $[0.45L, 0.55L]$
- ☐ Cargo properly secured against shift

**Clarke-Wright Merging Rules (Frequently Tested):**
- ☐ Capacity constraint must hold.
- ☐ Only endpoint customers can be merged (never interior).
- ☐ Cannot create sub-tours.
- ☐ Negative savings = do not merge.

**Common Numerical Values (Industry Benchmarks):**
- ☐ Target VFR in Indian trucking: **75–90%**.
- ☐ Typical route optimization savings: **10–30%**.
- ☐ Distance savings from Clarke-Wright: usually **20–40%**.
- ☐ NP-hard threshold for exact CVRP: roughly $n > 25$–$30$ customers.

**Typical KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern:**
- ☐ 7 marks: Solve a Clarke-Wright / Nearest Neighbor numerical with 4–6 customers.
- ☐ 7 marks: Conceptual discussion of VRP variant, load planning, or CG calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost Management in Fleet Operations - Cost Components of Fleet Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT633 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cost Management in Fleet Operations - Cost Components of Fleet Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Cost Management in Fleet Operations** is the systematic process of identifying, classifying, analyzing, and controlling every monetary expenditure incurred in acquiring, operating, maintaining, and disposing of a fleet of vehicles, with the objective of maximizing productivity while minimizing the **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)**.

**Cost Components of Fleet Management** refers to the structured breakdown of all fleet-related expenditures into standardized categories — **Fixed Costs**, **Variable (Operating) Costs**, and **Semi-Variable Costs** — that enable fleet managers to perform unit-cost analysis (e.g., cost per kilometre, cost per ton-kilometre).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Under the NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, this topic maps directly to **CO4** of PEAUT633 — *"Apply cost management techniques to optimize fleet operations and evaluate economic viability of transport decisions."* The cognitive level is **Apply / Analyze (Bloom Level 3 & 4)**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you own a **mobile food truck business** in Kerala with three delivery vehicles. Every day, you spend money on:
- **Diesel** (only when the truck runs) → *this is a Variable Cost*
- **The EMI on the truck loan** (whether the truck runs or sits in the garage) → *this is a Fixed Cost*
- **Mobile mechanic visits** (you pay a retainer monthly + extra per repair) → *this is a Semi-Variable Cost*

If you want to know the **real cost of delivering one parcel**, you cannot just divide the diesel bill by the distance covered. You must also recover the loan payment, the driver's salary, the insurance, and the maintenance. **Cost Components of Fleet Management is the science of adding up all these expenses and attributing them correctly to every kilometre the vehicle runs.**

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of Fleet Costing:**
> $$\text{True Cost per km} = \frac{\text{All Fixed Costs + All Variable Costs}}{\text{Total Kilometres Operated}}$$
> Ignoring any one component gives a **misleading** unit cost and leads to incorrect pricing, wrong vehicle replacement decisions, and unprofitable contracts.

---

### 1.3 Standard Industry Metrics Used in KTU Valuation

The following units and benchmark metrics must be memorized:

| Metric | Unit | Industry Benchmark |
|---|---|---|
| Cost per Kilometre | ₹/km | ₹8 – ₹15 (light CV), ₹25 – ₹45 (HCV) |
| Cost per Ton-Kilometre | ₹/tonne-km | ₹2.5 – ₹6.0 (goods carriage) |
| Cost per Hour | ₹/hr | ₹150 – ₹400 |
| Fleet Utilization | % | > 80% is healthy |
| Depreciation Rate (SLM) | % per year | **15%** (light), **12%** (heavy) |
| Standard Working Hours | hrs/year | **2400 hours** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Fleet Cost Components

#### Pillar A — **Fixed Costs (FC)**
These costs **do NOT vary** with the level of fleet usage. Whether the vehicle runs 100 km/day or 0 km/day, these expenses must be paid.

**Sub-components of Fixed Costs:**
1. **Depreciation (D)** — Loss in asset value over time.
2. **Insurance Premium** — Annual premium for comprehensive + third-party cover.
3. **Road Tax / Registration / Permit Fees** — Statutory levies.
4. **Garage / Depot Rent** — Parking and storage facility cost.
5. **Driver & Administrative Salaries** — Paid on monthly basis, not per km.
6. **Interest on Capital (IOC)** — Opportunity cost of money invested in the vehicle.
7. **License & Fitness Certificate Fees** — Statutory compliance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Salary a Fixed Cost even if drivers work extra hours?** In fleet costing, base salary is treated as fixed; overtime wages are treated as variable. KTU exam answers must distinguish clearly.

---

#### Pillar B — **Variable / Operating Costs (VC)**
These costs **vary directly and proportionally** with distance travelled (or hours operated). Zero kilometres = Zero variable cost.

**Sub-components of Variable Costs:**
1. **Fuel Cost** — The single largest operating expense (40–50% of VC).
2. **Tyre Cost** — Proportional to km run.
3. **Engine Oil, Lubricants, Greases** — Consumables.
4. **Maintenance & Repairs (M&R)** — Routine servicing + breakdown.
5. **Spare Parts Replacement** — Brakes, clutch plates, filters.
6. **Washing & Cleaning Expenses.**

---

#### Pillar C — **Semi-Variable Costs (SVC)**
These contain **a fixed base + a variable component**. Example: A maintenance contract might charge ₹5,000/month retainer + ₹200 per service.

---

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Cost Classification

| Question | Why We Classify | How It Helps |
|---|---|---|
| Why separate Fixed from Variable? | To compute **Break-Even** | Fixed costs determine the minimum revenue needed to avoid loss |
| Why track per-km cost? | To **price transport services** competitively | Logistics tenders demand per-tonne-km rates |
| Why include depreciation? | To enforce **vehicle replacement policy** | When accumulated depreciation ≈ market value, replacement is due |
| Why include interest on capital? | To reflect **true economic cost** | Else, cash-purchased vehicles appear "cheaper" — wrong decision |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D_{SLM} = \dfrac{C - S}{n}$ | Straight-Line Method Depreciation | ₹/year |
| 2 | $D_{WDV} = C \times r$ (Year 1) | Written Down Value Depreciation | ₹/year |
| 3 | $D_{WDV,\,n} = C(1-r)^n$ | Book value after *n* years (WDV) | ₹ |
| 4 | $\text{Fixed Cost per km} = \dfrac{FC}{\text{Total km}}$ | Fixed cost attribution | ₹/km |
| 5 | $\text{Variable Cost per km} = \dfrac{VC}{\text{Total km}}$ | Variable cost attribution | ₹/km |
| 6 | $\text{Total Cost per km} = \dfrac{FC + VC}{\text{Total km}}$ | Combined unit cost | ₹/km |
| 7 | $\text{Cost per tonne-km} = \dfrac{TC}{W \times D}$ | Goods carriage metric | ₹/tonne-km |
| 8 | $SVC = FC_{\text{base}} + (v \times \text{activity})$ | Semi-variable model | ₹ |
| 9 | $\text{Depreciation per km} = \dfrac{C - S}{\text{Total Life km}}$ | Distance-based depreciation | ₹/km |
| 10 | $\text{Break-Even km} = \dfrac{FC}{\text{Rate per km} - \text{VC per km}}$ | Break-even distance | km |
| 11 | $\text{Net Annual Cost} = FC + VC - \text{Salvage}/n$ | Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC) | ₹/year |
| 12 | $\text{Sum-of-Years Digits} = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$ | SYD depreciation weighting | dimensionless |

**Where:**
- $C$ = Capital cost (purchase price + registration) in ₹
- $S$ = Salvage value (resale value at end of life) in ₹
- $n$ = Useful life in years
- $r$ = WDV depreciation rate (e.g., **15.83%** for 5-year life using $r = 1 - (S/C)^{1/n}$)
- $W$ = Payload capacity in tonnes
- $D$ = Distance covered in km

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` is **banned** in KTU table cells. Use `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., write $S \mid C$ if needed) — never `|C - S|` inside a markdown table row.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Logistics

1. **Transport Pricing & Tendering** — Logistics companies quote per-tonne-km rates using these formulas.
2. **Vehicle Replacement Decisions** — EAC (Equivalent Annual Cost) method uses cost components to compare old vs new fleet.
3. **Route Profitability Analysis** — Identifies profitable vs loss-making routes.
4. **Government Policy** — Minimum freight rate fixation (e.g., by **Transport Commission of India**) relies on per-tonne-km cost norms.
5. **EV Fleet Migration** — Battery replacement cost (a depreciation item) becomes a critical component in EV fleet TCO.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Straight-Line Depreciation Per Kilometre

**Problem Context:** A KTU textbook problem typically asks: *"A truck is purchased for ₹25,00,000 with a salvage value of ₹5,00,000 after 8 years. The truck is expected to run 1,20,000 km per year. Calculate the depreciation cost per kilometre using SLM."*

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Identify the input parameters.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Capital Cost } (C) &= \text{Rs. } 25{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Salvage Value } (S) &= \text{Rs. } 5{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Useful Life } (n) &= 8 \text{ years} \\
\text{Annual Distance } (d) &= 1{,}20{,}000 \text{ km/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Straight-Line Method formula. The total depreciation over the vehicle's life is the difference between capital cost and salvage value, spread equally over *n* years.
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{SLM} &= \frac{C - S}{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values.
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{SLM} &= \frac{25{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000}{8} \\
