&= \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{8} \\
&= \text{Rs. } 2{,}50{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert annual depreciation into per-kilometre depreciation by dividing by annual distance.
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{per\,km} &= \frac{D_{SLM}}{d} \\
&= \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{1{,}20{,}000} \\
&= \text{Rs. } 2.083 \text{ per km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 (Cross-verification):** Compute the total life distance and total depreciation, then divide.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Life km} &= d \times n = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 8 = 9{,}60{,}000 \text{ km} \\
D_{per\,km} &= \frac{C - S}{\text{Total Life km}} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{9{,}60{,}000} = \text{Rs. } 2.083 \text{ per km} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Depreciation cost = **₹2.08 per km** (rounded to two decimals). This is the KTU-recommended precision level.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Cost per Kilometre (Composite Fleet Cost)

**Problem Context:** *"Using the data below, compute the Total Cost per km for a delivery van. Fixed costs: Insurance ₹40,000/yr, Permit ₹15,000/yr, Salary ₹3,60,000/yr, Depreciation ₹80,000/yr, Interest ₹50,000/yr. Variable costs: Fuel ₹2,40,000/yr, Tyre ₹60,000/yr, Maintenance ₹90,000/yr. Annual distance = 60,000 km."*

**Step 1:** Sum up the Fixed Costs (FC).
$$
\begin{aligned}
FC &= \text{Insurance} + \text{Permit} + \text{Salary} + \text{Depreciation} + \text{Interest} \\
&= 40{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 3{,}60{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 50{,}000 \\
&= \text{Rs. } 5{,}45{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Sum up the Variable Costs (VC).
$$
\begin{aligned}
VC &= \text{Fuel} + \text{Tyre} + \text{Maintenance} \\
&= 2{,}40{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 90{,}000 \\
&= \text{Rs. } 3{,}90{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute Total Cost (TC).
$$
\begin{aligned}
TC &= FC + VC \\
&= 5{,}45{,}000 + 3{,}90{,}000 \\
&= \text{Rs. } 9{,}35{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute per-km unit costs.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FC per km} &= \frac{FC}{\text{km}} = \frac{5{,}45{,}000}{60{,}000} = \text{Rs. } 9.08 \\
\text{VC per km} &= \frac{VC}{\text{km}} = \frac{3{,}90{,}000}{60{,}000} = \text{Rs. } 6.50 \\
\text{Total Cost per km} &= 9.08 + 6.50 = \text{Rs. } 15.58
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Verify using the direct ratio.
$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{per\,km} &= \frac{TC}{km} = \frac{9{,}35{,}000}{60{,}000} = \text{Rs. } 15.58 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** If the question gives the data **per month**, the student must multiply by **12** before dividing. Similarly, "per quarter" → multiply by 4. Many KTU students lose marks here by missing unit conversion.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Break-Even Kilometre for Fleet Operations

**Problem Context:** *"A taxi charges ₹18 per km. Fixed cost = ₹4,00,000/yr. Variable cost = ₹6 per km. Find the break-even distance per year."*

**Step 1:** At break-even, **Total Revenue = Total Cost**.
$$
\begin{aligned}
R \times x &= FC + (VC_{\text{per km}} \times x)
\end{aligned}
$$
where $x$ = distance in km and $R$ = revenue rate per km.

**Step 2:** Solve algebraically for $x$.
$$
\begin{aligned}
R \cdot x - VC_{per\,km} \cdot x &= FC \\
x \cdot (R - VC_{per\,km}) &= FC \\
x &= \frac{FC}{R - VC_{per\,km}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.
$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{18 - 6} \\
&= \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{12} \\
&= 33{,}333.33 \text{ km per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Verify. At 33,334 km:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Revenue} &= 18 \times 33{,}334 = 6{,}00{,}012 \\
\text{Cost} &= 4{,}00{,}000 + 6 \times 33{,}334 = 4{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}004 = 6{,}00{,}004 \\
\text{Difference} &\approx 0 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cost Behaviour Summary

| Cost Component | Type | Behaviour with ↑ km | Treatment in Tendering |
|---|---|---|---|
| Depreciation | Fixed | Unchanged | Add to FC |
| Insurance | Fixed | Unchanged | Add to FC |
| Driver Salary | Fixed | Unchanged | Add to FC |
| Interest on Loan | Fixed | Unchanged | Add to FC |
| Road Tax | Fixed | Unchanged | Add to FC |
| Fuel | Variable | Increases linearly | Per-km rate |
| Tyre | Variable | Increases linearly | Per-km rate |
| Engine Oil | Variable | Increases linearly | Per-km rate |
| Maintenance | Variable | Increases (often non-linearly) | Per-km rate + aging factor |
| Retainer + Usage Fee | Semi-Variable | Partial increase | Split into FC + VC parts |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchical Breakdown of Fleet Cost Components

The diagram below renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mapping the hierarchical classification of all fleet cost components as required by the KTU 2024 scheme.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[FLEET TOTAL COST OF OWNERSHIP] --> B[FIXED COSTS]
    A --> C[VARIABLE OPERATING COSTS]
    A --> D[SEMI VARIABLE COSTS]

    B --> B1[Depreciation SLM or WDV]
    B --> B2[Insurance Premium]
    B --> B3[Road Tax and Permit]
    B --> B4[Driver Salary Base]
    B --> B5[Interest on Capital]
    B --> B6[Garage or Depot Rent]
    B --> B7[License and Fitness Fees]

    C --> C1[Fuel and Diesel]
    C --> C2[Tyre Cost]
    C --> C3[Engine Oil and Lubricants]
    C --> C4[Maintenance and Repairs]
    C --> C5[Spare Parts]
    C --> C6[Washing and Cleaning]

    D --> D1[Maintenance Retainer]
    D --> D2[Telematics Subscription]
    D --> D3[Overtime Wages]

    B1 --> E[FC per km Calculation]
    B2 --> E
    B3 --> E
    B4 --> E
    B5 --> E
    B6 --> E
    B7 --> E

    C1 --> F[VC per km Calculation]
    C2 --> F
    C3 --> F
    C4 --> F
    C5 --> F
    C6 --> F

    E --> G[TOTAL COST PER KM]
    F --> G
    D1 --> H[SVC Decomp FC plus VC]
    D2 --> H
    D3 --> H
    H --> G
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Cost Component Interaction Flow (How Each Component Enters the Final Per-Km Rate)

```mermaid
graph LR
    INPUT1[Capital Cost in Rs] --> DEPN[Depreciation Calculation]
    INPUT2[Salvage Value in Rs] --> DEPN
    INPUT3[Useful Life in years] --> DEPN
    INPUT4[Annual km] --> DEPN

    DEPN --> UNITFC[Fixed Cost per km]
    INS[Insurance Annual] --> UNITFC
    TAX[Road Tax Annual] --> UNITFC
    SAL[Driver Salary Annual] --> UNITFC
    INT[Interest Annual] --> UNITFC

    FUEL[Fuel Cost Annual] --> UNITVC[Variable Cost per km]
    TYRE[Tyre Cost Annual] --> UNITVC
    MNT[Maintenance Annual] --> UNITVC
    LUB[Lubricants Annual] --> UNITVC

    UNITFC --> FINAL[TOTAL COST PER KM]
    UNITVC --> FINAL
    SVC[Semi Variable Split] --> FINAL

    FINAL --> DECISION{Vehicle Profitable}
    DECISION -->|Yes| KEEP[Continue Operations]
    DECISION -->|No| REPLACE[Plan Replacement]
```

---

### 4.3 Schematic Notes on Diagram Interpretation

- **Top-down hierarchy** in the first diagram clarifies how every rupee spent is classified.
- **Left-right flow** in the second diagram demonstrates how annual rupee amounts are mathematically converted into per-km unit costs.
- Both diagrams strictly avoid reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, or `style` as standalone node identifiers, complying with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguard rules.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define the term "Fixed Cost" in the context of fleet management. Give four examples.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Fixed costs are those fleet-related expenditures that **do not vary with the level of vehicle usage** — they must be paid irrespective of whether the vehicle operates or remains idle.

**Four Examples:**
1. Vehicle depreciation
2. Insurance premium
3. Road tax and permit fees
4. Driver's basic salary

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):** Definition = 1 mark + Examples (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks) = **3 marks**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
*Distinguish between Fixed Costs and Variable Costs in fleet operations. Why is this distinction important?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Fixed Cost | Variable Cost |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Independent of km run | Directly proportional to km run |
| Behaviour when km = 0 | Still incurred | Zero |
| Examples | Insurance, salary, depreciation | Fuel, tyres, lubricants |
| Recovery logic | Spread over expected km | Per-km basis |

**Importance of distinction:** It enables the fleet manager to compute **break-even distance**, perform **per-km cost analysis**, decide **vehicle replacement**, and quote **competitive transport tariffs**.

---

### PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, ESE Pattern]**

A logistics company purchases a heavy commercial vehicle for **₹35,00,000**. The expected salvage value after **10 years** is **₹5,00,000**. The vehicle is expected to run **80,000 km per year**. Annual fixed costs (excluding depreciation) are: Insurance ₹60,000, Road Tax ₹25,000, Driver Salary ₹4,80,000, Interest on Capital ₹1,20,000, Garage Rent ₹36,000. Annual variable costs are: Fuel ₹4,80,000, Tyres ₹1,20,000, Lubricants ₹40,000, Maintenance ₹1,60,000.

**Part (a)** [7 Marks] — Calculate the **Total Cost per kilometre** and break it down into Fixed and Variable components.

**Part (b)** [7 Marks] — If the company charges a freight rate of **₹28 per km**, find the **annual break-even distance** and the **annual profit at 80,000 km**.

---

##### **Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1: Compute Annual Depreciation (SLM).**
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{SLM} &= \frac{C - S}{n} = \frac{35{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000}{10} \\
&= \frac{30{,}00{,}000}{10} = \text{Rs. } 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Sum Fixed Costs (including depreciation).**
$$
\begin{aligned}
FC &= D + \text{Insurance} + \text{Tax} + \text{Salary} + \text{Interest} + \text{Rent} \\
&= 3{,}00{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 25{,}000 + 4{,}80{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 + 36{,}000 \\
&= \text{Rs. } 10{,}21{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Sum Variable Costs.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
VC &= \text{Fuel} + \text{Tyres} + \text{Lubricants} + \text{Maintenance} \\
&= 4{,}80{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 1{,}60{,}000 \\
&= \text{Rs. } 8{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Total Cost and Per-km Costs.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
TC &= FC + VC = 10{,}21{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 18{,}21{,}000 \\
FC_{per\,km} &= \frac{10{,}21{,}000}{80{,}000} = \text{Rs. } 12.76 \text{ per km} \\
VC_{per\,km} &= \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{80{,}000} = \text{Rs. } 10.00 \text{ per km} \\
TC_{per\,km} &= 12.76 + 10.00 = \text{Rs. } 22.76 \text{ per km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer (a):** Total Cost per km = **₹22.76** (FC = ₹12.76, VC = ₹10.00)

---

**Part (b) — Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1: Break-Even Distance Formula.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{BE} &= \frac{FC}{R - VC_{per\,km}} \\
&= \frac{10{,}21{,}000}{28 - 10} \\
&= \frac{10{,}21{,}000}{18} \\
&= 56{,}722.22 \text{ km per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Annual Profit at 80,000 km.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Revenue} &= 28 \times 80{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 22{,}40{,}000 \\
\text{Total Cost} &= \text{Rs. } 18{,}21{,}000 \\
\text{Profit} &= 22{,}40{,}000 - 18{,}21{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 4{,}19{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer (b):** Break-even distance = **56,722.22 km/year**, Annual Profit = **₹4,19,000**

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, ESE Pattern]**

A taxi fleet operator owns a fleet of **5 identical cars**, each purchased for **₹8,00,000** with a salvage value of **₹1,00,000** after **6 years**. Each car runs **40,000 km/year**.

Annual costs per car:
- Insurance: ₹18,000
- Road Tax: ₹8,000
- Driver Salary: ₹1,80,000
- Fuel: ₹2,00,000
- Tyres: ₹30,000
- Maintenance: ₹40,000

**Part (a)** [7 Marks] — Compute the **Total Cost per km** for the fleet (5 cars combined) using SLM depreciation. Show all fixed and variable components separately.

**Part (b)** [7 Marks] — If depreciation is recalculated using the **Written Down Value (WDV)** method at **20% p.a.**, compute the depreciation for Year 1 and Year 2. Comment on which method gives a higher tax shield in early years.

---

##### **Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1: Per-Car Annual Depreciation (SLM).**
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{per\,car} &= \frac{8{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000}{6} = \frac{7{,}00{,}000}{6} = \text{Rs. } 1{,}16{,}667
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Per-Car Fixed Costs.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
FC_{per\,car} &= D + \text{Insurance} + \text{Tax} + \text{Salary} \\
&= 1{,}16{,}667 + 18{,}000 + 8{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000 \\
&= \text{Rs. } 3{,}22{,}667
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Per-Car Variable Costs.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
VC_{per\,car} &= \text{Fuel} + \text{Tyres} + \text{Maintenance} \\
&= 2{,}00{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 40{,}000 \\
&= \text{Rs. } 2{,}70{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Fleet Totals (×5 cars).**
$$
\begin{aligned}
FC_{fleet} &= 3{,}22{,}667 \times 5 = \text{Rs. } 16{,}13{,}333 \\
VC_{fleet} &= 2{,}70{,}000 \times 5 = \text{Rs. } 13{,}50{,}000 \\
TC_{fleet} &= 16{,}13{,}333 + 13{,}50{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 29{,}63{,}333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Total Fleet Kilometres.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total km} &= 40{,}000 \times 5 = 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ km/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Per-km Costs.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
FC_{per\,km} &= \frac{16{,}13{,}333}{2{,}00{,}000} = \text{Rs. } 8.07 \\
VC_{per\,km} &= \frac{13{,}50{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = \text{Rs. } 6.75 \\
TC_{per\,km} &= 8.07 + 6.75 = \text{Rs. } 14.82
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer (a):** Fleet Total Cost per km = **₹14.82**

---

**Part (b) — Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1: WDV Year 1 Depreciation.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= C \times r = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.20 = \text{Rs. } 1{,}60{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Book Value at End of Year 1.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
BV_1 &= C - D_1 = 8{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}60{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 6{,}40{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: WDV Year 2 Depreciation.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_2 &= BV_1 \times r = 6{,}40{,}000 \times 0.20 = \text{Rs. } 1{,}28{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Comparison Table.**

| Year | SLM Depreciation | WDV Depreciation |
|---|---|---|
| Year 1 | ₹1,16,667 | ₹1,60,000 |
| Year 2 | ₹1,16,667 | ₹1,28,000 |

**Step 5: Tax Shield Comment.**
Since depreciation is a tax-deductible expense, **higher depreciation in early years = higher tax savings in early years**. The WDV method therefore provides a **greater tax shield in Year 1** (₹1,60,000 > ₹1,16,667), making it more attractive for fleet owners seeking early-year tax benefits.

> **Final Answer (b):** D₁ = ₹1,60,000; D₂ = ₹1,28,000; WDV gives a higher early-year tax shield.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** "Per month" data must be multiplied by 12 before dividing by annual km. Students frequently forget this — **2–3 marks lost**.
> 2. **Confusing FC with VC:** Treating driver overtime as Fixed, or insurance as Variable — **2 marks lost**.
> 3. **Salvage Value Forgotten:** In SLM formula, students sometimes use only C without subtracting S, giving inflated depreciation — **3 marks lost**.
> 4. **Decimal Rounding:** Avoid premature rounding. Carry full decimals until the final answer (e.g., ₹2.083, not ₹2).
> 5. **No Units in Final Answer:** Always write "₹/km" or "km/year" — answers without units lose 0.5–1 mark in strict KTU valuation.

---

### **TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST**

- [ ] **Cost Components = Fixed + Variable + Semi-Variable.** Always classify correctly.
- [ ] **Fixed Costs** do NOT change with km: Depreciation, Insurance, Salary, Tax, Interest, Rent.
- [ ] **Variable Costs** change proportionally: Fuel, Tyres, Oil, Maintenance, Spares.
- [ ] **Semi-Variable Costs** = Fixed base + Variable usage part (e.g., retainer + per-service fee).
- [ ] **SLM Depreciation:** $D = (C - S) / n$ — straight-line; equal annual charge.
- [ ] **WDV Depreciation:** $D_n = C(1-r)^n$ — diminishing balance; higher in early years.
- [ ] **Total Cost per km:** Sum all FC and VC, then divide by **total km run**.
- [ ] **Break-Even km:** $x = FC / (R - VC_{per\,km})$.
- [ ] **Cost per tonne-km:** Standard metric for goods carriage — used in tenders.
- [ ] **Real-World Use:** Pricing, replacement decisions, route profitability, EV TCO, government freight norms.
- [ ] **Always carry units** in the final answer (₹/km, ₹/tonne-km, km/year).
- [ ] **Sanity check:** If your per-km cost is < ₹5 (HCV) or > ₹50 (LCV), re-verify calculations.
- [ ] **Depreciation** is the largest single **Fixed Cost** component — calculate it first.
- [ ] **Fuel** is the largest single **Variable Cost** component — typically 40–50% of VC.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost Control Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT633 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cost Control Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cost Control Strategies** in the context of vehicle transport and fleet management refer to the systematic, planned, and continuous process of monitoring, analyzing, regulating, and reducing the overall expenditure incurred in acquiring, operating, maintaining, and disposing of a fleet of vehicles, while simultaneously maintaining or improving service quality, safety standards, and operational efficiency.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, cost control encompasses the managerial and engineering techniques used to:

- **Identify** the various cost heads (fixed, variable, semi-variable, direct, indirect)
- **Measure** the actual expenditure against budgeted standards
- **Analyze** variances and root causes of cost overruns
- **Rectify** deviations through corrective managerial or technical interventions
- **Monitor** the effectiveness of implemented controls

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Cost control is not merely cost *cutting*. It is the scientific management of fleet expenditure through proactive strategies, predictive analytics, and process optimization to achieve the **lowest sustainable Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** without compromising safety, regulatory compliance, or service delivery.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Household Budget" Approach

Think of a fleet manager as the head of a large joint family managing a **monthly household budget** of several lakhs of rupees.

- **Fixed Costs** are like the **house EMI (Equated Monthly Installment)** — they must be paid every month whether the family uses all rooms or not.
- **Variable Costs** are like the **groceries and electricity bills** — they go up if more people stay home, and down if the family goes out for dinner.
- **Fuel Costs** are like the **petrol for the family car** — directly tied to how much the car is driven.
- **Maintenance Costs** are like **servicing the car** — regular servicing is cheap, but skipping it leads to a costly engine overhaul.

**Cost Control Strategy** is like the family sitting down every month, looking at last month's expenses, identifying where the money "leaked" (like leaving the AC on all day), and setting rules to plug those leaks — without telling the kids they cannot use the AC at all (that would be cutting quality of life, not controlling costs).

## 1.3 Why Cost Control is Critical in Fleet Management

> [!NOTE]
> In commercial fleet operations, even a **2%–5% reduction in operating cost** can translate into **lakhs of rupees in annual savings** for a mid-sized fleet. Studies by industry bodies such as the **American Transportation Research Institute (ATRI)** and **Fleet Management Institute** consistently show that fuel and maintenance together account for **55%–65%** of total fleet operating cost — making them the prime targets of cost control.

## 1.4 Primary Objectives of Cost Control

The main objectives pursued in fleet cost control are:

1. **Minimize the Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** across the vehicle life cycle.
2. **Maximize the Cost Efficiency Ratio** — defined as *Output per Rupee of Cost*.
3. **Ensure Budgetary Compliance** by aligning actual spend with planned budgets.
4. **Improve Fleet Utilization Rate** (target: **≥ 80%** for commercial fleets).
5. **Extend Vehicle Useful Life** through preventive cost discipline.
6. **Enhance Profitability** of the transport operation.
7. **Strengthen Competitive Positioning** in the logistics market.
8. **Ensure Regulatory and Tax Compliance** (GST, road tax, permits, tolls).
9. **Support Strategic Decision-Making** (buy vs. lease, in-house vs. outsource).
10. **Build a Data-Driven Cost Culture** within the organization.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Fleet Costs

Fleet costs are classified along several dimensions. A KTU examiner expects you to clearly distinguish between these categories and provide examples for each.

### A. By Variability with Output

| Cost Type | Definition | Examples in Fleet |
|-----------|------------|-------------------|
| **Fixed Cost (FC)** | Cost that does **not vary** with distance travelled or hours of operation over a relevant range. | Vehicle loan EMI, road tax, insurance premium, depreciation (straight-line), driver salary (fixed component), depot rent. |
| **Variable Cost (VC)** | Cost that **varies directly** with distance or usage. | Diesel/fuel, tyres (per km wear), consumables, toll charges, trip-based driver incentives, loading/unloading charges. |
| **Semi-Variable Cost** | Cost having both a fixed base and a variable component. | Mobile phone bills (fixed rental + usage), maintenance (scheduled + breakdown), electricity at depot. |

### B. By Controllability

- **Controllable Costs** — within the fleet manager's direct authority (fuel efficiency, route choice, tyre rotation).
- **Uncontrollable Costs** — set by external market/regulation (fuel tax, road toll, statutory insurance, GST rate).

### C. By Function

- **Acquisition Cost** — purchase price, registration, fitments.
- **Operating Cost** — fuel, driver wages, tolls, permits.
- **Maintenance Cost** — preventive, breakdown, accident repair.
- **Administrative Cost** — office overhead, MIS, compliance.
- **Disposal Cost** — resale value loss, scrap handling.

## 2.2 The Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Framework

TCO is the **gold standard** metric for fleet cost control. It captures the *cradle-to-grave* cost of owning and operating a vehicle.

$$
\text{TCO} = C_{\text{acq}} + C_{\text{op}} + C_{\text{maint}} + C_{\text{admin}} + C_{\text{down}} + C_{\text{disposal}} - S_{\text{salvage}}
$$

Where each term is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{acq}} &= \text{Total acquisition cost (invoice + registration + fitments)} \\
C_{\text{op}} &= \text{Total operating cost over life (fuel, driver, toll, permits)} \\
C_{\text{maint}} &= \text{Total maintenance cost (preventive + breakdown + accident)} \\
C_{\text{admin}} &= \text{Administrative and overhead cost allocation} \\
C_{\text{down}} &= \text{Downtime cost (lost revenue during vehicle off-road periods)} \\
C_{\text{disposal}} &= \text{End-of-life disposal / scrapping cost} \\
S_{\text{salvage}} &= \text{Salvage / resale value at end of life}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.3 Cost Per Kilometer (CPKM) — The Operational Benchmark

CPKM is the most-watched KPI in fleet cost control. It is computed as:

$$
\text{CPKM} \; (\text{₹/km}) = \frac{\text{Total Cost of Vehicle over Period}}{\text{Total Kilometres Run in Period}}
$$

It can be broken down into:

$$
\text{CPKM} = \text{Fixed Cost per km} + \text{Variable Cost per km} + \text{Maintenance Cost per km}
$$

Where:

$$
\text{Fixed Cost per km} = \frac{\text{Total Fixed Cost per Year}}{\text{Annual Kilometres Run}}
$$

$$
\text{Variable Cost per km} = \frac{\text{Total Variable Cost over Period}}{\text{Total km in Period}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that **Fixed Cost per km is inversely proportional to utilization**. Running more kilometres *spreads* the fixed cost over more units of output, *lowering* the per-km cost. This is the mathematical justification for the managerial mantra — **"Run the vehicle more to reduce per-km cost"** — provided that additional revenue covers the marginal variable cost.

## 2.4 Depreciation in Fleet Cost Control

Depreciation is the systematic allocation of the vehicle's cost over its useful life. Two common methods are used:

### Straight Line Method (SLM)

$$
D_{\text{annual}} = \frac{P - S}{n}
$$

Where $P$ = Purchase price, $S$ = Salvage value, $n$ = Useful life in years.

### Written Down Value (WDV) Method

$$
D_{\text{annual},\,t} = r \times BV_{t-1}
$$

Where $r$ = WDV rate (e.g., 0.20 for 20%), $BV_{t-1}$ = Book value at end of previous year.

$$
BV_t = BV_{t-1} \times (1 - r)
$$

## 2.5 Break-Even Analysis for Fleet Operations

Break-even is critical in deciding fleet expansion, leasing vs. owning, and outsourcing decisions.

$$
\text{Break-Even Load Factor (BELF)} = \frac{\text{Total Fixed Cost per Trip}}{\text{Total Revenue per Trip at Full Load} - \text{Variable Cost per Trip}}
$$

Or in revenue terms:

$$
\text{Break-Even Revenue} = \frac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\left(1 - \dfrac{\text{Variable Cost}}{\text{Revenue}}\right)}
$$

## 2.6 Return on Fleet Investment (ROFI)

$$
\text{ROFI} \;(\%) = \frac{\text{Net Annual Fleet Profit}}{\text{Total Capital Invested in Fleet}} \times 100
$$

## 2.7 Fuel Cost Control Equation

Fuel is the largest variable cost. Fuel cost per km depends on:

$$
\text{Fuel Cost per km} = \frac{\text{Price per Litre}}{\text{Mileage in km/L}}
$$

Hence, mileage improvement is a direct lever for cost control:

$$
\% \text{ Fuel Saving} = \left(1 - \frac{\text{Old km/L}}{\text{New km/L}}\right) \times 100
$$

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Units | Use Case |
|---|-----------------|----------|-------|----------|
| 1 | **TCO** | $TCO = C_{acq} + C_{op} + C_{maint} + C_{admin} + C_{down} + C_{disposal} - S_{salvage}$ | ₹ | Life-cycle cost |
| 2 | **CPKM** | $CPKM = \frac{TC}{km}$ | ₹/km | Operational KPI |
| 3 | **Fixed Cost per km** | $FC_{km} = \frac{FC_{annual}}{km_{annual}}$ | ₹/km | Utilization impact |
| 4 | **Variable Cost per km** | $VC_{km} = \frac{VC_{period}}{km_{period}}$ | ₹/km | Fuel / tyres / toll |
| 5 | **SLM Depreciation** | $D = \frac{P-S}{n}$ | ₹/year | Accounting |
| 6 | **WDV Depreciation** | $BV_t = BV_{t-1}(1-r)$ | ₹ | Tax depreciation |
| 7 | **Break-Even Revenue** | $BER = \frac{FC}{1 - VC/Rev}$ | ₹ | Outsourcing decision |
| 8 | **ROFI** | $ROFI = \frac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Capital}} \times 100$ | % | Investment return |
| 9 | **Fuel Cost per km** | $FC_{km} = \frac{\text{Price/L}}{km/L}$ | ₹/km | Fuel efficiency KPI |
| 10 | **Fleet Utilization** | $U = \frac{\text{km actually run}}{\text{km possible}} \times 100$ | % | Capacity use |
| 11 | **Cost Variance** | $CV = AC - BC$ | ₹ | Budget control |
| 12 | **Cost Variance %** | $CV\% = \frac{AC-BC}{BC} \times 100$ | % | Cost control KPI |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to remember:**
> - CPKM is **valid only** when annual km > 0.
> - WDV book value **never reaches zero** mathematically; it approaches zero asymptotically.
> - Break-even formula assumes **linear** cost-revenue behaviour, valid for normal operating ranges.

## 2.9 Why This Matters in Real Engineering Practice

In Indian fleet operations (logistics companies, Ola/Uber-style aggregators, public transport corporations like KSRTC, BEST), TCO and CPKM are the two numbers that decide:

- **Procurement:** Which OEM/vehicle to buy
- **Replacement Policy:** When to retire a vehicle (typically at **80% of TCO recovery**)
- **Routing:** Which route is most cost-efficient
- **Pricing:** What freight rate to quote
- **Insurance:** Risk premium computation
- **Tax & Depreciation:** Income Tax Act Section 44AE (for goods carriages)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Proving that Higher Utilization Reduces CPKM

**Problem Statement:**
A truck has an annual fixed cost of **₹ 4,00,000** and a variable cost of **₹ 8 per km**. Show analytically that running **more kilometres reduces the cost per kilometre**, and compute the CPKM at 20,000 km/year and 50,000 km/year.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: State the total cost equation.**

The total annual cost (TC) is the sum of fixed and variable costs:

$$
TC = FC + VC
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given values into the equation.**

We have $FC = 4{,}00{,}000$ ₹/year, and $VC = 8 \times km$ (since variable cost is ₹ 8/km). Therefore:

$$
TC = 4{,}00{,}000 + 8 \times km
$$

**Step 3: Define CPKM mathematically.**

CPKM is the total cost divided by the kilometres run:

$$
CPKM = \frac{TC}{km} = \frac{FC + (VC \text{ rate} \times km)}{km}
$$

**Step 4: Algebraically separate fixed and variable components.**

Dividing each term by $km$:

$$
CPKM = \frac{FC}{km} + VC_{\text{rate}}
$$

**Step 5: Compute CPKM at 20,000 km/year.**

$$
CPKM_{20k} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{20{,}000} + 8 = 20 + 8 = 28 \; \text{₹/km}
$$

**Step 6: Compute CPKM at 50,000 km/year.**

$$
CPKM_{50k} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{50{,}000} + 8 = 8 + 8 = 16 \; \text{₹/km}
$$

**Step 7: Compute the percentage reduction in CPKM.**

$$
\% \text{ Reduction} = \left(\frac{28 - 16}{28}\right) \times 100 = 42.86\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of Derivation 1:** Increasing utilization from 20,000 km/year to 50,000 km/year reduces CPKM by **42.86%**. This proves the engineering principle that **utilization is the single largest controllable lever on per-km cost**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — TCO Computation for a Light Commercial Vehicle (LCV)

**Problem Statement:**
A transport company is evaluating a Tata Ace Gold (LCV) with the following data:

- Purchase price (P): **₹ 6,50,000**
- Registration, fitments, insurance (one-time): **₹ 1,20,000**
- Useful life: **8 years**
- Salvage value at end of life: **₹ 1,50,000**
- Annual fuel cost: **₹ 2,40,000**
- Annual driver salary: **₹ 2,00,000**
- Annual maintenance cost: **₹ 60,000**
- Annual toll and permits: **₹ 30,000**
- Annual admin overhead allocation: **₹ 25,000**
- Average downtime per year: **15 days**; daily lost revenue = **₹ 3,000**
- Disposal cost at end of life: **₹ 10,000**
- Total km in life: **4,00,000 km**

Compute the TCO and the CPKM.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Compute Acquisition Cost $C_{acq}$.**

$$
C_{acq} = P + \text{Regn + Fitments + Insurance} = 6{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 = 7{,}70{,}000 \; \text{₹}
$$

**Step 2: Compute total Operating Cost over life $C_{op}$.**

Operating cost includes fuel, driver, tolls, permits. We sum them per year and multiply by life:

$$
C_{op,\,annual} = 2{,}40{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 30{,}000 = 4{,}70{,}000 \; \text{₹/year}
$$

$$
C_{op,\,life} = 4{,}70{,}000 \times 8 = 37{,}60{,}000 \; \text{₹}
$$

**Step 3: Compute total Maintenance Cost over life $C_{maint}$.**

$$
C_{maint,\,life} = 60{,}000 \times 8 = 4{,}80{,}000 \; \text{₹}
$$

**Step 4: Compute total Administrative Cost over life $C_{admin}$.**

$$
C_{admin,\,life} = 25{,}000 \times 8 = 2{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹}
$$

**Step 5: Compute total Downtime Cost over life $C_{down}$.**

Each year has 15 days of downtime at ₹ 3,000/day lost revenue:

$$
C_{down,\,annual} = 15 \times 3{,}000 = 45{,}000 \; \text{₹/year}
$$

$$
C_{down,\,life} = 45{,}000 \times 8 = 3{,}60{,}000 \; \text{₹}
$$

**Step 6: Compute Disposal Cost $C_{disposal}$.**

$$
C_{disposal} = 10{,}000 \; \text{₹}
$$

**Step 7: Compute Salvage Value $S_{salvage}$.**

$$
S_{salvage} = 1{,}50{,}000 \; \text{₹}
$$

**Step 8: Sum up the TCO.**

$$
TCO = 7{,}70{,}000 + 37{,}60{,}000 + 4{,}80{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}60{,}000 + 10{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000
$$

$$
TCO = 54{,}30{,}000 \; \text{₹}
$$

**Step 9: Compute the CPKM over life.**

$$
CPKM = \frac{TCO}{\text{Total km}} = \frac{54{,}30{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} = 13.575 \; \text{₹/km}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion of Derivation 2:** The vehicle's all-in TCO is ₹ 54.3 lakhs over its 8-year life, translating to a CPKM of **₹ 13.58/km**. The cost breakdown shows operating cost is the **largest component** (≈ 69%), confirming it as the prime target for cost control.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Break-Even Analysis for Own vs. Lease Decision

**Problem Statement:**
A logistics firm can either:
- **Own** a truck requiring **₹ 15,00,000** capital (depreciation ₹ 1,87,500/year), insurance ₹ 60,000/year, and ₹ 12/km variable cost, OR
- **Lease** the same truck at **₹ 90,000/month** fixed + **₹ 4/km** variable.

Find the break-even annual km.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Compute annual fixed cost of Ownership.**

Assuming straight-line depreciation fully covers the capital, the relevant fixed cost includes depreciation + insurance:

$$
FC_{\text{own}} = 1{,}87{,}500 + 60{,}000 = 2{,}47{,}500 \; \text{₹/year}
$$

**Step 2: Compute annual fixed cost of Leasing.**

$$
FC_{\text{lease}} = 90{,}000 \times 12 = 10{,}80{,}000 \; \text{₹/year}
$$

**Step 3: Set up the break-even equation.**

At break-even, total cost of ownership = total cost of leasing:

$$
FC_{\text{own}} + VC_{\text{own}} \times km = FC_{\text{lease}} + VC_{\text{lease}} \times km
$$

**Step 4: Substitute the values.**

$$
2{,}47{,}500 + 12 \times km = 10{,}80{,}000 + 4 \times km
$$

**Step 5: Rearrange to isolate km.**

$$
(12 - 4) \times km = 10{,}80{,}000 - 2{,}47{,}500
$$

$$
8 \times km = 8{,}32{,}500
$$

**Step 6: Solve for the break-even km.**

$$
km_{\text{BE}} = \frac{8{,}32{,}500}{8} = 1{,}04{,}062.5 \; \text{km/year}
$$

**Step 7: Interpret the result.**

- If the firm expects to run **more than 1,04,063 km/year**, **ownership is cheaper**.
- If it runs **less than 1,04,063 km/year**, **leasing is cheaper**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of Derivation 3:** The break-even annual km is **1,04,063 km**. This is a typical KTU-style cost control decision problem, testing both the formula application and the managerial interpretation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Cost Control Levers

| Lever | Typical Savings | Implementation Cost | Time to Impact | KTU Weightage |
|-------|----------------|---------------------|----------------|---------------|
| **Tyre Management (rotation, alignment, pressure)** | 3%–8% on tyre cost | Low | 1–3 months | High |
| **Fuel Efficiency Training** | 5%–12% on fuel | Low–Medium | 3–6 months | Very High |
| **Route Optimization Software** | 8%–15% on fuel + time | Medium | 1–6 months | High |
| **Telematics / GPS Monitoring** | 10%–20% on combined cost | High | 6–12 months | Very High |
| **Preventive Maintenance Discipline** | 15%–25% on breakdown cost | Low | 1–2 years | High |
| **Right-Sizing of Fleet** | 10%–30% on TCO | High | 1–3 years | Medium |
| **Driver Incentive Schemes** | 5%–10% on fuel | Low | 1–3 months | High |
| **Outsourcing of Non-Core Logistics** | 5%–15% on admin | Low | 3–6 months | Medium |
| **Eco-Driving Programs** | 6%–10% on fuel | Low | 3–6 months | High |
| **Parts Standardization** | 5%–12% on maintenance | Low | 6–12 months | Medium |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Cost Control Strategy Framework (Module-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Identify Cost Heads] --> A2[Measure Actual vs Budget]
    A2 --> A3[Analyze Variances]
    A3 --> A4[Root Cause Analysis]
    A4 --> A5[Select Cost Control Lever]
    A5 --> A6[Implement Strategy]
    A6 --> A7[Monitor and Review]
    A7 --> A2

    A5 --> B1[Fuel Management]
    A5 --> B2[Maintenance Optimization]
    A5 --> B3[Route and Load Planning]
    A5 --> B4[Driver Management]
    A5 --> B5[Telematics and Technology]
    A5 --> B6[Procurement and TCO Analysis]

    B1 --> C1[Mileage Targets]
    B1 --> C2[Fuel Card Control]
    B1 --> C3[Idling Reduction]

    B2 --> D1[Preventive Schedules]
    B2 --> D2[Predictive Analytics]
    B2 --> D3[Vendor Negotiation]

    B3 --> E1[GPS Routing]
    B3 --> E2[Load Consolidation]
    B3 --> E3[Backhauling]

    B4 --> F1[Training]
    B4 --> F2[Incentive Schemes]
    B4 --> F3[Behaviour Monitoring]

    B5 --> G1[IoT Sensors]
    B5 --> G2[Fleet Management System]
    B5 --> G3[AI based Analytics]

    B6 --> H1[Vendor Scorecards]
    B6 --> H2[Right Sizing]
    B6 --> H3[Lease vs Buy Models]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — TCO Composition Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TCO1[Total Cost of Ownership TCO] --> A1[Acquisition Cost]
    TCO1 --> A2[Operating Cost]
    TCO1 --> A3[Maintenance Cost]
    TCO1 --> A4[Administrative Cost]
    TCO1 --> A5[Downtime Cost]
    TCO1 --> A6[Disposal Cost]
    TCO1 --> A7[Less Salvage Value]

    A1 --> A1a[Purchase Price]
    A1 --> A1b[Registration]
    A1 --> A1c[Insurance One Time]
    A1 --> A1d[Fitments and Accessories]

    A2 --> A2a[Fuel and Lubricants]
    A2 --> A2b[Driver Wages]
    A2 --> A2c[Toll and Permits]
    A2 --> A2d[Taxes and Duties]

    A3 --> A3a[Preventive Maintenance]
    A3 --> A3b[Breakdown Repairs]
    A3 --> A3c[Accident Repairs]
    A3 --> A3d[Tyre Replacement]

    A4 --> A4a[Depot Overheads]
    A4 --> A4b[IT and MIS]
    A4 --> A4c[Compliance and Audit]

    A5 --> A5a[Lost Revenue Off Road]
    A5 --> A5b[Replacement Vehicle Cost]

    A6 --> A6a[Scrapping Charges]
    A6 --> A6b[Pollution Certification]

    A7 --> A7a[Resale or Auction Value]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Cost Control Implementation Cycle (PDCA Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Plan: Set Cost Targets] --> D1[Do: Deploy Cost Control Tools]
    D1 --> C1[Check: Compare Actual vs Target]
    C1 --> A1[Act: Standardize or Correct]
    A1 --> P1

    P1 --> P1a[Define Budgets]
    P1 --> P1b[Set KPIs CPKM TCO]

    D1 --> D1a[Telematics Rollout]
    D1 --> D1b[Driver Training]
    D1 --> D1c[Route Software]

    C1 --> C1a[Monthly MIS Reports]
    C1 --> C1b[Variance Analysis]

    A1 --> A1a[Update SOPs]
    A1 --> A1b[Reward Best Practices]
    A1 --> A1c[Reassign or Retire Assets]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Strategic Decision Matrix for Cost Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Cost Control Decision] --> S2{Q1 Is Utilization High?}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Focus on Variable Cost Reduction]
    S2 -- No --> S4[Focus on Fixed Cost Reduction]

    S3 --> S3a[Fuel Efficiency]
    S3 --> S3b[Tyre Management]
    S3 --> S3c[Route Optimization]

    S4 --> S4a[Outsource Non Core Trips]
    S4 --> S4b[Right Size the Fleet]
    S4 --> S4c[Lease Short Term Excess]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) in the context of fleet management. List any four major components of TCO.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
TCO is the comprehensive, life-cycle cost of owning and operating a vehicle, from acquisition to disposal. It is computed as:

$$
TCO = C_{acq} + C_{op} + C_{maint} + C_{admin} + C_{down} + C_{disposal} - S_{salvage}
$$

Four major components:
1. **Acquisition Cost** — purchase, registration, fitments.
2. **Operating Cost** — fuel, driver, tolls, permits.
3. **Maintenance Cost** — preventive, breakdown, tyres.
4. **Downtime Cost** — revenue lost when vehicle is off-road.
*(1 mark for definition + formula, 2 marks for listing and brief explanation of 4 components.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Fixed Cost and Variable Cost in fleet operations with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Fixed Cost:** Does not change with the level of usage within a relevant range. Examples: Vehicle loan EMI, road tax, depot rent, insurance premium. (1.5 marks)
- **Variable Cost:** Varies directly with distance run or hours of operation. Examples: Diesel fuel, tyre wear, toll charges, trip-based driver incentives. (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**A logistics company is evaluating a fleet of Tata LPT 1109 trucks. The following data is given for one truck:**

- Purchase price: **₹ 18,00,000**
- Registration and fitments: **₹ 2,50,000**
- Expected life: **10 years**
- Salvage value: **₹ 2,00,000**
- Annual fuel cost: **₹ 5,50,000**
- Annual driver salary: **₹ 3,60,000**
- Annual maintenance cost: **₹ 1,20,000**
- Annual toll and permits: **₹ 80,000**
- Annual admin overhead allocation: **₹ 50,000**
- Annual downtime: **20 days**; daily lost revenue: **₹ 5,000**
- Disposal cost: **₹ 15,000**
- Total km in life: **6,00,000 km**

#### (a) Compute the TCO of the truck over its life. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Acquisition Cost $C_{acq}$**

$$
C_{acq} = 18{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 = 20{,}50{,}000 \; \text{₹}
$$

**Step 2: Total Operating Cost over life $C_{op}$**

$$
C_{op,\,annual} = 5{,}50{,}000 + 3{,}60{,}000 + 80{,}000 = 9{,}90{,}000 \; \text{₹/yr}
$$

$$
C_{op,\,life} = 9{,}90{,}000 \times 10 = 99{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹}
$$

**Step 3: Total Maintenance Cost over life $C_{maint}$**

$$
C_{maint,\,life} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 10 = 12{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹}
$$

**Step 4: Total Administrative Cost over life $C_{admin}$**

$$
C_{admin,\,life} = 50{,}000 \times 10 = 5{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹}
$$

**Step 5: Total Downtime Cost over life $C_{down}$**

$$
C_{down,\,annual} = 20 \times 5{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹/yr}
$$

$$
C_{down,\,life} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 10 = 10{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹}
$$

**Step 6: Disposal Cost $C_{disposal}$**

$$
C_{disposal} = 15{,}000 \; \text{₹}
$$

**Step 7: Salvage Value $S_{salvage}$**

$$
S_{salvage} = 2{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹}
$$

**Step 8: Total TCO**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCO &= 20{,}50{,}000 + 99{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 \\
&\quad + 10{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 \\
TCO &= 1{,}44{,}65{,}000 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(7 marks: 1 for each major step fully shown and summed correctly.)*

#### (b) Calculate the Cost Per Kilometre (CPKM) and comment on which cost head is the largest contributor. Suggest TWO cost control measures to reduce it. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: CPKM Computation**

$$
CPKM = \frac{TCO}{\text{Total km}} = \frac{1{,}44{,}65{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} = 24.108 \; \text{₹/km}
$$

**Step 2: Cost Head as % of TCO**

$$
\% \, C_{acq} = \frac{20{,}50{,}000}{1{,}44{,}65{,}000} \times 100 \approx 14.18\%
$$

$$
\% \, C_{op} = \frac{99{,}00{,}000}{1{,}44{,}65{,}000} \times 100 \approx 68.44\%
$$

$$
\% \, C_{maint} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{1{,}44{,}65{,}000} \times 100 \approx 8.30\%
$$

$$
\% \, C_{admin} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{1{,}44{,}65{,}000} \times 100 \approx 3.46\%
$$

$$
\% \, C_{down} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{1{,}44{,}65{,}000} \times 100 \approx 6.91\%
$$

**Step 3: Observation**

The **largest contributor is Operating Cost (≈ 68.44%)**, driven mainly by fuel and driver salary.

**Step 4: Two Cost Control Measures (for Operating Cost)**

1. **Fuel Efficiency Programme** — driver eco-driving training, route optimization, tyre pressure monitoring, idle-time reduction. Target: 5%–10% fuel savings.
2. **Driver Incentive Scheme** — pay performance-based incentive for mileage above benchmark and zero-accident record; this improves both fuel and maintenance outcomes.

*(7 marks: 1 for CPKM, 2 for percentage breakdown, 2 for observation, 2 for two well-explained measures.)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**A bus operator has the option to:**
- **Own** a bus with capital cost ₹ 25,00,000, depreciation ₹ 3,00,000/year, insurance ₹ 80,000/year, garage rent ₹ 60,000/year, and variable cost of ₹ 18 per km.
- **Lease** a bus at ₹ 1,20,000/month fixed + ₹ 5 per km variable.

**The operator expects to run the bus for 6 years.**

#### (a) Find the break-even annual kilometre at which Owning and Leasing yield the same total cost. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Annual Fixed Cost — Own**

$$
FC_{\text{own}} = 3{,}00{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 60{,}000 = 4{,}40{,}000 \; \text{₹/yr}
$$

**Step 2: Annual Fixed Cost — Lease**

$$
FC_{\text{lease}} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 12 = 14{,}40{,}000 \; \text{₹/yr}
$$

**Step 3: Set the Break-Even Equation**

$$
FC_{\text{own}} + VC_{\text{own}} \times km = FC_{\text{lease}} + VC_{\text{lease}} \times km
$$

**Step 4: Substitute the values**

$$
4{,}40{,}000 + 18 \cdot km = 14{,}40{,}000 + 5 \cdot km
$$

**Step 5: Rearrange**

$$
(18 - 5) \cdot km = 14{,}40{,}000 - 4{,}40{,}000
$$

**Step 6: Solve**

$$
13 \cdot km = 10{,}00{,}000 \;\Rightarrow\; km = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{13} = 76{,}923 \; \text{km/year (approx.)}
$$

**Step 7: Managerial Conclusion**

- If expected annual usage **> 76,923 km**, **Owning is more economical**.
- If expected annual usage **< 76,923 km**, **Leasing is more economical**.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per major step, 1 mark for managerial interpretation.)*

#### (b) If the operator actually runs the bus for **1,00,000 km/year**, which option is cheaper and by how much per year? Also, list FOUR cost control measures applicable to the chosen option. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Total Cost of Owning at 1,00,000 km/yr**

$$
TC_{\text{own}} = 4{,}40{,}000 + 18 \times 1{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}40{,}000 + 18{,}00{,}000 = 22{,}40{,}000 \; \text{₹/yr}
$$

**Step 2: Total Cost of Leasing at 1,00,000 km/yr**

$$
TC_{\text{lease}} = 14{,}40{,}000 + 5 \times 1{,}00{,}000 = 14{,}40{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 = 19{,}40{,}000 \; \text{₹/yr}
$$

**Step 3: Compare**

$$
\text{Saving by Leasing} = 22{,}40{,}000 - 19{,}40{,}000 = 3{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹/year}
$$

**Leasing is cheaper by ₹ 3,00,000 per year** at this utilization level.

**Step 4: Four Cost Control Measures for Leased Operation**

1. **Negotiate Variable Rate** — bulk lease tie-up for a lower ₹/km rate.
2. **Driver Training on Eco-Driving** — reduces fuel and tyre wear billed back to lessor.
3. **Strict Trip Planning** — avoid deadhead km to minimize variable cost.
4. **Maintenance Clause Audit** — clearly define lessor vs. lessee maintenance responsibility to avoid disputed bills.

*(7 marks: 1 for each TC calculation, 1 for comparison, 1 for correct decision, 2 for the four cost control measures.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make (and where marks are lost):**
> 1. **Forgetting to include ALL TCO components** — students often miss downtime cost or salvage value deduction. Each missed component = **−1 to −1.5 marks**.
> 2. **Not multiplying annual cost by useful life** in TCO problems. The examiner expects *life-cycle* cost, not annual cost.
> 3. **Confusing SLM and WDV depreciation** — write down which method you are using and stick to it.
> 4. **Wrong units in CPKM** — always quote **₹/km**, not ₹ or km alone.
> 5. **In break-even problems, not interpreting the result** — simply giving a number without the managerial implication loses **1–2 marks**.
> 6. **In cost control "list" questions, giving vague answers** like "reduce cost" — the examiner wants **specific** levers (e.g., "tyre pressure monitoring," "route optimization software," "driver incentive scheme").
> 7. **Skipping the salvage value subtraction** in TCO — this is the most common single error in 14-mark problems.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Recall Bullet Checklist

- **TCO** = full life-cycle cost minus salvage value. It has **six additions** and **one subtraction**.
- **CPKM** = Total Cost ÷ Total km. It is the operational efficiency yardstick.
- **Fixed Cost per km decreases with utilization**; Variable Cost per km is constant. Therefore, **CPKM falls as km increase**.
- **Fuel + Maintenance + Driver** together dominate ~ 70% of TCO in most commercial fleets.
- **Depreciation:** Use **SLM** for accounting simplicity; use **WDV** for income tax (Section 44AE for goods carriages).
- **Break-even km** in own-vs-lease problems is found by equating total costs: $\frac{\Delta FC}{\Delta VC} = km_{BE}$.
- **Telematics and route optimization** are the modern, technology-driven cost control levers — frequently asked in KTU answers.
- **Right-sizing** the fleet (matching vehicle capacity to load) eliminates empty-running cost.
- **Outsourcing** is preferred when in-house utilization is **< 60%–70%** of capacity.
- **Cost Variance = Actual − Budgeted**; positive variance = overspend (red flag).
- **Fuel Cost per km** = Price per Litre ÷ Mileage — a 1 km/L improvement at 1,00,000 km/year saves lakhs annually.
- **Cost control is a continuous cycle (PDCA)** — Plan, Do, Check, Act — not a one-time project.
- **The four biggest levers** (in order of impact) are: **(i) Fuel, (ii) Maintenance, (iii) Driver Behaviour, (iv) Route/Load Planning**.
- **Salvage value must always be subtracted**; disposal cost must always be added.
- **Downtime cost** = days off-road × daily lost revenue — often forgotten, but **always** counts in TCO.
- **TCO framework** is the recommended approach in KTU Module 4 — examiners expect you to **frame any cost question in TCO language**.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Day Strategy:** For any 14-mark cost problem, always start by writing the **TCO equation symbolically**, then **substitute values step by step**, **show intermediate arithmetic**, and **end with a managerial interpretation**. Examiners reward clarity and structure as much as the final numerical answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Budgeting and Financial Planning for Fleets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT633 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Budgeting and Financial Planning for Fleets # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Fleet Budgeting and Financial Planning** is the systematic process of forecasting, allocating, monitoring, and controlling the monetary resources required to acquire, operate, maintain, and replace a fleet of vehicles over a defined planning horizon. It integrates **capital expenditure (CapEx)**, **operating expenditure (OpEx)**, and **strategic replacement cycles** into a unified financial framework that aligns the fleet's lifecycle cost with the organization's operational and service-level objectives. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard Terminology):** > *Budgeting and Financial Planning for Fleets* refers to the engineering-economic methodology used by transport managers to determine the **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)**, **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**, **Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC)**, **depreciation schedules**, and **fleet replacement timing** to ensure optimal utilization of capital and minimum cost per kilometre (or per hour) of operation. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Family Monthly Budget" Imagine a family planning a one-year household budget: - **Fixed costs** are like the **EMI on a home loan** — the family MUST pay it every month whether the house is used or not. - **Variable costs** are like **groceries and electricity** — they rise when children study more (analogous to higher fleet utilization). - **Replacement reserve** is like a **sinking fund** the family sets aside monthly so that when the car breaks down after 7 years, money is already saved. - **Forecasting** is like the family checking last year's bills (actuals) to estimate this year's spending. - **Variance analysis** is the moment at month-end when the family compares "planned vs. actual" grocery spend. **The fleet is the family's garage of cars, and the financial plan is the household ledger that keeps the garage running without bankruptcy.** > [!NOTE] > **Key Metrics in Fleet Finance (KTU High-Yield Terms):** > - **TCO** = Total Cost of Ownership (₹/vehicle-life) > - **CPK** = Cost Per Kilometre (₹/km) > - **CPH** = Cost Per Hour (₹/hr) > - **CRF** = Capital Recovery Factor > - **SFF** = Sinking Fund Factor > - **PV** = Present Value, **FV** = Future Value ## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations In KTU's Outcome-Based Education framework, this module is mapped to the following Course Outcomes (typical for PEAUT633): - **CO4 (Module 4):** *Apply economic engineering principles to evaluate fleet replacement, depreciation, and cost-control decisions.* - This topic is **guaranteed** to appear in Part A (3-mark definitions) and frequently in Part B (14-mark numerical/analytical problems). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Fleet Financial Planning ### Pillar 1 — **Capital Budgeting (Acquisition & Replacement)** Capital budgeting answers: *"Should we buy, lease, or replace the vehicle now or later?"* It uses **time-value-of-money** techniques. - **Present Worth (PW)** analysis - **Annual Equivalent Cost (AEC)** or **EUAC** analysis - **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** — used when comparing revenue-generating fleets - **Benefit-Cost (B/C) Ratio** — used in public transport fleets ### Pillar 2 — **Operating Budget (Day-to-Day Cost Control)** This is the **recurring annual budget** that keeps the wheels turning. It includes: - **Fuel cost** (largest OpEx — typically **40–55%** of operating cost) - **Maintenance & repair (M&R) cost** - **Tyre cost** - **Driver wages & benefits** - **Insurance & taxes** - **Depot overheads** (rent, utilities, supervisor salaries) - **Administrative costs** ### Pillar 3 — **Disposal & Replacement Reserve** This is the **strategic reserve** built to fund future fleet replacement. It is mathematically modeled as a **Sinking Fund**. ## 2.2 Time-Value-of-Money Framework A rupee today is worth more than a rupee next year because of **earning potential** (interest) and **inflation**. Let: - $P$ = Present Value (initial outlay) - $F$ = Future Value (worth after $n$ years at interest $i$) - $A$ = Uniform Annual Amount (yearly series) - $i$ = Annual interest rate (in decimal, e.g., 0.10 for 10%) - $n$ = Number of years (life) ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize the table below. These six factors appear in 90% of KTU Part B numericals.** | Symbol | Factor Name | Formula | Purpose in Fleet Finance | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **F/P** | Single Payment Compound Amount | $(1+i)^{n}$ | Find future value of today's purchase price | | **P/F** | Single Payment Present Worth | $(1+i)^{-n}$ | Discount future resale/scrap value to today | | **A/P** | Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) | $\dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | Convert purchase price into yearly instalment | | **P/A** | Uniform Series Present Worth | $\dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$ | Capitalize annual operating cost into today's worth | | **A/F** | Sinking Fund Factor (SFF) | $\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | Yearly deposit to accumulate a future replacement fund | | **F/A** | Uniform Series Future Worth | $\dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i}$ | Total value of yearly deposits after $n$ years | > [!NOTE] > **Useful Engineering Identities:** > - $(A/P,i,n) \;=\; (A/F,i,n) \;+\; i$ > - $(A/F,i,n) \;=\; (A/P,i,n) \;-\; i$ > - When $i=0$ (no interest), $\;A/P \;=\; 1/n\;\;$ and $\;\;A/F \;=\; 1/n$ ## 2.4 Depreciation Methods (KTU Frequently Tested) | Method | Formula / Logic | Best Used For | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Straight Line (SLN)** | $D \;=\; \dfrac{P-S}{n}$ | Stable assets, accounting simplicity | | **Sum of Years' Digits (SYD)** | $D_t \;=\; \dfrac{n-t+1}{\sum_{k=1}^{n}k} \cdot (P-S)$ | Higher early-year wear (good for trucks) | | **Declining Balance (DB)** | $D_t \;=\; \alpha \cdot (P - \text{Accum. Dep.})$ | Assets that lose value rapidly (tax-favoured) | | **Double Declining Balance (DDB)** | $\alpha = 2/n$ (so $D_t = \frac{2}{n}\cdot \text{Book Value}$) | Common for new vehicles | Where: - $P$ = Original cost, $S$ = Salvage value, $n$ = Useful life in years - $D_t$ = Depreciation in year $t$ ## 2.5 Real-World Utility A transport manager uses this framework to: 1. **Decide replacement age** — the year where EUAC is minimum. 2. **Tender pricing** — accurately compute the per-km rate to quote to clients. 3. **Tax planning** — select a depreciation method that maximizes allowable expense. 4. **Loan structuring** — choose between EMI (CRF) and bullet payment with sinking fund. 5. **Make-vs-buy vs. lease** — convert all options to **EUAC** and pick the lowest. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) **Starting Premise:** You borrow ₹$P$ at interest rate $i$ and must repay it over $n$ years in equal annual instalments $A$. **Step 1 — Value at end of Year 1:** The loan $P$ grows to $P(1+i)$ at the end of year 1 before the first payment. **Step 2 — Series of payments:** The borrower pays $A$ at the end of each of the $n$ years. The future value of this annuity at the end of year $n$ is: $$F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i}$$ **Step 3 — Equate the two future values:** The accumulated loan amount must equal the accumulated payments: $$P(1+i)^{n} = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $A$ in terms of $P$:** $$\frac{A}{P} = \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$$ **Step 5 — Boxed result (the CRF used by every KTU examiner):** $$\boxed{\;(A/P, i, n) \;=\; \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}\;}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in fleet finance:** If a bus costs ₹25,00,000 and has a 10-year life with 8% interest, the yearly cost of *just the capital* is $A = 25{,}00{,}000 \times (A/P,\,0.08,\,10)$, which equals ₹3{,}72{,}188 per year. This is the floor of any realistic fare calculation. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Straight Line Depreciation Schedule A delivery van is purchased for ₹8,00,000 with a salvage value of ₹1,00,000 after 7 years. **Step 1 — Compute depreciable base:** $$\text{Base} = P - S = 8{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}00{,}000$$ **Step 2 — Compute annual depreciation (SLN):** $$D = \frac{P - S}{n} = \frac{7{,}00{,}000}{7} = 1{,}00{,}000 \;\text{per year}$$ **Step 3 — Build the depreciation book-value schedule:** $$\text{Book Value at year } t \;=\; P - t \cdot D$$ | Year $t$ | Depreciation $D_t$ (₹) | Accumulated Dep. (₹) | Book Value at year-end (₹) | | :---: | ---: | ---: | ---: | | 1 | 1,00,000 | 1,00,000 | 7,00,000 | | 2 | 1,00,000 | 2,00,000 | 6,00,000 | | 3 | 1,00,000 | 3,00,000 | 5,00,000 | | 4 | 1,00,000 | 4,00,000 | 4,00,000 | | 5 | 1,00,000 | 5,00,000 | 3,00,000 | | 6 | 1,00,000 | 6,00,000 | 2,00,000 | | 7 | 1,00,000 | 7,00,000 | 1,00,000 (salvage) | > [!NOTE] > The KTU examiner expects the **book value = salvage value** in the final year. A common error is to extend the schedule one row beyond $n$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC) of a Fleet Vehicle This is the most important KTU numerical type. Given a vehicle with purchase price $P$, salvage $S$, life $n$, annual operating cost $O$, and interest $i$, the EUAC is derived below. **Step 1 — Annualize the capital investment using CRF:** $$\text{Capital Recovery} = P \cdot (A/P, i, n)$$ **Step 2 — Subtract the annualized salvage (it offsets cost each year):** $$\text{Salvage Credit} = S \cdot (A/F, i, n)$$ **Step 3 — Add the uniform annual operating cost $O$ (already per year):** **Step 4 — Final boxed expression:** $$\boxed{\;\text{EUAC} \;=\; P \cdot (A/P, i, n) \;-\; S \cdot (A/F, i, n) \;+\; O\;}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner Insight:** $O$ in Step 3 is already a uniform annual amount, so it does NOT need a factor. Students often incorrectly apply $(P/A)$ to $O$. This costs 1 mark per wrong use. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Full Fleet Vehicle EUAC **Problem:** A transport company buys a truck for ₹20,00,000. Expected salvage after 6 years is ₹4,00,000. Annual operating cost is ₹3,50,000. Interest rate is 10% per annum. Find EUAC and CPK if the truck runs 30,000 km/year. **Step 1 — Find CRF $(A/P, 0.10, 6)$:** $$(A/P, 0.10, 6) = \frac{0.10 \times (1.10)^{6}}{(1.10)^{6} - 1}$$ $$\Rightarrow \;(1.10)^{6} = 1.771561$$ $$\Rightarrow \;(A/P, 0.10, 6) = \frac{0.10 \times 1.771561}{1.771561 - 1} = \frac{0.177156}{0.771561} = 0.22961$$ **Step 2 — Find SFF $(A/F, 0.10, 6)$:** $$(A/F, 0.10, 6) = \frac{0.10}{0.771561} = 0.12961$$ **Step 3 — Capital recovery component:** $$20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.22961 = 4{,}59{,}220 \;\text{₹/year}$$ **Step 4 — Salvage credit component:** $$4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.12961 = 51{,}844 \;\text{₹/year}$$ **Step 5 — Total EUAC:** $$\text{EUAC} = 4{,}59{,}220 - 51{,}844 + 3{,}50{,}000 = 7{,}57{,}376 \;\text{₹/year}$$ **Step 6 — Cost per kilometre (CPK):** $$\text{CPK} = \frac{7{,}57{,}376}{30{,}000} = 25.25 \;\text{₹/km}$$ **Final Answer (boxed):** $$\boxed{\;\text{EUAC} = ₹7{,}57{,}376 \text{/year} \quad ; \quad \text{CPK} = ₹25.25 \text{/km}\;}$$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Method | Decision Question | Best Method | Justification | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Replace vehicle now or next year? | **Marginal EUAC comparison** | Identifies the lowest-cost replacement cycle | | Buy vs. Lease (rent)? | **EUAC of both options** | Normalizes cash flows to a common annual basis | | Project selection (multiple routes)? | **B/C Ratio or NPV** | Public transport projects use B/C $\geq 1$ as a benchmark | | Tax-shield optimization? | **Accelerated depreciation (DDB)** | Higher expense in early years → lower taxable income early | | Build a replacement reserve? | **Sinking Fund (A/F)** | Compulsory annual deposit accumulates to future price | --- ## 3.6 Optimal Replacement Age — Logic The optimal replacement age $n^{*}$ is the year where **EUAC is minimum**. Logic: $$\text{EUAC}(n) = P(A/P,i,n) - S(A/F,i,n) + \text{AMC}(n) + \text{Fuel}(n)$$ As $n$ increases, $(A/P)$ decreases (capital is spread over more years) but $\text{AMC}$ and $\text{Fuel}$ (maintenance & fuel) increase steeply. The **minimum** of the curve gives $n^{*}$. > [!NOTE] > KTU may ask: "Justify with reasoning whether to replace at year 5 or year 7." Compute EUAC at both years and choose the lower. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Components of Fleet Financial Planning ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fleet Manager
Strategic Goal] --> B[Capital Budget
Acquisition + Replacement] A --> C[Operating Budget
Recurring Annual Costs] A --> D[Reserve Fund
Sinking Fund for Future Replace] B --> E[Time Value of Money
CRF and PW Analysis] C --> F[Cost per KM
CPK Calculation] D --> G[Sinking Fund Factor
A over F Computation] E --> H[EUAC Decision Matrix] F --> H G --> H H --> I[Optimal Replacement Age N Star] H --> J[Make or Buy or Lease Decision] H --> K[Fare and Tender Pricing] I --> L[Annual Budget Approval] J --> L K --> L L --> M[Variance Analysis
Planned vs Actual] M --> N[Corrective Action and Rebudget] ``` ## 4.2 Flowchart — Annual Budgeting Cycle for a Fleet ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Step1: Collect Last Year Actuals] --> S2[Step2: Forecast Demand KM] S2 --> S3[Step3: Estimate Fuel and Wage Inflation] S3 --> S4[Step4: Compute Capital Recovery CRF] S4 --> S5[Step5: Compute Sinking Fund SFF] S5 --> S6[Step6: Prepare OpEx Budget Lines] S6 --> S7[Step7: Roll Up to Total Annual Budget] S7 --> S8[Step8: Management Approval] S8 --> S9[Step9: Monthly Tracking and Variance] S9 --> S10[Step10: Mid Year Review and Rebase] S10 --> S1 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Capital Recovery Concept ```mermaid flowchart LR PV[Present Value P
Purchase Price] -->|Multiply by A over P factor| CRF[CRF Table Lookup] CRF --> ANN[Equivalent Annual Cost A] SV[Salvage Value S] -->|Multiply by A over F factor| SFF[SFF Table Lookup] SFF --> SVC[Annual Salvage Credit] ANN --> NET[Net EUAC] OPEX[Annual Operating Cost O] --> NET SVC --> NET NET --> CPKOUT[Cost per KM and Cost per Hour] ``` ## 4.4 Decision Tree — Buy vs. Lease vs. Outsource ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Need Vehicle
for More than 3 Years?} -->|Yes| Q2{Annual Utilization
Greater than 60 percent?} Q1 -->|No| R1[Lease from OEM
Lower Capital Lock] Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Capital Available
Without Loan Stress?} Q2 -->|No| R1 Q3 -->|Yes| R2[Buy and Operate
Lowest EUAC] Q3 -->|No| R3[Lease with Maintenance
Bundle] R1 --> FINAL[Compute EUAC of Each Option] R2 --> FINAL R3 --> FINAL FINAL --> DEC[Pick Lowest EUAC] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **How to read the diagrams in an exam:** The KTU examiner accepts labelled block diagrams as full-mark answers for "Explain the budgeting process" type 7-mark questions. Use **rectangles for stages**, **diamonds for decisions**, and **arrows for flow direction**. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** *Define the term **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** used in fleet financial planning. Write its formula and state its significance.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** The Capital Recovery Factor $(A/P, i, n)$ converts a present lump-sum investment into an equivalent uniform annual series of payments over $n$ years at interest rate $i$ per year. > **Formula (1 mark):** > $$(A/P, i, n) = \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$$ > **Significance (1 mark):** It allows a fleet manager to express the one-time capital cost of a vehicle as a yearly cost, making it directly comparable to the recurring operating cost and enabling EUAC-based replacement decisions. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest Paper]** *Distinguish between **Capital Expenditure (CapEx)** and **Operating Expenditure (OpEx)** in fleet management. Give two examples of each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | CapEx | OpEx | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Nature (1 mark)** | Long-term, one-time or infrequent investment creating an asset | Recurring, short-term expense for day-to-day running | | **Accounting treatment** | Capitalized and depreciated over asset life | Fully expensed in the year incurred | | **Examples (1 mark each pair)** | 1. Purchase of new buses; 2. Construction of depot; 3. Acquisition of ERP software | 1. Diesel fuel; 2. Driver salary; 3. Routine servicing; 4. Insurance premium | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse $A/P$ with $A/F$.** CRF is used for purchase price; SFF is used for salvage. Mixing them is a 2-mark deduction. > 2. **Always show the substitution step** for $(1+i)^{n}$ before applying the formula. Writing only the final answer earns **zero marks** for the derivation step. > 3. **Operating cost $O$ does NOT need a factor.** It is already annual. Using $(P/A)$ on $O$ is a 1-mark error. > 4. **Salvage must be SUBTRACTED**, not added, in the EUAC formula. A common sign error costs 1 mark. > 5. **Retain 2 decimal places** in CPK; KTU valuators penalize excessive rounding (e.g., 25 instead of 25.25). --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Suggested]** A logistics firm is evaluating two trucks **Truck-X** and **Truck-Y** for its long-haul fleet. The data is given below: | Parameter | Truck-X | Truck-Y | | :--- | :---: | :---: | | Purchase Price (₹) | 22,00,000 | 25,00,000 | | Useful Life (years) | 6 | 7 | | Salvage Value (₹) | 3,00,000 | 4,00,000 | | Annual Operating Cost (₹) | 4,00,000 | 3,60,000 | | Annual Kilometres Run | 40,000 | 45,000 | Assume the interest rate is **9% per annum**. #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** *Compute the **Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC)** for both trucks.* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — For Truck-X (n = 6, i = 0.09):** $$(1.09)^{6} = 1.67710$$ $$(A/P, 0.09, 6) = \frac{0.09 \times 1.67710}{1.67710 - 1} = \frac{0.15094}{0.67710} = 0.22292$$ $$(A/F, 0.09, 6) = \frac{0.09}{0.67710} = 0.13292$$ $$\text{EUAC}_X = 22{,}00{,}000 \times 0.22292 - 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.13292 + 4{,}00{,}000$$ $$\text{EUAC}_X = 4{,}90{,}424 - 39{,}876 + 4{,}00{,}000 = ₹8{,}50{,}548 \;\text{per year}$$ **Step 2 — For Truck-Y (n = 7, i = 0.09):** $$(1.09)^{7} = 1.82804$$ $$(A/P, 0.09, 7) = \frac{0.09 \times 1.82804}{1.82804 - 1} = \frac{0.16452}{0.82804} = 0.19869$$ $$(A/F, 0.09, 7) = \frac{0.09}{0.82804} = 0.10869$$ $$\text{EUAC}_Y = 25{,}00{,}000 \times 0.19869 - 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10869 + 3{,}60{,}000$$ $$\text{EUAC}_Y = 4{,}96{,}725 - 43{,}476 + 3{,}60{,}000 = ₹8{,}13{,}249 \;\text{per year}$$ #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** *Compute the **Cost Per Kilometre (CPK)** for both trucks and recommend which truck should be purchased. Justify your answer.* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — CPK of Truck-X:** $$\text{CPK}_X = \frac{8{,}50{,}548}{40{,}000} = ₹21.26 \;\text{per km}$$ **Step 2 — CPK of Truck-Y:** $$\text{CPK}_Y = \frac{8{,}13{,}249}{45{,}000} = ₹18.07 \;\text{per km}$$ **Step 3 — Comparison Table (1 mark):** | Metric | Truck-X | Truck-Y | | :--- | :---: | :---: | | EUAC (₹/year) | 8,50,548 | **8,13,249 (lower)** | | CPK (₹/km) | 21.26 | **18.07 (lower)** | **Step 4 — Recommendation (1 mark):** > Truck-Y is recommended because it has **both a lower EUAC and a lower CPK**, despite a higher purchase price. The longer useful life (7 vs 6 years) and higher annual utilization spread the capital cost more efficiently. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Retest]** A municipal corporation plans to procure **20 buses** for its city transport service. Each bus costs **₹35,00,000** with a useful life of **10 years** and a salvage value of **₹5,00,000**. The annual operating cost per bus is **₹6,50,000** (driver, fuel, maintenance, insurance, tyres). The corporation wants to set up a **Sinking Fund** to replace the entire fleet at the end of 10 years. The MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return) is **8% per annum**. #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** *Compute the **Total EUAC** for the 20-bus fleet and the **annual sinking fund deposit per bus** required to replace the bus after 10 years.* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — EUAC per bus (n = 10, i = 0.08):** $$(1.08)^{10} = 2.15892$$ $$(A/P, 0.08, 10) = \frac{0.08 \times 2.15892}{2.15892 - 1} = \frac{0.17271}{1.15892} = 0.14903$$ $$(A/F, 0.08, 10) = \frac{0.08}{1.15892} = 0.06903$$ $$\text{EUAC per bus} = 35{,}00{,}000 \times 0.14903 - 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.06903 + 6{,}50{,}000$$ $$\text{EUAC per bus} = 5{,}16{,}605 - 34{,}515 + 6{,}50{,}000 = ₹11{,}32{,}090 \;\text{per bus per year}$$ **Step 2 — Total EUAC for 20 buses:** $$\text{Total EUAC} = 20 \times 11{,}32{,}090 = ₹2{,}26{,}41{,}800 \;\text{per year}$$ **Step 3 — Sinking Fund deposit per bus per year:** To replace 1 bus (cost ₹35,00,000 at year 10): $$A_{\text{sink}} = 35{,}00{,}000 \times (A/F, 0.08, 10) = 35{,}00{,}000 \times 0.06903 = ₹2{,}41{,}605 \;\text{per bus per year}$$ #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** *If the corporation also needs to create a **depreciation reserve** using the **Straight Line Method (SLN)** to fully recover the capital (₹35,00,000 − ₹5,00,000) over 10 years, calculate the **annual SLN depreciation** per bus and find the **total yearly provision** (EUAC + Sinking Fund + SLN Depreciation) the corporation must budget for the fleet.* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Annual SLN Depreciation per bus:** $$D_{\text{SLN}} = \frac{35{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000}{10} = \frac{30{,}00{,}000}{10} = ₹3{,}00{,}000 \;\text{per bus per year}$$ **Step 2 — Total yearly provision per bus:** $$\text{Total Provision} = \text{EUAC} + A_{\text{sink}} + D_{\text{SLN}}$$ $$= 11{,}32{,}090 + 2{,}41{,}605 + 3{,}00{,}000 = ₹16{,}73{,}695 \;\text{per bus per year}$$ **Step 3 — Total yearly provision for 20 buses:** $$\text{Fleet Total} = 20 \times 16{,}73{,}695 = ₹3{,}34{,}73{,}900 \;\text{per year}$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):** > The corporation's annual transport budget must allocate at least **₹3.35 crore per year** to keep the 20-bus fleet operational, debt-free, and fully replaceable at the end of 10 years. The Sinking Fund covers the *future replacement price*; the SLN Depreciation covers the *present book-value recovery*; and the EUAC covers the *current annual cost of capital and operations*. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Module 4 (Budgeting & Financial Planning):** - **Three pillars** of fleet finance: **Capital Budgeting**, **Operating Budget**, **Sinking Fund Reserve**. - Master the **six time-value-of-money factors**: F/P, P/F, A/P, P/A, A/F, F/A. KTU expects the formula *and* the substituted numerical answer. - **EUAC formula (memorize verbatim):** $$\text{EUAC} = P \cdot (A/P, i, n) - S \cdot (A/F, i, n) + O$$ - **CPK formula:** $$\text{CPK} = \frac{\text{EUAC}}{\text{Annual Kilometres Run}}$$ - **Sinking Fund annual deposit** to accumulate $F$ in $n$ years: $$A = F \cdot (A/F, i, n)$$ - **SLN Depreciation:** $D = (P - S)/n$. - **DDB Depreciation rate:** $\alpha = 2/n$ applied on *diminishing book value*. - **Replacement decision rule:** Choose the year with the **lowest EUAC**. - **Sign convention:** Purchase price $P$ is a **cost (+)**; Salvage $S$ is a **credit (−)**; Operating cost $O$ is a **cost (+)**. - **Unit discipline:** Always quote EUAC in **₹/year** and CPK in **₹/km**. Mixing units is a 1-mark deduction. - **Real-world benchmarks:** Fuel ≈ 40–55% of OpEx; Tyre ≈ 5–8%; Maintenance ≈ 15–20%; Driver ≈ 15–25%. - **Tax tip (DDB > SLN):** Double Declining Balance gives higher expense in early years → larger tax shield early. - **Public transport project viability test:** Benefit-Cost Ratio $\geq 1.0$ is acceptable; $\geq 1.5$ is desirable. - **Make vs. Buy vs. Lease:** Always convert all options to **EUAC** on a common horizon before comparison. - **Variance Analysis:** Plan vs. Actual; flag any line item with $\geq 10\%$ deviation for management review. > [!NOTE] > **Pro-Tip for KTU Day:** In 14-mark numericals, KTU valuators award **2 marks for the formula**, **2 marks for the substitution**, and **3 marks for the final answer** of each major computation. Skipping the substitution step is the single biggest mark-loser reported by past batches.

topics.title: Environmental Considerations - Environmental Impact of Fleets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT633 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Environmental Considerations - Environmental Impact of Fleets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Environmental Impact of Fleets** refers to the cumulative effect of a group of motor vehicles (commercial, passenger, or mixed) on the natural environment across their entire life cycle — encompassing raw material extraction, vehicle manufacturing, fuel production, operational use (tailpipe and non-tailpipe emissions), and end-of-life disposal/recycling.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the environmental impact of a fleet is quantified through a basket of metrics:
- **Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Emissions** — measured in **grams of CO₂-equivalent per kilometre (gCO₂e/km)** or **tonnes of CO₂ per year**.
- **Air Pollutant Emissions** — measured in **g/km** for CO, HC, NOₓ, and **Particulate Matter (PM)**.
- **Energy Intensity** — measured in **MJ (megajoules) per 100 km** or **litres of fuel equivalent**.
- **Noise Pollution** — measured in **dB(A)** under pass-by conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - **Tailpipe Emissions** → pollutants released from the exhaust during vehicle operation.
> - **Well-to-Wheel (WTW) Emissions** → total emissions from fuel extraction ("Well") to vehicle motion ("Wheel"), including refining, transport, and combustion.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a fleet of 50 delivery trucks as a **herd of elephants walking through a forest**. Each elephant leaves footprints (carbon footprint), drops waste (emissions), makes noise (noise pollution), and disturbs soil (tyre wear particles). A small herd has minor impact, but a large fleet operating over thousands of kilometres creates an environmental footprint equivalent to a small town's pollution output.

**Simple Analogy — The "Fuel → Smoke → Atmosphere" Pipeline:**

```
Crude Oil → Refinery → Fuel Tank → Engine Combustion → Tailpipe → Atmosphere
   (Well)              (Fuel Production)        (Tank-to-Wheel)
```

Every arrow in this chain contributes to the fleet's environmental impact. The *fleet manager's* job is to reduce the total length and weight of this chain — by improving engine efficiency, switching to cleaner fuels, or downsizing the fleet.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Hook:** A single heavy-duty truck (≈ 12 tonnes GVW) emits roughly **60–100 times more PM₂.₅ per kilometre** than a typical passenger car. Hence, fleet-level environmental decisions have a multiplier effect compared to individual vehicles.

---

### 1.3 Key Environmental Pollutant Categories

| Pollutant | Symbol | Source | Health / Environmental Effect |
|-----------|--------|--------|------------------------------|
| Carbon Dioxide | CO₂ | Complete combustion of fossil fuel | Primary GHG → climate change |
| Carbon Monoxide | CO | Incomplete combustion | Toxic, binds haemoglobin |
| Unburnt Hydrocarbons | HC | Fuel not fully combusted | Smog, carcinogenic |
| Oxides of Nitrogen | NOₓ | High-temperature combustion | Acid rain, smog, respiratory disease |
| Particulate Matter | PM (PM₁₀, PM₂.₅) | Diesel soot, tyre/brake wear | Lung cancer, cardiovascular issues |
| Sulphur Dioxide | SO₂ | Sulphur in diesel/coal | Acid rain |
| Black Carbon (Soot) | BC | Incomplete diesel combustion | Climate forcing agent |

> [!NOTE]
> **CO₂ is not a "toxic" pollutant** in the local-air-quality sense — it is a **greenhouse gas** that causes global warming. This is a frequently-asked KTU conceptual distinction.

---

### 1.4 Regulatory & Standard Reference Framework

Fleet environmental impact is governed by:

1. **Bharat Stage (BS) Emission Norms** — India (BS-VI equivalent to Euro 6, implemented April 2020).
2. **Euro Emission Standards** — European Union (Euro 1 → Euro 6/7).
3. **Corporate Average Fuel Economy (CAFE) Norms** — India (Phase-II effective 2022–23).
4. **UNFCCC Paris Agreement Targets** — global GHG reduction commitments.
5. **FAME India Scheme (Faster Adoption and Manufacturing of Electric Vehicles)** — government push for EV adoption.
6. **ISO 14001** — Environmental Management Systems standard for fleet operators.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Three Pillars of Fleet Environmental Impact

The environmental impact of a fleet can be decomposed into three analytically distinct layers:

**Pillar 1: Fuel-Related Impact (Operational Phase)**
- Governed by **fuel type** (diesel, petrol, CNG, LPG, electric, hydrogen, biofuel blend) and **engine efficiency**.
- Dominant contributor: **tailpipe CO₂** and **NOₓ**.
- Mitigation: route optimisation, eco-driving training, vehicle right-sizing.

**Pillar 2: Vehicle Lifecycle Impact (Embodied Phase)**
- Includes manufacturing (steel, aluminium, battery production), maintenance (oil changes, tyre replacement), and end-of-life (scrap, battery recycling).
- For EVs, the **battery production phase** can emit **150–200 gCO₂e/km equivalent** before the vehicle even moves.
