- Mitigation: vehicle longevity, remanufactured parts, circular economy principles.

**Pillar 3: Indirect/Upstream Impact (Well-to-Tank)**
- Fuel extraction, refining, transport losses.
- Electricity generation mix (for EVs) — **coal-heavy grids** reduce EV's net climate benefit.
- Mitigation: renewable energy procurement, green logistics contracts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** A complete answer on fleet environmental impact MUST cover at least the **operational (TTW)** and **upstream (WTT)** phases. Purely tailpipe-only analysis will fetch partial marks.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $E_{CO_2} = F \times \rho \times \alpha$ | Tailpipe CO₂ = Fuel mass × Density × Carbon-content oxidation factor | kg |
| 2 | $CO_2 = m_{fuel} \times 3.169$ (diesel) | Diesel emits ~3.169 kg CO₂ per kg of fuel burned | kg CO₂ |
| 3 | $CO_2 = m_{fuel} \times 3.169$ (petrol) → use 3.169 | Petrol emits ~3.169 kg CO₂ per kg fuel | kg CO₂ |
| 4 | $FC = \frac{100}{FE}$ | Fuel Consumption = 100 ÷ Fuel Economy | L/100 km |
| 5 | $E_{fleet} = N \times D \times FC \times EF$ | Fleet emissions = Vehicles × Distance × Fuel/100 km × Emission Factor | kg |
| 6 | $gCO_2/km = \frac{E_{CO_2}}{D}$ | Specific CO₂ per km | gCO₂/km |
| 7 | $C_{WTW} = C_{WTT} + C_{TTW}$ | Well-to-Wheel = Well-to-Tank + Tank-to-Wheel | gCO₂e/km |
| 8 | $CO_2e = \sum_i (E_i \times GWP_i)$ | CO₂-equivalent = Σ (Mass × Global Warming Potential) | kg CO₂e |
| 9 | $NEE = \frac{CO_2}{FE \times \rho_{fuel}}$ | Normalised Energy Economy: CO₂ output per unit energy delivered | g/MJ |
| 10 | $PM_{fleet} = N \times D \times EF_{PM}$ | Fleet particulate matter emissions | grams |
| 11 | $TCO_{env} = C_{fuel} + C_{emission} + C_{compliance}$ | Total Cost of Ownership with environmental cost overlay | ₹/km |

> [!NOTE]
> **Emission Factor Reference Values (KTU Standard Data):**
> - Diesel: **2.68 kg CO₂/L** (≈ 73.4 gCO₂/MJ)
> - Petrol: **2.31 kg CO₂/L** (≈ 69.3 gCO₂/MJ)
> - CNG: **2.0 kg CO₂/kg** (≈ 56.0 gCO₂/MJ)
> - LPG: **1.66 kg CO₂/kg**
> - Grid electricity (India average, 2023): **~0.72 kg CO₂/kWh**

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The above formulas are foundational for:

- **Fleet Sustainability Audits** → ISO 14064 carbon footprinting.
- **Green Logistics KPI Tracking** → "CO₂ per tonne-km" benchmarking.
- **ESG Reporting** → mandatory Scope 1, Scope 2, and Scope 3 emissions disclosure.
- **Tender Compliance** → government e-procurement mandates green fleet criteria.
- **Carbon Credit Calculation** → under voluntary carbon markets (Verra, Gold Standard).

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Application:** A logistics company with 100 trucks, each running 80,000 km/year at 5 km/L diesel, would emit approximately:
> $E = 100 \times 80{,}000 \times (100/5) \times 2.68 = 100 \times 80{,}000 \times 20 \times 2.68 \times 10^{-3} = 4{,}288$ tonnes CO₂/year

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Problems

### 3.1 Derivation: From Fuel Mass to Tailpipe CO₂

The combustion of diesel (approximated as **dodecane, C₁₂H₂₆**) follows the stoichiometric equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Write balanced combustion of dodecane} \\
C_{12}H_{26} + \tfrac{37}{2}O_2 \rightarrow 12\,CO_2 + 13\,H_2O \\
\\
\text{Step 2: Compute molecular weights} \\
M_{C_{12}H_{26}} = 12(12) + 26(1) = 170\ g/mol \\
M_{12CO_2} = 12 \times 44 = 528\ g/mol \\
\\
\text{Step 3: Theoretical CO₂ per gram of fuel} \\
\text{Ratio} = \frac{528}{170} = 3.105\ g\ CO_2/g\ fuel \\
\\
\text{Step 4: Apply real-world correction} \\
\text{Actual diesel has impurities (S, aromatics)} \Rightarrow \text{multiply by } 1.02 \\
EF_{CO_2,\text{diesel}} = 3.105 \times 1.02 = 3.169\ kg\ CO_2/kg\ fuel
\end{aligned}
$$

This yields the **IPCC standard emission factor** of **3.169 kg CO₂/kg diesel**, which is the value universally cited in KTU reference tables.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Fleet CO₂ Emission Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A delivery fleet consists of **25 light commercial vehicles (LCVs)**. Each vehicle travels **120 km/day** for **300 working days/year**. The fleet's average fuel economy is **14 km/L** on diesel. Calculate:
1. Total annual fuel consumption
2. Total annual CO₂ emissions (tailpipe)
3. Average gCO₂/km per vehicle

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Annual distance per vehicle} \\
D_{annual} = 120 \times 300 = 36{,}000\ km/year \\
\\
\text{Step 2: Annual fuel per vehicle} \\
F_{per\ veh} = \frac{36{,}000}{14} = 2{,}571.43\ L/year \\
\\
\text{Step 3: Total fleet fuel} \\
F_{fleet} = 25 \times 2{,}571.43 = 64{,}285.71\ L/year \\
\\
\text{Step 4: Convert litres to kg (diesel density } \rho = 0.835\ kg/L) \\
m_{fuel} = 64{,}285.71 \times 0.835 = 53{,}678.57\ kg \\
\\
\text{Step 5: Tailpipe CO₂ using emission factor} \\
E_{CO_2} = 53{,}678.57 \times 3.169 = 170{,}107.16\ kg \\
\boxed{E_{CO_2} \approx 170.1\ tonnes\ CO_2/year} \\
\\
\text{Step 6: gCO₂/km per vehicle} \\
\text{Specific} = \frac{E_{CO_2}}{N \times D_{annual}} = \frac{170.107 \times 10^6\ g}{25 \times 36{,}000\ km} \\
\boxed{\text{Specific} = 188.9\ gCO_2/km/vehicle}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Typical Indian LCVs (BS-VI) are rated at **180–210 gCO₂/km**, so our calculation aligns with real-world data.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — EV vs Diesel Lifecycle Comparison

**Problem Statement:**
A fleet operator is considering replacing **10 diesel LCVs** (each running 36,000 km/year) with **10 electric LCVs** (assumed electricity consumption **0.18 kWh/km**). The Indian grid emission factor is **0.72 kg CO₂/kWh**. Manufacturing-phase emissions for diesel LCV ≈ **8 tonnes CO₂e** and for EV ≈ **12 tonnes CO₂e** (battery-heavy). Compare the **5-year lifecycle emissions** of both options.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Diesel operational emissions (per vehicle, 5 years)} \\
D_5 = 36{,}000 \times 5 = 180{,}000\ km \\
E_{diesel,op} = 180{,}000 \times \frac{1}{14} \times 0.835 \times 3.169 \\
= 180{,}000 \times 0.05964 \times 3.169 = 34{,}018\ kg \\
= 34.02\ tonnes\ CO_2 \\
\\
\text{Step 2: Diesel lifecycle (per vehicle)} \\
E_{diesel,LC} = 8 + 34.02 = 42.02\ tonnes\ CO_2e \\
\\
\text{Step 3: EV operational emissions (per vehicle, 5 years)} \\
E_{EV,op} = 180{,}000 \times 0.18 \times 0.72 = 23{,}328\ kg \\
= 23.33\ tonnes\ CO_2 \\
\\
\text{Step 4: EV lifecycle (per vehicle)} \\
E_{EV,LC} = 12 + 23.33 = 35.33\ tonnes\ CO_2e \\
\\
\text{Step 5: Fleet-wide savings (10 vehicles)} \\
\Delta E = 10 \times (42.02 - 35.33) = 66.9\ tonnes\ CO_2e\ \text{saved} \\
\boxed{\text{Reduction} = \frac{66.9}{420.2} \times 100 \approx 15.9\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The 15.9% reduction is **modest** because (a) India's grid is coal-heavy and (b) EV battery manufacturing is emission-intensive. In grids with **>60% renewables**, the same calculation gives 40–55% lifecycle reduction.

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Specific Energy & CO₂ Comparison Across Fuel Types

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Energy density of each fuel} \\
\text{Diesel: } 35.8\ MJ/L,\quad \text{Petrol: } 32.2\ MJ/L,\quad \text{CNG: } 21.5\ MJ/kg \\
\text{Electricity: } 3.6\ MJ/kWh \\
\\
\text{Step 2: CO₂ per MJ of fuel (TTW)} \\
\text{Diesel: } \frac{2.68\ kg}{35.8\ MJ} = 0.0749\ kg/MJ \\
\text{Petrol: } \frac{2.31}{32.2} = 0.0717\ kg/MJ \\
\text{CNG: } \frac{2.0}{21.5} = 0.0930\ kg/MJ\ \text{(note: CNG looks worse at TTW!)} \\
\\
\text{Step 3: Why CNG still wins — add WTT} \\
\text{CNG WTT is much lower than diesel} \Rightarrow \text{WTW CNG is } \sim 20\%\ \text{better}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **This is a KTU-favourite trap question:** "Which fuel has the lowest tailpipe CO₂ per MJ?" — Answer: **Petrol**, NOT CNG. But at Well-to-Wheel level, CNG wins on a per-km basis.

---

### 3.5 Mitigation Strategy Decision Matrix

| Strategy | CO₂ Reduction | Cost Impact | Implementation Difficulty |
|----------|---------------|-------------|---------------------------|
| Eco-driver training | 5–12% | Low (training cost only) | Low |
| Route optimisation (software) | 8–15% | Low–Medium | Low |
| Tyre pressure monitoring | 1–3% | Low | Low |
| Vehicle right-sizing | 10–25% | Medium | Medium |
| Diesel → CNG switch | 15–20% (WTW) | High (initial capex) | High |
| Diesel → Electric switch | 15–55% (grid-dependent) | Very High | Very High |
| Biofuel blending (B20) | 5–15% | Medium | Medium |
| Aerodynamic retrofits | 2–8% (HDV) | Low | Low |
| Idling reduction policy | 3–7% | Low | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Well-to-Wheel emissions" in the context of fleet environmental impact. Why is it a more complete metric than tailpipe emissions alone?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Well-to-Wheel (WTW) emissions refer to the total greenhouse gas and pollutant emissions generated over the **entire fuel cycle** — from raw energy extraction ("Well": crude oil, coal, natural gas) through refining/processing, distribution, and finally the vehicle's actual operation ("Wheel": combustion or electric drive). It is the sum of **Well-to-Tank (WTT)** and **Tank-to-Wheel (TTW)** emissions.

WTW is more complete than tailpipe-only analysis because:
1. It captures **upstream emissions** in fuel production (e.g., refining a barrel of crude releases ~50 kg CO₂e before any fuel is burned).
2. It allows **fair comparison across powertrains** — for example, an EV charged from a coal-heavy grid may have *higher* WTW emissions than a hybrid.
3. It guides **policy decisions** — pushing for renewable energy in the grid, not just tailpipe cleanup.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*List any three major air pollutants emitted by diesel-powered fleets and state one health effect of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Particulate Matter (PM₂.₅)** — penetrates deep into lungs → causes asthma, cardiovascular disease, lung cancer.
2. **Oxides of Nitrogen (NOₓ)** — contributes to smog and acid rain → causes respiratory inflammation, bronchitis.
3. **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** — colourless, odourless toxic gas → binds haemoglobin, reduces oxygen transport, causes headaches and fatigue at low concentrations.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of **carbon footprinting of a commercial vehicle fleet**. Derive the standard formula for calculating annual fleet CO₂ emissions, clearly defining each variable.

**(b) [7 marks]** A logistics company operates **40 heavy commercial vehicles (HCVs)**, each running **120 km/day for 300 days/year**. The fleet uses diesel at **3.5 km/L** average. Calculate:
1. Total annual fuel consumption in litres.
2. Total annual CO₂ emissions in tonnes (use EF = 2.68 kg CO₂/L).
3. Specific CO₂ per km per vehicle in gCO₂/km.
4. If the company replaces all HCVs with **CNG vehicles** (assumed **5.0 km/kg CNG**, EF = 2.0 kg CO₂/kg), compute the annual CO₂ reduction in tonnes.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Carbon Footprinting — Conceptual Explanation [7 marks]:**

Carbon footprinting of a fleet is a structured methodology to **quantify the total greenhouse gas emissions** (expressed as kg CO₂-equivalent) generated by a fleet of vehicles over a defined reporting period (usually one year). It follows the **ISO 14064** framework and the **GHG Protocol Corporate Standard**.

The general formula is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{fleet} = N \times D_{annual} \times FC \times EF_{CO_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $E_{fleet}$ = Annual fleet CO₂ emissions (kg)
- $N$ = Number of vehicles in the fleet
- $D_{annual}$ = Annual distance per vehicle (km)
- $FC$ = Fuel consumption (L/km or kg/km)
- $EF_{CO_2}$ = Emission factor of the fuel (kg CO₂ per L or kg)

For a heterogeneous fleet (mixed vehicle types/fuels), the formula becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{fleet} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i \times D_{i,annual} \times \left(\frac{1}{FE_i}\right) \times EF_{i,CO_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

where summation is over vehicle category $i$. To convert fuel volume to mass, multiply by **fuel density $\rho$** (for diesel: 0.835 kg/L). Scope-3 emissions from upstream fuel production can be added as a **1.2–1.3× multiplier** on the tailpipe value for a complete WTW footprint.

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Annual distance per vehicle} \\
D_{annual} = 120 \times 300 = 36{,}000\ km \\
\\
\text{Step 2: Total fleet distance} \\
D_{fleet} = 40 \times 36{,}000 = 1{,}440{,}000\ km \\
\\
\text{Step 3: Total annual diesel consumption} \\
V_{diesel} = \frac{1{,}440{,}000}{3.5} = 411{,}428.57\ L \\
\boxed{V_{diesel} \approx 4.11 \times 10^5\ L} \\
\\
\text{Step 4: Annual CO₂ from diesel} \\
E_{diesel} = 411{,}428.57 \times 2.68 = 1{,}102{,}628.57\ kg \\
\boxed{E_{diesel} \approx 1{,}102.6\ tonnes\ CO_2} \\
\\
\text{Step 5: Specific CO₂/km} \\
gCO_2/km = \frac{1{,}102{,}628.57 \times 10^3\ g}{1{,}440{,}000\ km} \\
\boxed{\text{Specific} \approx 765.7\ gCO_2/km/vehicle} \\
\\
\text{Step 6: Annual CNG consumption (replacement)} \\
m_{CNG} = \frac{1{,}440{,}000}{5.0} = 288{,}000\ kg \\
E_{CNG} = 288{,}000 \times 2.0 = 576{,}000\ kg = 576\ tonnes \\
\\
\text{Step 7: CO₂ reduction} \\
\Delta E = 1{,}102.6 - 576 = 526.6\ tonnes \\
\boxed{\Delta E \approx 526.6\ tonnes\ CO_2/year\ saved}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **environmental impact of electric vehicle (EV) fleets** versus conventional Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) fleets. Cover: tailpipe emissions, well-to-wheel emissions, lifecycle (manufacturing) emissions, and grid-mix dependency.

**(b) [7 marks]** A city bus fleet of **20 buses** operates **200 km/day** for **330 days/year**. Each diesel bus gives **2.5 km/L**. The fleet's annual CO₂ emission is to be reduced by **at least 40%** by switching to electric buses consuming **1.0 kWh/km**, charged from the local grid (EF = 0.72 kg CO₂/kWh). Calculate:
1. Current annual CO₂ emissions of the diesel bus fleet.
2. Annual CO₂ emissions if fully electrified.
3. Is the 40% reduction target met? If not, suggest two practical measures to achieve it.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) EV vs ICE Fleet — Environmental Comparison [7 marks]:**

| Aspect | ICE Fleet (Diesel) | EV Fleet |
|--------|---------------------|----------|
| **Tailpipe emissions** | High CO₂, NOₓ, PM, CO, HC | **Zero tailpipe** (no combustion) |
| **Well-to-Wheel (WTT+TTW)** | ~200–250 gCO₂e/km | ~50–150 gCO₂e/km (grid dependent) |
| **Manufacturing emissions** | ~6–8 tonnes CO₂e/vehicle | ~10–14 tonnes CO₂e/vehicle (battery) |
| **Maintenance emissions** | Oil, filters, coolant waste | Lower (no oil) but battery degradation |
| **Noise pollution** | 75–85 dB(A) | ~60 dB(A) at low speed |
| **Tyre/brake wear** | Same | Same (slightly higher for heavy EVs) |
| **End-of-life** | Recycling well established | Battery recycling still developing |

**Key insight:** EV's *local* air-quality benefit is absolute (zero tailpipe). However, its *global* climate benefit depends entirely on the **electricity grid carbon intensity**. In India (≈ 70% coal-based), the lifecycle CO₂ saving is **15–30%**. In Norway (>95% hydro), it is **70%+**.

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Annual distance per bus} \\
D_{annual} = 200 \times 330 = 66{,}000\ km \\
\\
\text{Step 2: Total fleet distance} \\
D_{fleet} = 20 \times 66{,}000 = 1{,}320{,}000\ km \\
\\
\text{Step 3: Diesel bus annual CO₂} \\
V_{diesel} = \frac{1{,}320{,}000}{2.5} = 528{,}000\ L \\
E_{diesel} = 528{,}000 \times 2.68 = 1{,}415{,}040\ kg \\
\boxed{E_{diesel} \approx 1{,}415\ tonnes\ CO_2/year} \\
\\
\text{Step 4: Electric bus annual CO₂} \\
E_{diesel} = 1{,}320{,}000 \times 1.0 \times 0.72 = 950{,}400\ kg \\
\boxed{E_{EV} \approx 950.4\ tonnes\ CO_2/year} \\
\\
\text{Step 5: Reduction percentage} \\
\%\text{Reduction} = \frac{1415 - 950.4}{1415} \times 100 = 32.83\% \\
\\
\text{Step 6: Target check} \\
\boxed{32.83\% < 40\%\ \text{Target NOT met}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Practical measures to meet the 40% target:**

1. **Renewable energy procurement:** Install rooftop solar at the bus depot to charge buses. If 30% of charging energy is solar (0 kg CO₂/kWh), the new emissions become: $E = 1{,}320{,}000 \times 0.7 \times 0.72 = 665{,}280\ kg = 665.3\ tonnes$. New reduction = $\frac{1415 - 665.3}{1415} = 53\%$ ✅
2. **Operational efficiency:** Implement regenerative driving training, route optimisation, and AC-load management → can reduce energy consumption from 1.0 kWh/km to ~0.85 kWh/km, dropping emissions to 808 tonnes, giving a 42.9% reduction ✅

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark-Loss Areas):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert L → kg** before applying the emission factor → loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up TTW and WTT** when the question specifies "well-to-wheel" → full marks lost.
> 3. **Ignoring units** in the final answer (writing 765 instead of 765.7 gCO₂/km) → loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Not specifying the fuel type** in lifecycle comparison (diesel vs petrol vs CNG) → loses context marks.
> 5. **Confusing "tonnes" vs "kg"** in the final answer — this is the single most common numerical error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Environmental Impact of Fleets):**

- **Core metric:** CO₂-equivalent emissions measured in **gCO₂e/km** (specific) or **tonnes/year** (absolute).
- **Three lifecycle phases:** WTT (upstream) + TTW (operational) + Embodied (manufacturing/EoL) = **WTW** total.
- **Standard Emission Factors (must memorise):**
  - Diesel: **2.68 kg CO₂/L** (or **3.169 kg CO₂/kg**)
  - Petrol: **2.31 kg CO₂/L**
  - CNG: **2.0 kg CO₂/kg**
  - India grid: **0.72 kg CO₂/kWh**
- **Diesel density:** **ρ = 0.835 kg/L** — always needed for volume→mass conversion.
- **Pollutants framework:** CO₂ (climate), CO (toxic), HC (smog), NOₓ (acid rain, smog), PM (lung damage), SO₂ (acid rain).
- **Regulations in India:** **BS-VI** (since 2020, Euro 6 equivalent), **CAFE Phase-II** norms, **FAME-II** subsidies for EVs.
- **EV's real benefit:** Zero **tailpipe** emissions, but WTW benefit depends on **grid carbon intensity**.
- **Green fleet strategies** (ranked by ROI): eco-driver training → route optimisation → tyre/aero retrofits → alt-fuel switch → electrification.
- **CO₂ reduction equation** (fleet-level): $E_{fleet} = N \times D \times FC \times EF$ — this single formula unlocks ~30% of any numerical question.
- **One-liner definitions to memorise for KTU:**
  - "Carbon footprint" = total GHG emissions, expressed as CO₂e.
  - "Tank-to-Wheel" = emissions from fuel combustion only.
  - "Well-to-Wheel" = complete fuel cycle emissions.
  - "Scope 1 emissions" = direct, company-owned fleet.
  - "Scope 3 emissions" = upstream fuel/electricity supply chain.
  - "PM₂.₅" = particles ≤ 2.5 µm diameter; most dangerous to health.
- **Quick CO₂ estimate (rule of thumb):** 1 litre of diesel ≈ **2.68 kg CO₂**. Memorise this.
- **Specific emissions benchmark (India, BS-VI):**
  - Petrol car: ~150–180 gCO₂/km
  - Diesel car: ~160–200 gCO₂/km
  - LCV diesel: ~190–220 gCO₂/km
  - HCV diesel: ~700–800 gCO₂/km
- **Carbon credit framing:** 1 tonne CO₂ saved ≈ **1 carbon credit**; market price ≈ **₹800–₹1,500/credit** (2024).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strategies for Reducing Environmental Footprint
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT633 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Strategies for Reducing Environmental Footprint # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Vehicle Transport and Fleet Management**, the **Environmental Footprint (EF)** of a fleet is formally defined as the cumulative measure of quantifiable environmental impacts generated directly or indirectly by a vehicle fleet across its entire operational lifecycle. It is quantified through three primary indicator families: 1. **Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Emissions** — measured in grams of **Carbon Dioxide Equivalent (gCO₂e)** per kilometer or per tonne-kilometer. 2. **Criteria Air Pollutant (CAP) Emissions** — measured in grams per kilometer of **NOx, PM (Particulate Matter), HC (Hydrocarbons), and CO**. 3. **Resource Consumption** — quantified as **fuel liters per 100 km**, **tire wear particles**, and **end-of-life vehicle (ELV) waste**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examiner Wording):** > "Strategies for Reducing Environmental Footprint" refer to the integrated set of managerial, technological, operational, and policy-driven interventions implemented by a fleet operator to minimize the negative ecological impact of vehicle operations while maintaining or improving **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** and **Service Level Agreements (SLAs)**. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Lung of the City" Imagine a large commercial fleet as the **circulatory system of a city** — trucks, vans, and buses are the red blood cells, fuel is the oxygen, and roads are the arteries. Every inefficient vehicle, every unnecessary kilometer, and every drop of wasted diesel is equivalent to **"dirty blood"** entering the city's lungs. - If the fleet uses **clean fuel** (electric/hydrogen) → the blood becomes oxygen-rich, healing the lungs. - If the fleet uses **route optimization + eco-driving** → the heart pumps more efficiently, requiring less oxygen. - If the fleet **regularly maintains tires & engines** → the arteries stay clear, no clots (emissions) form. The "Environmental Footprint" is the **shadow** this circulatory system casts on the atmosphere — reducing it means making every drop of fuel count. ## 1.3 Primary Pollutant Categories & Standard Units > [!NOTE] > **Core Pollutants — KTU Board Favorites** | Pollutant | Symbol | Source in Vehicles | Health Impact | Standard Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | Carbon Dioxide | CO₂ | Fuel combustion (1 L diesel ≈ **2.68 kg CO₂**) | Global warming | **gCO₂/km** | | Carbon Monoxide | CO | Incomplete combustion | Toxic to hemoglobin | **g/km** | | Nitrogen Oxides | NOx | High-temperature combustion | Smog, respiratory illness | **g/km** | | Particulate Matter | PM₂.₅ / PM₁₀ | Diesel soot | Lung penetration | **mg/km** | | Unburnt Hydrocarbons | HC | Fuel evaporation, misfiring | Carcinogenic (Benzene) | **g/km** | | Sulphur Dioxide | SO₂ | Sulphur in fuel/diesel | Acid rain | **g/km** | ## 1.4 The Strategic Pyramid of Environmental Footprint Reduction The KTU 2024 syllabus frames reduction strategies as a **four-tier hierarchical pyramid**: 1. **Tier 1 — Avoidance**: Eliminate the trip (telepresence, consolidation). 2. **Tier 2 — Mode Shift**: Switch to lower-impact transport (rail, sea, EV). 3. **Tier 3 — Operational Efficiency**: Eco-driving, route optimization, load factor improvement. 4. **Tier 4 — Technology Substitution**: Cleaner fuels, hybrid/electric drivetrains, BS-VI/Euro 6 vehicles. > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Heuristic:** Whenever a board question asks "List the strategies," always answer in the **Avoidance → Technology** sequence. This matches KTU's **Module Outcome (MO4.3)** cognitive expectation of *"Design sustainable fleet operations."* # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Strategic Framework — The 8 Pillars of Footprint Reduction The KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes footprint reduction strategies into **8 operational pillars**. Each pillar is explained with its **'Why'** (rationale) and **'How'** (mechanism): ### Pillar 1: Fleet Right-Sizing & Right-Typing - **Why**: Larger-than-needed vehicles consume disproportionate fuel per tonne-km. - **How**: Match vehicle GVW (Gross Vehicle Weight) and cargo volume to typical load profiles. Replace 16-tonne trucks used at 30% load with 7.5-tonne or LCVs. ### Pillar 2: Alternative Fuels & Propulsion - **Why**: Decarbonize the energy vector itself, not just efficiency. - **How**: Adopt CNG (methane), LNG, biodiesel (FAME), ethanol (E85/E100), or fully electric (BEV) drivetrains. ### Pillar 3: Vehicle Technology Upgrade - **Why**: Engines meeting **BS-VI / Euro 6** norms have after-treatment (SCR, DPF, EGR) reducing NOx by ~70% vs BS-IV. - **How**: Phase out pre-BS-IV vehicles; install **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** and **Diesel Particulate Filters (DPF)**. ### Pillar 4: Route Optimization & Load Consolidation - **Why**: Empty running ("deadheading") can account for **20–40%** of fleet km. - **How**: Use **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)** algorithms; consolidate backhauls. ### Pillar 5: Eco-Driving & Driver Behavior - **Why**: Aggressive driving increases fuel use by **20–30%**. - **How**: Training, telematics-driven feedback, speed governors (limit to **80 km/h**). ### Pillar 6: Tire & Aerodynamic Management - **Why**: At 80 km/h, ~50% of engine energy overcomes aerodynamic drag. - **How**: Low rolling-resistance tires (LRRT), side skirts, roof deflectors, trailer boat-tails. ### Pillar 7: Idling Reduction - **Why**: 1 hour of idling ≈ **0.8–1.5 L of diesel** wasted. - **How**: Auto-stop-start systems, anti-idling policies, auxiliary power units (APUs). ### Pillar 8: Monitoring, Reporting & Verification (MRV) - **Why**: "You cannot manage what you do not measure." - **How**: Telematics, **ISO 14001** Environmental Management System, **GLEC Framework** for Scope 1 emissions. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Footprint Quantification > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 ESE & Series Exams** | # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | CO₂ from Fuel | $E_{CO_2} = F \times FEF_{fuel}$ | F = fuel (L), FEF = fuel emission factor | Direct emissions (Scope 1) | | 2 | CO₂ from Distance | $E_{CO_2} = D \times e_{CO_2}$ | D = distance (km), e = specific emission (g/km) | Per-km benchmarking | | 3 | CO₂ from Energy | $E_{CO_2} = E \times EF_{grid}$ | E = electricity (kWh), EF = grid factor (kgCO₂/kWh) | EV fleet calculations | | 4 | Fuel Emission Factors | $FEF_{diesel} = 2.68$ kg CO₂/L, $FEF_{petrol} = 2.31$ kg CO₂/L | Standard Indian/GHG Protocol values | Default inputs | | 5 | Tonne-Kilometer Emissions | $EI = \frac{E_{total}}{Payload_{tonnes} \times Distance_{km}}$ | EI = emission intensity (gCO₂e/tkm) | Logistics efficiency | | 6 | Eco-Driving Savings | $\Delta F = F_{base} \times (1 - k_{eco})$ | k = eco-driving reduction factor (0.10–0.30) | Savings estimation | | 7 | Deadhead Penalty | $DH = \frac{D_{empty}}{D_{total}} \times 100$ | DH = deadhead % | Routing efficiency | | 8 | Load Factor Penalty | $LF = \frac{P_{actual}}{P_{max}} \times 100$ | LF = load factor % | Tonnage efficiency | | 9 | BS-VI NOx Reduction | $NOx_{BS6} = 0.30 \times NOx_{BS4}$ | 70% reduction vs BS-IV | Technology upgrade ROI | | 10 | Aerodynamic Drag Force | $F_d = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$ | ρ = air density, Cd = drag coeff, A = frontal area, v = velocity | Speed impact analysis | ## 2.3 Mathematical Foundation — The Carbon Balance Equation The **Total Fleet Carbon Footprint (TCF)** is computed as a sum of three **GHG Protocol Scopes**: $$ TCF = \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{N} F_i \times FEF_i}_{\text{Scope 1 (Direct)}} + \underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{M} E_j \times EF_{grid,j}}_{\text{Scope 2 (Electricity)}} + \underbrace{\sum_{k=1}^{P} IC_k}_{\text{Scope 3 (Indirect)}} $$ Where: - $F_i$ = fuel consumed by vehicle $i$ (liters) - $FEF_i$ = fuel-specific emission factor - $E_j$ = electricity purchased from grid $j$ - $EF_{grid,j}$ = grid emission intensity (kgCO₂/kWh) - $IC_k$ = upstream indirect emissions (manufacturing, maintenance, end-of-life) > [!TIP] > **Real-World Utility:** This equation is the basis of the **GLEC (Global Logistics Emissions Council) Framework v3.0**, mandatory for any fleet manager reporting under **CDP (Carbon Disclosure Project)** or **Science-Based Targets initiative (SBTi)** compliance. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — CO₂ Emission from Diesel Consumption **Problem Setup:** A fleet operator uses **12,000 liters** of diesel in a quarter. Calculate total Scope 1 CO₂ emissions. **Step 1 — Recall the base equation:** $$ E_{CO_2} = F \times FEF_{diesel} $$ **Step 2 — Identify standard emission factor (IPCC / GHG Protocol):** $$ FEF_{diesel} = 2.68 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{L} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute known values:** $$ E_{CO_2} = 12{,}000 \text{ L} \times 2.68 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{L} $$ **Step 4 — Perform multiplication (long form):** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{CO_2} &= 12{,}000 \times 2.68 \\ &= 12{,}000 \times (2 + 0.6 + 0.08) \\ &= 24{,}000 + 7{,}200 + 960 \\ &= 32{,}160 \text{ kg CO}_2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Convert to tonnes (standard reporting unit):** $$ E_{CO_2} = \frac{32{,}160}{1000} = 32.16 \text{ tCO}_2 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Final Answer:** **32.16 tonnes of CO₂** are released in one quarter. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Emission Intensity (gCO₂e per tonne-kilometer) **Problem Setup:** A truck carries **8 tonnes** of cargo over a distance of **500 km**, consuming **60 L of diesel**. Compute the emission intensity. **Step 1 — Compute total emissions:** $$ E_{CO_2} = 60 \text{ L} \times 2.68 \text{ kg/L} = 160.8 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 2 — Compute tonne-kilometer output:** $$ TKM = 8 \text{ t} \times 500 \text{ km} = 4{,}000 \text{ tkm} $$ **Step 3 — Convert kg to grams:** $$ E_{CO_2} = 160.8 \text{ kg} \times 1{,}000 = 160{,}800 \text{ g} $$ **Step 4 — Compute intensity:** $$ \begin{aligned} EI &= \frac{E_{CO_2}}{TKM} \\ &= \frac{160{,}800 \text{ g}}{4{,}000 \text{ tkm}} \\ &= 40.2 \text{ gCO}_2/\text{tkm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Industry benchmark check:** > [!TIP] > **GLEC Default for Heavy-Duty Diesel Truck in India ≈ 60–100 gCO₂e/tkm.** A value of 40.2 indicates excellent efficiency (likely full load, modern BS-VI vehicle). --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Eco-Driving Fuel Savings with Compound Effect **Problem Setup:** A fleet has **50 vehicles**, each running **40,000 km/year** at **baseline fuel economy of 5 km/L**. If eco-driving improves mileage by **15%**, calculate annual fuel & CO₂ savings. **Step 1 — Baseline fuel consumption per vehicle:** $$ F_{base} = \frac{40{,}000 \text{ km}}{5 \text{ km/L}} = 8{,}000 \text{ L} $$ **Step 2 — Improved fuel economy:** $$ FE_{eco} = 5 \times (1 + 0.15) = 5.75 \text{ km/L} $$ **Step 3 — New fuel consumption per vehicle:** $$ F_{eco} = \frac{40{,}000}{5.75} = 6{,}956.52 \text{ L} $$ **Step 4 — Savings per vehicle:** $$ \Delta F = 8{,}000 - 6{,}956.52 = 1{,}043.48 \text{ L/yr} $$ **Step 5 — Fleet-wide savings:** $$ \Delta F_{fleet} = 50 \times 1{,}043.48 = 52{,}174 \text{ L/yr} $$ **Step 6 — Convert to CO₂ savings:** $$ \Delta CO_2 = 52{,}174 \times 2.68 = 139{,}826.32 \text{ kg} \approx 139.83 \text{ tCO}_2\text{e/yr} $$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Strategy Effectiveness Ranking The following matrix ranks each strategy by **Cost (₹)** vs **CO₂ Reduction (%)** vs **Implementation Time**: | Strategy | CO₂ Reduction Potential | Implementation Cost | Payback Period | Implementation Time | |---|---|---|---|---| | Eco-Driving Training | **8–15%** | Very Low | < 6 months | 1–2 months | | Tire Pressure Management | **2–4%** | Very Low | < 1 month | Immediate | | Route Optimization Software | **5–12%** | Low | 6–12 months | 3–6 months | | Speed Limiting (80 km/h) | **10–20%** | Very Low | < 1 month | Immediate | | CNG/LNG Conversion | **15–25%** | Medium | 2–4 years | 6–12 months | | Hybrid Drivetrain | **20–30%** | High | 5–7 years | 1–2 years | | Full Battery Electric (BEV) | **60–100%** (tailpipe) | Very High | 6–10 years | 2–3 years | | Modal Shift (Road → Rail) | **50–75%** | Low–Medium | 1–2 years | 3–6 months | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** Most board questions test the student's ability to compute the **emission impact of a single intervention** (like eco-driving) and compare it against another (like fuel switch). Always show **both absolute (kg) and relative (%)** reductions. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 4 — EV Grid Emission Equivalent **Problem Setup:** An electric light commercial vehicle consumes **0.25 kWh/km**. India's grid emission factor = **0.72 kgCO₂/kWh**. Compare with a diesel van at **180 gCO₂/km**. **Step 1 — EV emissions per km:** $$ e_{EV} = 0.25 \text{ kWh/km} \times 0.72 \text{ kg/kWh} = 0.18 \text{ kg/km} = 180 \text{ g/km} $$ **Step 2 — Observation:** $$ e_{EV} = e_{diesel} = 180 \text{ g/km} $$ **Step 3 — Critical analysis:** > [!NOTE] > **Both vehicles emit equivalent CO₂ on a "well-to-wheel" basis in India's current grid mix.** However, EVs gain an advantage because: > 1. Grid is decarbonizing (renewables share increasing). > 2. EV has zero tailpipe & zero local pollutants (NOx, PM). > 3. EVs become **< 50 g/km** once grid RE share exceeds 30%. **Step 4 — Conclusion:** EV is a **forward-compatible strategy** even if today's emissions are comparable. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Integrated Footprint Reduction Architecture The following diagram depicts the **integrated functional architecture** of a sustainable fleet management system. It maps the four operational tiers, their interactions, and the feedback loop. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Strategic Policy Layer
ISO 14001 / ESG Goals] --> B[Fleet Planning Layer
Right Sizing and Mode Selection] B --> C[Operational Layer
Routes Drivers Loads] C --> D[Vehicle and Fuel Layer
Engines Fuels Aerodynamics] D --> E[Monitoring Layer
Telematics Emissions Tracking] E --> A A --> F[Scope 1
Direct Tailpipe Emissions] A --> G[Scope 2
Electricity Purchased] A --> H[Scope 3
Indirect and Upstream] B --> I[Vehicle Routing Problem VRP] B --> J[Load Consolidation Engine] C --> K[Eco Driving Training] C --> L[Idling Control Policies] D --> M[BS VI and Euro 6 Engines] D --> N[Alternative Fuels CNG LNG BEV] D --> O[Aero Devices LRRT Tires] E --> P[GLEC Framework Reporting] E --> Q[CDP and SBTi Disclosure] style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff style D fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000,color:#000 style E fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Strategy Selection Decision Tree This flowchart guides a fleet manager on **how to select the right strategy** based on fleet size and trip profile. ```mermaid flowchart TD S0[Fleet Operator Identifies High Emissions] --> S1{Annual km Traveled} S1 -- Greater Than 50000 km per vehicle --> S2{Has Return to Base Charging} S1 -- Less Than 50000 km per vehicle --> S3{Operates in Urban Area} S2 -- Yes --> S4[Strategy Battery Electric Vehicle BEV] S2 -- No --> S5[Strategy Plug in Hybrid PHEV] S3 -- Yes --> S6{Refuelling Network Available} S3 -- No --> S7[Strategy Diesel BS VI with Route Optimization] S6 -- CNG Available --> S8[Strategy CNG Conversion] S6 -- Only Diesel Available --> S9[Strategy Eco Driving plus LRRT Tires] S4 --> S10[Target Zero Tailpipe] S5 --> S10 S8 --> S11[Target Twenty Percent CO2 Cut] S9 --> S11 S7 --> S11 style S0 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style S4 fill:#00b050,stroke:#000,color:#fff style S5 fill:#92d050,stroke:#000,color:#000 style S8 fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000 style S9 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Telematics-Driven Eco-Driving Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Driver Action] --> A2[Vehicle CAN Bus Telemetry] A2 --> A3[Telematics Processor Edge] A3 --> A4[Cloud Analytics Engine] A4 --> A5{Performance Threshold Breach} A5 -- Yes --> A6[Coaching Alert to Driver] A5 -- No --> A7[Continue Baseline Tracking] A6 --> A1 A7 --> A4 A4 --> A8[GLEC and ISO 14001 Reporting] A8 --> A9[Management Review Meeting] style A1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style A4 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style A6 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style A9 fill:#00b050,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.4 Schematic — Avoidance-to-Technology Hierarchy (Mermaid Pyramid) ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Tier 1 Avoid the Trip
Telepresence and Consolidation] --> P2 P2[Tier 2 Shift the Mode
Rail Water Electric Pipeline] --> P3 P3[Tier 3 Improve Operations
Eco Driving Routing Load Factor] --> P4 P4[Tier 4 Substitute Technology
Clean Fuels BS VI BEV FCEV] style P1 fill:#00b050,stroke:#000,color:#fff style P2 fill:#92d050,stroke:#000,color:#000 style P3 fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000 style P4 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define "Environmental Footprint" in the context of a commercial vehicle fleet. List its three indicator families as per GHG Protocol. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Environmental Footprint** of a fleet is the cumulative ecological impact generated by vehicle operations, quantified by: 1. **Scope 1 — Direct Emissions:** Tailpipe CO₂, NOx, PM from fuel combustion. 2. **Scope 2 — Indirect Electricity Emissions:** Grid-supplied power for EVs and depots. 3. **Scope 3 — Value-Chain Emissions:** Manufacturing, maintenance, and end-of-life disposal. It is measured in **gCO₂e/km**, **gCO₂e/tkm**, or **kgCO₂e/L of fuel**, depending on the reporting framework. --- ### Question 2: What is "Deadheading" and how does it affect the environmental footprint? **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Deadheading** refers to operating a vehicle **without any payload** (e.g., returning empty after a delivery). It directly inflates the environmental footprint by: - Increasing **fuel burned per tonne-km** (denominator shrinks, numerator grows). - Empirically accounting for **20–40%** of total fleet km in poorly optimized fleets. - Mitigation strategies include **backhaul matching, load consolidation, and dynamic routing**. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### 📘 Question A (14 Marks): Eco-Driving and Route Optimization **(a)** A logistics company operates **80 diesel trucks** (BS-IV), each running **60,000 km/year** at **4.5 km/L** mileage. Calculate the **total annual CO₂ emissions** using the standard emission factor (2.68 kg CO₂/L). **[7 Marks]** **(b)** If the company implements **eco-driving training** that improves mileage by **18%** and **route optimization** that reduces total km by **8%**, compute the **new annual CO₂ emissions** and the **percentage reduction achieved**. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Baseline Emissions:** **Step 1 — Fuel per vehicle:** $$ F_{base} = \frac{60{,}000}{4.5} = 13{,}333.33 \text{ L/yr} $$ **Step 2 — Total fleet fuel:** $$ F_{fleet} = 80 \times 13{,}333.33 = 1{,}066{,}666.67 \text{ L/yr} $$ **Step 3 — CO₂ emissions:** $$ E_{base} = 1{,}066{,}666.67 \times 2.68 = 2{,}858{,}666.67 \text{ kg} \approx 2858.67 \text{ tCO}_2 $$ **Part (b) — Post-Intervention Emissions:** **Step 1 — New mileage after eco-driving:** $$ FE_{new} = 4.5 \times 1.18 = 5.31 \text{ km/L} $$ **Step 2 — New distance per vehicle (after 8% route cut):** $$ D_{new} = 60{,}000 \times 0.92 = 55{,}200 \text{ km} $$ **Step 3 — New fuel per vehicle:** $$ F_{new} = \frac{55{,}200}{5.31} = 10{,}395.48 \text{ L/yr} $$ **Step 4 — Total fleet fuel:** $$ F_{fleet,new} = 80 \times 10{,}395.48 = 831{,}638.42 \text{ L/yr} $$ **Step 5 — New CO₂ emissions:** $$ E_{new} = 831{,}638.42 \times 2.68 = 2{,}228{,}790.97 \text{ kg} \approx 2228.79 \text{ tCO}_2 $$ **Step 6 — Absolute & Percentage Reduction:** $$ \Delta CO_2 = 2858.67 - 2228.79 = 629.88 \text{ tCO}_2\text{e/yr} $$ $$ \text{Reduction \%} = \frac{629.88}{2858.67} \times 100 = 22.03\% $$ > [!TIP] > **Final Answer:** Fleet emissions drop from **2858.67 tCO₂** to **2228.79 tCO₂**, a reduction of **~22%** and **~630 tonnes of CO₂ saved annually**. --- ### 📘 Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Fuels and Modal Shift **(a)** Explain with proper reasoning why **CNG** is considered a cleaner alternative to diesel for urban commercial fleets. Compare emissions of CO, NOx, PM, and CO₂. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A 10-tonne truck runs **80,000 km/year** carrying an average **5-tonne load**. Diesel fuel economy = **4 km/L**. Compare the **emission intensity (gCO₂e/tkm)** against a **rail-based alternative** transporting the same freight, which emits **25 gCO₂e/tkm**. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — CNG vs Diesel Comparison:** | Pollutant | Diesel (g/km) | CNG (g/km) | Reduction | |---|---|---|---| | CO | 0.50 | 0.20 | **60%** | | NOx | 5.00 | 1.50 | **70%** | | PM | 0.03 | 0.001 | **97%** | | CO₂ | 250 | 180 | **28%** | **Reasoning:** CNG (methane, CH₄) combusts at a **lower carbon-to-hydrogen ratio**, producing less CO₂ per unit of energy. It has **higher octane rating** (120 vs 40 for diesel) enabling lean burn. No soot formation due to **gaseous fuel atomization**. Result: Significantly cleaner urban fleet. **Part (b) — Emission Intensity Comparison:** **Step 1 — Truck fuel consumption:** $$ F = \frac{80{,}000}{4} = 20{,}000 \text{ L/yr} $$ **Step 2 — Truck CO₂ emissions:** $$ E_{truck} = 20{,}000 \times 2.68 = 53{,}600 \text{ kg} = 53.6 \text{ t} $$ **Step 3 — Truck tonne-km output:** $$ TKM_{truck} = 5 \text{ t} \times 80{,}000 \text{ km} = 400{,}000 \text{ tkm} $$ **Step 4 — Truck emission intensity:** $$ EI_{truck} = \frac{53{,}600 \times 1000}{400{,}000} = 134 \text{ gCO}_2\text{e/tkm} $$ **Step 5 — Rail intensity (given):** $$ EI_{rail} = 25 \text{ gCO}_2\text{e/tkm} $$ **Step 6 — Comparison:** $$ \text{Ratio} = \frac{EI_{truck}}{EI_{rail}} = \frac{134}{25} = 5.36 $$ > [!TIP] > **Conclusion:** The diesel truck emits **5.36 times more CO₂ per tonne-km** than rail. Modal shift to rail for this freight would reduce emissions by **81.3%**, representing a powerful environmental footprint reduction strategy. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic** > 1. **Unit Mismatch (–1 mark):** Writing CO₂ in kg when question demands tonnes, or vice versa. **Always convert explicitly.** > 2. **Wrong FEF (–1 mark):** Using 2.31 (petrol) instead of 2.68 (diesel). Memorize: **Diesel = 2.68, Petrol = 2.31, CNG = 2.00 kgCO₂/kg**. > 3. **Confusing Scope 1/2/3 (–2 marks):** Writing "EV has zero emissions" instead of "EV has zero tailpipe, but grid-equivalent Scope 2 emissions." > 4. **Skipping the percentage comparison (–1 mark):** Board examiners want both **absolute and relative** reductions. > 5. **Omitting strategies in logical order:** Always present strategies as **Avoidance → Technology**, not randomly. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4** - **Environmental Footprint = Scope 1 + Scope 2 + Scope 3 emissions**, measured in **gCO₂e**. - **Diesel FEF = 2.68 kg CO₂/L**; **Petrol FEF = 2.31 kg CO₂/L**; **CNG FEF = 2.00 kg CO₂/kg**. - **Eight Pillars**: Right-Sizing, Alt Fuels, Vehicle Tech, Routing, Eco-Driving, Tires/Aero, Idling, MRV. - **Eco-driving** saves **8–15%** fuel; **BS-VI** reduces NOx by **~70%** vs BS-IV. - **Deadheading** can account for **20–40%** of fleet km; route optimization is the prime counter. - **EVs vs Diesel**: Comparable today on India's coal-heavy grid (~180 g/km both), but EVs become cleaner as grid decarbonizes. - **Modal shift (road → rail)** can reduce tonne-km emissions by **60–80%**. - **Tonne-km metric** is the gold standard for logistics emission intensity. - **ISO 14001** + **GLEC Framework v3.0** are the two mandatory MRV tools for global fleets. - **Aerodynamic drag ∝ v²** — speed limiters at 80 km/h can save **10–20%** fuel on highways. - **KTU Cognitive Levels tested**: L2 (Understand definitions), L3 (Apply formulas), L4 (Analyze trade-offs), L5 (Evaluate strategies).

topics.title: Green Fleet Management Practices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT633 - VEHICLE TRANSPORT AND FLEET MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Green Fleet Management Practices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Green Fleet Management** is the strategic, operational, and technological orchestration of a vehicle fleet to minimise its environmental footprint across the entire lifecycle — from procurement and fuelling to utilisation, maintenance, and end-of-life disposal — while simultaneously optimising Total Cost of Ownership (TCO), regulatory compliance, and corporate sustainability commitments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEAUT633 / Module 4):**
> *Green Fleet Management Practices* encompass the systematic deployment of alternative-propulsion technologies, eco-driving protocols, low-emission fuels, route optimisation algorithms, idle-reduction policies, and lifecycle emissions accounting to align fleet operations with national (BS-VI) and international (Euro VI) emission norms, the Paris Agreement NDC targets, and UN SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation & Infrastructure) and SDG 13 (Climate Action).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Eco-House" of a Fleet

Imagine a household trying to reduce its electricity bill. The family does four things:
1. **Replaces bulbs** with LEDs → equivalent to swapping diesel for **CNG/electric** powertrains.
2. **Insulates the roof** so the AC works less → equivalent to **aerodynamic retrofits** and **low-rolling-resistance tyres**.
3. **Turns off the geyser when not in use** → equivalent to **idle-reduction** policies on trucks.
4. **Plans grocery trips to combine errands** → equivalent to **route optimisation** and **load consolidation**.

A fleet manager does *exactly the same thing*, but with hundreds of vehicles producing **tonnes of CO₂ per year**.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Green Fleet Management

| Pillar | Focus Area | KTU-aligned Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Pillar A — Technology** | Alternative fuels & drivetrains | EVs, CNG, LNG, FCEVs |
| **Pillar B — Operations** | Driver behaviour & routing | Eco-driving, GPS routing |
| **Pillar C — Strategy** | Policy, measurement & reporting | Carbon accounting, BS-VI |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Metric — The "1 % Rule":** A reduction of just **1 %** in fleet fuel consumption translates to an approximate **2.5 %** improvement in operating profit for logistics fleets (ATRI, 2024). This is why even small green-fleet practices yield disproportionately large financial returns.

### 1.4 Key Environmental Metrics Used in Green Fleet Practice

- **CO₂e (Carbon Dioxide Equivalent):** Normalises all greenhouse gases into a single CO₂-weighed number.
- **g CO₂/km (Tailpipe emissions intensity):** Standard BS-VI passenger-car benchmark: **< 95 g CO₂/km**.
- **Well-to-Wheel (WTW) emissions:** Accounts for fuel production + vehicle use.
- **Tonne-Kilometre (tkm):** Productivity metric = load carried × distance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Green Fleet Operational Framework (6-Stage Logic Flow)

A KTU examiner expects students to know that green-fleet management is **not a single action** but a **closed-loop cycle**:

1. **Audit the Baseline** → measure current fuel use, km, g CO₂/km, idle hours.
2. **Set SMART Targets** → e.g., *reduce fleet CO₂ by 15 % in 24 months*.
3. **Choose Levers** → fuel switch OR driver training OR telematics OR route algorithm.
4. **Deploy Technology** → install GPS, OBD-II dongles, fuel cards with CO₂ analytics.
5. **Monitor KPIs Continuously** → monthly dashboard review.
6. **Re-Audit & Iterate** → the famous **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** loop.

### 2.2 Core "Why" Behind Each Lever

- **Why switch fuels?** → Tailpipe emissions of CO drop from **2.6 kg/L (diesel)** to **~ 0.04 kg/L (hydrogen FCEV)** — a **65×** reduction.
- **Why eco-drive?** → Aggressive driving inflates fuel use by **20–30 %**.
- **Why tyre-pressure checks?** → A 10 % under-inflation raises rolling resistance by **~ 3 %**, costing fuel and increasing CO₂.
- **Why route optimisation?** → A 10 % shorter route + 10 % less idling ≈ **5–8 %** fuel savings.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** a KTU 2024 student is expected to recall for ESE Module 4.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $CO_{2e} = m_{fuel} \times EF_{fuel}$ | $m_{fuel}$ = fuel mass (kg), $EF$ = emission factor (kg CO₂/kg fuel) | Converts fuel burned into CO₂-equivalent emissions |
| 2 | $C_{specific} = \dfrac{CO_{2}}{VKT}$ | $VKT$ = Vehicle-Kilometres Travelled | Fleet emission intensity in g CO₂/km |
| 3 | $W_{specific} = \dfrac{m_{payload}}{VKT}$ | $m_{payload}$ in tonnes | Logistics productivity in tkm |
| 4 | $\eta_{fleet} = \dfrac{\sum VKT_{actual}}{\sum VKT_{baseline}} \times 100$ | Fleet utilisation ratio | Measures route optimisation gains |
| 5 | $TCO = C_{cap} + C_{fuel} + C_{maint} + C_{ins} + C_{depr} + C_{reg}$ | Sum of all ownership cost components | Justifies EV vs ICE financial case |
| 6 | $BSFC = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{P_{engine}}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (g/kWh) | Engine efficiency benchmark |
| 7 | $P_{roll} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$ | Rolling resistance power | Energy needed to overcome tyres |
| 8 | $P_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_{d} \cdot A \cdot v^{3}$ | Aerodynamic drag power | Energy needed to push air; **cubic** in velocity |
| 9 | $\Delta f_{fuel} \approx 0.5 \cdot \left(\dfrac{\Delta v}{v}\right)^{2} \cdot 100$ | Fuel penalty of speed excursions | Quantifies eco-driving savings |
| 10 | $E_{battery} = \dfrac{VKT \cdot C_{specific}}{SOC_{usable}}$ | EV battery sizing equation | Determines kWh capacity needed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values for KTU Problems:**
> - Density of diesel $\rho_{d} \approx$ **0.832 kg/L**; gasoline $\approx$ **0.745 kg/L**.
> - $EF_{diesel} =$ **3.169 kg CO₂/kg** (IPCC Tier 1 default).
> - $EF_{CNG} =$ **2.750 kg CO₂/kg**; $EF_{electricity, India grid} \approx$ **0.72 kg CO₂/kWh** (CEA, 2023).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Logistics companies** (Amazon, DHL, Delhivery) use Formula 5 to choose between **CNG trucks vs EVs** for last-mile.
- **Municipal corporations** use Formula 2 to comply with the **National Clean Air Programme (NCAP)**.
- **Public-sector bus operators** (KSRTC, BMTC) use Formula 3 to argue for higher fares based on productivity.
- **Car OEMs** use Formula 8 to justify the **cubic penalty** of high-speed driving in owner manuals.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Aerodynamic Drag Impact on Fuel Consumption

The aerodynamic power required to overcome air resistance grows with the **cube of velocity**. This is the single most important reason eco-driving matters.

**Starting point — Newton's drag equation:**

$$
F_{d} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_{d} \cdot A \cdot v^{2}
$$

**Step 1:** Multiply both sides by velocity $v$ to convert force into power.

$$
P_{aero} = F_{d} \cdot v = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_{d} \cdot A \cdot v^{3}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute standard air density $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$, typical sedan $C_{d} = 0.30$, frontal area $A = 2.2 \text{ m}^{2}$.

$$
P_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.2 \cdot v^{3}
$$

**Step 3:** Carry out the constant multiplication.

$$
P_{aero} = 0.404 \cdot v^{3} \;\; \text{[W, with v in m/s]}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert v from km/h to m/s by $v_{ms} = v_{kmh} / 3.6$ and re-express in **kW**.

$$
P_{aero} = 0.404 \cdot \left(\dfrac{v_{kmh}}{3.6}\right)^{3} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1000} = 8.65 \times 10^{-6} \cdot v_{kmh}^{3} \;\; \text{[kW]}
$$

**Step 5 (Engineering Insight):** Doubling speed from **60 → 120 km/h** multiplies $P_{aero}$ by **2³ = 8**.

$$
P_{aero}(120) = 8 \times P_{aero}(60)
$$

**Conclusion:** A truck cruising at **80 km/h instead of 100 km/h** consumes roughly **~ 49 % less aerodynamic energy**, which directly becomes fuel savings of **~ 12–15 %** in real fleet trials.

---

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — CO₂ Reduction from Fuel Switch

**Problem (KTU-typical 7-mark style):**
*A logistics fleet of **50 trucks**, each running **40,000 km/year** at **6 km/L diesel**, is being evaluated for conversion to **CNG**. Calculate the annual CO₂ reduction. Assume $EF_{diesel}=3.169$ kg CO₂/kg, $EF_{CNG}=2.750$ kg CO₂/kg, $\rho_{diesel}=0.832$ kg/L.*

**Step 1: Per-truck annual fuel volume.**

$$
V_{fuel} = \dfrac{40{,}000 \text{ km}}{6 \text{ km/L}} = 6{,}666.67 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 2: Per-truck annual fuel mass.**

$$
m_{diesel} = 6{,}666.67 \times 0.832 = 5{,}546.67 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3: Per-truck annual CO₂ — Diesel baseline.**

$$
CO_{2,d} = 5{,}546.67 \times 3.169 = 17{,}577.4 \text{ kg} = 17.58 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 4: Per-truck annual CO₂ — CNG (assume CNG gives same mileage on energy-equivalent basis, mass conversion factor applied by the OEM).**

For simplicity, if CNG replaces diesel energy-for-energy:

$$
CO_{2,CNG} = 17.58 \times \dfrac{2.750}{3.169} = 15.26 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 5: Per-truck annual reduction.**

$$
\Delta CO_{2} = 17.58 - 15.26 = 2.32 \text{ t/truck/year}
$$

**Step 6: Fleet-wide reduction.**

$$
\Delta CO_{2,fleet} = 2.32 \times 50 = 116 \text{ t CO₂/year}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This **116-tonne saving** is equivalent to taking approximately **25 passenger cars off the road** for one year (each average car ≈ **4.6 t CO₂/year**).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Alternative Fuel Choice

| Criterion | Diesel | CNG | LNG | Biodiesel B20 | Electric (BEV) | Hydrogen FCEV |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tailpipe CO₂** | Baseline | −15 % | −12 % | −18 % | **0 %** | **0 %** |
| **NOx vs BS-VI** | Compliant | Lower | Lower | Similar | **0** | **0** |
| **Energy Density (MJ/L)** | 35.8 | 22.2 | 22.2 | 33.0 | 0.9 | 8.5 |
| **Refuel Time** | 5 min | 5 min | 8 min | 5 min | **60–90 min (DC)** | **5–10 min** |
| **Range** | 800 km | 400 km | 800 km | 800 km | **250–500 km** | **500–700 km** |
| **Capex Premium** | × 1 | × 1.3 | × 1.4 | × 1.05 | **× 2.5** | **× 3.5** |
| **KTU Recommendation** | Baseline | City bus / LCV | Long-haul HGV | Pilot | Urban delivery | Future HGV |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners frequently test the **"no zero-emission vehicle is truly zero"** point. An EV's *well-to-wheel* emissions depend on the **grid mix** (coal-heavy Indian grid ≈ **~ 700 g CO₂/kWh**, renewables-heavy ≈ **< 100 g CO₂/kWh**).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Green Fleet Management — Closed-Loop Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Baseline Fleet Audit] --> B[Set SMART Sustainability Targets]
    B --> C[Select Green Levers]
    C --> D[Technology Deployment]
    C --> E[Driver Training]
    C --> F[Route Optimization]
    D --> G[Continuous KPI Monitoring]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{Re-audit vs Target}
    H -- No --> I[Corrective Action]
    I --> C
    H -- Yes --> J[Certification and Reporting]
    J --> A
```

### 4.2 Green Fleet Hierarchy — Technology → Operations → Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph StratLayer[Strategic Layer]
        S1[Carbon Accounting]
        S2[BS-VI and Euro VI Compliance]
        S3[ESG and SDG 13 Reporting]
    end
    subgraph OpsLayer[Operational Layer]
        O1[Eco Driving Training]
        O2[Idle Reduction Policy]
        O3[Predictive Maintenance]
        O4[Tyre Pressure Monitoring]
    end
    subgraph TechLayer[Technology Layer]
        T1[Alternative Fuels CNG LNG Biodiesel]
        T2[Battery Electric Vehicles BEV]
        T3[Hydrogen FCEV]
        T4[Telematics and IoT Sensors]
    end

    OpsLayer --> TechLayer
    TechLayer --> StratLayer
```

### 4.3 Emissions Computation Pipeline (Data-Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fuel Card Data] --> D[Emissions Engine]
    B[OBD II Telematics] --> D
    C[Vehicle Master File] --> D
    D --> E[CO2 per Vehicle km]
    D --> F[Fleet Aggregated CO2e]
    D --> G[Driver Behaviour Score]
    E --> H[Sustainability Dashboard]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Regulatory Filing NCAP and MoEFCC]
```
